Document 162 of 500

FIRST READING: Another 'victory' declared by Canada's increasingly brazen anti-Israel movement

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-another-victory-declared-canadas/docview/2937607059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Another+%27victory%27+declared+by+Canada%27s+increasingly+brazen+anti-Israel+movement&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
From the beginning, Canada's anti-Israel movement has been shot through with extremist elements. Organizers such as Palestinian Youth Movement, Toronto4Palestine and Samidoun all openly endorsed the Oct. 7 massacre and called for the violent destruction of Israel, but they initially kept their public actions to street demonstrations and rallies.
But after nearly six months of meeting little to no police pushback, the activities have grown demonstrably more brazen, with illegal blockades, intimidation and open antisemitism now a regular feature of the movement.
This reached a new plateau over the weekend when an event at the Art Gallery of Ontario featuring Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Italian counterpart was forced to close due to a deliberate blockade of the entrances by demonstrators wearing keffiyehs and waving Palestinian flags.
"Tonight was a victory!" declared a Sunday Instagram post by protest organizer Palestinian Youth Movement Toronto. The blockade had been publicly advertised as an "Emergency Action," with materials referring to Trudeau as "Genocide Justin."
A post shared by PYM Toronto (@pymtoronto)
Palestinian Youth Movement is the same group who, on Oct. 7, praised Hamas's indiscriminate massacres in Southern Israel as the "active decolonization of Palestinian land" They were instrumental in the first wave of celebratory "All Out for Palestine" rallies held in the immediate aftermath of the attacks.
Emboldened by the cancellation of the Trudeau/Meloni event, on Sunday PYM Toronto warned of more blockades to come. "We will continue to come out and SHUT IT DOWN until our demands are met," they wrote.
The post also accused the Art Gallery of Ontario of being "complicit in genocide" by continuing to accept Jewish donations, which they branded as "Zionist funding."
Trudeau and his cabinet have long had their itineraries deliberately targeted by anti-Israel protests, but Saturday is the first time that law enforcement effectively lost control of the situation.
"The location was not secure. And that was their objective. They don't want their fellow Canadians to feel safe," said Toronto-area Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, in a series of social media posts reacting to the cancellation. He referred to the demonstrators as "thugs" and "disgusting antisemitic protesters" who were "screaming, shoving and spitting at seniors."
Prior incidents targeting Trudeau have included a Feb. 15 incident in which demonstrators aligned with the Palestinian Youth Movement were able get past Parliamentary security and briefly disrupt a sitting of the House of Commons - an action that was applauded by several members of the NDP caucus.
In mid-November, 100 Vancouver Police officers had to be dispatched to rescue Trudeau from a steakhouse that had its entrances barred by up to 250 anti-Israel demonstrators. In December, a similar-sized crowd attempted to force the cancellation of a Trudeau fundraiser at Vancouver's Westin Bayshore hotel.
Anti-Israel demonstrations have also taken a noticeable turn towards targeting Jewish sites with no apparent connection to Israel or the war in Gaza.
Just hours after the cancelled Trudeau/Meloni event, anti-Israel protesters massed outside a Thornhill synagogue.
Video posted by protest-watcher Caryma Sa'd shows an anti-Israel demonstrator appear to attempt to strike a police officer. When officers attempt to take the woman into custody, however, they are surrounded by demonstrators shouting, "Let her go." The officers are overwhelmed and the woman is carried away from police by her comrades.
Protester apprehended moments after allegedly being spat on by pro-Israel counter protester.

Other protesters intervene to demand her release. They form a barrier around her once she is extricated, chanting:

- Caryma Sa'd - Lawyer + Political Satirist (@CarymaRules) March 3, 2024
Last month, leaders from all major federal parties denounced the "antisemitism" of anti-Israel demonstrators who massed outside Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital - a facility founded by Jewish doctors that still carries a Star of David in its logo.
At the beginning of this year, Toronto's Avenue Road overpass became subject to a weeks-long blockade by Palesign, a registered non-profit whose actions have included an illegal stoppage of traffic on the Gardiner Expressway, and masked mobs blocking the entrances to a Toronto Zara location owned by a Canadian-Israeli.
The only apparent significance of the Avenue Road overpass was that it is the primary conduit into Canada's densest Jewish neighbourhood. "Zionism once again fails to break the spirit of Palestinians," read a post by Palesign after the blockade spurred counter-demonstrations from locals carrying Israeli flags. In a video, one blockader would refer to the overpass as lying within a "Zionist Infested Area."

HAPPENING NOW: An aggressive and physically intimidating mob is surrounding the Jewish community's central building in Cote-des-Neiges. The demonstrators are attempting to block access to the building and are harassing those trying to enter....

And just last week, anti-Israel demonstrators on the campus of McGill University would organize a concerted blockade of the Bronfman Building. The building is materially no different than any of the others on campus, save for the fact that it bears the name of Jewish philanthropist Samuel Bronfman.
Organizers included Solidarity for Palestinian Human Rights McGill, another group whose reaction to the Oct. 7 massacres was open jubilation
McGill - like most universities in Canada - has featured no shortage of demonstrations calling for "intifada" or employing slogans calling for Israel's complete destruction. But the Bronfman blockade would prompt a special reaction from administrators, who asserted that this action was "different."
"Our university cannot successfully operate with unpredicted interruptions that disrupt our activities," McGill University president Deep Saini would write in a letter to McGill students.
Although Saini's letter did not call out the protesters' targeting of a Jewish-named building, he did reject their demand that McGill "unilaterally sever its research and academic ties with Israeli institutions."
I used to think of these as black and white images telling a story of my grandparents that I thought was a horror in the past. To see this appearing in colour today on the streets of Toronto - the city where I was born and raised - shakes me to my core.
- Michael Geist (@mgeist) March 4, 2024
IN OTHER NEWS
Just after Canadians were handed a ream of documents detailing the sheer extent of the security breach at the National Microbiology Laboratory in Winnipeg, a coalition of Liberal and NDP MPs have voted down a proposal to immediately do anything about it. On Monday before an emergency session of the House of Commons ethics committee, Conservative MP Michael Chong proposed conducting an immediate probe into the documents' revelations. But Liberal representatives - along with the NDP's Matthew Green - shut down the proposal on the grounds that it wasn't their mandate, that it wasn't "urgent" and that it was "unfair" to propose this during a week on which the House of Commons is supposed to be on break.
This is the aftermath of a January incident where a 28-year-old Commissionaires security guard allegedly roamed around Edmonton City Hall shooting out windows. Although it carried all the hallmarks of an apparent terrorist attack, the motive was somewhat difficult to nail down. A video manifesto by the alleged shooter was an extended rant about everything from "wokeism" to the war in Gaza to the importance of eating healthy and filtering tap water. But this week, Edmonton Police announced that the shooter would be facing terrorism charges on top of his existing charges for arson and gun crimes. Specifically, "counselling commission of a terrorism offence" and "possession of property for terrorist purposes."
With Brian Mulroney gone, the National Post tallied up all the ex-prime ministers that Canada has left. It turns out there are six of them, which means that roughly one quarter of the 23 people who have ever led Canada are still alive. Arguably the most interesting political afterlife belongs to Kim Campbell. Immediately after her crushing defeat in 1993, she married an accomplished Montreal playwright 21 years her junior, and has since lived everywhere from Edmonton to Paris to Los Angeles to Boston.
One of the more dramatic moments of Brian Mulroney's political career was when one of his cabinet ministers, Lucien Bouchard, defected in an attempt to seek Quebec's independence and effectively destroy Canada as we know it (an effort that came remarkably close in 1995). Reportedly, Mulroney's funeral plans once included instructions that if Bouchard showed up, the service was to be stopped. But speaking to TVA last week, an emotional Bouchard said he'd managed to reconcile with his former boss just before the end. "It was a very, very painful rupture that ... shouldn't have happened," said Bouchard.
A recent feature in the Canadian military magazine Esprit de Corps argued that the Canadian Armed Forces are "in even worse shape than you may think." Their gist was that even if the military had proper equipment (it doesn't), there wouldn't be anybody to use it. As recently as 1989, the Canadian Armed Forces had 88,000 regular troops. Now, with a population that's larger by 14 million, "we are hovering around the 60,000 mark," the article reads. With all that in mind, the signature announcement of the Defence Minister this week is that the Edmonton home of the Third Canadian Division will be getting energy-efficient upgrades including LED lighting and "high efficiency water fixtures."

Anti-Israel movement grows more brazen; Demonstrations targeting Jewish sites

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-israel-movement-grows-more-brazen/docview/2937476848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Anti-Israel+movement+grows+more+brazen%3B+Demonstrations+targeting+Jewish+sites&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: From the beginning, Canada's anti-Israel movement has been shot through with extremist elements. Organizers such as Palestinian Youth Movement, Toronto4Palestine and Samidoun all openly endorsed the Oct. 7 massacre and called for the violent destruction of Israel, but they initially kept their public actions to street demonstrations and rallies.
But after nearly six months of meeting little to no police pushback, the activities have grown demonstrably more brazen, with illegal blockades, intimidation and open antisemitism now a regular feature of the movement.
This reached a new plateau Saturday when an event at the Art Gallery of Ontario in Toronto featuring Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Italian counterpart Giorgia Meloni was derailed due to a deliberate blockade of the entrances by demonstrators wearing kaffiyehs and waving Palestinian flags.
"Tonight was a victory!" declared an Instagram post by protest organizer Palestinian Youth Movement Toronto. The blockade had been publicly advertised as an "Emergency Action," with materials referring to Trudeau as "Genocide Justin."
Palestinian Youth Movement is the same group who, on Oct. 7, praised Hamas's indiscriminate massacres in Israel as the "active decolonization of Palestinian land." They were instrumental in the first wave of celebratory "All Out for Palestine" rallies held in the immediate aftermath of the attacks.
Emboldened by the cancellation of the Trudeau/Meloni event, on Sunday PYM Toronto warned of more blockades to come.
"We will continue to come out and SHUT IT DOWN until our demands are met," they wrote.
The post also accused the Art Gallery of Ontario of being "complicit in genocide" by continuing to accept Jewish donations, which they branded as "Zionist funding."
Trudeau and his cabinet have long had their itineraries deliberately targeted by anti-Israel protests, but Saturday is the first time that law enforcement effectively lost control of the situation.
"The location was not secure, and that was their objective. They don't want their fellow Canadians to feel safe," said Toronto-area Liberal MP Marco Mendicino in a series of social media posts reacting to the cancellation. He referred to the demonstrators as "thugs" and "disgusting antisemitic protesters" who were "screaming, shoving and spitting at seniors."
Previous incidents targeting Trudeau include one Feb. 15 where demonstrators aligned with the Palestinian Youth Movement were able get past parliamentary security and briefly disrupt a sitting of the House of Commons - an action applauded by several NDP members.
In mid-November, 100 Vancouver police officers had to be dispatched to rescue Trudeau from a steak house that had its entrances barred by up to 250 anti-Israel demonstrators. In December, a similar-sized crowd attempted to force the cancellation of a Trudeau fundraiser at Vancouver's Westin Bayshore hotel.
Anti-demonstrations have also taken a noticeable turn toward targeting Jewish sites with no apparent connection to Israel or the war in Gaza.
Just hours after the cancelled Trudeau/Meloni event, anti-Israel protesters massed outside a Thornhill, Ont., synagogue.
Video posted by protest-watcher Caryma Sa'd shows an anti-Israel demonstrator appear to attempt to strike a police officer. When officers attempt to take the woman into custody, however, they are surrounded by demonstrators shouting, "Let her go!" The officers are overwhelmed and the woman is carried away.
Last month, leaders from all major federal parties denounced the "antisemitism" of anti-Israel demonstrators who massed outside Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital - a facility founded by Jewish doctors that still carries a Star of David in its logo.
At the beginning of this year, Toronto's Avenue Road overpass became subject to a weeks-long blockade by Palesign, a registered non-profit whose actions have included an illegal stoppage of traffic on the Gardiner Expressway, and masked mobs blocking the entrances to a Toronto Zara location owned by a Canadian-Israeli.
The only apparent significance of the Avenue Road overpass was that it is the primary conduit into Canada's densest Jewish neighbourhood. "Zionism once again fails to break the spirit of Palestinians," said a post by Palesign after the blockade spurred counter-demonstrations from locals carrying Israeli flags. In a video, one blockader would refer to the overpass as lying within a "Zionist infested area."
And just last week, anti-Israel demonstrators on the campus of Montreal's McGill University organized a blockade of the Bronfman Building. The building is materially no different than any of the others on campus, save for the fact that it bears the name of Jewish philanthropist Samuel Bronfman.
Organizers included Solidarity for Palestinian Human Rights McGill, another group whose reaction to the Oct. 7 massacres was open jubilation.
McGill - like most universities in Canada - has featured no shortage of demonstrations calling for "intifada" or employing slogans calling for Israel's complete destruction. But the Bronfman blockade would prompt a special reaction from administrators, who asserted that this action was "different."
"Our university cannot successfully operate with unpredicted interruptions that disrupt our activities," university president Deep Saini would write in a letter to Mc-Gill students.
Although Saini's letter did not call out the protesters'targeting of a Jewish-named building, he did reject their demand that McGill "unilaterally sever its research and academic ties with Israeli institutions."
National Post

Document 164 of 500

Anti-Israel protesters leave Canada red-faced

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-israel-protesters-leave-canada-red-faced/docview/2937476546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Anti-Israel+protesters+leave+Canada+red-faced&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I realized something interesting over the weekend as I watched International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen try in vain, like a bewildered tourist, to find a way to enter the Art Gallery of Ontario.
Hussen, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the great-andgood of Italian-Canadian society were to fete visiting Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni at the AGO, but a few dozen pro-Palestinian supporters managed to block the entrances enough to scupper the whole event.
What I realized was that even after a solid decade, at least, of considering Canada's politics and many of its institutions fundamentally and possibly irredeemably unserious - from our Supreme Court to our National Hockey League franchises - I was still capable of being embarrassed on my country's behalf.
Meloni is a G7 leader. We should be able to host a dinner for her without risk of it being shut down by a few dozen people protesting something that has next-tonothing to do with Canada or Italy. Other G7 leaders might reasonably argue that's something like a prerequisite for membership.
"At least it was only Italy," I found myself chuckling ruefully. And Meloni specifically is not my cup of coffee, with her record of flattering Italian history's Fascists and her antediluvian attitudes toward same-sex unions and parenting. Were Meloni not a current G7 leader, she's the sort of politician Liberals would excoriate Stephen Harper for shaking hands with, never mind throwing her an arty shindig.
But that's false comfort, of course. Canada, a tumbleweed nation in the desert of world affairs, can't afford to make such judgments about more important, more serious countries like Italy. And just about any world leader heading to the AGO on Saturday night, save perhaps the leaders of Hamas itself, would have faced the same basic situation. Trudeau is worthy of cancellation all by himself, by the protesters'logic, on account of his quixotic, semi-consistent insistence that Israel has a right to exist.
The Toronto Police Service (TPS) says it did not advise Trudeau to cancel the event. The TPS says many attendees were already inside, and that it had guaranteed VIPs safe passage through the fray. The RCMP, who are in charge of Trudeau's and visiting dignitaries'safety, claims "the size and volatile nature of the crowds at the entrances to venue (meant) it was not safe to move both prime ministers to the event at the scheduled time."
It's tough to know what to make of that.
If you were the prime minister's security detail - any prime minister's, I mean - would you trust today's TPS to play any role in his or her safety? I sure wouldn't.
On Saturday, at a protest outside a synagogue north of Toronto, a woman was caught dead to rights, on video, striking a York Regional Police officer. She was apprehended by said police officer and other police officers, which I think we can all agree is a reasonable and predictable response to striking a police officer ... but then the assembled chanting rabble convinced the police to let her go.
I carry no water for police in general, or Toronto's or Ontario's in particular. I have, though, defended their general commitment to de-escalating rather than cracking down on potentially violent situations. But that particular catch-and-release scenario was wince-inducing. At the end of the day, none of us wants a police force that can be literally harangued into letting stuff slide.
For many conser vative-minded Canadians, Saturday's abject capitulation to the rabble was a notable data point in an already well-established narrative about the pro-Palestinian protesters, about policing, about the general decline of Canada under Trudeau. I feel that here more than I usually do. But Trudeau doesn't control Ontario's famously hands-off police forces. Premier Doug Ford does that, to the alarmingly limited extent any politician does, but the peculiar nature of Canadian politics and media means he never has to answer for it.
My biggest takeaway from this latest humiliation is a bit different. It's this: How could Trudeau and his office possibly have foreseen any other outcome? The AGO would have been swarmed with pro-Palestinian protesters even if Trudeau had just dropped by to see the Keith Haring exhibit. Had Hamas not massacred 1,200 people on Oct. 7 last year, triggering a predictably remorseless response from Israel, selfstyled anti-fascists would probably have shown up to protest Meloni's presence. And the only predictable response from Toronto police would have been to let them shut down the event. That's what they do.
How utterly out of touch does a prime minister, and the people around him, have to be to have thought this would go off without a hitch? National Post

Document 165 of 500

Rare company; Canada has had 23 prime ministers since 1867.here's a look at the six who are still alive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rare-company-canada-has-had-23-prime-ministers/docview/2937476509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Rare+company%3B+Canada+has+had+23+prime+ministers+since+1867.here%27s+a+look+at+the+six+who+are+still+alive&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since Confederation in 1867, Canada has had 23 different prime ministers. Canada's 10th PM, William Lyon Mackenzie King, was the longest serving, with a tenure of 21 years, 154 days over three non-consecutive terms. Sir Charles Tupper, the sixth PM, had the shortest time in office, serving only 69 days, from May 1, 1896, to July 8, 1896. With the passing of Brian Mulroney, now six people who held the title remain alive. JOE CLARK (SERVED JUNE 4, 1979 - MARCH 3, 1980)
Canada's 16th prime minister and a member of the Progressive Conservative Party, Clark defeated the Liberal government of Pierre Trudeau in the 1979 election.
His brief tenure with a minority government made it challenging to pass legislation and his government was brought down following a non-confidence vote on his first budget, leading to the 1980 election and ultimately Trudeau's return to power.
Clark later served in Mulroney's cabinet as the secretary of state for external affairs from 1984 to 1991 and as the minister responsible for constitutional affairs.
Clark opposed the merger of the Progressive Conservatives with the Conservative Party of Canada and sat as an independent Progressive Conservative until his retirement from Parliament at the end of the 2003 session.
Since then, Clark has worked for a number of non-profits, including the Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars, the Centre for North American Studies, the Jimmy Carter Center, the Global Leadership Foundation and the Centre for International Governance Innovation.
KIM CAMPBELL (SERVED JUNE 25, 1993 - NOV. 4, 1993) Canada's 19th PM, Campbell was the first and, to date, only woman to lead Canada.
A member of the Progressive Conservative Party, Campbell held several cabinet positions in Mulroney's government, including minister of justice and attorney general, and minister of national defence, becoming the first woman to hold these positions as well.
She won the party leadership following Mulroney's resignation and in her brief tenure implemented changes to reduce the size of the federal cabinet and established new laws on sexual assault and firearms control.
Her term ended following an overwhelming defeat in the 1993 federal election, including Campbell losing her Vancouver Centre seat.
Campbell has had an eclectic career post-premiership, including living in Edmonton, Paris, Los Angeles and Boston, where she spent three years teaching at Harvard.
She has also served as the secretary-general for the Club de Madrid, a Spanish non-profit organization.
JEAN CHRÉTIEN (SERVED NOV. 4, 1993 - DEC. 12, 2003)
Canada's 20 th PM, Chrétien's leadership saw the Liberal party win three consecutive majority governments in 1993, 1997 and 2000.
His government handled a number of high-profile events and policy decisions, including the 1995 Quebec referendum and its aftermath, same-sex marriage legislation, criminal justice reform and made efforts to eliminate the national deficit, which led to budget surpluses and debt reduction.
His government also faced controversies such as the sponsorship scandal and the "Shawinigate" affair.
His government also refused to participate in the Iraq War in 2003, a decision that was controversial at the time.
Internal party strife under his leadership eventually led to Chrétien announcing his resignation in 2003. He went on to join the now defunct law firm Heenan Blaikie, where Trudeau worked until his death in 2000. Chrétien later joined Denton's and his work has occasionally put the ex-PM into some controversial territory, such as when a business consortium hired him to lobby for a nuclear waste dump in Labrador in 2021.
PAUL MARTIN (SERVED DEC. 12, 2003 - FEB. 6, 2006) Martin succeeded Chrétien as Canada's 21st PM following his performance as finance minister, where he was credited with eliminating the federal deficit in the 1990s through major spending cuts and reforms to social services.
Martin's leadership saw the negotiation of the Kelowna Accord to improve conditions for Indigenous Peoples, an agreement with provinces on health care funding and the legalization of same-sex marriage.
Like Chrétien, Martin was also marred by the sponsorship scandal. He was defeated by Stephen Harper in the 2006 federal election and chose not to seek re-election in 2008, marking the end of his political career.
Martin remains one of the world's wealthiest former heads of government - a position he attained through his ownership of Canada Steamship Lines.
The end of his political career also signalled the end of the Kelowna Accords, a $5 billion initiative aimed at enhancing Indigenous education and promoting Indigenous entrepreneurship.
Through the Martin Family Initiative, established in 2008, he has focused on providing a wide range of educational programs for Indigenous communities across the country, including prenatal education for Indigenous mothers and courses in business and finance.
STEPHEN HARPER (SERVED FEB. 6, 2006 - NOV. 4, 2015)
Canada's 22nd PM, Stephen Harper was a founding member of the Reform Party of Canada and later became the first leader of the Conservative Party of Canada.
His government introduced an array of tax cuts and largely focused on economic issues, including reducing the Goods and Services Tax and leading Canada through the 2008 global financial crisis.
Harper's foreign policy was marked by a strong support for Israel and skepticism toward the United Nations.
After losing the 2015 federal election to Justin Trudeau, Harper stepped down as leader of the Conservative Party and eventually resigned his seat in the House of Commons in 2016. Post-politics, he founded Harper & Associates Consulting and assumed the role of chairman of the International Democratic Union (IDU) in 2018.
The IDU is a global alliance of centre-right and conservative political parties.
JUSTIN TRUDEAU (SERVING SINCE NOV. 4, 2015)
The 23rd and current PM, Trudeau has been in power for more than eight years.
After being elected as the MP for Papineau in 2008, Trudeau quickly rose up the Liberal party ranks.
He entered the leadership race in 2012, following Michael Ignatieff's resignation from the position.
He won the Liberal leadership in 2013 and ultimately led the Liberals to a majority government in 2015, followed by minority governments in both the 2019 and 2021 elections.
His government has focused on gender-balanced cabinet appointments, environmental commitments and cannabis legalization. His administration has also faced several controversies and scandals, such as the Aga Khan affair, the SNC-Lavalin affair, and the WE Charity scandal, which led to investigations by the ethics commissioner.
National Post

Document 166 of 500

'Safer Supply'Season 1; Parliamentary Committee Hearings On Opioid Crisis Make For Dramatic Viewing

Author: Finkle, Derek

Publication info: Finkle, Derek. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/safer-supplyseason-1-parliamentary-committee/docview/2937476272/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27Safer+Supply%27Season+1%3B+Parliamentary+Committee+Hearings+On+Opioid+Crisis+Make+For+Dramatic+Viewing&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Finkle%2C+Derek&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parliamentar y committees - even those born of public scandal - rarely make for good television. We do not have the same flair for the dramatic as our cousins who practice politics south of the border, where such committees have produced celebrities for decades. That said, a study underway at the House of Commons health committee on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis is getting close to mandatory viewing. Its first five episodes are as entertaining as any non-scripted, government-funded programming can get.
There are several factors cranking up the drama, including the fact that a significant number of Canadians have lost family members and loved ones to the crisis this committee is examining. I count myself among them - one of my closest friends since the age of 10 died of an accidental fentanyl overdose in July 2022. But my interest in the subject does not end there; my family lives across the street from a supervised injection site in Toronto that has become a flashpoint since a mother was killed in a gunfight between drug dealers who habitually do business near the property.
An added dimension of intrigue for this committee is that the opioid crisis and its management have very quickly ramped up to be a polarizing issue that will loom large in the next federal election. The committee's protagonists - a pack of Conservative MPs - belong to the party that is the clear front-runner according to current polls.
The word "pack" is used intentionally, as the Liberal and NDP members of this committee are not shy when it comes to accusing these Conservatives of opportunistic, wolf-like behaviour - such as confronting witnesses in a manner more designed for hits on social media than anything else. The leader of the pack, Dr. Stephen Ellis, an MP from Nova Scotia, has produced more than his fair share of highlights, including a grilling of Jennifer Saxe, an associate assistant deputy minister from the Department of Health, on Dec. 4. This was often painful to watch.
Ellis: The other question I would ask you is: On Canada.ca, it says, "A few grains (of fentanyl) can be enough to kill you." Is that true? Saxe: (long pause) That could be true, yes, depending on someone's - Ellis: It could be true? It's on the Health Canada website. It must be true. Is it there or not? Saxe: Fentanyl could in certain - Ellis: A few grains could kill you. I'm going to badger you a bit on this because it says right on your website - Saxe: Yes. Ellis: Thank you. Wow, that took a lot.
Tell me this, then: What sense is there to decriminalize 2.5 grams of fentanyl (in B.C.) if a few grains could kill you? Ellis and his Conservative colleagues on the committee are particularly committed to attacking the safe supply policy of the Liberal government. This policy, something the Conservatives refer to as an "experiment," involves hydromorphone, a medical-grade opioid, being prescribed by clinicians as an alternative to increasingly deadly street drugs. Ellis echoes the concern of his party's leader, Pierre Poilievre - that there is growing evidence these drugs are being diverted to the street market in significant quantities, thus causing more people (including young people and those who live on reserves) to get addicted to opioids.
Once the Conservatives started putting the phrase "so-called" in front of safe supply, those in the drug policy realm such as Saxe decided it was time for a rebrand. Safe supply suddenly became "prescribed pharmaceutical alternatives." If everyone watching at home on Dec. 4 had downed a shot every time Saxe used that phrase while desperately trying to stick to her talking points under fire, no one would have made it off the couch.
This committee took a significant step toward compulsory viewing status during its fifth meeting on the drug crisis, held Feb. 26. The star witnesses were Dr. Rob Tanguay, an addiction psychiatrist who co-founded Alberta's Rapid Access Addiction Medicine Community Clinic, and Dr. Andrea Sereda, the lead physician of a safer opioids supply program in London, Ont.
While these two doctors share some things in common, they have come to represent two very different approaches to combating the surging opioid crisis.
Tanguay embraces what has become known as the "Alberta model," which favours treatment instead of the Justin Trudeau government's harm reduction policies and the decriminalization path taken in British Columbia. Sereda, on the other hand, is an advocate for the safe supply of prescribed opioids, especially, she claims, for people who have not had success with other forms of treatment.
There was a bit of excitement before Sereda's testimony when Adam Zivo, a columnist with the National Post who has parried with the doctor over his reporting on the illegal diversion of hydromorphone prescriptions, announced on X that he has been investigating Sereda's so-called safer supply program since December and would soon publish a report demonstrating widespread diversion connected to her practice. Zivo even provided the committee members with five proposed questions to put to Sereda in the same post.
Laila Goodridge, the Conservative MP for Fort Mc-Murray and Cold Lake, Alta., decided to have a go with Zivo's questions. Goodridge cited "new information" that corroborated previous reporting about mass diversion of safe supply opioids around the pharmacy near Sereda's clinic and asked the doctor if she was aware of it.
Sereda came prepared. "What evidence has been released showing this?" she asked.
Goodridge had to admit what Sereda already knew - it came from some tweets by Zivo that morning.
With a considerable display of haughty satisfaction, Sereda stated that safe supply clinicians rely on good research and published evidence, not anecdotes and social media posts. (If I had one piece of advice for the Conservatives on the committee, it would be this: Put a little more thought into your questions.)
Within days of Sereda insisting to the committee that tales of diversion were unjustified and the product of right-wing political fearmongering, an Ottawa-based reporter with the CBC published a lengthy exposé about the impact the illicit trade of safe supply drugs is having on a Rideau Street neighbourhood downtown. Not only is a local police officer quoted saying that he and his colleagues regularly "observe people trafficking in diverted hydromorphone" from safer supply providers, a followup by CBC noted that a local drug dealer had propositioned a reporter on the story, offering to sell fentanyl and buy safe supply pills.
If Sereda doesn't view any of this as evidence of diversion, it would be difficult to say the same about another bit of recent news. In a search last month, RC-MP officers in Campbell River, B.C., seized more than two kilograms of fentanyl and roughly 3,500 hydromorphone pills, along with cocaine, methamphetamine and about $30,000 in cash. Based on evidence gathered at the scene, police believe the pills were diverted from safe supply prescriptions.
The issue of what constitutes good research and what distinguishes it from anecdote (possibly the most common insult thrown down by both sides of this debate) is one of the committee's most engaging subplots. Which brings us to the emerging hero of this streaming series: Dr. Marcus Powlowski, the snowy-haired MP from Thunder Bay, Ont. On paper, Powlowski is a member of the Liberal party, but in this committee (and likely everywhere else) he is a courageous and rare free agent who is quite willing to take a good rip at his own government's policies when he senses it is deserved.
Powlowski was an emergency room doctor for almost 40 years who also happens to have a law degree from the University of Toronto, along with a master's degree in health law and policy from Harvard. In an earlier committee meeting, Powlowski had the temerity to tell a pro-harm reduction witness that he would not want to live next to one of his party's beloved supervised injection sites, given all the drug dealers they are known to attract.
Powlowski may not be the only committee member who reads the various research studies and articles they are provided on supervised consumption sites and safe supply, but it has become clear he may be the only one who fully understands them. Sereda was not the first activist witness to have the published evidence she claims supports her position disassembled by Powlowski.
While other Liberal committee members were fond of using softball questions to promote their controversial policies, Powlowski promptly challenged Sereda: "Convince me," he said. "We've heard anecdotes on both sides."
Powlowski then cited a handful of different safe supply studies and slagged them with the very same slight Sereda had just levelled at Zivo.
"I looked at a lot of the other studies that were, quote-unquote, evidence," Powlowski said. "A lot of them were basically a bunch of anecdotes where they talked to people on safe supply who said, 'Yeah, I feel better on safe supply,'but not exactly good evidence." Ouch. Tanguay was similarly challenged by Gord Johns, a Vancouver Island NDP MP who feels that the country desperately needs supervised consumption sites to proliferate like dandelions. He took the Alberta government to task for closing supervised injection sites in cities such as Lethbridge, which subsequently experienced a rise in overdose deaths.
Tanguay retorted that there are record overdoses in communities across the country, whether they have supervised consumption sites or not. Johns wanted to know if Tanguay thought there should be more supervised consumption sites in Alberta.
"I believe that supervised consumption services are part of a pathway to treatment," came the reply. "Like all harm reduction services, they should be part of the treatment algorithm. The biggest problem we have is that we pick one little piece and then we focus on it and think that's all we need, and that's not an answer."
By pure coincidence, the evening before Tanguay's testimony at the committee, a 15-minute report on the Alberta model aired on CBC's The National. Host Ian Hanomansing toured one of the province's new call centres for those combating opioid addiction as well as a ninemonth-old treatment facility in Red Deer. (Alberta plans to build 11 such facilities over the next three years.)
Hanomansing also accompanied an outreach worker in Edmonton who delivers Naloxone kits and other drug supplies to those who are still using fentanyl and other drugs on the streets. He contrasted the views of this outreach worker, Shanell Twan, with those of Alberta's minister of mental health and addictions, Dan Williams.
Twan outright rejects the idea that treatment is going to lower the number of people Alberta is losing to drug overdose each day (five). In her view, supervised consumption sites and safe supply are what will save lives.
Williams disagrees: "Safe supply will facilitate more addiction," he told Hanomansing.
"What I say to Canadians watching this unfold on both sides is we have a choice in front of us. Is it that handing drugs out to drug addicts and the model of harm reduction that it has become is the path forward? Or is it going to be investing heavily in recovery, so we don't see any more of those scenes on the street you saw yesterday."
At the end of both Hanomansing's report and the parliamentary health committee meeting the following day, Twan and Sereda, respectively, each wanted to leave viewers with one final message. Twan laid a bunch of funeral remembrances on a table, representing those in the local drug-using community who had been lost. Sereda talked about a similar memorial on the wall of her office that she eventually took down because it became too overwhelming.
Both women shed tears as they recounted these losses. This grief seems to drive their burdensome desire to save lives above all else, even though that effort is currently applied in most of the country without a more comprehensive health-care approach attached to it.
When I saw on X that Hanomansing was working on his Alberta story, I will admit I expected it to favour harm reduction. It didn't. At the end of the story, a key statistic flashed on screen: recovery-committed Alberta has lower overdose deaths per 10,000 citizens than B.C., which is experimenting with decriminalization and more robust harm reduction.
In the end, the House of Commons health committee - and, by extension, our country - is, as Williams said, going to have to make a choice. Are we going to stem the tens of thousands lost to opioid overdose by providing users more drugs? Or are we going to ramp up the access to health care desperately needed by our most vulnerable, options that are woefully inadequate in most provinces, including mine, Ontario.
Williams'Ontario counterpart, Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Michael Tibollo, has put a pause on building any new supervised consumption sites. My sources tell me that his Conservative government is quietly planning a move away from the current harm reduction-focused model to something closer to what Alberta has done, putting a lot more resources toward recovery.
In the meantime, a very compelling debate continues to unfold as our two main political parties jostle in committee to decide the future of drug policy in Canada. When that happens and who will be in power when it does will be the cliffhanger at the end of Season 1.
National Post Derek Finkle is an award-winning writer and editor who is currently producing a documentary series, The Day Liz Disappeared, which examines the cold-case mystery surrounding the 1990 disappearance of Elizabeth Bain.

Document 167 of 500

Chris Selley: Canada gets more embarrassing after anti-Israel protestors thwart the Italian PM's reception

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-canada-gets-more-embarrassing-after/docview/2937377352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chris+Selley%3A+Canada+gets+more+embarrassing+after+anti-Israel+protestors+thwart+the+Italian+PM%27s+reception&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I realized something interesting over the weekend as I watched International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen try in vain, like a bewildered tourist, to find a way to enter the Art Gallery of Ontario. Hussen, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the great-and-good of Italian-Canadian society were to fete visiting Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni at the AGO, but a few dozen pro-Palestinian supporters managed to block the entrances enough to scupper the whole event.
What I realized was that even after a solid decade, at least, of considering Canada's politics and many of its institutions fundamentally and possibly irredeemably unserious - from our Supreme Court to our National Hockey League franchises - I was still capable of being embarrassed on my country's behalf.
Meloni is a G7 leader. We should be able to host a dinner for her without risk of it being shut down by a few dozen people protesting something that has next-to-nothing to do with Canada or Italy. Other G7 leaders might reasonably argue that's something like a prerequisite for membership.
Giorgia Meloni's Italy puts Justin Trudeau to shame
Escaped circus lion roams Italian streets for six hours
"At least it was only Italy," I found myself chuckling ruefully. And Meloni specifically is not my cup of coffee, with her record of flattering Italian history's fascists and her antediluvian attitudes towards same-sex unions and parenting. Were Meloni not a current G7 leader, she's the sort of politician Liberals would excoriate Stephen Harper for shaking hands with, never mind throwing her an arty shindig.
But that's false comfort, of course. Canada, a tumbleweed nation in the desert of world affairs, can't afford to make such judgments about more important, more serious countries like Italy. And just about any world leader heading to the AGO on Saturday night, save perhaps the leaders of Hamas itself, would have faced the same basic situation. Trudeau is worthy of cancellation all by himself, by the protesters' logic, on account of his quixotic, semi-consistent insistence that Israel has a right to exist.
The Toronto Police Service (TPS) says it did not advise Trudeau to cancel the event. The TPS says many attendees were already inside, and that it had guaranteed VIPs safe passage through the fray. The RCMP, who are in charge of Trudeau's and visiting dignitaries' safety, claims "the size and volatile nature of the crowds at the entrances to venue (meant) it was not safe to move both prime ministers to the event at the scheduled time."
It's tough to know what to make of that. The RCMP but if you were the prime minister's security detail - any prime minister's, I mean - would you trust today's TPS to play any role in his or her safety? I sure wouldn't.
On Saturday, at a protest outside a synagogue north of Toronto, a woman was caught dead to rights, on video, striking a York Regional Police officer She was apprehended by said police officer and other police officers, which I think we can all agree is a reasonable and predictable response to striking a police officer ... but then the assembled chanting rabble convinced the police to let her go.
I carry no water for police in general, or Toronto's or Ontario's in particular. I have, though, defended their general commitment to de-escalating rather than cracking down on potentially violent situations. But that particular catch-and-release scenario was wince-inducing. At the end of the day, none of us wants a police force that can be literally harangued into letting stuff slide.
For many conservative-minded Canadians, Saturday's abject capitulation to the rabble was a notable data point in an already well-established narrative about the pro-Palestinian protesters, about policing, about the general decline of Canada under Trudeau. I feel that here more than I usually do. But Trudeau doesn't control Ontario's famously hands-off police forces. Premier Doug Ford does that, to the alarmingly limited extent any politician does, but the peculiar nature of Canadian politics and media means he never has to answer for it.
My biggest takeaway from this latest humiliation is a bit different. It's this: How could Trudeau and his office possibly have foreseen any other outcome? The AGO would have been swarmed with pro-Palestinian protesters even if Trudeau had just dropped by to see the Keith Haring exhibit Had Hamas not massacred 1,200 people on Oct. 7 last year, triggering a predictably remorseless response from Israel, self-styled anti-fascists would probably have shown up to protest Meloni's presence. And the only predictable response from Toronto police would have been to let them shut down the event. That's what they do.
How utterly out of touch does a prime minister, and the people around him, have to be to have thought this would go off without a hitch?
National Post
cselley@postmedia.com

Document 168 of 500

Canadian PMs: We have had 23 Prime Ministers, a look at the seven that are alive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-pms-we-have-had-23-prime-ministers-look/docview/2937347618/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canadian+PMs%3A+We+have+had+23+Prime+Ministers%2C+a+look+at+the+seven+that+are+alive&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since Confederation in 1867, Canada has had 23 different Prime Ministers.
Canada's 10th PM, William Lyon Mackenzie King, was the longest serving, with a tenure of 21 years, 154 days over three non-consecutive terms. Sir Charles Tupper, the sixth PM, had the shortest time in office, serving only 69 days, from May 1, 1896, to July 8, 1896.
With the passing of Brian Mulroney, now seven people who ever held the title remain alive.
Brian Mulroney: How the former PM made millions in an industry that he once strongly opposed
Former prime minister Brian Mulroney dead at 84
Joe Clark (served June 4, 1979 - March 3, 1980)
Tory leader Joe Clark and former Tory leader and Prime Minister Brian Mulroney chat at a party fundraiser in Montreal in 2001.
Canada's 16th Prime Minister and a member of the Progressive Conservative Party, Clark defeated the Liberal government of Pierre Trudeau in the 1979 election.
His brief tenure with a minority government made it challenging to pass legislation and his government was brought down following a non-confidence vote on his first budget, leading to the 1980 election and ultimately Trudeau's return to power.
Clark later served in Mulroney's cabinet as the Secretary of State for External Affairs from 1984 to 1991 and as the Minister responsible for Constitutional Affairs.
Clark opposed the merger of the Progressive Conservatives with the Conservative Party of Canada and sat as an independent Progressive Conservative until his retirement from Parliament at the end of the 2003 session.
Since then, Clark has worked for a number of non-profits, including The Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars, the Centre for North American Studies, the Jimmy Carter Center, the Global Leadership Foundation and the Centre for International Governance Innovation.
Kim Campbell (served June 25, 1993 - Nov. 4, 1993)
Former Prime Minister Kim Campbell at Sage Bistro in Vancouver, BC. Dec. 9, 2021.
Canada's 19th PM, Campbell was the first and, to date, only woman to lead Canada.
A member of the Progressive Conservative Party, Campbell held several cabinet positions in Brian Mulroney's government, including Minister of Justice and Attorney General, and Minister of National Defence, becoming the first woman to hold these positions as well.
She won the party leadership following Mulroney's resignation and in her brief tenure implemented changes to reduce the size of the federal cabinet and established new laws on sexual assault and firearms control.
Her term ended following an overwhelming defeat in the 1993 federal election, including Campbell losing her Vancouver Centre seat.
Campbell has had an eclectic career post-premiership, including living in Edmonton, Paris, Los Angeles and Boston, where she spent three years teaching at Harvard.
She has also served as the secretary-general for the Club de Madrid, a Spanish non-profit organization.
Jean Chrétien (served Nov. 4, 1993 - Dec. 12, 2003)
Former prime minister Jean Chrétien delivers remarks at a Liberal party convention in Ottawa on May 5, 2023.
Canada's 20th PM, Chrétien's leadership saw the Liberal party win three consecutive majority governments in 1993, 1997 and 2000.
His government handled a number of high profile events and policy decisions, including the 1995 Quebec referendum and its aftermath, same-sex marriage legislation, criminal justice reform and made efforts to eliminate the national deficit, which led to budget surpluses and debt reduction.
His government also faced controversies such as the sponsorship scandal and the "Shawinigate" affair. His government also refused to participate in the Iraq War in 2003, a decision that was controversial at the time.
Internal party strife under his leadership eventually led to Chrétien announcing his resignation in 2003. He went on to join the now defunct law firm Heenan Blaikie, where Trudeau worked until his death in 2000. Chrétien later joined Denton's and his work has occasionally put the ex-PM into some controversial territory, such as when a business consortium hired him to lobby for a nuclear waste dump in Labrador in 2021.
Paul Martin (served Dec. 12, 2003 - Feb. 6, 2006)
Former PM, Paul Martin addresses Liberal supporters and party members at the 2013 Liberal Leadership convention in Toronto, on Saturday, April 6, 2013.
Martin succeeded Chrétien as Canada's 21st PM following his performance as Finance Minister, where he was credited with eliminating the federal deficit in the 1990s through major spending cuts and reforms to social services.
Martin's leadership saw the negotiation of the Kelowna Accord to improve conditions for Indigenous peoples, an agreement with provinces on health care funding and the legalization of same-sex marriage.
Like Chrétien, Martin was also marred by the sponsorship scandal. He was defeated by Stephen Harper in the 2006 federal election and chose not to seek re-election in 2008, marking the end of his political career.
Martin remains one of the world's wealthiest former heads of government - a position he attained through his ownership of Canada Steamship Lines.
The end of his political career also signalled the end of the Kelowna Accords, a $5 billion initiative aimed at enhancing Indigenous education and promoting Indigenous entrepreneurship. Through the Martin Family Initiative, established in 2008, he has focused on providing a wide range of educational programs for Indigenous communities across the country, including prenatal education for Indigenous mothers and courses in business and finance.
Stephen Harper (served Feb. 6, 2006 - Nov. 4, 2015)
Former prime minister Stephen Harper adelivers the keynote address at a conference, Wednesday, March 22, 2023 in Ottawa.
Canada's 22nd PM, Stephen Harper was a founding member of the Reform Party of Canada and later became the first leader of the Conservative Party of Canada.
His government introduced an array of tax cuts and largely focused on economic issues, including reducing the Goods and Services Tax and leading Canada through the 2008 global financial crisis.
Harper's foreign policy was marked by a strong support for Israel and skepticism toward the United Nations.
After losing the 2015 federal election to Justin Trudeau, Harper stepped down as leader of the Conservative Party and eventually resigned his seat in the House of Commons in 2016. Post-politics, he founded Harper & Associates Consulting and assumed the role of Chairman of the International Democratic Union (IDU) in 2018. The IDU is a global alliance of centre-right and conservative political parties.
Justin Trudeau (serving since Nov. 4, 2015)
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau waves as he boards a government plane in Ottawa, on Jan. 9, 2023.
The 23rd and current PM, Trudeau has been in power for more than eight years.
After being elected as the MP for Papineau in 2008, Trudeau quickly rose up the Liberal party ranks. He entered the leadership race in 2012, following Michael Ignatieff's resignation. He won the Liberal leadership in 2013 and ultimately led the Liberals to a majority government in 2015, followed by minority governments in both the 2019 and 2021 elections.
His government has focused on gender-balanced Cabinet appointments, environmental commitments and cannabis legalization. His administration has also faced several controversies and scandals, such as the Aga Khan affair, the SNC-Lavalin affair, and the WE Charity scandal, which led to investigations by the ethics commissioner.

Document 169 of 500

Derek Finkle: Conservatives give safe supply the grilling it deserves

Author: to, Special

Publication info: to, Special. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/derek-finkle-conservatives-give-safe-supply/docview/2937267609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Derek+Finkle%3A+Conservatives+give+safe+supply+the+grilling+it+deserves&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=to%2C+Special&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parliamentary committees - even those born of public scandal - rarely make for good television. We do not have the same flair for the dramatic as our cousins who practice politics south of the border, where such committees have produced celebrities for decades. That said, a study underway at the House of Commons health committee on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis is getting close to mandatory viewing. Its first five episodes are as entertaining as any non-scripted, government-funded programming can get.
There are several factors cranking up the drama, including the fact that a significant number of Canadians have lost family members and loved ones to the crisis this committee is examining. I count myself among them - one of my closest friends since the age of 10 died of an accidental fentanyl overdose in July 2022. But my interest in the subject does not end there: my family lives across the street from a supervised injection site in Toronto that has become a flashpoint since a mother was killed in a gunfight between drug dealers who habitually do business near the property.
An added dimension of intrigue for this committee is that the opioid crisis and its management have very quickly ramped up to be a polarizing issue that will loom large in the next federal election. The committee's protagonists - a pack of Conservative MPs - belong to the party that is the clear frontrunner according to current polls.
The word "pack" is used intentionally, as the Liberal and NDP members of this committee are not shy when it comes to accusing these Conservatives of opportunistic, wolf-like behaviour - such as confronting witnesses in a manner more designed for hits on social media than anything else. The leader of the pack, Dr. Stephen Ellis, an MP from Nova Scotia, has produced more than his fair share of highlights, including a grilling of Jennifer Saxe, an associate assistant deputy minister from the Department of Health, on Dec. 4 This was often painful to watch.
Ellis: The other question I would ask you is: on Canada.ca, it says, "A few grains (of fentanyl) can be enough to kill you." Is that true?
Saxe: (long pause) That could be true, yes, depending on someone's -
Ellis: It could be true? It's on the Health Canada website. It must be true. Is it there or not?
Saxe: Fentanyl could in certain -
Ellis: A few grains could kill you. I'm going to badger you a bit on this because it says right on your website -
Saxe: Yes.
Ellis: Thank you. Wow, that took a lot. Tell me this, then: what sense is there to decriminalize 2.5 grams of fentanyl (in B.C.) if a few grains could kill you?
Ellis and his Conservative colleagues on the committee are particularly committed to attacking the safe supply policy of the Liberal government. This policy, something the Conservatives refer to as an "experiment," involves hydromorphone, a medical-grade opioid, being prescribed by clinicians as an alternative to increasingly deadly street drugs. Ellis echoes the concern of his party's leader, Pierre Poilievre - that there is growing evidence these drugs are being diverted to the street market in significant quantities, thus causing more people (including young people and those who live on reserves) to get addicted to opioids.
Once the Conservatives started putting the phrase "so-called" in front of safe supply, those in the drug policy realm such as Saxe decided it was time for a rebrand. Safe supply suddenly became "prescribed pharmaceutical alternatives." If everyone watching at home on Dec. 4 had downed a shot every time Saxe used that phrase while desperately trying to stick to her talking points under fire, no one would have made it off the couch.
This committee took a significant step toward compulsory viewing status during its fifth meeting on the drug crisis, held Feb. 26 The star witnesses were Dr. Rob Tanguay, an addiction psychiatrist who co-founded Alberta's Rapid Access Addiction Medicine Community Clinic, and Dr. Andrea Sereda, the lead physician of a safer opioids supply program in London, Ont.
While these two doctors share some things in common, they have come to represent two very different approaches to combatting the surging opioid crisis.
Tanguay embraces what has become known as the "Alberta model," which favours treatment instead of the Trudeau government's harm reduction policies and the decriminalization path taken in neighbouring British Colombia. Sereda, on the other hand, is an advocate for the safe supply of prescribed opioids, especially, she claims, for people who have not had success with other forms of treatment.
There was a bit of excitement prior to Sereda's testimony when Adam Zivo, a columnist with the National Post who has parried with the doctor over his reporting on the illegal diversion of hydromorphone prescriptions, announced on X that he has been investigating Sereda's so-called safer supply program since December and would soon publish a report demonstrating widespread diversion connected to her practice. Zivo even provided the committee members with five proposed questions to put to Sereda in the same post.
Laila Goodridge, the Conservative MP for Fort McMurray and Cold Lake, Alta., decided to have a go with Zivo's questions. Goodridge cited "new information" that corroborated previous reporting about mass diversion of safe supply opioids around the pharmacy near Sereda's clinic and asked the doctor if she was aware of it.
Sereda came prepared. "What evidence has been released showing this?" she asked.
Goodridge had to admit what Sereda already knew - it came from some tweets by Zivo that morning.
With a considerable display of haughty satisfaction, Sereda stated that safe supply clinicians rely on good research and published evidence, not anecdotes and social media posts. (If I had one piece of advice for the Conservatives on the committee, it would be this: put a little more thought into your questions.)
Derek Finkle: Harm reduction missionaries are making my neighbourhood unsafe
Derek Finkle: 'Safe injection' ruined my community; about time for provincial review
Within days of Sereda insisting to the committee that tales of diversion were unjustified and the product of right-wing political fearmongering, an Ottawa-based reporter with the CBC published a lengthy exposé about the impact the illicit trade of safe supply drugs is having on a Rideau Street neighbourhood downtown. Not only is a local police officer quoted saying that he and his colleagues regularly "observe people trafficking in diverted hydromorphone" from safer supply providers, a follow-up by CBC noted that a local drug dealer had propositioned a reporter on the story, offering to sell fentanyl and buy safe supply pills.
If Sereda doesn't view any of this as evidence of diversion, it would be difficult to say the same about another bit of recent news: in a search last month, RCMP officers in Campbell River, B.C. seized more than two kilograms of fentanyl and roughly 3,500 hydromorphone pills, along with cocaine, methamphetamine and about $30,000 in cash. Based on evidence gathered at the scene, police believe the pills were diverted from safe supply prescriptions.
The issue of what constitutes good research and what distinguishes it from anecdote (possibly the most common insult thrown down by both sides of this debate) is one of the committee's most engaging subplots. Which brings us to the emerging hero of this streaming series - Dr. Marcus Powlowski, the snowy-haired MP from Thunder Bay, Ont. On paper, Powlowski is a member of the Liberal Party, but in this committee (and likely everywhere else) he is a courageous and rare free agent who is quite willing to take a good rip at his own government's policies when he senses it is deserved.
Powlowski was an emergency room doctor for almost 40 years who also happens to have a law degree from the University of Toronto, along with a master's degree in health law and policy from Harvard. In an earlier committee meeting, Powlowski had the temerity to tell a pro-harm reduction witness that he would not want to live next to one of his party's beloved supervised injection sites given all the drug dealers they are known to attract.
Powlowski may not be the only committee member who reads the various research studies and articles they are provided on supervised consumption sites and safe supply, but it has become clear he may be the only one who fully understands them. Sereda was not the first activist witness to have the published evidence she claims supports her position disassembled by Powlowski.
While other Liberal committee members were fond of using softball questions to promote their controversial policies, Powlowski promptly challenged Sereda: "Convince me," he said. "We've heard anecdotes on both sides."
Powlowski then cited a handful of different safe supply studies and slagged them with the very same slight Sereda had just levelled at Zivo.
"I looked at a lot of the other studies that were quote-unquote evidence," Powlowski said. "A lot of them were basically a bunch of anecdotes where they talked to people on safe supply who said, 'Yeah, I feel better on safe supply," but not exactly good evidence."
Ouch.
Tanguay was similarly challenged by Gord Johns, a Vancouver Island NDP MP who feels that the country desperately needs supervised consumption sites to proliferate like dandelions. He took the Alberta government to task for closing supervised injection sites in cities such as Lethbridge, which subsequently experienced a rise in overdose deaths.
Tanguay retorted that there are record overdoses in communities across the country, whether they have supervised consumption sites or not. Johns wanted to know if Tanguay thought there should be more supervised consumption sites in Alberta.
"I believe that supervised consumption services are part of a pathway to treatment," came the reply. "Like all harm reduction services, they should be part of the treatment algorithm. The biggest problem we have is that we pick one little piece and then we focus on it and think that's all we need, and that's not an answer."
By pure coincidence, the evening prior to Tanguay's testimony before the committee, a 15-minute report on the Alberta model aired on CBC's The National. Host Ian Hanomansing toured one of the province's new call centres for those combatting opioid addiction as well as a nine-month-old treatment facility in Red Deer (Alberta plans to build 11 such facilities over the next three years).
Hanomansing also accompanied an outreach worker in Edmonton who delivers Naloxone kits and other drug supplies to those who are still using fentanyl and other drugs on the streets. He contrasted the views of this outreach worker, Shanell Twan, with those of Alberta's minister of mental health and addictions, Dan Williams.
Twan outright rejects the idea that treatment is going to lower the number of people Alberta is losing to drug overdose each day (five). In her view, supervised consumption sites and safe supply are what will save lives.
Williams disagrees: "Safe supply will facilitate more addiction," he told Hanomansing.
"What I say to Canadians watching this unfold on both sides is we have a choice in front of us. Is it that handing drugs out to drug addicts and the model of harm reduction that it has become is the path forward? Or is it going to be investing heavily in recovery, so we don't see any more of those scenes on the street you saw yesterday."
At the end of both Hanomansing's report and the Parliamentary health committee meeting the following day, Twan and Sereda, respectively, each wanted to leave viewers with one final message. Twan laid a bunch of funeral remembrances on a table, representing those in the local drug-using community who had been lost. Sereda talked about a similar memorial on the wall of her office that she eventually took down because it became too overwhelming.
Both women shed tears as they recounted these losses. This grief seems to drive their burdensome desire to save lives above all else, even though that effort is currently applied in most of the country without a more comprehensive health-care approach attached to it.
When I saw on X that Hanomansing was working on his Alberta story, I will admit I expected it to favour harm reduction. It didn't. At the end of the story, a key statistic flashed on screen - recovery-committed Alberta has lower overdose deaths per 10,000 citizens than B.C., which is experimenting with decriminalization and more robust harm reduction.
In the end, the House of Commons health committee - and, by extension, our country - is, as Williams said, going to have to make a choice. Are we going to stem the tens of thousands lost to opioid overdose by providing users more drugs? Or are we going to ramp up the access to health care desperately needed by our most vulnerable, options that are woefully inadequate in most provinces, including mine, Ontario.
Williams' Ontario counterpart, Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Michael Tibollo, has put a pause on building any new supervised consumption sites. My sources tell me that his Conservative government is quietly planning a move away from the current harm reduction-focused model to something closer to what Alberta has done, putting a lot more resources towards recovery.
In the meantime, a very compelling debate continues to unfold as our two main political parties jostle in committee to decide the future of drug policy in Canada. When that happens and who will be in power when it does will be the cliffhanger at the end of season one.
National Post
Derek Finkle is an award-winning writer and editor who is currently producing a documentary series, The Day Liz Disappeared, which examines the cold-case mystery surrounding the 1990 disappearance of Elizabeth Bain.
