Document 214 of 500

Jordan Peterson: The awe-inspiring conservative counter-offensive against woke nonsense

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jordan-peterson-awe-inspiring-conservative/docview/2933514060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jordan+Peterson%3A+The+awe-inspiring+conservative+counter-offensive+against+woke+nonsense&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have worked with people of many political stripes now in many different countries in Europe, Southeast Asia, the U.K., the U.S. and Canada. This has provided me with the luxury of accurate comparative judgement and comparison.
Something is brewing in Canada - something of deeper than mere national significance. It's a veritable sign of the times. Just as our nation was and so unfortunately on the cutting edge of the moralizing woke globalist eco-utopian nonsense promulgated oh-so-charismatically by the beautiful Justin Trudeau, so are we leading the charge of counter-offensive.
To put it succinctly: Canadian conservatives are growing a spine. Or a pair. Pick your metaphor.
When I first started speaking with the leaders of the conservative movement in Canada - something that happened in earnest about eight years ago, when I rose to some degree of political notoriety - I was struck by the degree to which the progressive mob had managed its task of radical demoralization. Conservatives, who tend to be conscientious, orderly, dutiful and industrious by temperament are also in consequence of that proclivity easily led to feel guilty. The woke narcissists who on the other hand make up the progressive left are characterized by an absolute lack of such conscience, but are expert at exploiting its presence in others.
What are the allegations typically levelled at conservatives - and even, increasingly, at classical liberals? "You're racist, sexist, homophobic, transphobic, Islamophobic, ageist, ableist, privileged, colonialist, exploitative." For the bloody-minded left, life is a search for victimizer, to serve as target, and victim, to "identity with" - and for almost entirely self-serving purposes. And God help you if you are placed in the latter category (are you listening, my Jewish friends?).
It's a constant refrain, and the borders and rules shift constantly, because the point isn't to improve the life of the so-called marginalized but to gain the upper hand morally with no effort or sacrifice. And, if the lives of others can be ruined by unjustified and all-too-often anonymous or mob-inspired denunciation, so much the better. So say the bitter, as they forever have.
The accusing mob exploits the fact that in a civil society unwarranted allegations are frowned upon. This has created the place of public trust upon which the stability, prosperity and safety of a polity truly depend, while simultaneously making it so that those daring to bring forth an allegation are given duly serious attention. However - and there is always a however - this enables the psychopathic.
I saw this happen at the University of Toronto, as it has at all those once-great institutions, where I once served as professor. The administration and faculty at such places bent over backward to address even the smallest complaint, on the grounds that everyone deserved to have their concerns addressed. That is all well and good, when the complainants are playing a straight game, but absolutely counterproductive, when the Machiavellian, psychopathic, narcissistic and sadistic Dark Tetrad types come crawling out of the woodwork, as they did then and most definitely and dangerously are now.
The same types came for the conservatives, even within their political parties, with the so-called social conservatives bearing most of the brunt, sent away with their tails between their legs, in shame, for their sin of abiding by the traditional dictates that have served western culture so well not only for centuries but for millennia. All the conservatives I spoke with in Canada in 2016 were afraid and for good reason that if they stuck their heads above the parapets they would be lopped off and with plenty of accompanying hacking and all the pain thereof.
The demented accusers are particularly good at and also delight in reputation savaging. How do conservatives respond to allegations of X-phobia, privilege and oppression? Well, such conscientious types are extremely susceptible to and even often defenceless against manipulation of the guilt to which their temperament makes them prone. So, they're immediately back on their heels, mumbling shamefaced apology: "We've probably made some mistakes. We're probably more sexist, racist, ageist, etc., etc., than we could be if we were perfect and we'll try harder in the future. Are we good?"
This is a response pattern that can even be admirable, when your accusers are people who have been genuinely hurt by what has been truly abusive - and it's no easy matter to separate the wheat from the chaff concerning such matters. However, it's a seriously losing game when the psychopaths and the narcissists come out to play, as they so clearly have, occupying even the highest of ministerial positions in the land, as well as swarming online, where they can organize, even though no one can stand them long enough for such things to happen in the real world. So we had a generation or even two or three of conservatives who were positively ashamed of their old-fashioned, stodgy, un-cool, dull, traditional, oft-religious, work-hard-and-you'll-get ahead ways.
The assault by the self-righteous lefties was so effective that the conservatives did more than retreat: they adopted the agenda of the progressives, in self-defense, justifying what they were doing by its comparatively slow pace. Long-term defeat, my conservative friends, is the most fundamental form of defeat. And you true liberals should attend to this, too: you're no more to the radicals than useful idiots, and your usefulness is temporary, at best.
But something has changed in the last few years, and in many places, simultaneously. First, we see a worldwide phenomena, which is an increasingly conservative stance adopted by young men. This will happen among young women too, although there will be a lag, because young women need young men, and will eventually turn in their direction. This is not happening precisely because the young men are genuinely becoming more conservative, but because they are sick to the soul of the demoralization foisted on them from toddlerhood through maturity: sick of their innate play preferences being demonized ("we should socialize little boys to be more like little girls," as the demented social and developmental psychologists have continually brayed); sick of their ambition and proclivity for competition and victory being deemed toxic; sick of being portrayed as oppressors on the political and economic front and as pillagers and rapists on the sexual and environmental side; sick of facing resdistributive discrimination in the market place; sick of the constant accusations flung at them by the increasingly mentally-unstable radicals of the feminist left.
Young men are therefore not "more conserative"-they're merely rediscovering a necessary and sustaining sanity, as the political landscape increasingly shifts into the demented calling-unconsciously-for-the-strong-hand-of-a-tyrant neurotic narcissistic hedonism of the radical worshippers of nature and chaos. This shift is happening in Canada, the US, Europe and Australia/New Zealand, as well as South Korea and Japan. Conservatives could and should stand for that solidity and sanity. That's what conservatism is for.
But something more specific and specifically political is also happening in Canada. The conservatives have rediscovered their spines! - and not just the ones that enable them to stand up straight, but the ones that make them spikey and witty and able to spar with some truly devastating confidence and humour. They appear increasingly willing to withstand the slings and arrows of outrageous fortune inevitably aimed their way when they say anything remotely conservative, having discovered that they will be pilloried no matter what they say anyway, having noted that their noisy and noxious enemies squawk and crow loudly for a moment but don't have the discipline to keep up the show, having regained some faith in their foundational beliefs, developing some real strategic intelligence, and becoming increasingly willing to risk the adventure of the truth.
Let's consider, first, federal Leader of His Majesty's Loyal Opposition, the Right Honorable Pierre Poilievre. He has brilliantly circumvented the blighted legacy media, the anachronistic conspiracy of CBC, CTV, and Globe and Mail - all that elite central Canada self-congratulatory government-subsidized we-know-which-side-our-bread-is-buttered-on insufferable pseudo-intellectual coterie, and started his own media empire. Very successfully. His last two self-produced documentaries, one on housing, and one on the debt crisis, went truly viral, not only by Canadian standards, as have many of the artful ads he produced with his truly professional communication team. This is exceptionally forward looking, and places the man who will be our next prime minister at the very forefront of political communication, worldwide.
I have literally seen no one anywhere in the many countries I now have the privilege to have come to understand occupy the cutting edge of social media communication with the skill of Poilievre. If he can do the same on the political and economic front, we have reason to be optimistic. He also avoided the catastrophe of the "expert" political advisor class whose idiot bromides castrate most would-be conservative leaders, brought his message directly to the people, listened to what they were telling him, modified himself in appearance and approach as was right and necessary, and swept the conservative leadership race. He is therefore rightly poised not only to become the leader of Canada but to be a force on the international stage, and he has an extra year now, to get his act together, courtesy of the collusion of the unspeakable Jagmeet Singh with his puppet master, our current Prime Minister (he whose name cannot be spoken).
Then there's Danielle Smith. I have come to know Premier Smith and to appreciate her. She's brave enough to take on the rainbow brigade and its false claims of universal community. That is a daunting challenge, given their expert propensity to wield victimhood and exploit compassion. She's witty, smart and tough. I saw her show her fist in velvet glove recently on stage in Alberta to Tucker Carlson, whose sometimes excesses she opposed with grace and humour, while simultaneously noting his many contributions and advantages. In the aftermath of that spectacle, she stepped forward to protect the Canadian children, took the heat unapologetically, and stayed the course. God only knows how many young people she thereby saved from sterilization and surgical mutilation (I can't believe I have to write that about Canada: what's happened to our country?). In doing so, she followed in the courageous footsteps of Premier Blaine Higgs of New Brunswick, the relatively unheralded leader of that relatively isolated and oft-silent province, who took the first public stand against the tyranny of the gender-benders and manipulators and also stuck so forthrightly to his guns. Good work, Mr. Higgs. That kind of bravery is far rarer than we might hope.
Smith has also set herself firmly and unapologetically against the manipulative woke lies of the deluded Steven Guilbeault, who might well be the most dangerously bent political mind currently operating in the western world (although there's Gavin Newsom and Kamala Harris, as well as Trudeau himself as powerful competitors). She's set to double the oil and gas production that makes Canada work economically, that heats, cools, feeds and provisions the world, and that could be provided to our European allies to protect them against the still-tyrannical governments and economies that are now and will be for a long while the only alternative suppliers of the energy and much else their people so desperately need.
Jordan Peterson: Bureaucrats will rue the day they tried to shut me up
Jordan Peterson: Blame idiotic Marxism for the demented antisemitism oozing out of universities
She is allied intelligently on those fronts with Premier Scott Moe of Saskatchewan, a very popular leader in his home province, a sensible man who generally remains above the fray but who stands up when necessary and who is guiding his mostly-sensible and often rural western constituents toward the stable and ever-expanding prosperity that the prairie economies have within their grasp if they have the requisite courage and discipline. Even Doug Ford, who's a bit slow on the uptake with regard to standing forthrightly against the woke mob, has the self-possession, humility and good sense to surround himself with people who are smarter and more expert than he is and to run a government that is competent in the main. That ability is a true sign of character, and bodes well for a province that flirts all too dangerously with progressive ideology. Ford is perhaps a bit too nice, and a bit too prone to the guilt that I mentioned earlier, but the emergence of some stauncher allies on the more socially conservative side (which is where most Canadians now stand, in comparison to their Liberal "leadership") might well embolden him further.
This is all very promising. There's real leadership emerging on the center and on the right in Canada (which is now the entire political spectrum less to the left than Che Guevara). The sky's the limit for conservatives, in consequence, and they could truly embolden the world. Alberta's energy resources (and Saskatchewan's, as well, particularly on the nuclear side) put Canada in a potentially enviable and influential position - a position, as well, of domestic abundance.
What's the appropriate path forward, therefore, on the conservative side, and where are the associated pitfalls?
First: conservatives, look to Poilievre for mastery of social media. That is the future of political communication. You don't need the woke legacy media. Stop giving them attention and certainly stop giving them money, governmental and otherwise. No more subsidy and no more paid advertising. They're dead. You're pumping money into the corpse of a self-righteous suicide. Stop doing it. And shed your bloody handlers and advisors, your PR types. If they could run a campaign, successfully, they'd be the politicians, not you. Write your own speeches. Answer the questions you are asked with your own answers. Say what you believe to be true and learn to lead with that. It's what everyone everywhere increasingly needs and wants, particularly the young.
Second: conservatives, don't make the essentially Marxist mistake of assuming that "it's the economy, stupid." Material well-being is important, obviously, but it's by no means everything, as the increasingly misery that accompanies our increasing wealth indicates. People need virtue more than wealth - the treasure stored in heaven, more than the earthly treasure that rust and moth destroy and thieves plunder. Furthermore, material wealth itself is reflection of the underlying ethos of a community. The ethically well-situated flourish. Truthful hard-working generously productive people flourish because they are productive and can trust each other in trade. Conservatives know this, however implicitly, and young people are desperate to learn it.
Make the moral case, therefore, that you can and should make. Learn to make your implicit traditional convictions explicit and communicable and have some faith in their power. You are attracting young men for a reason. And here's something else, in that regard: Conservatives now have something to sell to young people. Responsibility. No one has made that offer in six decades, and the time has come. Enough of the continual rattling regarding rights. All rights come with responsibilities. Why shoulder them? Because there is earned self-regard and opportunity in responsibility. There is the adventure of calling and conscience in responsibility. There is the excitement, meaning and security of marriage, family and community in responsibility. Call the young - particularly the men - to responsibility. That will help them succeed, psychologically and socially. It will restore their competence and their confidence. It will also make them more attractive to young women, which is what they want more desperately than anything else.
Third: watch your backs. You need to be wise as serpents, as well as innocent as conservatives. Seriously. You are dealing with an adversarial enemy who will stop at nothing, and who is both expert and delights in the cancel-culture innuendo, slander and behind-the-scenes serpentine maneuvering that more and more characterizes our entire culture. When Poilievre wins the next election, as he almost certainly will - obliterating the dross of the Trudeau years in the doing - he will immediately be blamed for the crisis that worst of all possible leaders has produced.
We have lived through eight years of perma-scandal under Justin. The economy is in dire shape. Canadians now make on average less per capita than the inhabitants of the poorest of our U.S. cousins' state. Our health-care system is irreparably compromised, the judiciary is stacked with progressives, the federal civil service ballooned to cancerous proportions, the housing market bubbled, the debt load unsustainable, the deficit out of control, and the education system from K-12 through university demented.
This is a lot of trouble and Canadians are seriously asleep at the wheel. They plan to hand the whole mess over to the hapless conservatives, who will instantly be blamed for the catastrophes they inherited, which will be far more dire than we currently understand. Here's a future to avoid: Poilievre's government spends one term discovering and attempting to mend a million leaks, some very large. They are held responsible for producing whatever they uncover, not least by the now-reformed Liberals, under the influence of whatever glad-hander and reprobate the conniving progressives manage to rope into replacing whatever the hell they have leading the country at the moment. They lose and the bloody radicals take control again.
Don't let it happen, conservatives. Continue with the courage. Tell the truth. Call people to responsibility and the adventure of their lives - as you are signalling, Poilievre. Make our economies boom, Premiers Smith and Moe; keep your courage intact, Premier Higgs. Help Pierre understand how to manage a truly expert cabinet, Premier Ford.
Step up to the plate, boys and girls of tradition and competence, and take your best shot.
And watch out for the snakes. Seriously.
National Post

Document 215 of 500

The history of Section 13, the controversial hate speech law the Liberals just revived

Author: Brean, Jo seph

Publication info: Brean, Joseph. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/history-section-13-controversial-hate-speech-law/docview/2933465280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+history+of+Section+13%2C+the+controversial+hate+speech+law+the+Liberals+just+revived&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Brean%2C+Joseph&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The race to kill Section 13 was a circuitous thing.
In hindsight, given all its problems, it should have been quick and easy. The hate speech section of the Canadian Human Rights Act looked like it had been dying since it was born.
It was drafted almost 50 years ago mainly as a response to telephone hotlines that played recorded racist messages, promoted via street flyers by extremist hate groups. As a federal law, it drew its power over phone lines from federal juridiction over telecommunications, which did not originally include every word published every day in Canada.
But then along came the internet, and at a stroke of the legislative pen, the law about telephone voice recordings expanded to include effectively everything. It was not that nobody realized this. The change was motivated and justified not so much by the racist right anymore, but by rising Islamist extremism after 9/11, and the fear of online incitement and recruitment by al Qaeda.
But it was basically only ever used by one complainant, a lawyer named Richard Warman, who targeted white supremacists and neo-Nazis and never lost, and still holds the highest batting average among notoriously frequent participants at Canadian quasi-judicial tribunals, a clean 100 per cent.
Almost everyone eventually came to believe that Section 13 was a legal shambles, on the left wing and the right, at the newspapers and in the legislatures, even the tribunal chair who adjudicated it.
It was Canada's first online culture war, and it ended in total victory for the nay side, via a Conservative MP's private member's bill to repeal it. As a minority government in 2008, the Conservative Party under Stephen Harper had intervened to defend Section 13, arguing that "a little bit of chilling" around the hateful fringes of public discourse was "tolerable." But as a majority in 2014, and with the support of one lonely Liberal MP, the 2012 vote to repeal was as clear as public opinion. Section 13 was gone, and the general mood was good riddance. Better no law than bad law.
But it never actually died.
The key players who had pursued a Supreme Court showdown - Marc Lemire, a former leader of the racist Heritage Front, and the late Barbara Kulaszka, Canada's most prominent lawyer to hatemongers and war criminals, but also the civil liberties groups and Black and Jewish advocacy groups who had lined up their various positions, to say nothing of Canada's many human rights commissions - never got their big day in court.
It was an anti-gay crusader from Regina, William Whatcott, who caught the inside track to Ottawa, pleaded his case before the top court, and lost in a unanimous 2013 decision, such that the judgment against him saved the legal foundation of human rights hate law at both the provincial and the federal levels. In effect, it reinstated the last Section 13 case on the books, Warman's successful case against Lemire, which is enforced to this day with a legal order on Lemire not to publish hate online, on pain of contempt.
So when Parliament finally officially repealed Section 13 in 2014, a year or so after the Whatcott decision, it was only really putting it to bed. For a dead law, Section 13 still had remarkable vitality. It was a strange situation that inspired much colourful description.
The National Post reported at the time, for example, citing Monty Python: "Like the Norwegian Blue parrot, Section 13 is just resting. Like the Black Knight, its repeal by Parliament is just a flesh wound. Though it has been hoisted on the cross, its supporters may still, like Brian, always look on the bright side of life. Section 13 might be doomed, but it is good law. So sayeth the courts."
Today, perhaps inevitably, Section 13 is being reborn to new life, as part of the Liberal federal government's online harms legislation The war may be over, but the circus continues.
The history of Section 13
Section 13 was drafted and passed in 1977. It reached the Supreme Court in 1990 as part of a trio of cases about Canada's uniquely balanced approach to hate crimes. The ruling against neo-Nazi hotline operator John Ross Taylor, upholding Section 13, defined hate as "unusually strong and deep-felt emotions of detestation, calumny and vilification," the same language, with one minor tweak to delete "calumny," that appears today in the Liberal legislation. It was a split decision, though, and in her dissent, former Justice Beverley McLachlin (who was not yet the chief justice) said the idea of hate is "vague and subjective, capable of extension should the interpreter be so inclined." As she put it, "Where does dislike leave off and hatred or contempt begin?"
In 1998, a penalty section was added to Section 13 that allowed fines of up to $10,000, which would later become a major legal problem for the law's constitutionality. But it was the expansion to the internet in 2001 that caused the greatest headaches. New worries began to emerge.
One of the oddest things about Section 13 was that the hate speech did not require a specific victim. It was enough that published material was "likely to expose" a protected group to "hatred or contempt," for example on the basis of their race, religion or gender. It didn't have to actually do this, just make it likely.
This prompted criticism that Section 13 was only pretending to be remedial, like the rest of human rights law, and that in fact it was a punitive regime, closer to criminal law, but without all the procedural safeguards and rights of the accused.
There were many such contradictions. Truth was no defence, unlike in most other legal contexts such as defamation, to prevent tribunals becoming quasi-scientific inquiries into Holocaust revisionism or quack theories about genetic racial hierarchies.
Nor was benign intent, unlike the Criminal Code section on wilful promotion of hatred, for example, which offers various defences including truth, good faith religious argumentation, good faith and truthful discussion of matters of public interest, and pointing out hateful material "in good faith ... for the purpose of removal."
But these were arcane legal problems in the post-9/11 world, and they rarely made the front page. In hindsight, they could have. Strange things were going on.
Richard Warman
Richard Warman worked for the Canadian Human Rights Commission from 2002 to 2004, but after that started bringing online hate speech cases to the tribunal on more of a freelance basis, sometimes supporting the CHRC prosecution. On paper, it was all him. Of 33 cases referred for a tribunal since 2001, out of 71, Warman brought all but two, and he won them all, winning fines and cease and desist orders against offenders.
His methods were unorthodox and unsupervised. As part of his efforts to document his targets and confirm their identities, he would sometimes pose as an extremist in online forums. He would go so far that a 2009 tribunal ruling, which he nevertheless won, criticized him for "disappointing and disturbing" online behaviour, including posting hate messages written by others that "could have precipitated further hate messages."
He posted on the white supremacist Vanguard News Network, for example, referring to Jeff Schoep, former leader of the neo-Nazi National Socialist Movement: "Keep up the good work Commander Schoep!" He then signed the message with "88," a common neo-Nazi salute that means "Heil Hitler," because H is the eighth letter of the alphabet.
"I tried to make sure that I was not approaching that line (of entrapment, or participation in hate speech), and I did my best to avoid anything that might look that way," Warman once told the National Post.
He compared his method to "triage," going after the worst offenders and working his way down, but starting with clear cut calls for genocide or ethnic cleansing. "I never ran out of bright-red targets to address, so I never had to question myself, as to saying whether, well, this is getting a little grey, it's a little weak, maybe this is something that doesn't merit a human rights complaint. It was always situations where there was a crystal-clear violation of the law," Warman said in the interview in 2012.
Warman filed the fateful case against Lemire in 2003, but no one paid it much attention until the Mark Steyn affair.
That was when the writer published an excerpt of his book America Alone in Maclean's magazine, with provocative claims about the demographic rise of Muslims, titled "The Future Belongs To Islam." Soon after, Mohamed Elmasry, a computer engineering professor at the University of Waterloo, met with a small group of York University law students in the Al Madina restaurant in Waterloo, and decided upon a legal response on behalf of Canada's Muslims.
Having been refused equal space for a rebuttal in Maclean's, they filed human rights hate speech complaints in three jurisdictions: Ontario and British Columbia, where provincial human rights law applies to printed material; and federally, which applies to online material.
New Liberal 'online harms' bill to make online hate punishable up to life in prison
A recent survey by the Angus Reid Institute found that 63% of people who intend to vote for the Justin Trudeau's Liberals are doing it primarily to block the Conservatives from getting into power. Trudeau wants four more years. But what for?
They all eventually failed, and only B.C. actually heard the case on its merits at a tribunal, where the complaint was dismissed. But they drew attention to human rights law and commissions in a totally unprecedented manner.
Not long previously, a Calgary imam, Syed Soharwardy, brought a complaint under Alberta human rights law against Ezra Levant and his Western Standard magazine for publishing the Danish Muhammad cartoons, a set of satirical depictions of the prophet that were not published in any other mainstream Canadian outlet, and were the spark to a global outrage.
In response, Levant became the ringleader of a campaign to, as he put it, "denormalize" not just Section 13, but human rights law and quasi-judicial human rights tribunals in general. He recorded and broadcast his meeting with an officer of the Alberta Human Rights and Citizenship Commission, and developed online protest strategies he would later use for other political causes.
Seeing this national debate over free speech rise so quickly, Soharwardy had a change of heart and dropped out of the complaint, saying he had only wanted dialogue, not punishment. But another Muslim organization saw it through to a decision, which found the publication did not violate any human rights hate laws.
The issue was suddenly a hot topic, but in the early years, politicians avoided the issue like the plague, both Liberal and Conservative. The lone exception for many years was Liberal backbencher Keith Martin, who was an early advocate for getting human rights commissions out of the online hate speech game.
But political cover for bigger players was soon to be on offer. That came in 2008 when Richard Moon, a University of Windsor constitutional law professor with expertise in freedoms of speech and religion, released an independent review of Section 13 and the Canadian Human Rights Commission's whole hate speech apparatus. In effect, he called for them to shut it down.
"The use of censorship by the government should be confined to a narrow category of extreme expression - that which threatens, advocates or justifies violence against the members of an identifiable group," Moon wrote.
Suddenly everyone was all over the human rights commissions, provincial and federal. Legislatures called reviews, and invited bold faced names to testify. Newspapers that had never even sent a reporter to a tribunal hearing started calling for entire legal regimes to be repealed.
Next year came the bombshell. Finally, there was a decision in Warman v. Lemire, but not just that, a legal indictment of the system from inside. Tribunal chair Athanasios Hadjis ruled that Section 13 violates the Charter right to free expression because it carries the threat of punitive fines. He found Lemire had violated it in one instance, but had removed the offending material as soon as he learned of Warman's complaint against him, so Hadjis declined to issue a penalty in light of his wider decision.
This looked like the end. Other Section 13 cases were thrown into a strange limbo. How could they be prosecuted if the tribunal chair himself thinks the law is invalid? It would be appealed, of course, and everyone would intervene, and eventually the Supreme Court would side with either Warman or Lemire. Or so it seemed.
By the time Warman v. Lemire got to Federal Court, where tribunal decisions are appealed, the ruling Conservatives had a majority in Parliament and had signalled their support for Section 13 was over, and that they would now seek to repeal it as an affront to free speech. That vote went through in 2012, but the saga was far from over. The decision in Whatcott had yet to land.
Bill Whatcott at the Supreme Court of Canada in Ottawa in 2011.
Bill Whatcott was an attention seeker in a way Lemire never was. A nurse by profession, he was motivated in his anti-gay protests by shame at his own homosexual experiences as a troubled youth. His antics got him attention, such as running for office as a no-hoper single issue candidate, or starting a Heterosexual Pride Day Parade in Regina, which got him a spot on the The Daily Show where he was mocked as a closeted gay man.
But it was his anti-gay flyers that got him in trouble, especially those he distributed headlined "Keep Homosexuality out of Saskatoon's Public Schools!"
Found to violate Saskatchewan's human rights hate law about printed material, but overturned on appeal, the case was argued at the Supreme Court in 2011, attracting more than two dozen intervenors. It was a gong show, and not without moments of levity. Justice Marshall Rothstein, who would go on to write for the unanimous court in 2013, for example, asked a question that indicated he was at least momentarily unclear on the difference between "buggery" (a pejorative word for anal sex which, in an archaic legal context, can also mean sex with animals) and "bestiality" (a more technical term for sex with animals). The chief justice set him straight, to chuckles from the gallery.
The Supreme Court also struggled with the archaic word "calumny," part of the definition of hate set by the 1990 Taylor decision, which means malicious misrepresentation. So they removed it, in light of its "general disuse" in "everyday vocabulary," and because it is unnecessary given the other two elements, detestation and vilification.
Otherwise, they ruled against Whatcott, and upheld human rights hate speech laws. Canada does not punish people for the emotion of hatred. But it can hold them civilly accountable for the effect of public hate messages, which "rises beyond causing emotional distress to individual group members. It can have a societal impact," according to the Supreme Court.
This, in effect, is why Section 13 was always good law, even if it was applied poorly. To drive the point home, the Federal Court of Canada even overturned the Hadjis decision about Section 13 being unconstitutional.
In the early days of the Section 13 scandal, when she announced Richard Moon's independent review, the late former chief commissioner of the CHRC, Jennifer Lynch, used a popcorn analogy for online hate speech.
"It's kind of like microwaving popcorn, you know? For the first while on the internet, there was this little pop, pop, pop. And now, the popcorn is in full popping formation. It's just omnipresent, 24/7, popping up here, popping up there, and so it seems to make it difficult for measured voices to respond," she told National Post in 2008.
Expressions of hate on the internet can seem like that sometimes, like an unstoppable barrage of little explosions.
Today, Richard Moon knows everything has changed online. Hate speech is no longer on special or private or little-known websites used by like-minded people, the sort who get invited to Marc Lemire's chat rooms, or who seek out malicious theories about Jewish global domination or Black genetic inferiority. Today it proliferates on social media, out in the open, mainstream to the point of being familiar to anyone paying attention. Traditional legal responses seem not only powerless, but hopelessly antiquated.
As Moon writes in a book soon to be published by University of Toronto Press, a new version of Section 13 might offer a more "expeditious" process. Maybe.
"But even if the human rights process is speedier and less formal than the criminal process, it is not nearly quick or efficient enough to address the volume of hateful material that spreads rapidly and widely online," Moon writes. "The human rights code complaint and investigation processes, even if streamlined, could quickly become overwhelmed by the sheer volume of hateful material online. However, if, under the new version of s. 13, the commission receives a manageable number of complaints, or as few as it did under the old law, then not only will the law be ineffective in addressing the problem of online hate, but it will appear to be unfairly selective in its application."
Section 13 is back, baby, and it looks as troubled as it ever was.

Subject: Extremism; Nazi groups; White supremacy; Hate speech; Racism; Race; Muslims; Human rights; Tribunals & commissions; Criminal law

Location: Canada

Document 216 of 500

Chris Selley: Governments seeking 'like-minded' judges is Canada's latest non-scandal

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-governments-seeking-like-minded/docview/2933371554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chris+Selley%3A+Governments+seeking+%27like-minded%27+judges+is+Canada%27s+latest+non-scandal&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government in Ontario clearly think they have a political winner in vowing to appoint "like-minded" judges - which is to say, per Ford in the legislature on Tuesday, "judges that believe in throwing someone in jail when they kick the doors in, put a gun to people's heads, terrorizing their kids, terrorizing the parents to the point that the kids don't want to stay at home anymore."
Ford is unapologetic about his anti-terror agenda, and also about appointing "like-minded" people to the committee that recommends judicial appointments. The most recent such appointments are two former Premier's Office staffers, one of whom - the committee's new chair, Matthew Bondy - is also a registered lobbyist at Queen's Park.
It's a classic example of Ford taking a mile when he really only needs a few yards. Why not pursue this goal without the blatant patronage? And it's frustrating, because his self-described "tripling down" on appointing "like-minded" judges puts a vituperative face on an entirely reasonable and well-understood notion: Governments want to appoint judges who more or less share their worldview.
The U.S. Supreme Court is certainly a cautionary example of what can happen when that principle is taken to an extreme - especially on a single issue, namely abortion. But the fact is, those extremes aren't really available in Canada to judicial appointment committees. There can't be more than five jurists in this country who are remotely qualified to sit as judges and who also think abortion should be illegal in every circumstance. There might be none.
(It always strikes me as ironic that Canadian Supreme Court justices all go to the same few law schools and think mostly the same, while American Supreme Court justices all go to the same few law schools and would struggle to agree on the law of gravity.)
There are conservative jurists out there in Canada who could certainly make a difference. And if they are willing to take a harder line on granting bail to repeat violent offenders, or on sentencing those offenders - within the boundaries of law, of course - then that is an entirely reasonable policy goal for any government to pursue.
Those who believe bail, sentencing and parole provisions in this country are too strict - which is to say roughly 95 per cent of the legal academy - will note that it's actually harder to get bail nowadays than it used to be. And that's certainly an important data point. It's just not as important a data point as the number of flamboyantly recidivist criminals out there making life miserable-to-intolerable while they're out on bail or parole.
If I have a complaint about Ford's performance on this file, and I do, it's that tougher judges can really only move the needle so far. I'm all for incapacitating violent offenders for a good long while in prison, and not letting them out on bail if they're accused of offending again, but the vast majority of them are going to get out at some point, most under fairly strict parole and probation conditions.
If bail, parole and probation conditions were being enforced half-properly, several of the most awful cases we've seen in recent years, and which have propelled this tougher-on-crime movement, would not have happened. The most outrageous, but not by much, might be the case of Jordan O'Brien-Tobin, who racked up 28 convictions in a single year in Newfoundland, but was free to stab 16-year-old stranger Gabriel Magalhaes to death for no reason on a Toronto subway platform last year.
O'Brien-Tobin's parole conditions included not leaving Newfoundland. Clearly no one bothered to check.
That's on the provincial governments to manage, Ford's included. Unless parole and bail conditions are enforced properly in future, which costs money, these horrendous cases will keep occurring. A few conservative-minded judges won't change much in that regard.
But good lord, is the pearl-clutching among Ford's opponents ever ridiculous.
For eight years we have watched Justin Trudeau's government tear its hair out trying to find potential judges who are fluently bilingual, female and Indigenous, in addition to hailing from the proper part of the country. To the evident shock and confusion of those inside Trudeau's weirdo bilingual bubble, it turned out there weren't exactly thousands of such lawyers!
And so we got, for example, Supreme Court justice Michelle O'Bonsawin - who, as very reasonable law professor Leonid Sirota argued in the National Post, is unqualified in every traditional respect to serve.
O'Bonsawin did however write the following on her questionnaire: "A constitutional democracy will face threats, not only from within its borders, but also from abroad which is further facilitated with social media."
"This would have been music to the government's ears, what with its worries about foreign interference," Sirota wrote, "and bodes ill for the prospects of O'Bonsawin standing up to its ongoing attempts to censor online communications."
Perhaps more to the point, O'Bonsawin wrote the following: "As Canadians, we must stop focusing on our differences and embrace diversity in order to move our country forward in a progressive manner."
That's a clearly stated political viewpoint. It would be silly to argue that a Liberal government should overlook a potential judge for stating such an opinion. Indeed, a Liberal government should be seeking out judges who think that way (in addition to meeting all the necessary linguistic, genital and ethnic requirements). Equally logically, a Conservative government should be seeking out judges who aren't so outwardly progressive. If Conservative governments are willing to admit that's what they're doing, unlike Liberal governments, that should be considered a feature rather than a bug.
Chris Selley: The Liberals' 'online harms' legislation will disappoint its supporters most
Chris Selley: Locking up murderers is a slam-dunk issue for the Conservatives

Subject: Law schools; Abortion; Bilingualism; Government; Judges & magistrates

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: premier; progressive; conservative; government; ontario; clearly

Title: Chris Selley: Governments seeking 'like-minded' judges is Canada's latest non-scandal

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 29, 2024

Section: NP Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-governments-seeking-like-minded/docview/2933371554/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 217 of 500

How to win back the 905; Conservatives pin hopes on Parm Gill

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-win-back-905-conservatives-pin-hopes-on-parm/docview/2933291570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=How+to+win+back+the+905%3B+Conservatives+pin+hopes+on+Parm+Gill&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the dust settled after the 2011 federal election, Stephen Harper's Conservatives had secured a first majority government, after minorities in 2006 and 2008.
The road to majority ran through the Greater Toronto Area, where the party added 19 seats: nine in the 416 area code of downtown Toronto (where it previously held none) and 10 more in the 905 commuter belt (where it now held 21 of 22 seats).
The GTA victories contributed to a national seat count that rose to 166 from 143. The party elected 73 MPs in Ontario, up from 51, by securing 44 per cent of the vote in Canada's most populous province.
Coincidentally, a new poll by Abacus Data has the federal Conservatives back at 44 per cent support in Ontario. If that level of support is maintained to the next election, Pierre Poilievre's party is on course to double its seat count from the current 36.
The 2011 election was a case of preparation meeting opportunity. The Conservatives had woken up to the fact that the leap forward to majority necessitated an ethnic-outreach strategy that went beyond the tokenism of the past.
Then immigration minister Jason Kenney spent many weekends in the banquet halls of multicultural communities, spreading the message that the Conservatives shared their entrepreneurial spirit and family values. The late finance minister Jim Flaherty acted as GTA lieutenant, spending the election campaign at GO Transit stations meeting commuters. Policies were narrowcast at target communities, such as ending visa requirements from Poland and Taiwan.
The Conservatives may have thought that they discovered the Holy Grail of Canadian politics and that their alliance with conservative ethnic voters would cement their dominance in perpetuity.
If they believed that, they were quickly disabused of it in 2015 when the Liberals swept the 905 and kicked the Tories out of most of their seats in the 416.
Justin Trudeau and his Ontario lieutenant, Navdeep Bains, appealed to multicultural communities with a promise to double family reunification numbers and invest in public transit to reduce commute times. Trudeau subsequently appointed Bains and three other Sikhs to his first cabinet.
However, it is the lack of brand loyalty that looks set to benefit Poilievre at the next election. The multicultural vote is very much up for grabs.
"The Sikhs, in particular, are very politically astute and like to side with the party in power or perceived to be coming into power," said one Conservative who has been involved in ethnic outreach for decades.
He said the momentum is apparent in fundraising, where the Conservatives raised $35 million last year, their best ever total in a non-election year.
As part of his concerted effort to replicate the success of 2011, Poilievre has persuaded his old caucus colleague Parm Gill to leave his post as Ontario's minister of red tape reduction for the rather less secure position of Conservative candidate in the Liberal-held riding of Milton.
At face value, it seems a poor trade-off: giving up a job as a sitting provincial cabinet minister to become a mere candidate.
But Gill's task is not only to take Milton from Adam van Koeverden but to prepare the ground for a Conservative sweep through Brampton and Mississauga.
If he is successful, he is well-positioned to be the senior minister responsible for the GTA, in similar fashion to Flaherty.
Gill is a former MP who defeated Liberal Ruby Dhalla in the Brampton-Springdale riding in the 2011 sweep but was himself defeated in the new riding of Brampton North by Ruby Sahota in 2015. He has represented Milton at Queen's Park since 2018.
Poilievre has already made his own inroads in the Toronto area, attending events and micro-targeting communities like the Tamils, where he has promised a Conservative government will call for Sri Lanka's former president Gotabaya Rajapaksa to answer for alleged war crimes before an international court.
And, as Kenney did, he has pointed out that many ethnic communities are closer to the Conservatives when it comes to public safety and social issues.
Gill has a long history of speaking out on both.
As the MP for Brampton- Springdale, he introduced a private member's bill that made it a crime to target someone for recruitment into a gang, which passed into law in 2014.
As a provincial candidate in Milton, he was a vocal critic of the Ontario Liberal government's changes to the sexual education curriculum that he said were an attack on family values.
Gill's latest career move is a gamble. The Conservatives know they have to secure a majority, or face the prospect of a formal Liberal-NDP coalition.
But it is a calculated gamble. The same Abacus poll that has the Conservatives at 44 per cent support in Ontario, suggests 85 per cent of the population thinks it is time for a change of government. Betting against those kinds of numbers has never been a profitable endeavour.
National Post jivison@criffel.ca Twitter.com/IvisonJ

Business indexing term: Subject: Brand loyalty; Industry: 81394 :‎ Political Organizations

Subject: Conservatism; Brand loyalty; Multiculturalism & pluralism; Values; State elections; Political parties

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Harper, Stephen

Identifier / keyword: settled; federal; election; stephen; conservatives; secured

Title: How to win back the 905; Conservatives pin hopes on Parm Gill

Publication title: National Post; Don Mills, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 29, 2024

Section: Canada

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Don Mills, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: Business And Economics--Banking And Finance

ISSN: 14868008

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2933291570

Document 218 of 500

Governments should be expected to seek out 'like-minded' judges

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/governments-should-be-expected-seek-out-like/docview/2933286703/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Governments+should+be+expected+to+seek+out+%27like-minded%27+judges&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

If Conservative governments are willing to admit that's what they're doing, unlike Liberal governments, that should be considered a feature rather than a bug.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Appointments & personnel changes; Industry: 61131 :‎ Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools

Document 224 of 500

FIRST READING: The heinous offenders Canada has freed in just the last three months

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-heinous-offenders-canada-has-freed/docview/2932985128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+The+heinous+offenders+Canada+has+freed+in+just+the+last+three+months&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
This month, one of Canada's most notorious serial killers officially became eligible to apply for day parole.
It was 22 years ago that Robert Pickton was first arrested at his Port Coquitlam pig farm under suspicion of being personally responsible for scores of women disappearing from Vancouver's Downtown Eastside. Convicted for the second-degree murder of six women, Pickton has confessed to killing as many as 49.
But under Canadian criminal law, there is zero mechanism to sentence an offender to life imprisonment. While Pickton was technically handed a "life" sentence, that just means his parole conditions never expire. Like any convicted murderer, he can apply for day parole just 22 years after his arrest, and full parole after 25 years.
On the day before Pickton's parole eligibility, families of his victims gathered outside the farm where he had committed the murders. Speaking to the Canadian Press, they said lawyers had assured them that Pickton would never be paroled, but that they had few reasons to believe them. "I don't trust the system. There's always going to be that fear," said Lorelei Williams, cousin of Pickton victim Tanya Holyk.
While Pickton may yet remain a special case, the Canadian justice system has indeed granted parole to any number of heinous criminals that the public assumed would never get out, including mass shooters, serial killers, cannibals, cop-killers and the murderers of children.
Below, a not-at-all comprehensive list of instances from just the last three months involving serious offenders who were given parole, bail or early release.
A rapist who burned his victim to death given prison day passes
On the same week that Pickton became eligible for day parole, the Parole Board of Canada handed 37-year-old Jeremy Wade Vojkovic a 60-day unescorted absence from prison.
This is despite the fact that Vojkovi violated his release conditions the last time they gave him day parole in 2022. The Parole Board also brushed aside a psychiatric assessment finding that Vojkovic posed a "grave concern" to public safety.
Vojkovic's crime, committed just 22 years ago, was to randomly attack Maple Ridge woman Colleen Findlay on her own property. After binding and raping Findlay, he set fire to her home, killing her in the resulting blaze.
Bail for a killer and serial child rapist
Jatin Patel is a former dangerous offender, a designation that is ostensibly supposed to incur indefinite prison terms in the interest of public safety. But he had the designation overturned, resulting in a string of offences and violated parole conditions that continues to this day.
He had already served prison time for the 2003 killing of a Vancouver prostitute when he racked up a string of sexual offences against children in the 2010s; he groped a 13-year-old girl at a Safeway and sexually assaulted another 13-year-old at a Surrey motel.
Just last week, Patel received bail on his latest charge of violating a long-term supervision order. The bail conditions were exactly the same as the parole conditions that he had just finished violating.
'No joke': Ottawa to give Ukraine $4 million to fund gender-inclusive demining
RCMP never tried to interview Justin Trudeau as part of SNC-Lavalin scandal probe, ethics committee told
A notorious child kidnapper faces zero consequences for escaping
In 2011, Randall Hopley made national headlines after he kidnapped a three-year-old boy from his bedroom.
A massive police response ultimately convinced Hopley to return the boy unharmed, but it was the culmination of a long history of Hopley sexually victimizing children - including one instance in which he was found to have built a remote forest cabin with a room filled with children's toys and sexual paraphernalia that locked from the outside.
Regardless, Hopley began receiving parole in 2019 - and continued to receive parole even after multiple breaches of his conditions, including hanging out near children at public libraries.
After absconding from a Vancouver halfway house in November, Hopley was again made the subject of an all-out police search. But once Hopley was caught after 10 days, the Parole Board decided in early February to impose no new conditions and to keep granting him leave from prison.
Day parole only eight years after beating a woman to death, covering up the murder
When convicted murderer Grace Elinor Robotti, 73, was granted day parole just before Christmas, it prompted statements from First Nations leaders that Robotti's light treatment was emblematic of a system that didn't care about murdered Indigenous women. Grand Chief Stewart Phillip, president of the Union of B.C. Indian Chiefs, called it "another tragic page in the history of missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls."
In 2015, Robotti used a crowbar to kill 26-year-old Roxanne Louie, the mother of her great-grandson. Robotti then reported Louie missing, disposed of the murder weapon and dumped her victim's body in a remote area. At trial she would also claim to have acted in self-defence, which a jury rejected.
Nevertheless, she still managed to serve the lightest sentence possible for murder in Canada: A second-degree conviction, with day parole granted at the first available opportunity. She received a mandatory life sentence in 2017, but was granted day parole in January.
Parole just 13 years after murdering a neighbour over a dispute about a dog
The justice system actually worked exactly as intended in the case of convicted murderer Michel Langlois. If anything, he receiving harsher treatment than is typical.
Convicted of second-degree murder, Langlois technically only had to wait 10 years before applying for full parole. The Parole Board also found Langlois had demonstrated "appreciable introspection" and was generally not considered at high risk to murder anyone else.
But his granting of full parole this week comes just 13 years after Langlois confronted 27-year-old Justyna Kozyra outside her Montreal apartment and stabbed her 14 times, mostly in the neck - and in front of witnesses. Langlois had objected to Kozyra allowing her dog to defecate in front of his building.
Unescorted absences from prison less than four years after helping to gun down two men
In March, 2020, Alberta farmer Roger Bilodeau saw a truck containing two men briefly slow down in front of his rural property. After deciding they must be thieves, Bilodeau marshalled his two sons into a high-speed chase of the truck - that ended with one of the Bilodeau sons shooting and killing both men.
The occupants of the truck were hunters who just happened to be passing by - and Bilodeau didn't even have a history of being targeted by thieves. He would later chalk up the double-killing to a "dumb judgement call."
Although handed a 10-year sentence for manslaughter, just this week the Parole Board of Canada granted unescorted absences from prison to Bilodeau This marked a rare instance in which the RCMP had openly advised the Parole Board to not approve release - to no avail. "It would create a greater divide within the area that is unnecessary and frankly displays the holes in the justice system," wrote the Mounties.
Father who helped kill daughter given day parole
The name of the man in this case is subject to a publication ban in order to protect the identity of his victim. But in 2019, he participated in the forcible confinement of his seven-year-old daughter, reportedly because she was repeatedly attempting to run away to avoid abuse in the home.
The man restrained his daughter with a shirt and left her in the care of the girl's stepmother - who wrapped her so tightly with tape that she suffocated and died. The tragedy caused shockwaves throughout Quebec in 2019 because this all occurred despite the family already having been under supervision by youth protection services.
The stepmother would be convicted of second-degree murder, but the father would only get manslaughter and a 42-month sentence. However, he hadn't even served two thirds of the sentence before he was given day parole in early December
Man who murdered and desecrated remains of Saskatchewan woman starts stalking coworker upon release
On Dec. 5, the Parole Board of Canada decided to revoke the parole of brutal murderer Kenneth Mackay after it was discovered that he had immediately used his release to conduct himself in a way that was eerily similar to the circumstances that had precipitated his incarceration in the first place.
In 2000, MacKay offered a ride home to Crystal Paskemin, a 21-year-old woman he had met in a bar. Instead, he drove her to the outskirts of town, raped and beat her, murdered her and then desecrated her remains by dragging them behind his truck and also setting them on fire.
Despite prison officials deeming MacKay a high risk to reoffend violently, in 2023 he was granted day parole and released to a facility on Vancouver Island. After only a few months, MacKay began stalking a female coworker whom he had begun giving rides to work. Only after blocking MacKay's phone number and receiving numerous unsolicited visits from him did the coworker learn of his criminal history.
IN OTHER NEWS
When the Trudeau government took office, the Canadian internet was generally unregulated - as it always had been. Two pieces of legislation have since made it one of the most tightly regulated internets in the democratic world. The Online Streaming Act mandates Canadian content quotas for everything from YouTube to Netflix. The Online News Act, meanwhile, requires web giants to pay a fee to media outlets in exchange for sharing news links (which is why Facebook has simply shut down the sharing of news entirely).
One of the more bizarre Canadian political traditions is for finance ministers to purchase a new pair of shoes before tabling a budget. Naturally, the practice has turned into an increasingly elaborate pageant of political branding. Above is a screenshot of a two-minute video posted to social media by Alberta finance minister Nate Horner in which he showcases his new boots and elaborates on his background as a down-to-earth, fiscally responsible rancher.
And the Trudeau government is not yet done with e-legislation. On Monday, they tabled the Online Harms Bill, which would appoint a commission with broad powers to shut down anything deemed to be "harmful content" - a category including everything from revenge porn to a statement that "foments hatred." The bill would also technically make it possible to jail someone for life for hate speech. It's a scaled down version of a much more extreme Liberal bill that proposed to make it a crime to say anything online "likely to expose a person or persons to hatred or contempt." But a critique by the National Post's John Ivison notes that the bill could well be abused by power-hungry bureaucrats. "If all these new regulatory bodies and online adjudicators are overzealous in their application of the law, it will have a chilling effect on the behaviour of Canadians online," he wrote
It's been exactly 10 years since HMCS Protecteur - a Royal Canadian Navy supply ship that was long past its scheduled retirement date (it's the one in the middle in the 2002 photo above) - caught fire during an exercise in the Pacific. The fire burned for 11 hours in the ship's main engine room, forcing crew members to fight the flames in darkness and in the midst of rough seas - at one point needing to crack open a shipment of humanitarian aid in order to press a donation of gas generators into service. A decade later, there still isn't an official replacement for Protecteur; all of Canada's naval resupply is done by a converted container ship that isn't allowed to sail into war zones.
It's nothing new that the Conservatives are nearly 20 points ahead of the Liberals in polls, but a new Nanos survey is interesting in that it shows a near tie between the Liberals (23 per cent) and the NDP (22 per cent). As Prime Minister Justin Trudeau heads a minority government, he's entirely dependent upon NDP support to remain in power. But the NDP could soon be faced with a situation in which they either continue supporting the Trudeau government in exchange for goodies like pharmacare, or they precipitate an election with the chance of becoming official opposition.
Tom Green - the Canadian comedian who rose to prominence in the 1990s for weird pranks and shock humour - now spends a lot of his time on a giant ranch in the middle of rural Ontario. In a recent appearance on the hit podcast The Joe Rogan Experience, Green (dressed in a Canadian Army surplus tunic) talked about being a licensed Canadian gun owner who often walks around with a lever-action rifle for protection against bears. After the interview, Green posted the above photo to X.com.
Get all of these insights and more into your inbox by signing up for the First Reading newsletter here

Business indexing term: Industry: 92215 :‎ Parole Offices and Probation Offices

Subject: Women; Sex crimes; Murders & murder attempts; Manslaughter; Police pursuit driving; Parole boards; Serial crime; Public safety; Rape; Criminal sentences; Prisons; Serial murders; Parole & probation; Kidnapping; Imprisonment

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: first; reading; daily; newsletter; keeping; posted

Title: FIRST READING: The heinous offenders Canada has freed in just the last three months

Document 225 of 500

Raymond J. de Souza: Will Justin Trudeau follow in his father's footsteps and resign on Feb. 29?

Author: Far Raymond J. de Souza

Publication info: Far Raymond J. de Souza. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/raymond-j-de-souza-will-justin-trudeau-follow-his/docview/2932835454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Raymond+J.+de+Souza%3A+Will+Justin+Trudeau+follow+in+his+father%27s+footsteps+and+resign+on+Feb.+29%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Far+Raymond+J.+de+Souza&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A warm winter likely means no snowstorm for the second Prime Minister Trudeau on Wednesday night. El Niño denies le fils.
This week offers the dramatic flair beloved by Justin Trudeau - the Mark Antony eulogy at his father's funeral, the blackface costumes, the Bollywood tour of India. Forty years ago this 29th of February, Pierre Elliott Trudeau announced his decision to resign as Liberal leader. He said the idea crystallized while walking in an Ottawa snowstorm the night before. But it was more likely that he wanted to resign on a memorable date offered by a timely leap year.
Thus the heir, facing the same late-term unpopularity as his father, may find it compelling to resign on the same date as his father did. Curse that climate change, though. A wintry landscape will be lacking, not an insignificant consideration for the man who replaced the cross and fleur-de-lis on the Canadian Royal Crown with maple leaves and snowflakes.
Pierre Trudeau had lost the May 1979 election to the Conservatives' Joe Clark. By the fall of that year, he had planned to retire, but Clark fumbled away his government and Trudeau returned with a majority in February 1980. He could hardly believe his luck - a triumphal comeback without ever having to leave.
The next four years were dizzying - the sovereignty referendum in Quebec, patriation of the Constitution, the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, recession, massive deficit spending, the national energy program. By 1984, the country was exhausted and found Trudeau exhausting. A leap year meant another day to aggravate the country. Trudeau knew as much and took advantage of Feb. 29 to resign.
For the election on May 22, 1979, the Progressive Conservatives used the slogan "Let's get Canada working again." They were borrowing in part from the Conservatives in the United Kingdom. "Labour Isn't Working" was their powerful slogan, and Margaret Thatcher drove the Labour Party from office less than three weeks before the Canadian election, on May 3, 1979.
Clark would last less than seven months before being defeated in the House. Thatcher would last 11 years. The Iron Lady had a majority and all the qualities that Clark so spectacularly lacked. She got on with three straight majority governments, and her successor won a fourth.
Trudeau père won four of five elections, with three majorities (1968, 1974, 1980) and one minority (1972). Trudeau fils has won three out of three, one majority (2015) and two minorities (2019, 2021), roughly similar to the Conservatives' Stephen Harper, who also had one majority (2011) and two minorities (2006, 2008) but also lost twice (2004, 2015).
Were Justin Trudeau to fight and win again he would have accomplished something - four consecutive election victories - not done since Sir John A. Macdonald won four straight majority governments.
But that appears to be an unlikely prospect. And the Conservatives appear to be ready with a slogan comparable to Thatcher's in 1979: "Everything feels broken."
Canada remains a blessed country in which to live; one of our current stresses is that a very great number of people want to move here as quickly as they can. And while many parts of the world are suffering various horrors, our problems pale in comparison. But it is not the comparison that matters. Saying that Canada is not Venezuela is no reason to boast. Saying that Canada may one day become Argentina - a once rich country pauperized by malgovernance - is reason to worry.
Raymond J. de Souza: The meaning of Justin Trudeau's petty falsehoods
Raymond J. de Souza: Guilbeault looks to maintain Canada's Third World road network
Everything does seem broken, but the prime minister seems to think that Canadians are broken, too grumpy, too bigoted, too ungrateful, too stuck in the past where we would sell our precious natural resources to our allies.
The headlines from the past few weeks pile up one upon another, a veritable snowdrift suitable for a leap year stroll. There was the elderly man who broke his arm, and then was so broken by his hospitalization that he eventually died Too many Canadians have similar stories to tell about family and friends.
We now think it normal to check ER wait times like we do a flight status before heading to the airport. Many intercontinental flights are shorter than ER wait times. The national daycare program now has couples trying to get on a wait list before they go for their first ultrasound. And that's before a wave of child-care facilities shut down altogether under the new federal scheme.
There is the ArriveCan fiasco, a mind-boggling combination of supposed incompetence and negligence. The same "budgeting" that spent $60 million on a bug-ridden app has allocated $5 billion to renovate the Centre Block of Parliament. No amount of money seems able to renovate 24 Sussex. The Olympic Stadium in Montreal is due for some $870 million in the latest salvage attempt, justified on the preposterous grounds that it would cost $2 billion to demolish it.
The Royal Ontario Museum in Toronto is spending $130 million on a "transformative renovation" in order to fix the inadequacies of the last transformative renovation. The much ballyhooed "Crystal" was eye-candy for the architecture magazines but was not really suited to, well, museum exhibitions.
Canadian governments shovel out billions so that foreign workers can be employed by foreign companies to build electric vehicle batteries. Meanwhile, the electricity grid is insufficient to power today's needs, let alone millions of electric vehicles.
And so it goes. Once the national pharmacare program is launched, should Canadians expect to queue up overnight at pharmacies to obtain the now free but scarce - scarce because now free - diabetes drugs? The last government adventure in free pharma - the "safe supply" of opioids - does not build confidence.
It's not all Justin Trudeau's fault, to be sure. Some of it certainly is, like the dubious imposition of the Emergencies Act and the suppression of civil liberties. On that he followed his father's script.
Will he do so again? His father knew when it was time to walk in the Ottawa snow. El Niño being what it is, the Tofino surf might be a better bet for le fils. The place doesn't matter. The date does. Will he take the leap?
National Post
