Document 270 of 500

Matthew Lau: Failing national daycare is Liberals' fault, not anyone else's

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/matthew-lau-failing-national-daycare-is-liberals/docview/2929399732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Matthew+Lau%3A+Failing+national+daycare+is+Liberals%27+fault%2C+not+anyone+else%27s&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Trudeau government's national child care plan proves to be an expensive shambles, creating widespread shortages and destroying private child care businesses, Liberals and their supporters are trying to scapegoat provincial governments, notably in Alberta and Ontario where nominally Conservative governments are a politically convenient target. But while provincial implementation has often been poor, the national child care plan was doomed from the start and the Trudeau government must accept the blame for the program's misguided vision and bad design.
The national program is failing, not only in Alberta and Ontario, but from coast to coast, including in NDP-governed British Columbia and Newfoundland and Labrador, whose provincial government is Liberal. In fact, Statistics Canada reports that among families using child care, an astounding 67.2 per cent in Newfoundland and Labrador had difficulty finding care last year - higher than in any other province and double the 33.5 per cent who had trouble finding it in 2019.
This is a problem created, not only by government-inflated demand, but also by lower supply: the number of children aged 0-5 years using child care in Newfoundland and Labrador is down by 20 per cent versus 2019. Meanwhile, the province second on the list for difficulty finding care is British Columbia, with 58.5 per cent of families using it saying they had trouble, up from 46.5 per cent in 2019 - also with fewer children in child care than pre-pandemic.
That the numbers are so bleak did not stop Jenna Sudds, federal minister responsible, from joining Premier David Eby earlier this month to trumpet expansion of the government program that produced them. "More families in British Columbia will save thousands of dollars every year as more than 700 child care spaces move into" the $10-a-day program, the provincial announcement said. That many more Canadian families will now be worse off financially because they must pay higher taxes to subsidize those 700 spaces, the government did not mention. And note that these are not new child care spaces, but rather existing spaces now being brought under the government program. Child care is not expanding; only government control of child care is, which means the province's shortages will at least continue and likely get worse.
No region of British Columbia seems exempt from child care shortages. "Vancouver's child care needs continue to be significantly unmet," Vancouver School Board Trustee Jennifer Reddy said last week, "with a total estimated shortfall of approximately 15,000 ... spaces for 0-12 year olds. Some communities in Vancouver face over 70 per cent ... unmet need for licensed childcare spaces." Moving away from the big city doesn't help: Smithers, population about 5,400, estimated at the end of last year that there "is a current gap of 220+ spaces" with "only 11 infant/toddler spaces available for every 100 children under 36 months of age." Same story in Dawson Creek: wait lists for infants and toddlers exceed two years
In Kelowna, as well, child care is said to be in crisis, with government more hindrance than help. A Kelowna daycare that has operated for nearly 30 years recently announced it has no new availability in either of its two locations until the fall, and "due to the current political environment towards private daycares all plans for expansion or to create additional spaces are on hold." In Kamloops, too, according to a CBC story last week, parents are desperate and struggling to find child care, while daycare educators say the waitlists are very long. All around the province, there are similar stories of crises and shortages
If national child care is failing whether Conservatives, Liberals, or the NDP are in charge, as is the case, how about in the Northwest Territories, where the legislative assembly is non-partisan? The agreement signed with the federal government, "has had a negative effect on daycare in the Northwest Territories," according to MLA Denny Rodgers. Daycares are facing financial shortfalls due to revenue controls, and "many daycares are considering closing entirely due to the uncertainty and accumulating more debt." Meanwhile "wait lists for child care spaces are getting longer each year," and will increase as subsidies inflate demand. "Some daycares across the country are pulling out of the program altogether," Rodgers observed.
Don't tax grocers, shut government food ministries
Canadians are overtaxed and underserved
Higher education would benefit from lower subsidies
The Trudeau government and its supporters clearly cannot get away with blaming nominally Conservative provincial governments for their own program's failures. From coast to coast, and regardless of the political stripe of the provincial or territorial government, the Trudeau government's national child care program is a shambles - a policy failure with disastrous outcomes for which the Liberals themselves are accountable.
Bookmark our website and support our journalism: Don't miss the business news you need to know - add financialpost.com to your bookmarks and sign up for our newsletters here

Document 271 of 500

Kelly McParland: As their party starts to crack, Liberals look the other way

Author: McParland, Kelly

Publication info: McParland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kelly-mcparland-as-their-party-starts-crack/docview/2929398823/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Kelly+McParland%3A+As+their+party+starts+to+crack%2C+Liberals+look+the+other+way&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McParland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rob Oliphant seems an unlikely candidate to serve as a fiery, chest-thumping rebel, chronic troublemaker or ego on legs searching out microphones and cameras he can spout off to.
A Liberal loyalist since youth, he has party credentials reaching back to the first Trudeau regime almost 50 years ago. He served in several capacities for David Peterson's Ontario government, has worked on more Liberal campaigns than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has made promises and was tasked with important roles by former leaders Stéphane Dion and Michael Ignatieff. In 2019, Trudeau appointed him parliamentary secretary to the minister of foreign affairs, a post he still holds.
A United Church minister, Oliphant has a Master of Divinity from the University of British Columbia, a doctorate from the Chicago Theological Seminary and a lengthy record of humanitarian and human rights activities both in Canada and abroad.
His standing may explain why Oliphant has escaped largely unscathed since delivering a scathing attack on his government's handling of the crisis in Israel.
In a leaked recording reported by the CBC, Oliphant denounced the Liberal decision to halt funding to the United Nations Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA), the main channel for aid to Palestinians, as "the wrong decision" and a political move aimed at placating Canada's allies, presumably meaning the United States given that it was the only other country to have cancelled its aid.
"You don't stop aid to Gaza because of 12 or 13 employees out of 13,000. It drives me crazy," he raged. "It is opportunistic, it is unfair and it is maligning the operation of a UN organization that is doing, not perfect work - there's no organization that's made of human beings that's perfect, UNRWA has its faults - but it is the best we have for education, for medical care, for food, all of those things."
The proper course, he said, would have been to shift an equal amount of money to other agencies aiding civilians in Gaza.
"Our government bungled that and we should have announced it the same day. All that money and more needs to go to Gaza for instantaneous humanitarian aid.
"We have half a million people at starvation levels. We're going to have cholera. We're going to have all kinds of things in Gaza. We need an immediate ceasefire."
He also bashed Trudeau's response to South Africa's attempt to have Israel condemned by the International Court of Justice, saying he warned the government it would be misunderstood and dismissing the result as "a communications disaster."
As a sign of the divisions troubling the Liberals, it far exceeds a suggestion by Ken McDonald, an otherwise mild-mannered MP from Newfoundland, that Trudeau had reached his "best-before date" and should face a leadership review. Yet while McDonald was swiftly browbeaten into claiming he didn't mean what he so clearly said, Trudeau responded to Oliphant's critique with the kind of bland airiness that has become a hallmark of his tenure.
"The kinds of conversations that go on within our party are not always easy, but they reflect that diversity of conversations happening across the country," he assured reporters, as if the issue had been which sandwich to order for lunch.
"We are continuing, as a country, to engage in the full complexities of the situation with a steadfast focus on bringing forward peace, stability and standing up for the rights of people to live in peace and stability."
Unlike McDonald, Oliphant was elected before Trudeau became leader, and thus can't be accused of owing him his career. Nor can he be dismissed as a know-nothing backbencher: he's on his fourth foreign minister, having started under Marc Garneau and remained for subsequent ministers Chrystia Freeland, François-Philippe Champagne and current minister Mélanie Joly.
As Joly's deputy, it fell to Oliphant to rise in her absence in the House of Commons and defend the UNRWA decision he so deeply disagreed with.
"Mr. Speaker, let me be very clear," he told the House on Feb. 8. "The funding that Canada is giving to civilians in Gaza has increased: just last week, $40 million more on top of the $60 million that was already there. This makes Canada a top donor for aid, helping with the crisis in Gaza. We are proud, and Canadians want us to help. Every time there is a time of emergency, we stand up and we are clear. We will always be there."
That must have hurt, and may have been a moment in which Oliphant considered resigning, something else he confessed to considering "many times" in his leaked conversation. If he did quit, there's a good chance the damage to the Trudeau government would be greater than the hurt to Oliphant. There's always his flock to go back to, while the prime minister is testing ever-deeper depths of unpopularity while striving to convince New Democrats to keep propping up his minority.
There's also a qualitative difference between Oliphant's remarks and other recent Liberal embarrassments. McDonald knew he was speaking to a reporter when he offered up his opinion, and thus had to realize the words would become public. Similarly, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault was addressing a conference on public transit when he declared Canada has enough roads already and Ottawa wouldn't be paying for any additions, a flub he had to "clarify" after Trudeau insisted there'd been no change in the government's position.
Oliphant, in contrast, was speaking on a private call to a constituent, who evidently chose to record the call without his knowledge and let the CBC have a listen. That may not impress voters weary of politicians preaching doctrines they don't believe, but is enough of a fig leaf for Liberals to hide behind. Given that Oliphant obediently defended the party line in public, he can't be accused of outright betrayal. This is the government, after all, that declared it acceptable for candidates to reject its abortion stand as long as they didn't vote according to their beliefs. And going after Oliphant risked the danger that fellow Liberals who share his views might come to his defence.
Why the media didn't make as much noise about the Oliphant outburst as they did about McDonald or Guilbeault is another issue. For the government, it was enough to hope ignoring one embarrassment was better than the danger of sparking another.
National Post
Avi Benlolo: Liberals to spend $100M to prop up terror-supporting Palestinian governments
Stephen Harper: Israel's war is just, Hamas must surrender or be eliminated

Document 272 of 500

National child care plan failing and Liberals to blame

Author: Lau, Matw

Publication info: Lau, Matw. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-child-care-plan-failing-liberals-blame/docview/2929297323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=National+child+care+plan+failing+and+Liberals+to+blame&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.8&amp;au=Lau%2C+Matw&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Trudeau government's national child care plan proves to be an expensive shambles, creating widespread shortages and destroying private child care businesses, Liberals and their supporters are trying to scapegoat provincial governments, notably in Alberta and Ontario where nominally Conservative governments are a politically convenient target. But while provincial implementation has often been poor, the national child care plan was doomed from the start and the Trudeau government must accept the blame for the program's misguided vision and bad design.
The national program is failing, not only in Alberta and Ontario, but from coast to coast, including in NDP-governed British Columbia and Newfoundland and Labrador, whose provincial government is Liberal. In fact, Statistics Canada reports that among families using child care, an astounding 67.2 per cent in Newfoundland and Labrador had difficulty finding care last year - higher than in any other province and double the 33.5 per cent who had trouble finding it in 2019.
This is a problem created, not only by government-inflated demand, but also by lower supply: the number of children aged 0-5 years using child care in Newfoundland and Labrador is down by 20 per cent versus 2019. Meanwhile, the province second on the list for difficulty finding care is British Columbia, with 58.5 per cent of families using it saying they had trouble, up from 46.5 per cent in 2019 - also with fewer children in child care than pre-pandemic.
That the numbers are so bleak did not stop Jenna Sudds, federal minister responsible, from joining Premier David Eby earlier this month to trumpet expansion of the government program that produced them. "More families in British Columbia will save thousands of dollars every year as more than 700 child care spaces move into" the $10-a-day program, the provincial announcement said. That many more Canadian families will now be worse off financially because they must pay higher taxes to subsidize those 700 spaces, the government did not mention. And note that these are not new child care spaces, but rather existing spaces now being brought under the government program. Child care is not expanding; only government control of child care is, which means the province's shortages will at least continue and likely get worse.
No region of British Columbia seems exempt from child care shortages. "Vancouver's child care needs continue to be significantly unmet," Vancouver School Board trustee Jennifer Reddy said last week, "with a total estimated shortfall of approximately 15,000 ... spaces for 0-12 year olds. Some communities in Vancouver face more than 70 per cent ... unmet need for licensed child care spaces." Moving away from the big city doesn't help: Smithers, population about 5,400, estimated at the end of last year that there "is a current gap of 220-plus spaces" with "only 11 infant/toddler spaces available for every 100 children under 36 months of age." Same story in Dawson Creek: wait lists for infants and toddlers exceed two years.
In Kelowna, as well, child care is said to be in crisis, with government more hindrance than help. A Kelowna daycare that has operated for nearly 30 years recently announced it has no new availability in either of its two locations until the fall, and "due to the current political environment toward private daycares, all plans for expansion or to create additional spaces are on hold." In Kamloops, too, according to a CBC story last week, parents are desperate and struggling to find child care, while daycare educators say the wait lists are very long. All around the province, there are similar stories of crises and shortages.
If national child care is failing whether Conservatives, Liberals, or the NDP are in charge, as is the case, how about in the Northwest Territories, where the legislative assembly is non-partisan? The agreement signed with the federal government, "has had a negative effect on daycare in the Northwest Territories," according to MLA Denny Rodgers. Daycares are facing financial shortfalls due to revenue controls, and "many daycares are considering closing entirely due to the uncertainty and accumulating more debt." Meanwhile "wait lists for child care spaces are getting longer each year," and will increase as subsidies inflate demand. "Some daycares across the country are pulling out of the program altogether," Rodgers observed.
The Trudeau government and its supporters clearly cannot get away with blaming nominally Conservative provincial governments for their own program's failures. From coast to coast, and regardless of the political stripe of the provincial or territorial government, the Trudeau government's national child care program is a shambles - a policy failure with disastrous outcomes for which the Liberals themselves are accountable.
Financial Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Shortages; Industry: 62441 :‎ Child Care Services

Document 278 of 500

MPs to demand that Guilbeault explain his 'no more new roads' policy in committee

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mps-demand-that-guilbeault-explain-his-no-more/docview/2929170362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=MPs+to+demand+that+Guilbeault+explain+his+%27no+more+new+roads%27+policy+in+committee&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault is not done explaining what he meant when he said that the federal government has decided to stop investing in new road infrastructure.
On Wednesday, the House of Commons committee on transportation will be holding an "urgent meeting" - at the request of Conservative, NDP and Bloc Québécois MPs - to shine a light on Guilbeault's controversial comments which have drawn criticism across the country.
"His public comments are shocking to hear as they have massive consequences on infrastructure development for Canadian communities and on the ability of communities to grow and prosper," wrote the opposition members in a letter sent to the chair of the committee on Feb. 16.
"Canadians deserve to know how this decision will impact their communities and their ability to drive their vehicles and live their lives."
In an interview, Conservative transport critic Mark Strahl said that Guilbeault's "insane policy of no more roads" has united Canadians across the political spectrum and all levels of government, and that Guilbeault needs to testify in front of the committee to answer questions on government policy.
"The question is could you be any more out of touch? Do you not know people that need to drive to work on our road network? Do you not know people who drive their kids to school? Do you not realize that our supply chains are incredibly reliant on new roads and improved roads?"
"We don't all live in downtown Montreal," added Strahl. "So, I think there are questions that he needs to answer including how exactly does this work for the majority of Canadians?"
'That's not what I said': Bicycling minister Guilbeault tries to walk back road building comments
Adam Pankratz: Anti-road Guilbeault drives down the slippery slope to climate tyranny
Opposition committee members are also calling on the Minister of Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Sean Fraser to come testify on government infrastructure policy.
"We either need to hear him condemn the remarks of the environment minister or explain to communities and Canadians why they are no longer going to have road projects built in partnership with the federal government," said Strahl.
"So, if Minister Fraser has a different idea of what the infrastructure programs are going to be, he should come and explain it to committee and to Canadians."
Guilbeault said in a conference on public transit in Montreal last week that all levels of government must make the hard decision to stop expanding the road network, and that adding more roads and new lanes would only encourage people to continue using their cars and call for further road expansions.
"Our government has made the decision to stop investing in new road infrastructure," he said, according to the Montreal Gazette which first reported on his remarks.
Guilbeault also said that the federal government would continue to support cities, provinces and territories in maintaining the existing road network, but that there would be "no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network."
His remarks caused wide condemnation from politicians from all stripes.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he was "gobsmacked" by Guilbeault's remarks, while Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said that "most of us can't... just walk 10km to work every day." Even B.C. Premier David Eby said that the federal minister's comments "made a lot of us very nervous."
When confronted by reporters in Ottawa, Guilbeault said he should have more specific, and mentioned that the federal government would not be funding large projects like the Quebec government's plans for a highway tunnel across the St. Lawrence River connecting Quebec City to its south shore.
He said that the federal government will continue to fund roads while providing massive investments in public transit and electrification of transportation.
Strahl said he does not "buy it for a second" that Guilbeault did not mean it when he backtracked on his comments and that "he probably faced more backlash than he thought."
"He was very, very clear and he is trying to walk it back now, doing a poor job of it and still causing alarm across the country. Because those major projects, communities are counting on those. You cannot address even the housing crisis without building new roads," he said.
"It's a ridiculous policy. It's an extreme policy. And it's a radical activist in a radical government proposing this war on cars that people need to live their lives."

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

FIRST READING: What it would look like if Canada was even attempting to build sufficient housing

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-what-would-look-like-if-canada-was/docview/2928827711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+What+it+would+look+like+if+Canada+was+even+attempting+to+build+sufficient+housing&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
The Trudeau government continues to see its popularity plummet to all-time lows, largely due to issues of worsening affordability. In response, the Liberals have touted their Housing Accelerator Fund, which they say could result in 100,000 new homes. They even slashed approvals for student visas in a bid to alleviate "pressures on housing."
But by any measure, this is all laughably inadequate. Canada already has the most acute housing shortage in the G7. Tack on immigration levels that are well beyond anything in our history, and housing construction isn't even close to keeping pace, much less patching up the shortage.
The Canadian housing crisis long ago passed the point at which it could be addressed with normal government action. Below, a quick portrait of just how disastrous the problem is, and what would be needed to fix it.
Housing construction would need to triple
According to the most recent estimates from the Canadian Mortgage and Housing Corporation, if Canada was to return to any semblance of housing affordability, we'd have to build as many as four million additional homes.
The CMHC defines "affordability" as the state of Canadian real estate in 2004, when an average home cost the 2024 equivalent of $368,000 (the current average price is $659,000). And we'd have to build those 4 million homes in addition to the current rate of homebuilding (which stood at 220,000 home completions for last year).
So, if Canada were to set a 10-year timeline to restore affordability in the housing market - and to do it while keeping immigration at historic highs - the country would need to build an average of at least 400,000 additional homes each year. Or, 1,100 new homes every single day.
Even if we're able to continue building homes at 2023 numbers (which seems doubtful, given a recent decline in residential construction), this would represent an incredible 270 per cent increase in Canadian homebuilding.
Stephen Harper makes surprise 'solidarity' trip to Israel
'Embarrassing': MPs react to Catherine Tait's refusal to rule out bonuses amid CBC job cuts
It would be akin to building another Nova Scotia each year (in addition to the homes we were already building)
Statistics Canada keeps figures on the number of "private dwellings" in each province, and according to the results of the 2016 census, there were 401,990 "occupied private dwellings" in Nova Scotia.
To build 4,000,000 extra homes, in short, Canada would only need to build an entire Nova Scotia worth of dwellings each year - and to do that every year for 10 years. To put it another way, four million homes is roughly equivalent to the entire housing stock of Quebec, Canada's second most-populous province.
Another way to visualize the sheer scale of the housing gap is to calculate how much U.S. territory Canada would need to annex until we finally had enough houses to stabilize prices. If the Americans suddenly evacuated Maine and gave it to us, for instance, that would add 651,000 housing units - so, 16 per cent of the four million total.
To get to that four million, Canada would also need to take every single house, apartment and row home in Vermont (339,000 homes), New Hampshire (649,000 homes), Montana (529,000 homes), Idaho (797,000 homes), North Dakota (378,000 homes) and South Dakota (408,306 homes).
This is basically impossible without drastic regulatory changes
The all-time record for Canadian home completions still belongs to 1978. In that year, a series of aggressive federal incentives on home construction helped drive the total to 246,533 new homes.
Given that the population at the time was only 24 million, this is proportionally equivalent to 2024 Canada managing to build a record-shattering 411,000 homes in a single year. Nevertheless, this would still be well short of the 620,000 new builds needed each year to both keep pace with immigration and build down the four-million-unit housing shortage in 10 years.
What's more, in the 1970s most Canadian cities still had plenty of cheap farmland within reach of downtown that could be turned into subdivisions. With most of that now built up (or closed to development as in the case of the Ontario Greenbelt), any increase now has to mostly be secured through densification of existing residential neighbourhoods.
As such, the primary roadblock to new homes is zoning rules; municipalities either prohibiting the construction of multi-unit homes, or subjecting every redevelopment to a months-long odyssey of red tape.
Getting anywhere near 620,000 new homes per year would require the feds to slap draconian curbs on housing byalws: Municipalities limited to 3 or 4 zoning classifications, with single-family zoning effectively abolished. At the same time, it wouldn't hurt for regulators to both harmonize Canadian building codes and delete anything unrelated to safety or consumer protection (like, say, Montreal's ban on gas heating).
The closest analogue to this approach would probably be New Zealand. In a mass-upzoning in 2016, the city of Auckland instantly approved multi-family residential units to be built in 75 per cent of all lots. Within just six years, construction of multi-unit dwellings had gone up 110 per cent.
In a blog post last year, housing analyst Leo Spalteholz ran the numbers on what would happen if B.C. followed Auckland's lead and experienced a similar building boom. His results? It would almost certainly curb rents and prices - and could well put the province on course for affordability by 2040.
But as Spalteholz told National Post, "under no circumstances are the CMHC targets achievable by 2030, or even 2034."
We could also just build a second Canada
The most outlandish solution to the housing crisis would be to simply revisit one of the more radical proposals from the last time Canada had a chronic housing crunch: The Mid-Canada Corridor. Proposed in 1971 by war hero (and bestselling thriller author) Richard Rohmer, the plan was to set up an entirely new network of communities and infrastructure in the Boreal Forest - the sparsely inhabited subarctic region located just north of places like Edmonton or Thunder Bay.
A dialled-down version of the plan (the Canadian Northern Corridor) still gets brought up every once in a while at conferences, although it mostly involves building highways, electricity and rail through the corridor and letting the communities form organically around resource sites.
If Canada were to simply to enact the original Rohmer plan, there are any number of reasons why a latticework of cold-weather government planned communities without any immediate reason for being would quickly become a landscape of ghost towns.
But if there's any real estate trend that's emerged in recent years, it's that Canadians desperate for affordable homes will move almost anywhere for the right price.
In recent years, a mass exodus of priced-out homebuyers to places like Atlantic Canada has resulted in those regions (which used to be famous for $30,000 heritage homes) suddenly having their housing stock bid up to unaffordable levels. Meanwhile, from 2018 to 2021 the money poured into residential construction in rural Canada soared by 70 per cent (as compared to 30 per cent in the cities).
In other words, there's never been a better time to get thousands of Canadians to consider buying a duplex in Flin Flon, Man.
IN OTHER NEWS
This is the key takeaway from a new Postmedia-commissioned Leger poll asking Canadians about whether transgender surgery (ie: mastectomies and genital surgery) should be legal for minors. A clear plurality says "no," about a third of respondents say "maybe," and only 11 per cent give a somewhat qualified "yes." Banning hormone therapies and surgical interventions for minors with gender dysphoria was one of the key planks of a suite of recent transgender policy reforms introduced in Alberta.
Amid the Trudeau government's often wishy-washy rhetoric on Israel-Hamas, the comments from Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre have often stood apart. This week, for instance, he told a press conference that his take on the war was that Hamas either has to surrender or "be destroyed." So it's notable how Poilievre's tone abruptly changed this week in regards to news of the suspicious death in custody of Russian opposition politician Alexey Navalny. "Russian opposition leader Alexey Navalny has died in prison. Putin imprisoned Navalny for the act of opposing the regime. Conservatives condemn Putin for his death," he wrote
A reminder to readers that it remains inadvisable to attack the United States under any circumstances. In a recent speech, New York Governor Kathy Hochul defended Israel's war on Hamas by attempting to give the conflict a local context. "If Canada someday ever attacked Buffalo, I'm sorry my friends, there would be no Canada the next day," she said.
One in four Canadians will soon lack access to a family doctor. A feature by the National Post's Sharon Kirkey found that 10 million Canadians will soon have no access to primary care; the highest since the introduction of socialized medicine. As to why, the reasons are well-known: Family doctors make shabby wages and have high overhead, so nobody wants to do it.
The Canadian Armed Force's bizarre fight with a Kingston prostitute has come to an end. Christina Lea Gilchrist offers 25 per cent discounts for serving members of the Canadian Armed Forces, which has prompted official warnings at CFB Kingston for members to avoid patronizing her services (although Gilchrist has reported that it had the opposite effect.) Military Police then threatened criminal charges against Gilchrist over explicit photos on her website in which she is wearing pieces of surplus Canadian Armed Forces uniforms. Only on Wednesday did they officially withdraw the investigation with the warning "if you have any authentic uniforms in your possession, even if they were purchased from a surplus store or gifted to you, they should be returned to the Canadian Armed Forces."

Document 280 of 500

Guilbeault now a road hazard

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/guilbeault-now-road-hazard/docview/2928716112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Guilbeault+now+a+road+hazard&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault's plan to stop funding roads because the existing network "is perfectly adequate to respond to the needs we have," is the sort of lunacy you'd expect from a government whose climate change policies consist largely of hostility to normal human behaviour. Like, say, leaving the house. But as "gobsmacked" as Ontario Premier Doug Ford says he is, there is a solution that would protect Canadians from the future the Liberals have planned for us: privatize the roads.
It really shouldn't matter what Guilbeault says, as roads and highways are a provincial responsibility. What the premiers are really worried about is the end of federal funding. Heaven forbid the provinces manage and pay for infrastructure projects within their own jurisdiction. If anything, Ford, and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith who also complained about the environment minister's comments, should welcome Ottawa removing itself from an area supposedly under their control.
As funding is always the concern, privatization would move roads off public expense sheets, as well as guard against a world where governments refuse to build transportation projects because they lack the imagination to address climate change without making modern life unbearable. If the roads are all private, who cares if Ottawa, or a green-minded premier, says they won't fund any new ones? This assumes, of course, the provinces would be open to ideas that require a bit of principle, but this isn't just some libertarian fever dream. Private roads, which were often the norm in the 19th century, are not uncommon in the rest of the developed world, most notably in France and Italy, and the emergence of automated tolling is making them a more attractive option for other countries.
If the prospect of selling off existing roads is too much of a leap, provinces could, at the very least, contract the maintenance of roads to private bidders that could fund them through the use of tolls. New transportation networks could then be built by auctioning off land for the purpose of building roads.
Privatization would, of course, not be without its challenges, given the historical role governments have played in the development of road infrastructure, and the difficulty, real or perceived, of converting such systems to private use in a coherent fashion, especially when you consider roads that cross borders. Existing private roads are often heavily regulated, and governments regularly limit competition, at the insistence of the road-builder, due to the expense of building roads and highways in the first place.
These challenges are real, but not insurmountable, and just because a service was once public does not mean it must remain so in perpetuity. If the alternative is government planning to shut down the service altogether, as Guilbeault wants it to be, then privatization may be the only option, regardless of how hard it might be.
A change in government in Ottawa, as seems likely, does mean that any moratorium on road building, or funding, could be reversed, but the only way to truly ensure such policies don't return is to take roads out of government hands entirely.
In Canada, there is the added wrinkle of interprovincial networks, but the beauty of federalism is that if Alberta wants to experiment with tolls and privatization, it is free to do so, even if British Columbia would prefer not to.
In any case, there is little reason why roads must be the exclusive domain of the state. Government incentives to maintain and improve the road system are at best questionable.
Everyone uses the roads, so irritation over congestion, damage caused by potholes and decaying highways is spread across the population. When compared to splashier agendas that benefit a more concentrated group of people - like, say, funding for an arts festival - it's easy to see why roads so often fall into disrepair.
At the municipal level, city councillors who advocate for core services such as the maintenance of roads are often dismissed as small-minded right-wing cranks who don't care about bigger issues, like climate change - or condemning Israel.
Private companies, on the other hand, would be incentivized to actually maintain roads at the lowest cost possible, especially if other companies owned competing networks.
Roads are not "public goods" in the technical sense of the term, which economists reserve for services that only governments can offer. Classic examples being water dams and lighthouses, where there is no way to exclude people from benefiting from their use unless they pay. A lighthouse lights the way for all ships, whether they paid for it or not, just as dams prevent flooding for everyone in a valley, regardless of who paid.
Private road systems can easily get around the free-rider issue through the use of tolls. Tolls provide the added benefit of managing congestion, as rates could increase during peak hours, thereby discouraging non-urgent trips at busier times.
This would naturally result in the need for fewer roads. Government subsidies, on the other hand, only serve to encourage congestion. When the cost of making a trip is essentially zero, there are no limits on the demand for roadways, which is why congestion never seems to decrease, no matter how many public roads get built.
Private roads would also be more environmentally friendly because they discourage unnecessary trips and encourage carpooling. In fact, Guilbeault himself should be making this proposal.
National Post

Document 285 of 500

NP View: 'Cheap' daycare program wastes billions to barely move labour needle

Author: View

Publication info: View. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/np-view-cheap-daycare-program-wastes-billions/docview/2927963480/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=NP+View%3A+%27Cheap%27+daycare+program+wastes+billions+to+barely+move+labour+needle&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=View&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few years and some billions of dollars later, Canada doesn't have a national daycare program. Instead, it has a national waitlist. It's a Liberal policy failure - a highly expensive and predictable one - and it will best be scrapped under new management.
Under the current model of federally-funded cheap daycare, announced in 2021 under the "$10-a-day" tagline, the Liberals give each province money to pass along to care providers in exchange for reduced fees - though, subsidized spaces don't necessarily drop to $10 per day, as the program is geared at lowering the average fee. The intent is to free mothers up for work, and income up for tax collection.
The program fails to do what it says on the tin. Instead of meeting need, it creates need. Instead of bringing in revenue, it recoups only one-18th of the cost to run it. As for raising female labour participation, a stated goal of the Trudeau government, it barely moves the needle. The only thing it confidently achieves is the waste of about $6 billion per year on average, the equivalent of roughly one-seventh of Canada's annual military budget.
That money is best spent elsewhere.
This is the experience that taxpayers have subsidized so far: thousands of names on Waterloo's daycare queue, a quadrupled waitlist in London, dialysis nurses unable to return to work post-maternity leave, parents unable to find daycare space despite applying back when their children were in utero. The list can go on.
The anecdotes are corroborated by Statistics Canada: in 2019, 36 per cent of parents using daycare had trouble finding spots; this rose to 49 per cent in 2023. In that time, the percentage of parents with kids on waitlists went from 19 to 26 per cent. To give the program minor credit, daycare expenses (for those who can find a spot) decreased in 2023.
This, however, comes at a great cost. The Liberal program has triggered rolling daycare closures in Alberta due to the cost cuts it imposes; in that province, industry advocates estimate that the average child-care operator will see half of their expected earnings wiped out in future years. In Ontario, advocates warn that closures are on the horizon if their funding formula doesn't change. Daycares nationwide have seen their business become unsustainable overnight, and it's fair to expect some to eventually pull out of the subsidized program and others to simply fold.
Since its initiation, it's barely nicked a dent in the macroeconomic picture. Canada's female labour participation rate is about the same now as it was in 2015, according to analysis released last week by the Fraser Institute. As for the participation of mothers with young children, the institute found a slight rise between January 2020 and June 2023 - but the increase was about the same as in the interval between January 2016 and January 2020. In short, cheap daycare hasn't impacted the existing trend.
Meanwhile, analysis by TD attributes the slow but steady rise of working mothers with kids under six to two things: affordable child care and flexible work. It didn't break down the extent to which factor contributes, but it did find that employment growth among mothers of young children was stronger in sectors that rely on work-from-home arrangements. Trudeau essentially blew billions to make the market move from a sloth's pace to a snail's pace.
So, parents in need are stuck without care, daycares are struggling to stay afloat and mothers are working at only slightly higher rates. Does it pay off?
The promise was that it would: "Studies show that (publicly-funded) child care alone has raised Quebec's GDP by 1.7 per cent," reads the 2021 budget read-out. "TD Economics has pointed to a range of studies that have shown that for every dollar spent on early childhood education, the broader economy receives between $1.50 and $2.80 in return." (Unacknowledged was the inconclusiveness of the TD figures, which came with the qualifier that "a large margin of error likely exists.")
The sobering reality is far worse. In 2022, the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated that, from 2021 to 2026, only $1.8 billion in costs would be recovered from $26 billion spent (a result of more moms paying income taxes and fewer families claiming federal child benefits). The labour boost would be slim, with GDP projected to rise by a slim 0.4 percentage points long term.
The solution to helping families manage the child-care burden should be simple and flexible. This was the case in the past: Canada's old system, under the previous government of former prime minister Stephen Harper, involved modest cash transfers to families with kids under six, and an additional tax-free payment to low-income families.
Though one Liberal staffer famously dismissed universal transfers as wasteful cash for parents to blow on "beer and popcorn," the program managed to be respectful of family choice while coming at a low overhead cost. When the Liberals won government in 2015, they ultimately embraced the cash transfer, adding to the amounts paid (along with adjusting for income). Under both governments, these funds provided decent support to families who could use it to spend on care as needed, whether that go to daycare, babysitting or anything in between.
Whatever the next government ends up deciding, its solution to child care should not involve tanking an industry that once had a healthy balance of supply and demand.
National Post
Matthew Lau: Alberta daycares brace for losses as Trudeau deal kicks in
Danielle Kubes: Universal daycare bad for women, children and the economy

Document 286 of 500

Can this man solve Canada's housing crisis?; Public Policy; Mike Moffatt Has No Shortage Of Solutions.It Turns Out The Powerful Are Listening

Author: O' Connor, Joe

Publication info: O' Connor, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-this-man-solve-canadas-housing-crisis-public/docview/2927900214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Can+this+man+solve+Canada%27s+housing+crisis%3F%3B+Public+Policy%3B+Mike+Moffatt+Has+No+Shortage+Of+Solutions.It+Turns+Out+The+Powerful+Are+Listening&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=O%27+Connor%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mike Moffatt was 27 when he and his life partner Hannah paid an absolutely "insane amount of money" for their first house. It was a two-storey, three-bedroom, fully detached place in London, Ont., and the insanity of the purchase, in hindsight, was that the home in a thriving university town in southwestern Ontario only cost $168,000 in 2004.
"At the time, I can remember thinking, 'Oh my god, I am never going to see that money again,'" he said.
But the couple has since shelled out $1.5 million for their current home in Ottawa. Were they to try to buy the same house today, it would likely fetch closer to $2 million. It is those kinds of eye-popping dollar figures that convinced Moffatt, an economist by training, to apply his talents to tackling Canada's affordable housing crisis.
Not, mind you, by grabbing a hammer and banging away on the 3.5 million new units that Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. estimates the country will need by 2030 - on top of what's already being built - but by pitching potential solutions to the country's housing mess to a slew of different audiences.
Those audiences include the prime minister and his inner circle, several senior Conservative members of Parliament, assorted policymakers, private industry players, Ontario politicians, big city mayors, media outlets, conference attendees and, depending on the day, neighbours, friends and anyone else who happens to ask for his two cents on housing.
"It is always hard to know how seriously you are being taken," Moffatt, the senior director of policy and innovation at the Ottawa-based Smart Prosperity Institute think-tank, said of his meetings with power players. "But what I can tell you for certain is that many of our ideas have been adopted, and I don't know whether or not they would have been without us."
Becoming the go-to guy on the housing file wasn't the master plan when he and his partner purchased their starter home. The young Moffatt imagined becoming a full-time university professor and, to be fair, the middle-aged Moffatt still teaches some business courses at Western University's Ivey Business School, albeit on a part-time basis.
But his true calling appears to be as a housing nerd, a thinker with a bunch of good ideas, whose area of interest is a subject that keeps homeowners, renters and aspirants to either category awake at night, fretting over how they are going to find a place to live and pay for it.
"Part of what motivates me to do the work I am doing now is that had I been born 20 years later, buying a house would have been out of the question," he said.
Life at the think-tank isn't all glamour and meetings with important people. A big chunk of the work involves doing research and sifting through history in search of lessons from the past that could prove instructive in the present.
Moffatt collaborates with a team to pump out papers with practical sounding titles, such as the National Housing Accord: A Multi-Sector Approach to Ending Canada's Housing Rental Crisis, and for an Ontario audience, Working Together to Build 1.5 Million Homes.
The challenge for many thinkers is they rely on others - in this case, the government - to get things done. And if the doers aren't listening to what a thinker has to say, even the brightest ideas will die a lonely death.
But Moffatt and Co.'s ideas are clearly being heard.
"I think Mike is in a unique position right now, where he is influencing both provincial public policy in Ontario and federal public policy, despite the fact they are different levels of governments of different political stripes," Patrick Brown, the mayor of Brampton, Ont., said. "He is a stakeholder in the industry with significant influence."
Moffatt first popped up on Brown's mayoral radar six years ago when he was talking about housing before anyone else was. Prior to last Christmas, the mayor invited Moffatt to Brampton to speak to city council.
The booming suburb perched on Toronto's northwest edge has a population of 650,000, give or take, and growing, and has a major housing crisis on its hands related to international students. The foreign kids shell out big bucks to go to school, pull into town and, Brown said, are often preyed upon by slumlords, while the institutions they enrolled at cash in $20,000 tuition cheques.
"I have seen situations where 25 students are crammed into a basement apartment," he said. "I asked Mike to help us work on some advocacy around international student housing, and to present the data on it."
The takeaway Brown and his team subsequently took to meetings with the federal immigration minister was that before a student gets approved to come to Canada, and before the universities and colleges cash any cheques, a guarantee of adequate housing should be part of the student visa approval process upfront.
No decent housing, no student, no cheque.
"Mike has been talking about housing for years," Brown said. "If only people had been listening to him earlier, we'd be in a much better position than we are in today."
On the better-late-thannever front, Moffatt 's team released its National Housing Accord report in mid-August 2023. The report had 10 recommendations to the federal government to "accelerate the completion of purpose-built rental units."
Recommendation No. 3 was eliminating the GST/HST to entice builders into building. A month later, presto, the government announced it was doing away with the tax on purpose-built rental units to accelerate construction.
Coincidence? Recommendation No. 7 called for the government to create a set of pre-approved building designs to streamline the building approval process. This was not an idea Moffatt pulled from the ether, but something he gleaned from the past.
Canada did not build many houses in the 1930s. But then along came the Second World War, and industry cranked up to service the military - that is, except for housing. But one million Canadian veterans flooded home when the war ended in 1945 and, wouldn't you know it, there were not enough houses for them. That crisis gave rise to the postwar bungalow, and plenty of those homes are still in evidence as anyone who has ever taken a spin through east-end Toronto can see.
To fast-track the bungalow build, the government of the day created a catalogue of pre-approved design blueprints and made them available to builders. Great idea, so good that Moffatt poached it for his report, and then watched as the federal Liberals announced just before Christmas that they would be doing the same thing.
"After the Second World War, when many thousands of soldiers were returning home to be reunited with their families, Canada faced enormous housing crunches," Minister of Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Sean Fraser told reporters in announcing the program. "One of the tools that was deployed at the time to respond to the challenges they faced at that time, was the development of simple pre-approved designs."
There can be a chickenand-egg-like quality to policy work, but Moffatt isn't possessive of his ideas. On the contrary, if people are listening to him, terrific; if not, then his goal is to continue trying to be heard.
"One of the things Canada needs to be doing is simply recognizing that this isn't our first housing crisis," he said. "We have had them before, and so what lessons can we learn from the past to deal with the present crisis?" Fortunately, Moffatt has a voice made for radio, and he also has some delightfully nerdy quirks, including being the former head coach of Canada's national dodge ball team - yes, there is one. If that sounds goofy, he was not only the coach, but he guided the squad to back-toback world titles.
The game Canadians grow up with in gym class also has some lessons for dealing with the housing crisis, such as keep "looking forward." In other words, it's best not to take one's eye off the ball, or, in this case, the need to build more houses.
Speaking of which, Moffatt has another pluckedfrom-the-past solution he says would be ripe for putting into action today. Once all the bungalows were built for the returning soldiers, they started having kids in big numbers, giving rise to the baby boom generation.
The boomers hit the housing market in the 1960s and early '70s, sparking another housing crisis. To address it, the government adopted a nifty tax provision that incentivized the building of apartments, although with a catch. If a builder finished a tower and subsequently sold it to take advantage of the tax provision, they were then required to roll the resulting funds into building another apartment.
By the mid-1970s, the crisis was over. The tax provisions were discontinued, partly because they were perceived politically to benefit wealthy developers, which is what they did, but they also got homes built.
That's why government, industry and, yes, even the policy nerds need to adopt the attitude of doing whatever it takes to solve the housing crisis, particularly since the starter home Moffatt and his partner bought in 2004 for less than $200,000 now costs closer to $700,000.
"There is no one silver bullet that is going to solve things; it is more like there are 100 interlocking problems, and they are all around the fact that it is difficult and expensive to build homes in Canada," he said. "We need to massively scale up."
Financial Post joconnor@postmedia.com

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing developments; Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices

Document 287 of 500

Child care plan a costly disaster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/child-care-plan-costly-disaster/docview/2927899991/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Child+care+plan+a+costly+disaster&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few years and some billions of dollars later, Canada doesn't have a national daycare program. Instead, it has a national wait-list. It's a Liberal policy failure - a highly expensive and predictable one - and it will best be scrapped under new management.
Under the current model of federally funded cheap daycare, announced in 2021 under the "$10 a day" tag line, the Liberals give each province money to pass along to care providers in exchange for reduced fees - though, subsidized spaces don't necessarily drop to $10 per day, as the program is geared at lowering the average fee. The intent is to free parents up for work, and income up for tax collection.
The program fails to do what it says on the tin. Instead of meeting need, it creates need. Instead of bringing in revenue, it recoups only one-18th of the cost to run it. As for raising female labour participation, a stated goal of the Liberal government, it barely moves the needle. The only thing it confidently achieves is the waste of about $6 billion per year on average, the equivalent of roughly one-seventh of Canada's annual military budget.
That money is best spent elsewhere.
This is the experience that taxpayers have subsidized so far: thousands of names on Waterloo, Ont.'s daycare queue; a quadrupled waitlist in London, Ont.; dialysis nurses unable to return to work after maternity leave, parents unable to find daycare space despite applying back when their children were in utero. The list can go on.
The anecdotes are corroborated by Statistics Canada: in 2019, 36 per cent of parents using daycare had trouble finding spots; this rose to 49 per cent in 2023. In that time, the percentage of parents with kids on waitlists went to 26 per cent from 19 per cent. To give the program minor credit, daycare expenses (for those who can find a spot) decreased in 2023.
This, however, comes at a great cost. The Liberal program has triggered rolling daycare closures in Alberta due to the cost cuts it imposes; in that province, industry advocates estimate the average child-care operator will see half of their expected earnings wiped out in future years. In Ontario, advocates warn that closures are on the horizon if their funding formula doesn't change. Daycares countrywide have seen their business become unsustainable overnight, and it's fair to expect some to eventually pull out of the subsidized program and others to simply fold.
Since its initiation, the program has barely nicked a dent in the macroeconomic picture. Canada's female labour participation rate is about the same now as it was in 2015, according to analysis released last week by the Fraser Institute. As for the participation of mothers with young children, the institute found a slight rise between January 2020 and June 2023 - but the increase was about the same as in the interval between January 2016 and January 2020. In short, cheap daycare hasn't affected the existing trend.
Meanwhile, ana lysis by Toronto-Dominion Bank attributes the slow but steady rise of working mothers with kids under the age of six to two things: affordable child care and flexible work. It didn't break down the extent to which factor contributes, but it did find employment growth among mothers of young children was stronger in sectors that rely on workfrom-home arrangements. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau essentially blew billions to make the market move from a sloth's pace to a snail's pace.
So, parents in need are stuck without care, daycares are struggling to stay afloat and mothers are working at only slightly higher rates. Does it pay off? The promise was that it would: "Studies show that (publicly funded) child care alone has raised Quebec's GDP by 1.7 per cent," says the 2021 budget readout. "TD Economics has pointed to a range of studies that have shown that for every dollar spent on early childhood education, the broader economy receives between $1.50 and $2.80 in return." (Unacknowledged was the inconclusiveness of the TD figures, which came with the qualifier that "a large margin of error likely exists.") The sobering reality is far worse. In 2022, the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated that, from 2021 to 2026, only $1.8 billion in costs would be recovered from $26 billion spent (a result of more parents paying income taxes and fewer families claiming federal child benefits). The labour boost would be slim, with GDP projected to rise by a slim 0.4 percentage points long term.
The solution to helping families manage the childcare burden should be simple and flexible. This was the case in the past: Canada's old system, under the government of former prime minister Stephen Harper, involved modest cash transfers to families with kids under six, and an additional taxfree payment to low-income families.
Though one Liberal staffer famously dismissed universal transfers as wasteful cash for parents to blow on "beer and popcorn," the program managed to be respectful of family choice while coming at a low overhead cost. When the Liberals won government in 2015, they ultimately embraced the cash transfer, adding to the amounts paid (along with adjusting for income). Under both governments, these funds provided decent support to families who could use it to spend on care as needed, whether that go to daycare, babysitting or anything in between.
Whatever the next government ends up deciding, its solution to child care should not involve tanking an industry that once had a healthy balance of supply and demand.
National Post

Document 292 of 500

The bicycling minister needs to change gears

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bicycling-minister-needs-change-gears/docview/2927739473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+bicycling+minister+needs+to+change+gears&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive politicians aiming to triumph over right-leaning political adversaries can only succeed if they can do a better job of bridging their lofty goals with people's day-today struggles.
Who said so? Justin Trudeau, that's who. The prime minister told a roomful of progressives at a conference in Montreal last fall that politicians who don't respond to issues affecting the daily lives of citizens are not going to connect with them.
One wonders what he thought of the comments by his environment minister, Steven Guilbeault, that the Liberal government has decided to stop investing in new road infrastructure because the current network is "perfectly adequate" to respond to the needs of Canadians.
Commuters in Brampton, Ont., who voted for the Ford government's proposal to build a new highway to relieve congestion, may not agree with the bicycling minister from downtown Montreal that the current network is perfectly adequate.
Polling suggests that one of the main criticisms of Trudeau's Liberals is they preach more than they listen.
Guilbeault is the prime example of a government dominated by activists turned politicians who see themselves in a special state of grace, holding views on a morally higher plane, while their opponents are not only wrong, but unworthy.
Few incidents have exposed the disconnect between the government and the governed more than Guilbeault's brutally frank comments.
He clearly knew he had stepped in it because, when confronted by reporters on Parliament Hill on Wednesday, he first claimed that was not what he said, and then conceded he should have been more specific.
He said the government is still building roads but will no longer fund large projects like the third link for cars between Quebec City and Lévis on the south shore of the St. Lawrence that was being pushed by Francois Legault's CAQ government (that will now be transit only).
The minister's office was in damage-limitation mode, saying the original comments were taken "slightly out of context" and there is no policy change.
But that is rationalizing what was said after the fact.
Guilbeault was clear that, while existing networks would receive maintenance funding, "there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network."
"We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network," he said.
It would be interesting to see the ana lysis such a conclusion was based upon. (I asked for it, but it was not forthcoming.)
For example, what population base was used? Canada's population hit 40 million last year and Statistics Canada's high-growth projection is that it will reach 50 million by 2040. Will the current network be able to cope with 10 million more people? Guilbeault's model evidently suggests a marked decline in road use, which is conceivable as the carbon tax moves toward $170 a tonne by 2030, which would add another $1,000 a year to fuel costs for the average pickup truck.
But Robin Lindsey, CN chair of transportation at UBC's Sauder School of Business, said the decision to invest in roads should depend on many considerations, ranging from population growth to fuel prices and beyond.
"A blanket decision not to invest in road infrastructure across all of Canada seems ill-advised," he said. "Cities may vary in population growth and density, traffic congestion, pollution problems and public transit services (as an alternative to driving). Policy should not be one-size fits all."
Lindsey pointed out that the eventual advent of autonomous vehicles (AVs) will have an effect on road capacity. Demand may increase, he said, because as passengers, people are able to make better use of their time and won't mind taking longer trips. "AVs may not come in soon but since roads last for decades, investment decisions should be made with a long time horizon in mind," he said.
The claim by Guilbeault's office that this does not represent a major policy shift has some validity. Sources suggest Infrastructure Canada avoided road projects when Catherine McKenna was the minister.
Yet, major highway projects are still partly funded by Ottawa, such as the upgrading of Highway 1 between Kamloops and the Alberta border in British Columbia. The B.C. government has committed $728 million to pay for the eight or so separate upgrade projects over the next three years, but the federal government is also on the hook for $571 million.
Guilbeault's comments suggest that will be the last big highway contribution by this government - bad news for the Ontario government's pledge to build a new Highway 413 in northwest Toronto.
You might imagine Guilbeault might see electric vehicles as a means of mitigating emissions from road traffic.
He has just introduced electric vehicle sales mandate regulations that say 100 per cent of vehicle sales must be zero emission by 2035.
Yet, the environment minister seems to have fallen out of love with EVs. At the conference in Montreal on Monday, he said we should stop thinking that electric cars will solve all our problems.
Overestimating the effects of electric-powered transportation would be "an error, a false utopia," he said.
Hence, the goal of moving Canadians out of cars and onto public transit or onto bicycle paths - Guilbeault boasts that he bikes 12 months a year.
That suggestion had Conservative premiers frothing at the mouth.
Alberta's Danielle Smith said Guilbeault needs to understand not all Canadians live in downtown Montreal. "Most of us can't just head out the door in the snow and rain and walk 10 kilometres to work each day. Can we return to the real world, minister?" she asked.
Ontario's Doug Ford said Guilbeault "doesn't care that you're in bumper-to-bumper traffic. I do. We're building roads and highways with or without a cent from the feds."
Guilbeault's moral certitude might have worked a few years ago when his government articulated the aspirations of many more voters.
But all the signs point to Canadians being tired of being lectured to by a government that appears so out of touch with their daily lives it is telling them to get on their bikes in mid-winter.
National Post jivison@criffel.ca

Title: The bicycling minister needs to change gears

Document 293 of 500

Taxpayers given tab for Ontario's lofty public sector

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/taxpayers-given-tab-ontarios-lofty-public-sector/docview/2927739457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Taxpayers+given+tab+for+Ontario%27s+lofty+public+sector&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What a triumph for the oppressed workers of the Ontario public sector. The PC government's 2019 wage restraint bill was ruled unconstitutional this week by the Ontario Court of Appeal. After some token grumbling, Premier Doug Ford has begun to dismantle the legislation, deciding not to appeal the decision to the Supreme Court.
The story, as typically told, is one of plucky public sector unions standing up for their legal rights in the face of the heavy-handed Ford regime. Forgotten are the taxpayers who will ultimately pay the cost of righting this hideous injustice. This week, the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario estimated that bill at about $13.7 billion.
When passed in 2019, Ontario's Bill 124 imposed three years of annual pay increases of one per cent for workers in the broader public sector, unionized or not. The Ford government was facing what proved to be an $8.7-billion deficit. Controlling public sector wages was one way to fight it. At the time, negotiated contracts in public sector were averaging 1.6 per cent annually, according to this week's judgment.
There was reason to think Ontario public sector workers were well-placed to handle this modest constraint on future earnings. Union leaders would have you believe their members are terribly hard done by, but the comparison between public and private sector workers in Ontario is instructive.
A 2023 Fraser Institute report, based on Statistics Canada numbers, found government sector employees in Ontario were paid 34.4 per cent more than those in the private sector. Even after adjusting to compare only to those with similar education and type of work, there was still a 10.9 per cent pay gap. That's not all. Nearly 84 per cent of public sector workers are covered by pension plans, compared to 25 per cent in the private sector. Ontario public sector workers retire 2½ years earlier on average and take more days of personal leave each year. Their job security is outstanding. In 2021, 5.5 per cent of private sector employees lost their jobs compared to 1.3 per cent of government workers.
These are the sort of factors that go into making a public policy decision, but two of the three judges on the Appeal Court panel ruled that Ontario's situation was not the sort of "pressing and substantial" matter that would enable it to override the collective bargaining rights protected by the Charter of Rights and Freedoms. The problem was with the process. "Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good faith bargaining," they wrote. Right. Of course. Why didn't the provincial government think of that? Perhaps the same unions who found one per cent raises abominable would have agreed to them if the government had asked nicely.
In the dissenting opinion, Justice William Hourigan said: "Courts are public policy amateurs who lack the expertise, experience, and resources to understand where a policy fits in the bigger picture. Thus, it is not the role of judges to second guess the policy choices made by governments because this is a role they are wholly unqualified to undertake."
Hourigan added that the PC government had campaigned on fiscal restraint and such decisions should be made by government because it is accountable to the public and courts are not. Hourigan further noted the Supreme Court has never found that temporary wage legislation violates the Charter.
The Ford government could have appealed its legislation to the Supreme Court, but controlling public expenses is just so 2019. Although it wanted to fight deficits back then, it has since become rather comfortable with them. It still vows to balance the books, in the fullness of time.
The government expects a $4.5-billion deficit this fiscal year, up from the $1.3 billion forecast in last spring's budget. This week, Ford said the province's finances are starting to balance out and his government has been "prudent fiscal managers." In 2021-22, spending on base programs was $149.9 billion. This fiscal year, it is projected to be $193 billion.
So what does government do to control public sector wage costs in the future, should it be inclined to do so? The new approach is evident in the way the government is trying to manage negotiations with the teaching unions. Two major unions and the government have resolved everything they could at the bargaining table. Now they will leave the final contract decisions, including wages, up to arbitrators. That way, the government gets to avoid labour disruptions, but it also relinquishes any control over its wage bill.
That certainly makes life simpler for government. Too bad for the taxpayers.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Subject: Legislation; Fiscal years; Private sector; Public sector; Collective bargaining; Public policy; Wages & salaries; Workers
