Top VR headsets to buy in 2024

Author: Maxwell, Jordan

Publication info: Maxwell, Jordan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-vr-headsets-buy-2024/docview/2923902071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Top+VR+headsets+to+buy+in+2024&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Maxwell%2C+Jordan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The top virtual reality (VR) headsets can give every player an immersive gaming experience. But because there are so many options available right now, it might be challenging to understand how they differ, especially if you're a first-time VR shopper.
Most come with a headset and motion tracking to allow users to enter an alternative gaming universe that's growing year to year. In recent years, new games have been released, increasing VR's popularity among the VR community. Titles like Cities VR, Among Us, Moss: Book 2, NFL Pro Era, Bonelab and Wanderer have captured the imaginations of audiences worldwide. Of course, classic sports games such as boxing, snowboarding, golf and table tennis have also been hits, even rock climbing!
You'll likely need a strong gaming PC to operate a VR headset, as most models have tethered connections that need to be hooked up to a computer. However, you can buy an untethered VR headset that is cable-free, like the Meta Quest 2. There's also a wave of new VR headsets being released this year, including the Meta Quest 3, the Lynx R1 and the Pimax Portal, to name a few, which might be cable-free.
We have created a list of the best VR headsets on the market to help you with your shopping and to help you decide. Check it out below!
Summary
Best overall: Meta Quest 3
Best runner-up: Valve Index
Best resolution: HTC Vive Pro 2
Best for gaming consoles: PlayStation VR2
The VR headsets Best overall: Meta Quest 3
Meta Quest 3.
Key specs
Internal Memory: 128 GB
Controller connectivity: Bluetooth
Built-in sensors: Yes
Compatible platforms: Windows PC
Why buy: The Meta Quest 3 is one of the best VR headsets you can buy. It allows users to play virtual reality games without needing a gaming PC, laptop or smartphone. Unlike some other models, the headset is light and comfortable instead of bulky and heavy. It has a straightforward design with four camera sensors and a basic strap, making it easy to mount on your head, standing up or sitting on a desk. In addition, it's vastly improved from its original version, thanks to more RAM and better chip specs that deliver a higher resolution during gameplay. It also has an improved Guardian system, which automatically designs a gaming area to prevent you from bumping into furniture and walls.
Best of all, it's cable-free and doesn't require a high-end GPU to function properly. But if you have those items, it works seamlessly with them - the best of both worlds; it depends on your game. It's an awesome option to use with games like Beat Saber and Superhot VR. Vader Immortal, Tetris Effect or Polyarc's Moss are also great ones to start with if you choose this VR headset.
Price: $650
Where to buy: Best Buy, Amazon, Walmart
Fine print: Included in the pack are two-touch controllers, a charging cable, two AA batteries, a power adapter and a glasses spacer.
Also consider: Meta Quest 2 ($350)
Best runner-up: Valve Index
Valve Index.
Key specs
Internal Memory: 8 GB RAM
Processor: Dual Core with Hyper-Threading
Graphics: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 970, AMD RX480
Controller connectivity: Internet connection
Compatible platforms: Windows PC (10)
Why buy: The Valve Index is designed to be used with the company's Steam gaming platform and is available for a little over $1,300. Users can therefore choose from well over 200 games and productivity tools. Despite having a lesser per-eye LCD screen resolution than the Meta Quest 2, HP Reverb G2 and HTC Vive Pro 2, the Valve Index has the highest refresh rate - crucial for the most fluid gameplay. If you have a high-end GPU, this is the unit for you.
In addition, PC gamers who have already invested in the Steam ecosystem and have sizable budgets will have a lot of choices with the Valve Index, including great headsets and superb controllers.
Price: Starting from $1,319
Where to buy: Amazon
Fine print: A USB (3.0+) port is required for the headset pass-through camera and USB port support.
Best resolution: HTC Vive Pro 2
HTC Vive Pro 2.
Key specs
Display resolution: 2448 x 2448 pixels per eye (4896 x 2448 pixels combined)
Display type: Dual RGB low persistence LCD
Field of view: Up to 120 degrees
Weight: 5.9 kg
Processor compatibility: Intel Core i5-4590 or AMD Ryzen 1500 equivalent or greater
Memory compatibility: 8 GB RAM or more
Why buy: The Vive Pro 2, the most recent iteration of the HTC Vive line, has a 5K resolution (2448 x 2448 pixels per eye), a 120-degree field of vision and a 120Hz refresh rate. As a result, it's the most realistic and clear display you can find in consumer VR, provided you have a strong GPU to support it. Some users note that the machine can be a little front-heavy, and its screen can run hot after prolonged use, but it's a strong choice for a tethered option with a better-than-average field of view and a library of great games from which to choose.
Price: $1,850
Where to buy: Amazon, Vive
Fine print: Includes Vive Pro 2 headset, two base stations and two controllers
Best for gaming consoles: PlayStation VR2
PlayStation VR2.
Key specs
Built-in sensors: 6-Axis motion sensing system; 3-Axis gyroscope and 3-Axis accelerometer
Compatible platforms: PS5
Field of view: 110 degrees
Why buy: It takes a PS4 or PS5 console to use Sony's PlayStation VR, so this VR headset might be the best option if you already own one and need a powerful gaming PC. Sony has addressed customer complaints with its latest release, providing PlayStation VR bundles with devices like the PlayStation Camera included. It also has PlayStation VR2 Sense technology that improves eye tracking, headset feedback and 3D audio. That said, PlayStation Move controllers are only included in select bundles, and there may be an additional cost. Keep that in mind when choosing a bundle, especially if you aren't fully sold on the game.
Price: $820
Where to buy: Amazon, Best Buy, Walmart
Fine print: You might have to pre-order depending on the retailer
What to know about VR headsets
Now that we've highlighted the best VR headsets to buy, here are some facts that might be helpful when you're choosing a model.
What is VR?
The term "virtual reality," or VR, describes the ability to immerse oneself completely in a created environment. It's typically given using head-mounted technology that monitors a person's movements. However, this is only sometimes the case. With these VR headsets, your head is attached to a strap or a frame that houses a screen (or two display panels, one for each eye). To make it seem like what you see via the headset is your entire world, a pair of lenses are often mounted between the panels and your eyes, blocking the outside world. Importantly, every headset tracks your movement and modifies the image you view accordingly. But different headsets track different amounts of activity.
Terms to remember
You'll likely come across frequently used terms when shopping for a new VR headset. Here are some you might come across:
Field of view (or vision): In VR headsets, the field of view refers to all you can see while wearing the headset at any time in the virtual world.
Degrees of freedom: When talking about movement and tracking in VR, the term is usually degrees of freedom. More degrees of freedom means that the headset will track and transfer more of your actual movements onto your simulated representation.
Tracking: Inside-out tracking is currently the most popular. The device uses cameras incorporated into the headset to track movement from inside the headset outward. In addition, advanced simultaneous location and mapping algorithms keep an eye on the elements of the physical world that the headset wearer is nearby. Outside-in tracking is another option. It involves additional hardware not included in the headset and can take many different shapes.

Document 352 of 500

Fewer cases and increased division: Canada's Supreme Court is flashing warning signs

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fewer-cases-increased-division-canadas-supreme/docview/2923765079/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Fewer+cases+and+increased+division%3A+Canada%27s+Supreme+Court+is+flashing+warning+signs&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Supreme Court of Canada is hearing fewer cases on average, raising concerns in the legal community that Canadian judicial law is failing to keep up with emerging issues. Meanwhile, of the cases it does hear, the justices are increasingly divided.
In 2023, the court granted leave to appeal to 40 cases out of 581 cases brought forward, an improvement over the historic low of 24 leaves granted (of 429 applications) in 2022, according to a data analysis by lawyer Paul-Erik Veel.
"I think we're still below where we should be. The number of cases the Supreme Court has decided has been a steady trend downward," said Veel, a partner at Toronto law firm Lenczner Slaght, where he leads the data-driven decisions program.
Veel argues that fewer leave applications granted means less new law from the SCC. And it means fewer opportunities for litigators to bring the court emerging and unresolved issues.
On top of that, the court is deciding more issues from the bench, meaning it renders its decision immediately after a case is heard with very brief reasons (sometimes only a few paragraphs long).
"When you add in the fact that the Supreme Court has been deciding more decisions from the bench, the number of decisions that the Supreme Court decides with fulsome written reasons rendered after considered judgment has seen a really long-term decline over the last quarter century," he said. As Canada's court of last resort, the SCC's job is to decide legal issues of public or national importance and "supervise the growth and development of Canadian jurisprudence," according to its website. In doing so, it gets to choose which cases it accepts to hear, with a few notable exceptions. For example, criminal cases in which a panel of judges in a provincial court of appeal either sets aside an acquittal or features a dissenting opinion on a legal question are automatically granted appeals to the SCC "as of right." In civil and commercial cases and most criminal cases a party must apply for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Recent SCC landmark rulings include in 2021 declaring the federal government's carbon tax constitutional after it was challenged by three provinces -Alberta, Ontario and Saskatchewan - and striking down Ottawa's environmental Impact Assessment Act (colloquially known as the "no more pipelines" law) in 2023 because it infringed on provincial powers. Since at least 2018 there has been a noticeable drop in the percentage of cases the SCC accepts to hear compared to the number of requests (in legal jargon: applications for leave to appeal) it receives annually, Veel's data show. In 2018, the first year Veel's firm began compiling statistics, the court granted leave to appeal to 39 cases, or 9.1 per cent of requests it received. Over the next three years, the court received a larger number of leave applications but only granted between 33 and 38 per year, meaning the percentage of cases that received leave dropped between 5.6 per cent and 8.5 per cent, according to Lenczner Slaght's calculations. In 2022, the same year the court accepted to hear a record low number of cases, the percentage of applications for leave granted plummeted to 5.6 per cent. It then ticked back up slightly to 6.9 per cent the following year, although still much lower than 2018, 2020 and 2021. Many of Veel's findings are confirmed by theSCC's own data, which differ slightly because the court classifies a granted leave by year of the application, whereas Veel classifies them by year they were decided. Veel said no one expects the Supreme Court to be hearing up to 200 cases per year like it did in the two decades following the adoption of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms in 1982. "What doesn't quite make sense to me is the further drop we've seen," he said. "I think the institution could support a higher case volume, giving more guidance than it does at the moment." Asked about the 2022 numbers in June, Supreme Court Chief Justice Richard Wagner said the court had not changed its selection criteria when choosing cases to hear. Instead, Wagner laid part of the blame on the pandemic, which led to fewer rulings throughout the lower courts and ultimately meant fewer applications for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. He also noted that 25 per cent of applications are from self-represented litigants, whose cases are virtually never heard by the SCC. SCC deputy executive legal officer Renée Maria Tremblay declined to provide further comment because the court is still compiling its annual statistics for 2023. Veel's data show the pandemic might be part of the reason for the lower percentage of leaves granted in 2020 and 2021, but not in 2022. "In 2022, there were both fewer cases, and fewer leaves being granted," he said. "And even when you take into account the fact of self-represented litigants, that doesn't represent the drop in cases the Supreme Court decided to hear in 2022." Veel's data show that the Supreme Court is taking longer to decide if it will hear a case. The median wait between an application for leave and a decision on if the case would be heard was 136 days in 2020 compared to 185 days in 2023. He also found it is publishing longer rulings, which are likely more time consuming to produce, with decisions increasingly divided. The period since 2018 also saw the highest proportion of decisions with a dissenting opinion since 1984, according to another study he co-authored last year.
"One interpretation of what's going on potentially... is there is a more divided Supreme Court that needs to put more work into every decision because there isn't the same degree of unanimity," Veel said. "It's taking them more effort per decision to produce that output."
Veel's data show that civil and commercial legal issues have borne the brunt of the SCC's lower caseload. In 2023, only one-quarter of cases the SCC agreed to hear were private law matters, a broad category that includes class action lawsuits, employment and labour claims, family law issues and contract disputes. "There is roughly half as many civil cases that are getting leave now compared to 2018, which I think a lot of people would say is sub-optimal in terms of the development of huge swaths of our law," Veel said. He pointed to an ongoing question in Ontario courts over whether plaintiffs who are suing multiple defendants are required to tell all of them if one of them opts to settle out of court. "The Supreme Court has been asked to take up this particular issue by a number of different parties on a number of different occasions and they've said no every time," he said.
'Stiff' 14-year prison sentence for high-ranking RCMP intelligence leaker Cameron Ortis
Canadian conservatives have been using courts to thwart the Liberal government - and winning
Patrick Taillon, a constitutional law professor at Université Laval, said he too has noticed an increasing number of cases that he expected would be heard by the Supreme Court but haven't been. "It happens more and more often that I see a case that the Supreme Court refuses to hear and I think to myself, 'That's impossible, how did they refuse that?" he said. Université de Montréal law professor Martine Valois said she's not surprised that the SCC is taking on fewer cases because the issues at hand are increasingly complex. She also noted that it's not because the Supreme Court doesn't hear certain cases that the law isn't evolving thanks to rulings from other appeal courts. Taillon agreed, noting that the concerning drop in the SCC's case-count shows that the government will likely have to increase the size of the court in the future. "The Supreme Court will need to feature more judges. It's a debate that will have to happen eventually," he said, acknowledging that the idea will likely be very controversial. Veel, Taillon and Vallois all insist that the current Supreme Court bench is far from weak, but it is more divided, and there have been six new judges appointed to the top court since 2015. "I do think there are some very strong, excellent judges on that court. But the dynamic has changed," Veel said. "You have different strong pulls and strong opinions on that court and the court's just working out how to move forward as an institution." The trend, he says, appears to be that the court is putting out fewer but longer and more complex written decisions. Thus, the court isn't less productive, it's just putting more energy into fewer cases, he noted. "So, I wouldn't say I want them to be more productive. I would say, point blank, I want them to hear more cases." National Post cnardi@postmedia.com

Subject: Law; Pandemics; Litigation; Judges & magistrates

Location: Canada

Document 354 of 500

Michael Taube: The Ontario government needs to get out of the booze business

Author: Morrison-Flint, Laura

Publication info: Morrison-Flint, Laura. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-ontario-government-needs-get-out/docview/2923260536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+The+Ontario+government+needs+to+get+out+of+the+booze+business&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Morrison-Flint%2C+Laura&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government recently announced that convenience stores in the province will be able to sell wine, beer, cider and pre-mixed cocktails by 2026. It's a good decision, but why stop there? Ford should go even further and privatize the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
It's a step that no Ontario government has been willing to take since the LCBO was founded in 1927. PC governments under then-premiers Mike Harris and Ernie Eves both briefly toyed with LCBO privatization, but didn't pursue it vigorously. Then-Liberal finance minister Greg Sorbara ordered a review of the Crown corporation's liquor distribution model, but quickly rejected former LCBO board member John Lacey's July 2005 report that was more supportive of privatization over public ownership.
Why will Ontario not follow through on liquor retail privatization? Fear of voter retaliation, for the most part.
Left-leaning Ontario premiers have mostly refused to consider reforming this valuable asset, which reported revenues of $7.21 billion and a net income of $2.54 billion in 2020-21. They also go out of their way to show voters how much they support the more than 8,000 unionized employees of the near century-old Crown corporation. Right-leaning premiers also recognize that the LCBO is a cash cow. They worry about their poll numbers if they choose to target it because some Ontarians (and potential voters) view it as a sacred institution that capitalists should leave alone.
Yet, it's ridiculous that Ontario is in the booze business in the first place. If you believe in concepts like a free market economy, private enterprise and the need to enhance consumer choice, a publicly funded LCBO doesn't make a lick of sense. And anyone with a rudimentary understanding of economics would know the only reason that the LCBO has reaped these profits is because it exists in a (mostly) non-competitive marketplace without real, serious private competition.
Ford and the Ontario PCs should therefore examine other provincial models related to the privatization of beer, wine, cider and spirits in different venues. It could help them get new ideas, learn from past mistakes and serve as a form of inspiration to devise their own privatization model - and hopefully do it as well or better.
Alberta is the best place to start. Then-premier Ralph Klein deregulated the public monopoly in alcohol in 1992, and the province has never looked back. University of Alberta economics professor Doug West made some important observations about Alberta's shift to privatization in a few Fraser Institute papers This includes: overall product selection of beer, wines and spirits doubled with liquor privatization, retail liquor prices fell on average and basically followed wholesale price adjustments, full-time equivalent employment levels nearly tripled in more than a decade, and there was an astonishing 134 per cent increase in the number of Alberta-based liquor stores.
West also noted that Alberta government revenues increased after Klein introduced liquor privatization. Whereas the province made $427 million from its government monopoly in alcohol in 1992, those revenues soared to about $540 million in liquor taxes from private outlets in 2002. These profits continue today, and it's something that Ontario could take advantage of by bringing an end to the LCBO.
B.C. has also engaged in some liquor privatization experiments, but the results have often resembled a yo-yo. Liberal premier Gordon Campbell, for instance, seemed ready to follow Alberta's lead in 2001 and proposed privatizing up to 90 per cent of his province's liquor-retailing industry. Alas, the public sector unions fought back and it never moved forward. No premier has investigated it since.
Chris Selley: Ontario's liquor status quo begs to be smashed (not the bottles, though)
Jesse Kline: Trudeau's war on bringing things home from the store
B.C. also introduced private liquor retail stores back in 1988. This proved to be successful in increasing consumer choice and available options for wine, beer and spirits. Unfortunately, the window of opportunity for investors to apply for a private liquor store licence in the province has been closed more often than it's been open. Most recently, the NDP government announced in 2021 that the moratorium on applications and approvals would be extended to July 2032.
As Mark Milke nicely put it in an Aug. 19, 2013 Business in Vancouver column, "There has always been a grand degree of irony that British Columbia, the most liberal province in Canada, has an approach to liquor retailing that was born in Prohibition and survives because of special-interest pressure." Little has changed in Lotusland, it seems.
Meanwhile, Manitoba has allowed private wine shops and private beer retailers to operate in the province for years. Nova Scotia announced a trial program for private wine shops and specialty stores in 2002, some of which still exist and thrive today. Even Quebec, which has a public liquor monopoly, Société des alcools du Québec (SAQ), gradually allowed wine and beer to be sold in grocery stores.
While Ontario has already done some of these smaller experiments, it needs to do more.
Ford, to his credit, has attempted to change the Ontario model by ending the Beer Store monopoly within five years and introducing the buck-a-beer program. When he announced his forthcoming changes to beer, wine and other alcohol in convenience stores, he said, "we made a promise to the people of Ontario to deliver more choice and convenience." Privatizing the LCBO would bring him even closer to that important promise he needs to keep.
National Post

Document 359 of 500

February 6, 2024 (Page A4)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/february-6-2024-page-a4/docview/2925321520/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apdnnationalpost&amp;atitle=February+6%2C+2024+%28Page+A4%29&amp;title=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=26&amp;issue=85&amp;spage=A4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Not available.

Title: February 6, 2024 (Page A4)

Publication title: National Post (2011-); Toronto, Canada

Volume: 26

Issue: 85

First page: A4

Number of pages: 1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 6, 2024

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto, Canada

Black-led brands to shop in 2024 (and beyond)

Author: Tamfo, Emily

Publication info: Tamfo, Emily. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/black-led-brands-shop-2024-beyond/docview/2923006869/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Black-led+brands+to+shop+in+2024+%28and+beyond%29&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Tamfo%2C+Emily&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Supporting Black businesses is easier than you might think. According to Statistics Canada, there are nearly 145,000 Black-owned businesses in Canada alone, with many more being founded across the country each day. When we support Black businesses, we're supporting Canadians who are building better futures for communities as diverse as the make up of our country. The best part is that we can support these businesses all year round. By doing so, we're celebrating the legacy of Black Canadians and creating new legacies to come, hence the theme of Canada's 2024 Black History Month " Black Excellence: A Heritage to Celebrate, a Future to Build."
Here's a list of Black-founded or Black-led brands and highlighted products for your every need - from hair care to home decor and everything in between. We've also featured brands founded or based in Canada.
Topicals - Hydrating and Soothing Mist
Topicals Like Butter Body Hydrating and Soothing Mist for Dry, Sensitive and Eczema-Prone Skin.
Why we love it: When Olamide Olowe co-founded Topicals in 2015, all she wanted to do was create a brand for skin with chronic conditions. Today, at just 27 years old, she's the youngest black woman to raise $10M in venture capital funding. This hydrating mist is a game changer for anyone struggling with dry, eczema-prone skin.
Price: $30
Where to buy: Sephora Topicals
'Black Cake' - a novel by Charmaine Wilkerson
'Black Cake.'
Why we love it: If you're a fan of black cake - the popular Caribbean cake made of fruits, rum, and brandy - you might also be a fan of this compelling novel by Charmaine Wilkerson, following the story of a family of Caribbean immigrants in America uncovering family secrets after the death of their matriarch. If the name sounds familiar, yes, this is the book that was recently made into a television show of the same name.
Price: $24.95
Where to buy: Amazon Indigo
SmithErickson - SmithErickson Jacinta Stoneware Vase
SmithErickson Jacinta Stoneware Vase.
Why we love it: Dynamic duo Stephanie Smith and Jenelle Erickson have used their shared love of travel, fashion, and architecture to build an interior design studio renowned across Canada. Through this speckled ceramic vase, you can bring a taste of the SmithErikson aesthetic into your home. As a bonus, SmithErickson has a curated home decor collection available at The Bay
Price: $70
Where to buy: SmithErickson
Melissa x Telfar - Medium Jelly Shopper
Melissa x Telfar Medium Jelly Shopper.
Why we love it: If you don't know Telfar by name, you've surely seen their signature bags (ultra mini to extra large) in the hands of anyone who loves fashion, in part due to the brand's commitment to accessible pricing. This bag, in collaboration with sustainable jelly brand Melissa, brings the best of both design worlds in one and joins the list of Telfar collaborations that includes Moose Knuckles, UGG, Converse and more.
Price: $250
Where to buy: Browns Telfar
Blair + Jack - Anti-Bump Treatment
Blair + Jack Anti-Bump Treatment.
Why we love it: This one's for the boys. After watching her husband struggle with painful razor bumps, Canadian board-certified plastic surgeon Dr. Bimpe Ayeni created a brand formulated for men's most common skin concerns. For those suffering from irritation and razor bumps, Blair + Jack's Anti-Bump Treatment uses Azelaic acid to smoothen the skin and prevent future irritation.
Price: $45
Where to buy: Blair + Jack
Briogeo - Farewell Frizz Rosarco Milk Leave-In Conditioner
Briogeo Farewell Frizz Rosarco Milk Leave-In Conditioner.
Why we love it: This clean beauty brand was inspired by the childhood memories of founder Nancy Twine and her mother (a doctor and chemist) concocting hair treatments in their kitchen. Briogeo works for all hair types and textures, but for those with curly hair in particular, this leave-in treatment will help control the dryness and humidity that often causes frizz.
Price: $34
Where to buy: Sephora
KINApparel - Black Pepper Long Utility Puffer
KINApparel Black Pepper Long Utility Puffer.
Why we love it: Inspired by her Ghanaian, content creator Philomina Kane, built KINApparel, where KIN stands for Keep it Naturally. KINApparel specializes in satin-lined apparel from hoodies to onesies, like this long lightweight puffer coat that transforms into a tote.
Price: $405
Where to buy: KINApparel
FORVR Mood - Champagne Showers Candle
FORVR Mood Champagne Showers Candle.
Why we love it: For those who learned how to do makeup through YouTube, you'll recognize Jackie Aina as an OG makeup guru who began influencing before it even had a name. Aina's cruelty and paraben-brand FORVR Mood candles burn for 65 hours, so you can smell the fresh, citrusy scent of this Champagne Showers candle for nights on end.
Price: $51.50
Where to buy: Sephora
Isrealla Kobla - Azad Cap Sleeve Midi Dress
Isrealla Kobla Azad Cap Sleeve Midi Dress.
Why we love it: Engineer turned fashion designer Emefa Kuadey created the Toronto-based brand using sewing techniques she learned from her aunt during an extended visit in 2012. Today, the brand creates all pieces in small batches with local Canadian sewers, all made to order to reduce production waste.
Price: $549
Where to buy: The Bay Israella Kobla
Studio Anansi - Arc Bronze Brown Velvet Bench
Studio Anansi Arc Bronze Brown Velvet Bench.
Why we love it: Studio Anansi, whose name is inspired by a spiderlike figure in West African and Caribbean folklore, was established in 2018 by Canadian-born designer Evan Jerry. Jerry uses a love of storytelling and exploration to create unexpected sculptural objects, many of which can be seen in his collaboration with CB2.
Price: $2,599
Where to buy: CB2
My Sticky Little Secret - The Complete My Sticky Little Secret Set
My Sticky Little Secret 'The Complete My Sticky Little Secret Set.'
Why we love it: As the long-awaited first product launch from Canadian content creator Raschelle Smith Clare (popularly known as Ms Rosh Posh), My Sticky Little Secret is a line of wig-related hair care and styling tools (with a cheeky name) that help you achieve the flawless styling finish Clare's most viral videos are most known for. This set includes everything you need to get started, including skin protector, glue, edge brushes and lace glue remover.
Price: $99.99
Where to buy: My Sticky Little Secret
Fear of God - Essentials Green Drawstring Sweatpants
Fear of God 'Green Drawstring Sweatpants.'
Why we love it: Jerry Lorenzo Manuel Jr. (yes, son of famed MLB star Jerry Manuel) started Fear of God after pivoting to fashion after a career in corporate sports, party promotion and marketing. What started as a menswear fashion brand focused on minimalist t-shirts and athleisure has now become a luxury streetwear staple, mostly recently adding gender-neutral and womenswear items to their collection.
Price: $130
Where to buy: Holt Renfrew SSENSE
It's Souper - Moroccan Chickpea & Chicken Stew
It's Souper 'Moroccan Chickpea and Chicken Stew.'
Why we love it: Nothing like a good Dragon's Den success story. Lola Adeyemi danced her way onto Dragon's Den (watch the pitch to see more) and left with $180,000 for 20 per cent of her brand of Nigerian-inspired soups, sauces, and stews. Now, the Richmond Hill, Ontario-based brand's products are available in food retailers across Canada, including Whole Foods, Sobey's and McEwan.
Price: $44.99
Where to buy: It's Souper
Shea Moisture - Intensive Hydration Conditioner
Shea Moisture Manuka Honey and Mafura Oil Intensive Hydration Conditioner
Why we love it: Shea Moisture's rich history reaches as far back as 1912, when the founder's grandmother began selling shea butter soaps in a village market in Sierra Leone. With Manuka honey as the main ingredient in this product line, this Shea Moisture conditioner has become a holy grail product for hair stylists (and at-home hair care enthusiasts) who want to give a moisture boost to extremely dry hair.
Price: $16.99
Where to buy: Shoppers Drug Mart Well.ca
Daily Paper - Rajub Quilted Jacket
Daily Paper 'Rajub Quilted Jacket.'
Why we love it: Based in Amsterdam, this streetwear brand has been an industry favourite for artists around the world, most recently seen on Beyonce, who (along with her dancers and production crew, wore custom Daily Paper tracksuits throughout her most recent Renaissance tour.
Price: $329
Where to buy: Farfetch Due West

Document 362 of 500

Kelly McParland: Don't trust politicians - Liberal, NDP or Tory - to fix housing

Author: McParland, Kelly

Publication info: McParland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kelly-mcparland-dont-trust-politicians-liberal/docview/2923000953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Kelly+McParland%3A+Don%27t+trust+politicians+-+Liberal%2C+NDP+or+Tory+-+to+fix+housing&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McParland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People in Toronto like to think of themselves as progressive.
Every election they faithfully return a boatload of federal Liberals. So many, in fact, that without Toronto Liberal hopes of majorities would fade dramatically, and even minorities could get dicey. When Torontonians periodically tire of the Liberals they tend to opt for the NDP, because Conservatives might do bad things like balance the budget (known in progressive-speak as "austerity.")
Over decades of non-austerity, Toronto has been progressive to the point of going broke. Successive mayors have refused to increase property taxes to match a surging population and increased bills from transit, housing, policing, homelessness, refugees and other costs. City budgeting consists of hoping for a miracle, kicking costs down the road, letting services slip or demanding bailouts from Ottawa or the province. If those don't appear, everyone complains about how badly the city is treated, amid warnings that its status as everyone's favourite non-American nice place is in danger.
It turns out you can only get away with Toronto's way of doing things for so long until the bills come due. That has been happening big time since former mayor John Tory left the scene a year ago and was replaced by Olivia Chow, NDP stalwart, former federal MP and widow of much-liked opposition leader Jack Layton. Unusual for a New Democrat, Chow has raised the shocking notion that the city should start paying its way for some of its expenses.
During her mayoral campaign, Chow pledged to keep property tax increases "moderate" and "similar to increases seen over the past eight years," which were tied to inflation. Instead the budget released this month would raise rates 9.5 per cent, close to three times December's official inflation rate of 3.4 per cent.
She did it, she said, because "We can't avoid hard choices anymore... So I'm fixing the financial mess and getting Toronto back on track."
Federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre keeps insisting the next election will be all about the carbon tax, but while that may be the case in parts of the country it seems more likely the issue in Toronto will focus heavily on housing. If it's hard to find affordable homes in much of Canada, in Toronto it verges on the impossible. While prices have abated somewhat in the past year, higher interest rates ensure ownership remains well beyond reach of all but the wealthiest. Rental costs are stratospheric. The general consensus is that, unless you already own something or have family money, forget it.
The reasons for this have been much-explored. The Toronto Star helpfully encapsulated them in an article that warned: "Regular families will never again be able to buy a house in Toronto" before offering strategies to change things.
Interestingly, the causes it outlined stem overwhelmingly from government decisions. Ottawa cancelled all new federal funding for social housing in 1993 and never quite picked up the ball again. "There continues to be little appetite to return to the deeply subsidized, very low rent approaches used prior to 1994," one study notes.
Faced with financial crises through much of the 1990s, governments started downloading costs onto lower levels: feds dumped them on the provinces, the provinces dumped them on municipalities. Since towns and cities have fewer money-raising options, they got stuck with higher costs they couldn't handle. Despite growing populations, local councils responded to voter pressure by passing measures to prevent multi-unit, multi-storey projects that might offset the escalating demand but upset residents, while slapping developers with ever-greater fees and charges that were passed on to buyers via higher prices. Most recently, Ottawa opened the door to a sharp increase in immigration without consideration for where the new arrivals might all live.
Being politicians, the same people who largely brought about the situation are now declaring themselves the very folk who can get us out of it. After initially abjuring responsibility - "I'll be blunt ... housing isn't a primary federal responsibility" - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is busy announcing measures meant to soften the pain. No more GST on new rental apartments, a "housing accelerator fund" with a requirement to cut red tape, with money going to cities for specific housing plans.
Poilievre in turn has consistently campaigned on a pledge to tie federal funding to cities to their record of housing starts and elimination of bureaucratic barriers.
Whether any of it works or not remains to be seen. On Sunday Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland announced Ottawa would extend a ban on foreign buyers snapping up Canadian homes, a measure quickly dismissed by industry officials as largely meaningless given how little impact the existing ban has had.
Just because they agree housing is an emergency doesn't mean leaders at different levels will learn to get along with one another, or voters will put up with any discomfort their remedies produce. Liberal MPs are already sniping at Chow over her repeated demands for more money and her tactic of blaming Ottawa for failing to pony up. Ontario Premier Doug Ford agreed to take on hundreds of millions of dollars in road costs to ease pressure on Toronto - he and Chow even hugged for photographers - but when he sought to free up land for housing by removing a few sections from the protected green belt around Toronto he ran into a storm that eventually forced a full retreat.
For the moment, Chow is in a strong position. It's difficult to accuse a New Democrat with her record of being a greedy capitalist out to enrich developers, and there's no dispute that Toronto's ills threaten the liveability of a city whose size, skills and population have made it an attractive draw well beyond Canada's borders.
But likeability doesn't guarantee good policy: one of Toronto's most popular mayors earned that status based on a 1970s bylaw imposing a 45-foot height restriction on new construction; decades of Ontario rent controls helped ensure new apartment construction all but dried up.
Bad housing policies can be credited to Liberals, Conservatives and NDP alike. The definition of an optimist might be someone who thinks today's policymakers will be better at trying to manipulate the market than past ones.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Federal funding Housing starts; Industry: 92113 :‎ Public Finance Activities

Subject: Federal aid to cities; Federal funding; Housing starts

Document 363 of 500

FIRST READING: Canadian rents continue their ruinous move into insanity

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-canadian-rents-continue-their/docview/2922851386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Canadian+rents+continue+their+ruinous+move+into+insanity&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
The Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation just dropped their annual Rental Market Report, and in a conclusion that likely surprises nobody with any peripheral connection to the rental market, everything is worse. Canadian rents are way up, vacancy rates are way down, and there is no sign of any of this getting better.
And the CMHC report is only a survey of average rents; the picture is even bleaker for anybody currently on the hunt for an apartment. As one recent RBC analysis put it: "It's never been harder to rent in Canada."
Below, a quick guide to the ruinous state of Canadian rents right now.
In just the last year, rents have risen at twice the rate of inflation
CMHC said that rents within the "purpose-built rental market" rose by eight per cent over the last year.
That's about the same as the figure found by the research firm Urbanation, which runs the website Rentals.ca. Urbanation has a slightly broader analysis of the Canadian rental markets, and they found average rents going up by 8.6 per cent in just the last year - or, about $14 per month.
Both the CMHC and Urbanation figures are twice as high as the rate of inflation, which came to 3.9 per cent for 2023.
... and the figures are completely off the chain when it comes to "asking rents"
Most Canadian renters are already locked into a lease, which often limits how much their rent will go up each year. (Most provinces also enforce an "allowable rent increase.") But it's in the realm of "asking rents" - as in, the advertised rents for vacant units - that prices have been absolutely soaring.
In just the last two years, Urbanation found asking rents have spiked by 22 per cent - a total increase of about $390 per month. This means that a unit that was renting for $1,788 in January 2022 is now being advertised at close to $2,200.
Rahim Mohamed: Nope, Alberta's gender policy isn't a matter of 'life and death'
Trudeau Liberals' 'affordability' cabinet retreat featured $50K banquet
Availability is lower than at any point in most renters' lifetimes
All of the above figures are dependent on whether it's even possible to find somewhere to rent in the first place. The reason rents have been able to spike so prodigiously is because vacancies are at historic lows - and thus any unit hitting the market is immediately barraged by desperate renters.
"The national vacancy rate declined for a second consecutive year. This notable decrease has left the vacancy rate at a new low of 1.5 per cent in 2023," reads the CMHC Rental Market Report.
The agency has only been tracking vacancy rates since 1988. But this means that any renter younger than 36 is encountering a market tighter than anything that's happened in their lifetime.
The typical Canadian now has to work 80 hours per month just to afford shelter
According to figures maintained by the federal government, Canada's "median hourly wage" ranges between $22.50 in P.E.I. and $30 per hour depending on the province.
In Ontario right now, the typical worker is bringing in $27 per hour before tax. With average asking rents at $2,178, this means they'll have to work at least 80 hours - so, two straight weeks - before rent is paid. All other life expenses (including tax) have to be earned in the rest of the month.
And the economics get really dismal when it comes to those earning minimum wage. Even if you're earning the highest provincial minimum wage in the country ($16.75 in B.C.), it would take 91 hours until you can pay the average rent on a Canadian bachelor apartment.
On every available metric, this is all poised to get worse
Historically, whenever things got too out of whack in Canadian real estate, it was due to some unexpected phenomenon that would eventually resolve itself. Vacancy rates took a weird and temporary plunge in mid-2001, apparently because a booming economy was causing scores of young people to move out and find work.
But this current rental crisis is caused by two very simple factors: Unprecedented numbers of people are entering the country, and homebuilders aren't even close to keeping up with demand.
While the Trudeau government has recently promised to slash the intake of international students, Canada is still on course to bring in historically high rates of migrants for 2024.
In the meantime, the growth of new Canadian rental stock is actually getting more sluggish. RBC analysts found that it dropped to a four-year low in 2023, growing by only 1.7 per cent. Incredibly, the number of rentals in Toronto actually shrunk by 0.5 per cent. As RBC wrote, it "couldn't come at a worse time."
IN OTHER NEWS
The Government of Quebec has announced it will spend $870 million to replace the roof of its Olympic Stadium, the central structure built for the 1976 Montreal Olympics. This marks the latest dispensation of cash to be lost to one of the most famous money pits in Canadian history. The "Big O" went well over budget and wasn't actually completed in time for the Olympics. By the time it was finally paid off in 2006, accumulated charges had risen to an estimated $1.5 billion.
There's been a new twist to the September scandal in which a veteran of the Waffen-SS was brought to Parliament Hill as a VIP and then applauded by the entire membership of both the House of Commons and the Senate (as well as visiting Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy). The consistent line of the Trudeau government was that the invite was entirely the fault of Speaker Anthony Rota, and Rota was eventually forced to resign as a result. But the Globe and Mail recently obtained evidence that Trudeau's office knew well in advance that Hunka was coming - and had even invited him to dinner. "Dear Yaroslav Hunka, The Right Honourable Justin Trudeau, Prime Minister of Canada, is pleased to invite you to a special event," read an invitation sent to the former veteran of the Nazi-allied Galicia Division. As the National Post has noted before, it would have been extraordinarily easy to uncover Hunka's Nazi background with only a few Google searches. The mere fact that he is a Ukrainian who "fought the Russians" during the Second World War should have been a clue, as there were very few ways to do that without some form of alliance with Nazi Germany.
Canada's head of state has just been diagnosed with cancer. King Charles III was in hospital due to "benign prostate enlargement," and according to the statement above, further diagnostic tests revealed a "form of cancer." While Buckingham Palace has not revealed what kind of cancer or how advanced it is, this is still an unprecedented level of openness for a U.K. monarch. Although Queen Elizabeth II showed multiple signs of illness in the months preceding her 2022 death, there's still been no official confirmation of what specifically killed her or whether she was even sick.
Get all of these insights and more into your inbox by signing up for the First Reading newsletter here

Subject: Vacancies; Cancer; Rentals

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Document 364 of 500

Canadian kids are targets of sextortion and online sexual luring over social media, experts say

Author: Kirkey, Sharon

Publication info: Kirkey, Sharon. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-kids-are-targets-sextortion-online/docview/2922788391/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canadian+kids+are+targets+of+sextortion+and+online+sexual+luring+over+social+media%2C+experts+say&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kirkey%2C+Sharon&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As part of the extortion tactics, the boy's tormentor had pizzas sent to his house, to prove to his victim he knew where he lived.
The intimidation can escalate quickly into something more sinister - threats against family members, or ultimatums that would ruin the child's life. "We've seen some pretty dark stuff in terms of the violence that occurs towards kids, or threats of violence," says Stephen Sauer, director of cybertip.ca, a national tip line for the reporting of online child sexual abuse and exploitation, a growing threat to Canada's kids.
Reports of online sexual violence like sextortion, a form of blackmail where the perpetrator coerces a young person into sending sexual images or video, and then threatens to distribute those images unless they send them money or more sexual images, have been rising sharply. In recent days alone, American lawmakers have accused Meta's Mark Zuckerberg and four other big tech bosses of having "blood on your hands;" allegations have surfaced that up to 100,000 children a day using Facebook and Instagram in 2021 were exposed to online sexual harassment, including pictures of adult genitalia; and researchers have warned that a network of West African cybercriminals known as the Yahoo Boys are "bombing" youth across the U.S., Canada and Australia with fake accounts "to coerce victims into compromising situations."
Nigerian police have arrested a man in an alleged sextortion case linked to the "sudden death" of a 14-year-old Canadian boy. The suspect, who has pleaded not guilty to an 18-count charge, allegedly hacked into social media accounts posing as an alluring young woman "to entice unsuspecting victims and seeking their sexually explicit photographs," including nude images from the 14-year-old that were used to blackmail him, "a situation that led to his death," according to a release from a Nigerian law enforcement agency. The arrest was made in August following intelligence received by Surrey RCMP, which is scheduled to release information Tuesday about a "financial sextortion investigation."
Only months ago, Carson Cleland, a 12-year-old Prince George, B.C., boy, died by suicide, another victim of sextortion. These deadly scams gained national attention more than a decade ago, following the death of 15-year-old Amanda Todd, who died by suicide at her home in Port Coquitlam, B.C., in 2012 after posting a video, watched by millions the world over, in which she used flash cards to describe being extorted by an online predator for two years.
Operated by the Canadian Centre for Child Protection, cybertip.ca receives about 10 reports every day involving kids and teens caught in online sextortion. Those are just the youth that are in crisis, Sauer said.
"We know a lot of kids are trying to manage these situations on their own."
NP view: Governments must halt ads on platforms that enable child abuse
Sabrina Maddeaux: How Instagram, Liberals are failing to protect kids
The volume of reports involving online sexual luring - offenders who troll the internet looking to entice minors for sexual purposes, even manipulating kids into meeting in person - has increased dramatically, from 220 in 2018, to 2,013 in 2022.
Allegations that children on Facebook and Instagram were targets of sexual harassment were revealed in a recent filing in a lawsuit against Meta by New Mexico's attorney general, who claims Meta has allowed its platforms to become a "marketplace for predators," according to Forbes.
The federal Liberals have for years promised an online harms bill, first in 2019 and then again in 2021 when the party promised that a re-elected Liberal government would introduce legislation within its "first 100 days" targeting child sexual abuse material, the non-consensual distribution of intimate images and other online harms. Following 12-year-old Carson Cleland's suicide, public safety minister Dominic LeBlanc said the government is still working on it. "It's a complicated space to legislate in and on," LeBlanc told reporters in November. "But the urgency of moving, I think, is real."
The abuse and exploitation come in different forms. With sextortion, youth (boys and young males in particular) are typically tricked into believing they're communicating with someone their own age. Sextorters often share a sexual photo first, then manipulate the victim into sending sexual photos or videos or expose themselves over a livestream that's secretly recorded.
If the victim is a girl, the predator often demands more sexual photos or videos. If a boy, money is almost always the ransom, sent via platforms like PayPal, gift cards, or sometimes cryptocurrencies. According to cybertip.ca, kids have been extorted for as little as $9 (what the child had in their bank account at the time) to $5,000 to $10,000. "We have situations where kids have been paying the offenders for months, because they're worried about their images going out," Sauer said. "Twenty dollars here, 50 dollars there. Over time, that's a huge amount of money for that kid."
With luring, a perpetrator connects with youth, through direct messaging, or chatting in an app or game, to groom them into providing sexual images, or engage in sexual acts on livestream, even to meet up with them in person.
The extortion tactics can be intense, aggressive and dangerous. One explicit recording can be used as leverage for more. "(Offenders) will say, 'I'm going to share this with your family, let them know what you're doing online,' or, 'what you've done here is illegal, you've created child pornography and I'm going to let the authorities know unless you continue to give me further material.'"
Some of that material is circulated among pedophiles, Sauer said. "What we've seen on the dark web is conversations within these communities about awards for getting this material - 'who has the best material of the month?'"
Kids as young as 10 and 11 have contacted cybertip.ca. Often parents discover the abuse and report it. Instagram and Snapchat are the main platforms where sextortion is committed, though increasing reports now involve Wizz, a Tinder-like app for kids. Often multiple platforms are used. The offender might begin a conversation or connect with a youth on an online game like Roblox, and then move them over to Discord, or find them on Instagram first, and then move them over to Snapchat for more direct conversations.
The pandemic didn't help. More children were engaged online than ever before, often while alone at home. Tormentors "understood they had intimate access to kids on an unprecedented level," Sauer said.
The primary targets of financial sextortion are boys aged 15 to 17. Boys are vulnerable. They can be "pretty quick to respond," quick to get excited when someone shows an interest in them, Sauer said. Sexual sextortion, where tormentors ask for more material in exchange for not sending the victim's nudes to friends and family, more often affects girls from 13 to 14.
Offenders are sometimes people working alone. but police are seeing a growing trend of cybercriminals from overseas who realize kids in North America have money, or access to it. "I think there's an understanding that there's a vulnerable population out there and this is one way to get money," Sauer said.
The exploitation can move from online to off. A recent Ontario-wide luring investigation led to 10 arrests after undercover police posed as underage children online. Police seized Barbies, baseball cards, candies and condoms from people who'd come to see the "children" they'd spoken to online. In one case, it took just 90 minutes from the time a suspect first engaged with an undercover cop, to the moment they arrived at the scheduled meeting place.
"Child sexual exploitation is a horrific crime and online predators are determined criminals," Meta said in a blog post updated the day before the U.S. Senate judiciary committing hearing. The company said that, between Aug. 1 and Dec. 1 alone it disabled more than 2.6 million accounts for violating its child sexual exploitation policies.
"In addition to developing technology that roots out predators, we hire specialists dedicated to online chid safety and we share information with our industry peers and law enforcement," Meta said.
But Saeur said it's not enough to allow companies to take voluntary action. "There needs to be some regulation put in place here." One senator likened a recent rush of protective measures announced by big social media platforms to "putting paint on rotten wood."
Companies need to be held accountable when kids are exploited on their platforms, Sauer said. "We really need to start getting out ahead of this, and it needs to be fast." Popular platforms are enabling offenders by making it easy to create fake accounts to find potential victims and access their contact lists, he said.
His advice to youth: "Be aware that not everyone you're intersecting with has good intentions. Especially online, you want to be really conscious about who's connecting with you. If you don't recognize them, maybe don't communicate with them." In an analysis of financial sextortion victim's posts on a reddit support forum, the Centre for Child Protection found that complying with demands for money typically leads to further demands for money. Even when they paid, 40 per cent of victims had their nudes shared anyway.
Victims said there were red flags they were being targeted by a scammer, including the use of poor grammar, and how quickly the conversation was moved to a different platform and turned sexual. In several cases, the offender sent screenshots of the victim's SnapMap to prove they knew their whereabouts, "further intensifying the threats."
For parents: "Be aware of what apps and services your kids have access to," Sauer said. Understand that in a lot of cases those apps don't have proper safeguards (or they aren't set by default) ... Have regular conversations with your kids about online sexual violence, what that looks like ... and that they know that you are there to help them through these situations. That no matter how far it's gone, they can still come to you for help."
National Post

Premier smith goes to Ottawa; Gender fracas follows her on economic mission

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-smith-goes-ottawa-gender-fracas-follows/docview/2922687488/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Premier+smith+goes+to+Ottawa%3B+Gender+fracas+follows+her+on+economic+mission&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith came to Ottawa to talk about economic collaboration, but most of the attention was on her recently announced policies around children's trans and sex-ed issues.
Smith's presence prompted reporters to press federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre if he supported Alberta's policy plans. In response, Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of "spreading disinformation" about the Alberta policies to distract from his record on the economy, before saying that the federal government should mind its own business.
"It's time for Justin Trudeau to stop distracting and dividing Canadians, let parents raise kids and provinces run schools and hospitals. That's my common sense approach," said Poilievre.
Smith started off her day in Ottawa meeting Alberta's Conservative MPs to discuss ways they can continue "to move Alberta and Canada forward together," and relaunching Alberta's office in Ottawa to advance the province's interests at the national level.
She was the guest speaker of a luncheon hosted by the Economic Club of Canada and was to appear at a reception hosted by conservative think tank the Canada Strong and Free Network in the evening.
"We need to highlight the need for a new consensus between Alberta and Canada," she told the luncheon audience. "Hopefully, all sides can get on the same page and move forward together. That's not going to happen in just one visit, but it can happen with time and patience."
But Smith's message was overwhelmed by the political drama over her trans and sex policies.
Alberta Liberal MP and Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said Monday Smith should "reconsider these harmful and dangerous policies" on Monday and called on Albertans to reach out to their UCP MLAs to make sure that this legislation does not see the floor of the legislature in the fall.
"This is Ron de Santis''Don't Say Gay'bill coming up north," he said, referring to the Florida governor's policies around sex-ed for minors.
Boissonnault also slammed Poilievre's Conservatives for staying mostly silent on the issue after the Globe and Mail revealed that an email was sent to caucus members asking them not to comment publicly on the issue and to notify the leader's office about all media requests.
"If we let Danielle Smith go this far, what are other jurisdictions going to do? We have to stand up and call this out now," he said.
Smith unveiled her new policies last week, which include banning transitioning surgery for minors, banning puberty blockers for children under the age of 16, creating biological female-only sports leagues and requiring parental consent to attend sexual education in schools.
She also announced a new rule that would oblige schools to notify parents and obtain their consent if a child aged 15 and under wants to change his or her name or pronouns.
While similar policies on parental consent have been announced in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick, Alberta's set of proposed policies would be the most restrictive in the country for transgender, genderfluid or questioning minors.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Monday he was not considering any similar action.
"We're leaving everything alone," he said in a press conference in Toronto, before quickly changing the subject.
Speaking to reporters in Ottawa, Smith said there would be "lots of time for consultation" on her proposed policies in the next few months before the legislation will be tabled this fall but also noted that there is not "a single voice" or "a single perspective" among the trans community.
"I want to make sure that young people are not closing off options to themselves prematurely. I want to make sure that processes are followed so that it's a well-considered decision and that the child is mature enough to understand the consequences of the decisions that they're making," she said.
"I will keep on consulting with the transgender community because it's really important that we give those who do make the transition the support they need in their family environment, and with their medical needs on a lifetime basis. So, we'll continue to have those conversations," she added.
Smith plans to visit Toronto on Tuesday and Washington, D.C. later in the week.
National Post

Gender politics overwhelms Danielle Smith's economic visit to Ottawa

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gender-politics-overwhelms-danielle-smiths/docview/2922584442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Gender+politics+overwhelms+Danielle+Smith%27s+economic+visit+to+Ottawa&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Alberta Premier Danielle Smith came to Ottawa to talk about economic collaboration, but most of the attention was on her recently announced policies around children's trans and sex ed issues.
Smith's presence prompted reporters to press federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre if he supported Alberta's policy plans. In response, Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of "spreading disinformation" about the Alberta policies to distract from his record on the economy, before saying that the federal government should mind its own business.
"It's time for Justin Trudeau to stop distracting and dividing Canadians, let parents raise kids and provinces run schools and hospitals. That's my common sense approach," said Poilievre.
Smith started off her day in Ottawa meeting Alberta's Conservative MPs to discuss ways they can continue "to move Alberta and Canada forward together," and relaunching Alberta's office in Ottawa to advance the province's interests at the national level.
She was the guest speaker of a luncheon hosted by the Economic Club of Canada and was to appear at a reception hosted by conservative think tank the Canada Strong and Free Network in the evening.
"We need to highlight the need for a new consensus between Alberta and Canada," she told the luncheon audience. "Hopefully, all sides can get on the same page and move forward together. That's not going to happen in just one visit, but it can happen with time and patience."
But Smith's message was overwhelmed by the political drama over her trans and sex policies.
Liberals 'deeply disturbed' by Alberta's new transgender rules, will 'talk' to Smith's government
Alberta's new transgender rules: From restricted treatments to pronouns and parental rights
Alberta Liberal MP and Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said Monday Smith should "reconsider these harmful and dangerous policies" on Monday and called on Albertans to reach out to their UCP MLAs to make sure that this legislation does not see the floor of the legislature in the fall.
"This is Ron de Santis' 'Don't Say Gay' bill coming up north," he said, referring to the Florida governor's policies around sex ed for minors.
Boissonnault also slammed Poilievre's Conservatives for staying mostly silent on the issue after the Globe and Mail revealed that an email was sent to caucus members asking them not to comment publicly on the issue and to notify the leader's office about all media requests.
"If we let Danielle Smith go this far, what are other jurisdictions going to do? We have to stand up and call this out now," he said.
Smith unveiled her new policies last week, which include banning transitioning surgery for minors, banning puberty blockers for children under the age of 16, creating biological female-only sports leagues and requiring parental consent to attend sexual education in schools.
She also announced a new rule that would oblige schools to notify parents and obtain their consent if a child aged 15 and under wants to change his or her name or pronouns.
While similar policies on parental consent have been announced in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick, Alberta's set of proposed policies would be the most restrictive in the country for transgender, gender-fluid or questioning minors.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Monday he was not considering any similar action.
"We're leaving everything alone," he said in a press conference in Toronto, before quickly changing the subject.
Speaking to reporters in Ottawa, Smith said there would be "lots of time for consultation" on her proposed policies in the next few months before the legislation will be tabled this fall but also noted that there is not "a single voice" or "a single perspective" amongst the trans community.
"I want to make sure that young people are not closing off options to themselves prematurely. I want to make sure that processes are followed so that it's a well-considered decision and that the child is mature enough to understand the consequences of the decisions that they're making," she said.
"I will keep on consulting with the transgender community because it's really important that we give those who do make the transition the support they need in their family environment, and with their medical needs on a lifetime basis. So, we'll continue to have those conversations," she added.
I am grateful to Alberta's Conservative Members of Parliament for their service to our country.
This morning, in Ottawa, we sat together and discussed ways we can continue to move Alberta and Canada forward together.
- Danielle Smith (@ABDanielleSmith) February 5, 2024
Smith plans to visit Toronto on Tuesday and Washington, D.C. later in the week.
