Letters: The 'spectre of apartheid' is manifesting in Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-spectre-apartheid-is-manifesting-canada/docview/2921956484/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+The+%27spectre+of+apartheid%27+is+manifesting+in+Canada&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'I immigrated from South Africa to escape apartheid'
Re: Black-only swim times, Black-only lounges: The rise of race segregation on Canadian universities - Tristin Hopper, Jan. 31
I am aghast having just read the article by Tristin Hopper about the Black-only swim times at the University of Waterloo athletic centre.
I immigrated to Canada 56 years ago from South Africa to escape the apartheid that was foisted upon me, a person who was classified as "Indian" and "Asiatic." Till now I have lived fearlessly in the relatively comfortable belief that I never have to go to any facility in Canada designated for specific racial groups.
Now I see the spectre of apartheid rising in this fair land. What's next? Separate toilets, dining areas, entrances, back-of-the bus line-ups?
I am afraid the police will stop me and ask me for my "passbook" listing my race to determine whether I am allowed to be in a certain area or not. I saw Blacks being thrown into paddy wagons 60 years ago for not carrying a passbook, and it was an ugly sight.
I now have to seriously start thinking about returning to the "Rainbow nation" of South Africa where racial segregation has been abolished.
Kanti Makan, Cambridge, Ont.
'Students taught to hate and support violence against Jews'
Re: The UNRWA teachers who slaughtered Jews and the Canadian profs who admire them - Jesse Kline, Jan. 30
Students and educators across many of our educational institutions are being given a false, biased narrative against Israel. They are taught to hate and support abhorrent violence against Jews. They are taught that the horrific, barbaric acts perpetrated in Israel by Hamas on Oct. 7 are to be applauded.
The individuals in our educational institutions who spout this hate should be fired from their positions. They are inculcating our future leaders with a world view that can only lead to disaster. Hatred against one group inevitably leads to hatred of others.
My suggestions to these teachers of hate - move to Gaza and see firsthand what it is like to live under the rule of Hamas. Live in poverty because money for humanitarian aide is used to be build underground tunnels to support acts of terror. Know what it is like to be used as a human shield in the current war. Most importantly, look at the vibrant country of Israel and ask why, in the same number of years since the State of Israel was declared, is Gaza not flourishing in the same way?
Then, please, stay in Gaza. Canada does not want you.
Phyllis Levin, Toronto
Shame on NHL alumni
Re: 'Hockey is my safe place': Teen Israeli hockey players evacuated from their homes tour Canada - Jamie Casemore, Jan. 29
Today's National Post, as with all media, was awash with distressing news. However, it was a line from the article about the young Israeli hockey players visiting Canada that I found most chilling. How was it that NHL alumni (apparently of the calibre to warrant championship rings) met with these young, traumatized players and then did "not want to be identified" as having done so?
Unfortunately, I know the answer: the boys are Israeli and the NHL alumni were afraid of the consequences of having their names associated with them. Shame on these players and the NHL. How about some backbone?
Laura Bowley, Belleville, Ont.
Thanks, Conrad
Re: Tucker Carlson arrives just in time to exploit a humiliated Trudeau - Rahim Mohamed, Jan. 25
On behalf of a majority of Canadian conservatives, I would like to thank Conrad Black. During discussions at the Tucker Carlson event in Edmonton, his challenging of some of the headliner's crowd-baiting hyperbole was both principled and appreciated.
By calling out Carlson's claim that Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland is a "fascist," Black displayed the pragmatic centre-right sensibility that will be required to remove this ideologically toxic Liberal government from power.
He tactfully described her as "quite a nice lady" despite being "politically misguided." Now, that's how it's done in Canada, Mr. Carlson.
Al Willey, Edmonton
'Remove all public funding from our universities'
Re: It will take more than a few resignations to fix our woke universities - Gad Saad, Jan. 25
In the first half of the 1900s when churches occupied the apex of intellectual life in Canada, social norms were such that most families contributed financially to their churches as much by choice as by social imperative. Today, universities have supplanted churches at the top of the intellectual heap and universities have secured their place there with immense financial support from our governments. Universities deserved their newfound allegiance from the public because they substituted academic and scientific rigour for the assorted dogmas and religious practices that many people no longer found compelling or alluring.
Today, universities are the ones promoting ideas that are not founded on rigour and that lack public appeal. All it will take, therefore, to fix the problem is to remove all public funding from our universities. Quickly, those schools that survive will revert to promoting ideas that flow from logic, reason, common sense and the scientific method. Government financial support for universities has allowed universities to ignore growing public disenchantment with their product for far too long.
Patrick Cowan, North York, Ont.
No correction between IQ and virtue
Re: Dear children, be very, very afraid of the coming age of AI rule - John Robson, Jan. 18
I quite agree with John Robson in his disinclination to place faith in the moral rectitude of the intelligentsia and would even state that there is evidence to prove that those who believe there is a "positive correlation between IQ and virtue" are delusional and potentially dangerous.
A case in point is the career of the Soviet dictator Joseph Stalin. It is an historical fact that this man of steel, who many esteemed as a genius, was a voracious reader who left for posterity a library of approximately 19,500 books.
Included in his legacy, however, are graves in which rest some 20 million of his countrymen, or rather, their corpses.
Carol-Faye Petricko, Toronto
Students are the biggest victims of visa cuts
Re: Ontario is not prepared for a cap on international students - Randall Denley, Jan. 25
To radically increase the number of foreign students in recent years, the Liberal government put its foot on the gas. Now it purports to put its foot on the brakes by limiting student visa numbers, even while it leaves its heavy foot on the gas.
When applying the gas the federal government didn't ask for provincial input but now it's a problem; suddenly, provincial co-operation (and blame) is critical.
The victims of this policy include not just the provincial educational systems, first seduced and now jilted. The main victims are the students themselves, both those billed as international students and those among the declining number of domestic students attending Canada's universities and colleges. And then there are the domestic students refused access entirely.
It's shameful. This is what happens when a policy file is managed purely for political ends.
John L Riley, Mono, Ont.
'Pretty sure Henry Ford did not have the benefit of subsidies'
Re: Canada wants to be an EV nation, but first it has to convince consumers - Marisa Coulton, Jan. 26
Manufacturers seem to believe they have a right to government subsidies as a means to encourage the general public to purchase an electric vehicle. This is likely a result of their success in extorting market-distorting subsidies for electric battery plants from our provincial and federal governments.
But wait! There is more. These same manufacturers and their dealers also believe that governments are responsible for investing in the necessary charging stations, the absence of which is a further disincentive for the general public to purchase an EV. I am pretty sure that Henry Ford did not have the benefit of the gold-plated government subsidies handed to EV manufacturers and their supply chain partners, nor the benefit of government investment in gas stations to enable internal combustion vehicles to replace horse and buggy transportation.
Moving to a government-subsidized EV-only transportation system is a pipe-dream being sold to us by a doctrinaire federal government attempting to bribe us with borrowed money. It is highly unlikely that our children and their children will inherit the promised net-zero world in 2050, but they will inherit an obscene debt load that will reduce their standard of living for many generations to come.
That will be the legacy of Justin Trudeau, Steven Guilbeault and the Liberals if Canadians are misguided enough to hand them another four years in power.
Paul Clarry, Aurora, Ont.
'Do something about York University and CUPE'
Re: FIRST READING: York University teaching assistants told to hijack tutorials into anti-Israel sessions - Tristin Hopper, Jan. 39
The article from Tristin Hopper on the very mediocre York University's complicity with CUPE 3903's allegiance with the anti-Israel movement should be another wake-up call for someone to do something about this abomination. The backers of this drivel (tool kit for TAs) should be identified and thrown out if York wants to retain or get back one iota of credibility.
Canada's universities have become a haven for bigots to deliver their hateful diatribes and York seems to be at the forefront of it all. I for one would like to see York University shut down.
Ted Mead, Winchester, Ont.
Unions have been a bastion of fairness and protection for union members in the workplace, ensuring decent wages and safe working conditions.
By providing members with tutorial materials in support of Palestine "liberation," CUPE 3903 was encouraging them to violate their contracts. Those members were hired to teach in their respective fields, not to indoctrinate.
CUPE 3903 has damaged the reputation of its union, put members' jobs at risk and stolen instruction time from students who didn't go to university for this.
In simple words: Stay in your lane, CUPE 3903.
Sharon Wiesenfeld, Toronto
CUPE giving instruction to York University teaching assistants is like a dentist receiving instructions from a carpenter - not to insult carpenters.
Allan Freedman, Toronto
Letters: Singh's enabling of Liberal spending is like holding the door open for a bank robber
Letters: Judge was right to rule Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act was unjustified
'The times they are a-changin' - and so are the Conservatives'
Re: Liberals melt down in the face of diverse Conservative candidates - Rahim Mohamed, Jan. 30
As the legendary Bob Dylan famously wrote, "The times they are a-changin'." And so are the Conservatives.
They are growing, very successfully, into a diverse and well-rounded, big-tent party that has a broad spectrum of very smart and thoughtful people.
Sabrina Maddeaux and Jamil Jivani are both brilliant, rising stars who are a great asset to the party and to Canada.
I cannot say the same of the Liberals, who seem to be made up of a smorgasbord of untalented, unqualified and inept people hired only for the DEI boxes they tick.
The hallmarks of the Liberals is their hubris, their incompetence and their hypocrisy. They are out of touch with Canadians and need to go.
Corey Needer, Vaughan, Ont.
Missing the point on Emergencies Act decision
Re: Court rules Liberals' use of Emergencies Act was unjustified, unreasonable - Christopher Nardi, Jan. 23
Many civil libertarians and apostles of democracy cheered the Federal Court decision declaring that the Emergencies Act and the Constitution were violated by the Trudeau government. The Emergencies Act, a legislative overreaction to the War Measures Act, made the mistake of allowing CSIS to define what constitutes a threat justifying resort to emergency measures.
It would have been enough to require the government to consult with the intelligence service, but civil libertarians and democracy's crybabies miss the point when they are happy leaving the last word to an unelected body other than a court, instead of to a duly elected government.
Howard Greenfield, Montreal
'Hardly surprising Liberals inclined to pathological obsession'
Re: Conservatives accuse Liberals of being 'pathologically obsessed' with carbon tax as House returns - Catherine Lévesque, Jan. 29
As the Liberals are openly ideological anyway, and have an aversion to individuality and common sense, it is hardly surprising that they would be inclined to pathological obsession.
Common sense requires keeping track of change in the external world, whereas an ideology can skip the facts and indulge in visions of how things are supposed to be.
The Liberals do recognize that there are certain political advantages to ideologies - as it is far easier to imagine how things are - than to figure out what's really going on.
Still! As ideology is a phenomena of the mind and not realty, there is always pain when presumption is undone.
Gordon Watson, Rocky Mountain House, Alta.
'Troubling aspects of Justin Trudeau's carbon policy'
Re: Parliament's carbon tax drama is ripped right out of Poilievre's script - John Ivison, Jan. 31
Three very troubling aspects of Justin Trudeau's carbon policy mentioned by John Ivison in his column should be highlighted.
The carbon tax is itself taxed by the HST; taxing a tax must end.
Neither should we accept Quebec's mystifying and totally unmerited lower carbon tax, or the Maritime carbon tax exemption for heating oil. A "national" tax that exempts parts of the nation divides the nation.
Finally, the Trudeau plan to arrange long-term contracts for carbon "offsets" intrigues me as I have planted a forest of carbon-sequestering trees. But I don't benefit from sale of the carbon offset, and I'm very confident that the next federal government will not want to be tied to such a long-term commitment.
Charles Hooker, East Garafraxa, Ont.

Document 376 of 500

Pressure social media on harms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pressure-social-media-on-harms/docview/2921588841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Pressure+social+media+on+harms&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'I can't do it anymore." That was the last message sent by 16-yearold Chase Nasca, who took his own life in February 2022, after allegedly being exposed to more than 1,000 unsolicited videos featuring violence and suicidal messages on TikTok, according to a lawsuit filed by his family.
This was just one of the heart-wrenching stories of online child exploitation, including sexual exploitation, and abuse that brought Democratic and Republican lawmakers in the United States together this week in a rare display of unity, hauling the chief executives of major social media companies in front of a Senate committee to account for their actions.
In one striking moment, Meta's Mark Zuckerberg apologized to the families who say their children have been harmed, exploited or driven to suicide by social media use. "I'm sorry for everything you have all been through," Zuckerberg said.
Despite that emotional moment, however, the tech CEOs testifying at the Senate hearing mostly deflected criticism of their products. They argued they were already doing enough, or they tried to blame others, as Zuckerberg did when he said it was up to Apple, not Meta, to ensure age verification for using the Instagram app.
This is nonsense. And the only reason the tech platforms, whether Instagram or TikTok, are able to disregard the harm their platforms cause to young people is because they are funded almost entirely through advertising. In addition to Canada's blue chip corporations, among the biggest advertisers on social media are governments. While the federal government no longer advertises on Facebook, the Liberal party still does, as does the Conservative party and most provinces, including Doug Ford's Ontario government. Ottawa still runs ads on Tik-Tok.
All Canadian governments and publicly funded agencies, as well as major corporations, must stop advertising on social media in order to put pressure on the platforms to truly change their practices to protect youth. Otherwise, they will continue to enable some pretty horrific outcomes.
For example, a recent Wall Street Journal investigation found that users of Instagram Reels who follow "young gymnasts, cheerleaders and other teen and preteen influencers" were "served jarring doses of salacious content," including sexualized images of young girls.
Last spring, another WSJ investigation found that the algorithms used by Facebook and Instagram help connect large networks of pedophiles. Those same social networks also provide fertile ground for child predators to find new victims.
There have been a number of high-profile cases of Canadian children committing suicide after being sexually extorted on social media, including a 12-yearold British Columbia boy who took his life in October, and Amanda Todd, who died in 2012 after being tormented by a middle-aged Dutch man who has since been convicted of possessing child pornography, child luring and other crimes.
TikTok, in particular, has come under increased scrutiny recently for failing to protect kids from harmful content and fears that it is being used by the Chinese Communist Party to collect personal information and spread discord throughout the West.
Research has found that TikTok and its rivals - Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts, which feed users a constant stream of brief, personalized videos - activate the reward centres in the brain, feeding kids a rush of dopamine that can lead to addiction. There is also emerging evidence that these sites are reducing attention spans.
TikTok has been accused by numerous western countries of sharing its users'private information with the Chinese government and using the platform to spread misinformation and antiwestern propaganda.
It's telling that the Chinese version of TikTok produces specialized content for teens, including science and art videos, and subjects children under the age of 14 to strict usage controls, which is not the case in the rest of the world.
Although these companies repeatedly claim that they are working to make their platforms safer for children, the reality is that they have consistently acted in bad faith. Last year, Ireland handed TikTok a hefty fine after finding its default settings failed to protect the privacy of young people and it used shady practices designed to encourage them to accept settings that weren't in their best interests.
The claim has also been contradicted by Arturo Béjar, a former Facebook engineer who says he warned management after his 14-year-old daughter and her friends began getting "unwanted sexual advances" on Instagram, but nothing was done. Béjar characterized the company's efforts to protect children as a "placebo for press and regulators."
What we need is for these companies to start taking this issue seriously - to do a better job of monitoring their networks for child predators, to make it easy for youth to report abuse and to design tools to give parents greater control over what their children do online.
A campaign led by the federal and provincial governments to refrain from advertising on these platforms until they take online harms seriously would go a long way (the federal government spent $40 million on Meta ads alone between 2017 and 2022). If big businesses followed suit, these companies would have little choice but to comply.
National Post View

Document 381 of 500

NP View: Governments must halt ads on platforms that enable child abuse

Author: View

Publication info: View. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/np-view-governments-must-halt-ads-on-platforms/docview/2921514754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=NP+View%3A+Governments+must+halt+ads+on+platforms+that+enable+child+abuse&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=View&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I can't do it anymore." That was the last message sent by 16-year-old Chase Nasca, who took his own life in February 2022, after allegedly being exposed to over 1,000 unsolicited videos featuring violence and suicidal messages on TikTok, according to a lawsuit filed by his family.
This was just one of the heart-wrenching stories of online child exploitation, including sexual exploitation, and abuse that brought Democratic and Republican lawmakers in the United States together this week in a rare display of unity, hauling the CEOs of major social media companies in front of a Senate committee to account for their actions.
In one striking moment, Meta's Mark Zuckerberg apologized to the families who say their children have been harmed, exploited or driven to suicide by social media use. "I'm sorry for everything you have all been through," Zuckerberg said.
Despite that emotional moment, however, the tech CEOs testifying at the Senate hearing mostly deflected criticism of their products. They argued they were already doing enough, or they tried to blame others, as Zuckerberg did when he said it was up to Apple, not Meta, to ensure age verification for using the Instagram app.
This is nonsense. And the only reason the tech platforms, whether Instagram or TikTok, are able to disregard the harm their platforms cause to young people is because they are funded almost entirely through advertising. In addition to Canada's blue chip corporations, among the biggest advertisers on social media are governments. While the federal government no longer advertises on Facebook, the Liberal party still does, as does the Conservative party and most provinces, including Doug Ford's Ontario government. Ottawa still runs ads on TikTok.
All Canadian governments and publicly funded agencies, as well as major corporations, must stop advertising on social media in order to put pressure on the platforms to truly change their practices to protect youth. Otherwise, they will continue to enable some pretty horrific outcomes.
For example, a recent Wall Street Journal investigation found that users of Instagram Reels who follow "young gymnasts, cheerleaders and other teen and preteen influencers" were "served jarring doses of salacious content," including sexualized images of young girls.
Last spring, another WSJ investigation found that the algorithms used by Facebook and Instagram help connect large networks of pedophiles. Those same social networks also provide fertile ground for child predators to find new victims.
There have been a number of high-profile cases of Canadian children committing suicide after being sexually extorted on social media, including a 12-year-old British Columbia boy who took his life in October, and Amanda Todd, who died in 2012 after being tormented by a middle-aged Dutch man who has since been convicted of possessing child pornography, child luring and other crimes.
TikTok, in particular, has come under increased scrutiny recently for failing to protect kids from harmful content and fears that it is being used by the Chinese Communist Party to collect personal information and spread discord throughout the West.
Research has found that TikTok and its rivals, Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts, which feed users a constant stream of brief, personalized videos, activate the reward centres in the brain, feeding kids a rush of dopamine that can lead to addiction There is also emerging evidence that these sites are reducing attention spans.
TikTok has been accused by numerous western countries of sharing its users' private information with the Chinese government and using the platform to spread misinformation and anti-western propaganda.
It's telling that the Chinese version of TikTok produces specialized content for teens, including science and art videos, and subjects children under the age of 14 to strict usage controls, which is not the case in the rest of the world.
Although these companies repeatedly claim that they are working to make their platforms safer for children, the reality is that they have consistently acted in bad faith. Last year, Ireland handed TikTok a hefty fine after finding that its default settings failed to protect the privacy of young people and that it used shady practices designed to encourage them to accept settings that weren't in their best interests.
The claim has also been contradicted by Arturo Béjar, a former Facebook engineer who says that he warned management after his 14-year-old daughter and her friends began getting "unwanted sexual advances" on Instagram, but that nothing was done. Béjar characterized the company's efforts to protect children as a "placebo for press and regulators."
What we need is for these companies to start taking this issue seriously - to do a better job of monitoring their networks for child predators, to make it easy for youth to report abuse and to design tools to give parents greater control over what their children do online.
A campaign led by the federal and provincial governments to refrain from advertising on these platforms until they take online harms seriously would go a long way (the federal government spent $40 million on Meta ads alone between 2017 and 2022). If big businesses followed suit, these companies would have little choice but to comply.
Sabrina Maddeaux: How Instagram, Liberals are failing to protect kids
Opinion: The social media bullying crisis children face

Document 382 of 500

FIRST READING: Poilievre pulling in nearly double the donations that Trudeau ever did

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-poilievre-pulling-nearly-double/docview/2921470112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Poilievre+pulling+in+nearly+double+the+donations+that+Trudeau+ever+did&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
The Conservative Party is not only breaking records for political fundraising, but they are now taking in an amount of money nearly double to what was being collected by Justin Trudeau at the height of his pre-election popularity.
In 2014 - the first full year that Trudeau was Liberal leader - the Liberal Party brought in $15.5 million, a 37 per cent increase over the year prior. In 2015 - the year Trudeau became prime minister - they'd up that to $16 million.
Even when adjusting for inflation, however, both totals are massively dwarfed by a record-breaking haul just announced by the Conservatives.
In 2024 dollars, the Liberals' 2015 fundraising total comes out to $20 million. According to a statement this week by the Conservatives, the party brought in $35.2 million in 2023, the first full year of the leadership of Pierre Poilievre.
The total "broke all fundraising records for any political party in Canadian history," claimed the Tories.
The money probably has some relation to the soaring poll numbers of the Conservatives. For more than a year now, the Conservatives have enjoyed a consistent 10-point lead over the Liberals, and every available model has them on track for a majority government.
But the $35 million figure is also a function of the fact that the Conservatives routinely beat the Liberals in terms of fundraising numbers. Even in the otherwise lacklustre year of 2020, for instance, Conservatives pulled in $20.7 million as opposed to $15.1 million for the incumbent Liberals.
As to why, the easy answer is that the Conservatives have their strongest bases of support in the parts of Canada that also enjoy the highest median incomes.
Alberta, for instance, is a near-impenetrable Tory stronghold, and in 2020 it enjoyed a median after-tax household income of $83,000, as compared to the national median of $73,000.
But while the Tories have traditionally been the party of higher-income voters, this is one of many electoral metrics that appear to have been turned on its head. In just the last few months, Tory support has particularly exploded among younger and lower-income demographics.
The Conservatives also traditionally spend more time and resources on scaring up donations. In 2022, for instance, they pulled in $23 million in donations, but spent $6.3 million on fundraising.
As to why the Liberals may not be trying as hard to match Conservative fundraising totals, one major reason might be that Canadian political parties are extremely limited in how much of it they can spend in an election cycle.
In the 2021 federal election, for instance, the three major political parties were all required to cap their spending at $30 million
Which is to say that while the Conservatives are bringing in record quantities of donated cash, they are in the awkward position of having to spend most of it before the next election is called.
Liberals 'deeply disturbed' by Alberta's new transgender rules, will 'talk' to Danielle Smith's government
Black-only swim times, Black-only lounges: The rise of race segregation on Canadian universities
ALBERTA TRANSGENDER REFORMS
It's been about six months since Canada first plunged into controversy surrounding the issue of gender identity in schools. On the one side was a series of new provincial policies under which teachers and schools administrators were required to immediately "affirm" a student's request to change their gender identity - and to keep this identity a secret from parents if asked. On the other, a massive groundswell of opposition from Canadians saying that if schools were going to be socially transitioning minors, they should at least be telling parents.
A new middle-ground may be in the offing, and it's come from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, of all people. Smith easily ranks among Canada's most populist right-wing figures (she was on stage with former Fox News host Tucker Carlson just a few days ago, for instance). But she also has a long history of being laissez-faire on LGBT issues, and was notably hesitant to weigh in at the height of last year's "parental rights" protests on gender identity.
Anyways, here are the basic details of Alberta's new in-school gender ID policy, which thus far appear to have equally disappointed both extremes of the debate:
No hormone treatments for children younger than 16. For those aged 16 and 17, treatment requires parental consent.
Parental consent required for social transitioning of children younger than 16. For those aged 16 and 17, parents will be notified, but consent isn't required.
A blanket ban on transgender surgeries for minors.
Pending regulations barring trans women from competing in female sport leagues. Said Smith, "There are obvious biological realities that give transgender female athletes a massive competitive advantage over women and girls."
Gender identity can be a hard thing to talk about, especially when you are involved. But this conversation is extremely important and parental involvement is critical. Kids need to know we love and support them.
My message to all Albertans:
- Danielle Smith (@ABDanielleSmith) January 31, 2024
IN OTHER NEWS
The job of a press photographer can be a lonely, thankless task. But every once in a while, you manage to get an image of Ontario Premier Doug Ford standing in front of the York Regional Police logo in just the right angle and lighting that it looks like he's wearing a crown.
The Trudeau government has announced that it is once again delaying the scheduled date at which it would become legal to prescribe assisted suicide for the mentally ill. On March 17, MAID provisions were supposed to be extended to those whose only underlying condition was a mental illness. But on Thursday, Health Minister Mark Holland announced that the date had been moved back - possibly forever. He said that a new date wouldn't be coming until 2027, well after the next federal election. (And as a side note, Holland made this impactful announcement while wearing a suit, a purple bowtie and a pair of red sneakers).
Health Minister Mark Holland announces he has tabled legislation delaying expansion of MAiD solely for mental illness until 2027 #cdnpoli #ParlCA
- Marco Vigliotti (@Marco_Vigliotti) February 1, 2024

Document 383 of 500

Ontario's economic future looks like old-fashioned pork for favoured businesses

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-economic-future-looks-like-old-fashioned/docview/2921313900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+economic+future+looks+like+old-fashioned+pork+for+favoured+businesses&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is running radio and television ads boasting about how it is building the economy of the future. What taxpayers might not realize is that this is a painstaking task, one that has to be done job by job, using government subsidies. It's the most old-fashioned approach possible.
When it comes to handouts by the Ontario government, the billions of dollars "invested" in a variety of electric-car battery plants get all the headlines. The argument is that the federal and provincial governments must throw a fortune at battery manufacturers because their plants are so eagerly sought by other jurisdictions.
Even if you accept that, why does the provincial government keep giving big cheques to little businesses that bring a handful of jobs? Consider some of the "investments" made on a magical money tour across the province.
On Jan. 17, Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli was in a place called Smithville in Niagara Region, supplying $6.7 million in tax dollars to three local manufacturers. The companies are in food packaging, pre-fab storage buildings and "cold, freezer and ambient storage solutions to food and beverage manufacturers." In total, the money will "create" 46 jobs.
Earlier that week, Premier Doug Ford showed up in London, Ont. at the announcement of a new gluten-free pasta plant. An Italian company is spending $33.6 million on the plant, which will create 42 jobs. Ontario taxpayers chipped in $1.5 million toward this worthy goal. The premier offered the thought that international companies were choosing Ontario "because of our competitive business environment."
While in London, Fedeli found time to dispense $2.3 million to a liquid silicone injection moulding company, to help create 26 jobs.
London seems to be an investment hot spot. A company there is building a new plant to produce medical-grade nitrile gloves, creating 135 of those "new, good-paying jobs" the Ford government likes so much. The provincial government is throwing $30 million at that one.
Also in January, a Kitchener medical technology company received $1.4 million to create 38 jobs. It has developed an AI-powered monitor that detects post-operative gastrointestinal leaks. The provincial money will help build an automated production line.
In Windsor, the government got a bargain. A Korean electric-vehicle parts manufacturing company is building a $35 million plant, which will create 144 new jobs. That only required $1.5 million from the taxpayers.
It takes more than one government to build Ontario's economy. Naturally, the Ford government works with its friends in the federal Liberal government. In mid-January, the two governments announced $8 million to help the dairy industry modernize its technology. One might have thought that Canada's non-competitive supply management regime would have been assistance enough. Cow, goat, sheep and buffalo milk producers are eligible for help. Where would Ontario be without its buffalo milk industry? The gravy train has not stopped. This week Graydon Smith, minister of natural resources, was in Thunder Bay to announce $9.4 million for 14 "research, innovation and modernization" projects. Of that, $5 million will go to Thunder Bay Pulp and Paper Mill for electrical and equipment upgrading.
That should please the mill's new owners Atlas Holdings, an American conglomerate with $16 billion in annual revenue.
What change does this spending create? Would any of these projects not have gone ahead without a government handout? These cheques have become the government version of giving a houseplant to welcome a new neighbour.
Perhaps the little job announcements provide a useful distraction from the big economic picture, which is not overly encouraging. In 2022, Ontario's gross domestic product exceeded $1 trillion for the first time and real economic growth was up 3.6 per cent. That sounds good until one considers Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta all had higher economic growth rates than Ontario's. B.C. grew at the same pace as Ontario.
What matters is not so much the total size of the economy, but the number of people it must support. Ontario grew by nearly 450,000 people in 2022. That means the economic pie must be cut into more slices.
GDP per capita is not only a way to measure standard of living, it is also a better indicator of how provinces'economies are doing compared to each other. By that measure, Ontario is in fifth place, trailing B.C., Alberta, Saskatchewan and Newfoundland and Labrador.
The economic picture is even more discouraging when Ontario and other provinces are compared to American states.
Economist Trevor Tombe did an analysis last year that showed Ontario ranked 51st on the list of U.S. and Canadian jurisdictions, on a par with Alabama. Don't expect to hear that in an Ontario government ad any time soon.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail. com

Document 384 of 500

Reason beats gender hysteria; Smith's plan is 'a ... lot of common sense'

Author: Sarkonak, Jamie

Publication info: Sarkonak, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reason-beats-gender-hysteria-smiths-plan-is-lot/docview/2921313750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Reason+beats+gender+hysteria%3B+Smith%27s+plan+is+%27a+...+lot+of+common+sense%27&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Sarkonak%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Danielle Smith isn't taking the easy route in Alberta when it comes to gender policy, but she's taking the right one. On Wednesday, she announced that the province will introduce age restrictions on gender-hormone treatments and surgeries and require schools to obtain parental consent to socially transition students aged 15 and under. Sex-education will become opt-in and sex-based sport protections will be explored, as well. The changes will come about through a mix of policy and law, advanced likely around the fall, according to a statement from the premier's office.
It's a whole lot of common sense, but you can expect Smith to face a slew of lawfare from state-supported academics and non-profits regardless.
Smith's position on surgeries isn't radical. "Gender-affirming bottom surgery," is already prohibited for minors in Canada. Mastectomies for youth are permitted in Canada, the youngest recipient being 14, but they are uncommon - and delaying to 18 isn't really all that much. It's a common convention in our society to save life-changing decisions for adulthood, anyway.
As for blocking cross-sex hormone treatment and puberty suppression, Alberta is the first in Canada to cut off access for 15-year-olds and under. But the province is behind European states like Sweden in doing so. The United Kingdom, meanwhile, added intense restrictions on such treatments for minors last year.
Nor is it radical to require a school to get parental consent to socially transition a young child. Consent is required for administering medication and going on school trips. An extreme psychological intervention that puts a kid on the path to genital surgeries shouldn't be any different.
The Alberta restrictions won't satisfy everyone - that goes for both sides - but they strike an incredibly reasonable balance between preserving the independence of older teens with the need to protect impressionable young children from schools willing to perform social transitions behind the backs of parents (as happened in a Calgary Grade 6 class in 2022-23).
No, they don't enable abuse, as other critics might argue, because Alberta child protection law still applies to every kid in the province regardless of identity. And no, Smith hasn't shaken legal precedent by creating novel parental rights: parental rights have always existed in common law, and are codified in many Canadian statutes already (particularly in the areas of adoption, welfare and education), no matter what some legal scholars tell you.
And no, Smith doesn't reject gender ideology outright. The premier, for the most part, is just codifying the norm. She is also adding guardrails where necessary, though, because some teachers and some doctors, bolstered by institutional support of political leaders who have bought into a movement of total gender deconstruction, are going far beyond acceptable bounds.
Look, I don't agree with everything Smith has committed to. I'm of the view that irreversible, fertility-altering treatments for minors - hormones and surgeries - should be prohibited for all minors, including 17-yearolds.
Overall, though, it's a win.
It's already far outside the comfort zone of Smith, a libertarian at heart and a progressive on gender and sexuality. This is where today's battle line has been drawn, and conservatives should be ready to defend it.
Battle line re-draws are indeed possible. Just two years ago, the federal conservatives (well, the ones who were physically in Ottawa at the time) unanimously caved to legislation that banned "conversion therapy" for transgender-identifying children, but was silent on "conversion therapy" for regular, non-trans children. Back then, there wasn't room for debate. Today, in Alberta, there is. The momentum is on the side of conservatives.
In the coming days and months, Smith faces an opponent that was never going to be reasonable: the gender ideologues, a group of loud but organized voices that have been amplified thanks to a mountain of state funding. They have the support of the progressive political sphere: see Edmonton Liberal MP Randy Boissonnault immediately called Smith's announcement "dangerous"; Janis Irwin of the Alberta NDP said she was "heartbroken."
They demand total adherence to an ideology that only became popular in the last decade. Spurned by the democratically elected legislature, they will take the fight to the courts, as they've done in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick. It's like clockwork now: province draws reasonable line in sand, activists cry wolf, activists launch legal challenge, province invokes notwithstanding clause ... you get the idea.
The assault on Alberta's policy is being led by Egale Canada, which is 67 per cent taxpayer-funded (with much of that coming from the federal government), and the Skipping Stone Foundation, which obtains 38 per cent of its revenue from taxpayers. The movement doesn't have the genuine support of the public - 78 per cent of Canadians believe parents should at least be informed of gender transitions in schools - but instead relies on ideological allies in government to funnel it money to exact punitive lawfare against unruly provinces.
Gender ideologues like Fae Johnstone, whose firm has welcomed more than $1 million in federal funding under the current government, complain of being "outgunned," but that's not exactly accurate. Zealous gender advocates have developed litigation skill and a network of on-demand "experts" thanks to the help of years of sustained public funding for their cause.
Conservatives have none of that. The consequences of this were seen when Saskatchewan tried to fend off an injunction to halt its gender policy: the government was unprepared, and didn't bring much for evidence. Government lawyers, who are often left-liberal in belief themselves and therefore not inclined to do the best work at the direction of a conservative minister, are left to do the job of arguing the novel but evidence-based position that sex changes for kids are not OK.
Smith has the advantage on tone: she's calm, collected and firm in the face of hysteria and hyperbole.
But to keep the momentum in her favour, she'll need to have experts on speed dial, a mountain of evidence and an effective legal team committed to defending what's right.
If any province is going to pull it off, it's Alberta.
National Post

Document 388 of 500

Randall Denley: Ontario's economic future looks like old-fashioned pork for favoured businesses

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-ontarios-economic-future-looks/docview/2921219328/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Ontario%27s+economic+future+looks+like+old-fashioned+pork+for+favoured+businesses&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is running radio and television ads boasting about how it is building the economy of the future. What taxpayers might not realize is that this is a painstaking task, one that has to be done job by job, using government subsidies. It's the most old-fashioned approach possible.
When it comes to handouts by the Ontario government, the billions of dollars "invested" in a variety of electric-car battery plants get all the headlines. The argument is that the federal and provincial governments must throw a fortune at battery manufacturers because their plants are so eagerly sought by other jurisdictions.
Even if you accept that, why does the provincial government keep giving big cheques to little businesses that bring a handful of jobs?
Consider some of the "investments" made on a magical money tour across the province.
On Jan. 17, Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli was in a place called Smithville in Niagara Region, supplying $6.7 million in tax dollars to three local manufacturers. The companies are in food packaging, pre-fab storage buildings and "cold, freezer and ambient storage solutions to food and beverage manufacturers." In total, the money will "create" 46 jobs.
Earlier that week, Premier Doug Ford showed up in London, Ont. at the announcement of a new gluten-free pasta plant. An Italian company is spending $33.6 million on the plant, which will create 42 jobs. Ontario taxpayers chipped in $1.5 million toward this worthy goal. The premier offered the thought that international companies were choosing Ontario "because of our competitive business environment."
While in London, Fedeli found time to dispense $2.3 million to a liquid silicone injection molding company, to help create 26 jobs.
London seems to be an investment hotspot. A company there is building a new plant to produce medical-grade nitrile gloves, creating 135 of those "new, good-paying jobs" the Ford government likes so much. The provincial government is throwing $30 million at that one.
Also in January, a Kitchener medical technology company received $1.4 million to create 38 jobs. It has developed an AI-powered monitor that detects post-operative gastrointestinal leaks. The provincial money will help build an automated production line.
In Windsor, the government got a bargain. A Korean electric-vehicle parts manufacturing company is building a $35 million plant, which will create 144 new jobs. That only required $1.5 million from the taxpayers.
Frank Stronach: Canada starting to look neo-feudal as rich-poor gulf widens
Randall Denley: Ontario is not prepared for a cap on international students
It takes more than one government to build Ontario's economy. Naturally, the Ford government works with its friends in the federal Liberal government. In mid-January, the two governments announced $8 million to help the dairy industry modernize its technology. One might have thought that Canada's non-competitive supply management regime would have been assistance enough. Cow, goat, sheep and buffalo milk producers are eligible for help. Where would Ontario be without its buffalo milk industry?
The gravy train has not stopped. This week Graydon Smith, minister of natural resources, was in Thunder Bay to announce $9.4 million for 14 "research, innovation and modernization" projects. Of that, $5 million will go to Thunder Bay Pulp and Paper Mill for electrical and equipment upgrading. That should please the mill's new owners Atlas Holdings, an American conglomerate with $16 billion in annual revenue.
What change does this spending create? Would any of these projects not have gone ahead without a government handout? These cheques have become the government version of giving a houseplant to welcome a new neighbour.
Perhaps the little job announcements provide a useful distraction from the big economic picture, which is not overly encouraging. In 2022, Ontario's gross domestic product exceeded $1 trillion for the first time and real economic growth was up 3.6 per cent. That sounds good until one considers Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta all had higher economic growth rates than Ontario's. B.C. grew at the same pace as Ontario.
What matters is not so much the total size of the economy, but the number of people it must support. Ontario grew by nearly 450,000 people in 2022. That means the economic pie must be cut into more slices.
GDP per capita is not only a way to measure standard of living, it is also a better indicator of how provinces' economies are doing compared to each other. By that measure, Ontario is in fifth place, trailing B.C., Alberta, Saskatchewan and Newfoundland and Labrador.
The economic picture is even more discouraging when Ontario and other provinces are compared to American states. Economist Trevor Tombe did an analysis last year that showed Ontario ranked 51(st) on the list of U.S. and Canadian jurisdictions, on a par with Alabama. Don't expect to hear that in an Ontario government ad any time soon.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 389 of 500

Jamie Sarkonak: Danielle Smith brings reason in the face of hysterical gender activists

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jamie-sarkonak-danielle-smith-brings-reason-face/docview/2921212987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jamie+Sarkonak%3A+Danielle+Smith+brings+reason+in+the+face+of+hysterical+gender+activists&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Jamie Sarkonak: Pronoun disclosures - a growing tool of the DEI bureaucracy

Ontario to receive $121M federal funding boost to curb auto thefts, gang violence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-receive-121m-federal-funding-boost-curb/docview/2920723467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario+to+receive+%24121M+federal+funding+boost+to+curb+auto+thefts%2C+gang+violence&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford vowed to use "every single tool" available to put an end to the surge in auto thefts and carjackings across the province during a press conference on Wednesday.
Speaking alongside law enforcement officials and government representatives, including Minister of Public Safety Dominic LeBlanc, it was announced the federal government would be providing $121 million over five years for police in Ontario to tackle the issue, along with gun and gang violence.
"People are in fear," said Ford. "I've talked to friends that are sleeping with baseball bats beside their bed because someone's going to kick the door in and put a gun to their head and say hand over your keys," Ford said while speaking in Aurora, Ont.
His comments arrive just over a week away from a planned national summit for combating auto theft that will include municipal and provincial police forces, border agents, auto industry executives and politicians.
Canada has become a very scary place to own a vehicle right now
Stolen Canadian cars are ending up in Africa and auto industry says not enough is being done to stop it
Planned for Feb. 8, the summit aims to identify both short and long-term solutions to a problem that has resulted in one car being stolen across Canada every six minutes
According to the not-for-profit insurance industry group Équité Association, vehicle thefts in Canada soared in 2022, with both Quebec and Ontario seeing spikes of around 50 per cent, while Atlantic Canada saw an increase of more than 34 per cent.
Last June, a report from the Canadian Finance and Leasing Association found that Toronto saw a 300 per cent increase in vehicle thefts from 2015 to 2022, with the problem growing progressively worse each year. In 2022, more than 9,600 vehicles were stolen in Toronto alone.
"Our government is making the necessary investments to ensure that our police services have the tools and the resources they need," Ford said, before highlighting Canada's bail reforms under Bill C-48, which made several amendments to the Criminal Code.
The amendments are intended to "strengthen Canada's bail system, particularly in relation to repeat violent offending and offences involving firearms or other weapons," according to the federal government.
Justice minister and attorney general of Canada, Arif Virani, also spoke on Wednesday and highlighted the "multisectorial features" of vehicle thefts.
Following a meeting with regional police forces in the GTA, Virani said there is a need to address the issue comprehensively
"It's about the responsibility of the auto industry. It's about insurance pieces. It's about looking at the ports. It's about looking at the CBSA. It's about, yes, looking at the Criminal Code for which I'm responsible, and I'm very open to looking at the Criminal Code," he said.
He also noted the influence of organized crime and large institutions that "are actually behind a lot of this theft."
Virani added that Bill C-59, which is currently before the House of Commons, has provisions that deal with money laundering and is part of a broader strategy to tackle organized crime.
"We'd love to see the same type of co-operation we had on bail reform applied to that bill so we can get those anti-money laundering provisions across the finish line," he said.
York Regional Police Chief Jim MacSween added that a provincial carjacking task force is working in collaboration with a number of the GTA police services as well as the Ontario Provincial Police to address auto thefts.
"It's having a direct impact across the province on public safety, and it's definitely having an impact here in York Region," he said, before noting that officer safety is also an issue.
"Some of these bandits are driving intentionally at our officers who are trying to make the arrest. They're young people. They're out there brandishing firearms, doing home invasions, and it's just completely unacceptable to any of us," he said. "But we cannot do this alone. We need collaboration and we need all partners and all sectors at the table."
Last May, the Ontario government announced it had allocated $51 million over three years to assist police in identifying and dismantling the criminal networks behind auto theft rings.
"People shouldn't have to live in fear of their cars being stolen," Ford said Wednesday. "This landmark deal between Canada and Ontario is going to enhance our efforts to help tackle gun crime and gang violence in our communities."

Gwyn Morgan: The runaway cost of government construction projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gwyn-morgan-runaway-cost-government-construction/docview/2920609336/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Gwyn+Morgan%3A+The+runaway+cost+of+government+construction+projects&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Not so long ago, a $10-million government infrastructure project was regarded as a significant expenditure. But now $10 million doesn't come close to funding projects as simple as a firehall or new police station. Here in the Victoria region, a new firehall in the District of Saanich originally budgeted at $27 million has jumped to $45 million. In the Langford District the estimated cost of a new RCMP building is an incomprehensible $82 million. Just north of Victoria, the cost of what was to be a simple flyover eliminating a dangerous left turn across the busy Patricia Bay Highway has spiked from its original estimate of $44 million to $77 million.
These cost increases seem big to us here on Fantasy Island, but they are a rounding error in mega-city Toronto. The Ontario Line, a 15.6-kilometre transit line connecting the Science Centre to Ontario Place, was budgeted at $10.9 billion when first announced in 2019. A series of updates has seen the cost balloon to an estimated $19 billion, an increase of more than 70 per cent.
These are just a few examples of municipal and provincial cost overruns. No doubt the total of overruns for all projects is immensely higher. Who is the project mismanagement grand champion? You're not likely to be surprised.
Statistics Canada data for 2021, the latest available, show that federal capital infrastructure project expenditures totaled $108 billion. Given that Ottawa bureaucrats are famous for mismanagement of virtually every project (e.g., the ArriveCAN app) does anyone doubt that many of those billions went to overruns resulting from a combination of sloppy design specifications and project mismanagement? And now the Trudeau government has added costly "social justice" obligations to its projects, including gender diversity, participation by ethnic minorities and disabled persons, as well as other elements of woke ideology.
Consider "The Great Helicopter Hangar Saga," a story I have from sources I know to be completely reliable.
For many years, the Pacific Maritime Helicopter Squadron Hangar had been located adjacent to the Victoria Airport. In 2004, the Department of National Defence (DND) announced the award of a $1.8-billion contract for 28 Sikorsky CH148 Cyclone helicopters. A new hangar was required. DND engineers designed one that would meet their needs at an estimated cost of roughly $18 million. Then they handed the project to Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC). That's when the project entered an eerie part of the space-time continuum resembling the old science fiction TV series The Twilight Zone.
PWGSC decided the hangar needed to "sustain operations" in the event of a magnitude 8.0 earthquake, an incomprehensible decision for several reasons. First, that's seven times stronger than the most severe earthquake ever recorded on Vancouver Island. Second, severity of earthquake damage depends on the subsurface. Buildings sitting on a soil and gravel subsurface suffer much more damage than those resting on bedrock because the soft material changes from behaving like a solid to behaving like a thick liquid, further amplifying the shaking. But the hangar location was solid bedrock.
But the PWGSC technocrats were oblivious to those facts. Steel piles were driven into the bedrock at a cost of $8 million and a two-year delay. Cross-bracing of the interior wall openings added more millions.
When actual construction finally began, government bureaucrats specified more office space, locker and "administrative security" facilities than the DND had considered necessary, adding more costs. Then came the woke costs. In determining the contract award, PWGSC required First Nations involvement both as subcontractors and in the workforce, extensive gender diversity and complete disabled access. The elevator had to have braille and voice recognition along with full wheelchair accessibility. Members of the military joked that it must be for the "blind and disabled pilots." By the time the new hangar was handed back to the military, the cost of the DND's $18-million hangar had skyrocketed to a staggering $155 million.
In July 2019, Philip Cross published an insightful column in FP Comment entitled "Why governments keep screwing up major infrastructure projects." He wrote: "Prominent studies of domestic and international public infrastructure projects found cost overruns averaged between 45 and 86 per cent." How come? "Public projects suffer from a lack of accountability. Governments evaluate projects not according to the performance-based criteria of the private sector, but by their conformity to rules and processes."
Canada's decline into irrelevance
Canadians are overtaxed and underserved
Refurbishing nuclear station right decision for Ontario
This evidence of dysfunctional project mismanagement comes at a time when public infrastructure spending is at record levels, dominated by the federal government's $33-billion 2023-24 infrastructure project budget. It's sure to be made even more dysfunctional and costly by the Liberals' implementation of woke ideology. When will Canadians wake up and finally dismiss a government that defies the values of honesty and openness our country was built on?
Gwyn Morgan is a retired business leader who has been a director of five global corporations.

Document 397 of 500

Jamie Sarkonak: Conservatives need to take back the Senate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jamie-sarkonak-conservatives-need-take-back/docview/2920601056/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jamie+Sarkonak%3A+Conservatives+need+to+take+back+the+Senate&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the 80th time, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has done the prime ministerial duty of appointing a senator. This time, the lucky recipient is Mohammad Al Zaibak, a businessman and donor of around $80,000 to the federal Liberals. He will sit in the upper house of government as an "independent," but it's pretty clear what's going on here.
It's not the most disappointing thing in the world. Senate appointments are political, no matter how many (Non-)Independent Advisory Boards inform senator selection under Trudeau. If there's anything the next government needs to do differently though, it's to drop Trudeau's facade of false non-partisanship and abandon the even worse Senatorial strategy of former prime minister Stephen Harper.
In 2015, Harper did the equivalent of pulling the goalie and ordering his team to stand aside, or in classical terms, leaving the border with Persia ungarrisoned. After making 59 appointments over nine years, he left office with one-fifth of the Senate empty and a vague hope of reform. Guess what happened next? The Senate filled up with Liberals. At 40 seats of 105, the "Independent Senators Group" holds a plurality in the upper house, and has voted with the government the vast majority of the time; there's also the post-Liberal "Progressive Senate Group" which is at 14. That's a Liberal majority, folks.
The Senate is key because all legislation must pass through it to become law, but its actual use comes down to its function as a common sense filter. There are a few reasons for this: senators aren't trying to hold on for dear life (re-election) because their appointment lasts until age 75; senators ideally are people with expertise in an area useful for legislating, but perhaps don't have campaign personalities; senators have their political alignments, but aren't bound by the House of Commons whip. They can think a bit more freely.
The result is actually refreshing. While the current House of Commons tends to rush shoddy legislation through as a function of the NDP's propping-up of the Liberal government, like the Online Streaming Act (or Bill C-11), the Senate is a place of measured revision.
As keeper of the Reform Party flame, Harper hoped to transform the Senate into a democratically-elected body with members limited to nine-year terms. He didn't succeed, and, sticking to his principles, refused to play the game entirely. He gave up.
Trudeau's approach to Senate reform has been the same as his approach to the judiciary: give the appointment process for each the illusion of independence. Create a "third-party" advisory panel, stack it with ideological allies, tack on a "diversity" mandate to flip women and minorities to the top of the deck and, voilÃ , the veneer of independence is complete. It's the same old, though, and the Conservatives would do well to do it the old-fashioned way.
Jamie Sarkonak: Trudeau's 'nonpartisan' Supreme Court advisory panel skews left
Jamie Sarkonak: Liberals barely pretend Supreme Court nomination is independent
The solution to Conservative skepticism should be to appoint more, appoint better and ensure those appointees have the resources to do the job properly. If only he took the advice from this paper's editorial board before he left office: "Prime ministers don't appoint hacks, donors, bagmen, failed candidates and television personalities who don't actually live in Prince Edward Island by accident," went a 2014 editorial.
"They have the wherewithal, all on their own, to decide who might be truly qualified to provide the House of Commons with sober second thought. If Mr. Harper really wants a better Senate, he can start doing that today simply by making better appointments, thus setting the bar higher for his successor."
Harper kind of had the right idea with Senator Patrick Brazeau: get someone in there who can stay there long-term. Appointed at age 34, Brazeau had promise in that department. There was only one flaw: Brazeau himself is a disaster. He's known for: missing child support payments, being arrested for domestic violence, pleading guilty to domestic assault, calling reporters "b-tch" and "racist" for no good reason. You get the idea. Not a great poster child for appointing younger.
The Conservatives have a lot to gain by taking advantage of the upper house next time around. They can appoint promising individuals who are mid-career as opposed to late-career; people who would make decent legislators but wouldn't stand a chance in the House of Commons; people who can stick around long-term and hold down the fort as governments come and go.
Keep in mind that at Confederation, the average age of all senators was 55. In 2023, it was about 66. The 2015 House of Commons, meanwhile, was roughly 50. Age-wise, the Senate of old was actually kind of young.
They also have a shot at making the Senate more robust, by funding it better and ensuring it is financially attractive for quality candidates. Right now, a single senator has an office budget of $222,000, which is supposed to pay for two to four staffers (on likely sub-private sector salaries), some of whom are supposed to live in the cost-of-living hell that is Ottawa. Also remember that the salary of a senator of $170,000 per year - higher than that of the average Canadian, but still lower than the pay of a high-calibre lawyer, executive or judge.
The reality is, a lot of quality, mid-career people who would serve the conservative movement well in the Senate can make more in the private sector. That goes for entry-level staffers, as well as senators themselves.
It's understandable to feel some tightened cynicism at the news of yet another Trudeau appointment of an obvious ally, but it's a waste of energy. What conservatives should be doing is carefully curating a list of appointees to ensure no time is wasted when the vacancies ultimately become their responsibility to fill.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Careers

Company / organization: Name: Senate-US; NAICS: 921120

Title: Jamie Sarkonak: Conservatives need to take back the Senate

Document 398 of 500

Matthew Lau: Canadians are overtaxed and underserved

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/matthew-lau-canadians-are-overtaxed-underserved/docview/2920447785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Matthew+Lau%3A+Canadians+are+overtaxed+and+underserved&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A colleague lamented last week, on receiving his annual bonus, that he saw only 42 per cent of it. The other 58 per cent, though it departed his employer's bank account, never reached his. Messrs. Trudeau and Ford intercepted it in the form of income taxes, provincial health taxes, and employment insurance and Canada Pension Plan premiums (this last of which the Trudeau government made more expensive for the fifth year in a row). Even the 42 per cent that finally did reach his bank account, my colleague groaned, is not all his. When he earns interest on that cash, Messrs. Trudeau and Ford take another cut of it, and when he withdraws the money and spends it, he's taxed yet again. We live in a country, he concluded, that is severely overtaxed.
What do we get for all the taxes we pay? Two core government functions are public safety and national defence, yet violent crime is rising and the military is severely underfunded There are over 10,000 job vacancies in the Canadian Armed Forces, where morale is said to be the lowest in recent memory. The commander of the Royal Canadian Navy says it "faces some very serious challenges right now that could mean we fail to meet our force posture and readiness commitments in 2024 and beyond" and that "the air force and the army are facing similar challenges."
Money not going to national defence is instead being poured into health care, but with little to show for it. The Fraser Institute's annual national survey of physicians across 12 specialties finds the median wait time to receive medical treatment after referral by a general practitioner increased last year to 27.7 weeks. This delay of over half a year to receive medical treatment is the highest in the survey's history and triple the 9.3-week median wait time 30 years ago. Compared to other developed countries with universal health-care systems, Canadians pay more and get less - the predictable result of government policy that aggressively shuts out the private sector.
Public schools are also a catastrophic shambles that keep costing more The 2022 PISA results (for Programme for International Student Assessment), which measure student achievement among 15-year-olds in developed countries across the world, found the average math score in Ontario fell to 495, down from 513 in 2018. A 20-point decline represents roughly a year's worth of schooling, which means Ontario's 15-year-olds are about a year behind where they were four years ago. For years, PISA results have documented the declining quality of education, not only in Ontario, but across Canada, in math, reading, and science. The 2022 PISA scores for Canada as a whole were 497 in math, down from 512 in 2018 and from 532 in 2003. Test scores in reading and science, though their trend is not quite as disastrous as in math, are also marching alarmingly downward.
In addition to more expensive health care and schooling, the tax beating that Canadians are forced to endure has gone to fund Ottawa's attempted takeover of the country's childcare sector, with billions in new annual spending, expanded government control and - again - disastrous outcomes. Statistics Canada data show there were actually 117,900 fewer children in childcare in 2023 than in 2019. And despite lower childcare attendance, in every province a greater percentage of families using such care reported they had difficulty finding it.
The federal climate program is also increasing the tax assault. A spokesperson for Chrystia Freeland recently boasted "our government is making over $120 billion in historic investments to grow Canada's clean economy." Two weeks ago in Davos, the finance minister herself said the government is determined "that decarbonization for us will mean more jobs, more growth." But this growth has yet to materialize, as in addition to childcare and climate, every one of the Trudeau government's central planning initiatives has been a disaster. Canada is in a years-long crisis of economic stagnation When it comes to business investment, "the numbers are really awful," as the C.D. Howe Institute's William Robson bluntly puts it.
Higher education would benefit from lower subsidies
Budget advice for Trudeau Liberals: Cut spending!
'Policymaker of the year' Guilbeault is killing economic growth
Economic stagnation and declining productivity and investment should not be a surprise. What else would we expect in a country where, after putting in a year of hard work to earn a performance bonus, people see 58 per cent of it confiscated before it reaches their bank accounts and even more taken away when they try to spend the 42 per cent that does make it, all in aid of providing government services that are deteriorating markedly?
Bookmark our website and support our journalism: Don't miss the business news you need to know - add financialpost.com to your bookmarks and sign up for our newsletters here
