Document 419 of 500

Changing gears; Electric vehicles; Canada wants to be an EV nation.are consumers along for the ride?

Author: Coulton, Marisa

Publication info: Coulton, Marisa. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/changing-gears-electric-vehicles-canada-wants-be/docview/2918833565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Changing+gears%3B+Electric+vehicles%3B+Canada+wants+to+be+an+EV+nation.are+consumers+along+for+the+ride%3F&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=Coulton%2C+Marisa&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government wants electric vehicles to be the obvious choice - and eventually the only choice - for Canadian drivers, but so far that doesn't appear to be the case.
At Raman Grewal's Kia and Subaru dealership in Whitehorse, Yukon, for example, there is considerable demand for EV and plug-in hybrid vehicles, but many of his customers still have "range anxiety." That means they're worried the battery will die before they can find a charging station, and that they'll be left stranded on the Alaska Highway between B.C. and the Yukon.
"Currently, EVs work if you are commuting in around (the) 200 kilometres per day range," Grewal wrote in an email. "(Especially) in our cold climate in the north. Some customers are living at greater distances or are living off grid without hydro."
In urban centres, which currently have lower rates of EV adoption than the surrounding suburbs, there are other issues, said Jimmy Hearn, author of a recent report by S&P Global that found zero-emission vehicles accounted for a record 13.3 per cent of new vehicles purchased in the third quarter of 2023.
Urbanites tend to rely on street parking, where it is harder to find EV charging stations, Hearn said, whereas suburbanites can install charging stations at their homes.
Even in Montreal, which is home to more than 2,000 public charging station ports, such concerns are front of mind, according to Norman John Hébert, president and chief operating officer of Groupe Park Avenue, which operates a Mercedes-Benz dealership in the city that sells EVs.
Wait times can be an issue, too. And then there's the sticker price.
"Generally, our customers find EV prices to be too high compared with an equivalent format of ICE (internal combustion engine) vehicle," Hébert said in an email.
The average sticker price of an EV is $73,500, according to Canadian Black Book, around $6,000 more than a new gas powered car, which tops $67,000 according to a recent price index report by AutoTrader, an online car marketplace.
Though S&P forecasts EV market share will only continue to grow, reaching 18.4 per cent by the end of 2024, there are signs consumer demand is not keeping up with soaring expectations.
In recent months, some automakers have scaled back EV production targets, citing sluggish sales and faltering demand.
Ford Motor Co. recently announced it would reduce production of its F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck because demand was lower than expected. General Motors Co. announced that it, too, would slow plans for electric truck production, delaying the launch of a Detroit-based factory to 2025.
As Ottawa embarks on its ambitious mandate of having 100 per cent of all new car sales be electric by 2035 - a target that goes hand in hand with tens of billions of dollars in subsidies for EV supply chain projects - getting consumers on board is seen as critical by many in the industry. If they can't be convinced EVs are the best option, reaching those goals will be an uphill climb.
"We have to make sure the consumer is coming along at the pace required to support this transition," said Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturer's Association.
To ensure there is adequate demand for EVs, the government must enhance the grid, provide consumers with incentives, keep investment flowing into the North American EV supply chain, and build more charging infrastructure, he said.
"Achieving these very aggressive sales targets requires a 'Team Canada'approach to boosting EV sales across Canada," he said.
Without it, governments could be forced to rely more heavily on the costly subsidies that continue to play a major role in convincing consumers to buy EVs.
People who buy EVs from BMW Aurora, a dealership north of Toronto, generally qualify for federal incentives of up to $5,000, said Thomas An, assistant sales manager.
At Grewal's dealership in Whitehorse, meanwhile, customers can take advantage of the federal government rebate of around $5,000 on purchases and leases, plus a provincial rebate of up to $5,000 - a perk that is unavailable in Ontario because that province's $14,000 rebate was eliminated by Doug Ford when he became premier in 2018.
See EV on FP7 Some of the variance in demand for EVs across dealerships - they account for 20 per cent of car sales for An, but 43.5 per cent for Grewal - is likely due to the differences in subsidies, said S&P's Hearn, noting that Quebec and B.C., which lead the pack in terms of EV sales, also both have strong EV incentives.
In the third quarter of 2023, EV sales made up around 26.4 per cent of all new vehicle registrations in B.C., where consumers can get up to a $4,000 rebate on a new EV, and 22.8 per cent of sales in Quebec, where consumers can get an even higher rebate of $7,000.
"If you compare Toronto's ZEV (zero-emission vehicle) growth to Montreal and Vancouver, Toronto ... is trailing behind those two smaller cities," said Hearn.
There are around 8,800 EVs in Toronto, compared to 10,100 in Vancouver and 12,100 in Montreal, likely because Ontario lacks a provincial EV incentive, notes the S&P report.
"From what I see in the data, the vehicles that people are registering in British Columbia and Quebec are the vehicles that meet the provincial ZEV initiatives and rebates as well as the federal rebates," Hearn said.
Customers are stacking the incentives in order to get the most bang for their buck.
While Bibra said recent news coverage suggests EV uptake at some dealerships has slowed, the big picture data says otherwise.
"When we look at just the hard facts, the sales continue to increase," she said. "It's really important to look at what's happening as an overarching trend."
Indeed, new ZEV sales fluctuate year to year, but are increasing on the whole: they totalled 55,600 countrywide, up from just 5,200 in the same quarter in 2017.
At the national level, Canada has made good progress in securing investments in the EV supply chain, part of its bid to become an EV superpower, said Kingston.
The signature investments so far include Volkswagen AG's plans to build a battery plant in St. Thomas, Ont., supported by $13 billion in government funding and Stellantis NV-LG Energy Solution Ltd.'s battery plant in Windsor, Ont., supported by $15 billion in funding.
Others include Ford's plan to retool its factory in Oakville to assemble EVs and battery packs, as well as a $35-million investment by Bobaek America Inc., a Korean electric vehicle parts manufacturer, toward the construction of an EV factory in Windsor.
But Kingston and a number of dealerships pointed to the need for better infrastructure as a key stumbling block to widespread adoption.
"The government should definitely implement more EV charging infrastructures in the cities," said An.
Grewal, too, called for better charging infrastructure on the Alaska Highway.
Though most customers appreciate the savings on gas, which amount to around $3,000 a year, EVs are still only the favoured option for a particular subset of consumers: those commuting short distances, those who are willing to wait several months to over a year for their car, and those who are "innovators that are interested in new technology," said Hébert.
Many of his customers seem willing to pay for EVs that have found a "sweet spot" for pricing, such as the Audi Q4 e-Tron, which retails for $64,000, the BMW i4 which retails for between $55,000 to $80,000, and the Volvo XC40 Recharge, which starts at around $60,000.
But it's not enough to cater to Canadians with means, said Bibra; the federal government needs to work harder to reach "mainstream" Canadians who fall into the low-to-middle income brackets. Simply mandating that 100 per cent of car sales be electric by 2035 does not ensure the goal will be met, said Kingston.
"I'd like to see a more co-ordinated approach led by the federal government but with other levels of government participating - provinces and municipalities - on a very well-thought-through plan to achieve the targets that the government has established," Kingston said. "We can't do this on our own."
mcoulton@postmedia.com

Document 424 of 500

Trudeau wants four more years. But what for?

Author: Thomson, Stuart

Publication info: Thomson, Stuart. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-wants-four-more-years-what/docview/2918721381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trudeau+wants+four+more+years.+But+what+for%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Thomson%2C+Stuart&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has made one thing clear: he's not going anywhere.
Faced with plummeting poll numbers and a surging Conservative Opposition, Trudeau has said in numerous recent interviews that there is too much unfinished business for him to walk away now. He has also painted Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as a threat to the entire Liberal project and vowed not to run away from an electoral showdown with the man who would dismantle his government's work.
"You know me well enough, you know what I believe ... do you actually think I could walk away from this fight right now?" said an animated Trudeau in a holiday interview with his friend and former radio host Terry DiMonte.
But what will inspire seemingly exhausted Liberals to endure another few sittings of the House of Commons and a gruelling election campaign against a fierce opponent?
It could be that Poilievre himself will be enough motivation for Liberal partisans who are inclined to agree with Trudeau that Poilievre will dismantle their progressive agenda. And Poilievre would likely agree with the prime minister, too.
The Opposition leader has vowed to cancel some of the government's most cherished accomplishments, like the federal carbon tax, and to defund the CBC, which still attracts most of its remaining support from the Canadian left.
But some Liberals warn that asking for votes just to protect Liberal polices or painting Poilievre as a threat to democracy likely won't be enough.
Trudeau attacks Conservatives, from Poilievre to MPs and candidates, in address to Liberal caucus
Justin Trudeau laughs off suggestions he'll take a 'walk in the snow'
"We watched political parties on the opposition benches think that they would be successful in elections trying to demonize Stephen Harper or tell Canadians just how scary and shadowy he was," said Carlene Variyan, a former Liberal strategist and staffer.
"That type of campaign in and of itself wasn't successful, so I think there's an understanding that it can't just be that the other guy loses, you have to win the hearts and minds of the voters that that you're trying to get out to the ballot box," said Variyan.
And without new ideas, the government runs the risk of looking tired or worn out, said Dan Arnold, the chief strategy officer at Pollara Strategic Insights, who oversaw the Liberal party's research operation in the last three federal elections.
"It's important for governments to always be fresh and to have that drive and that energy. That's something voters certainly look towards, especially for governments that have been around for a long time," said Arnold.
Polls suggest that voters aren't feeling particularly inspired by Liberal ideas these days. A survey released this week by the Angus Reid Institute found that 63 per cent of people who intend to vote for the Liberals are doing it primarily to block the Conservatives from getting into power.
But if Liberals are searching for a big-spending agenda that will knock Canadians off their feet and bring them back to Trudeau's camp, they may be out of luck.
Finances are tighter than they have ever been during the government's tenure, probably ruling out any pricey new ideas the Liberals may have considered in better times, said Tyler Meredith, the former head of fiscal and economic policy for Trudeau and the founding partner of Meredith Boessenkool Policy Advisors.
That should push the Liberals to think more creatively, he said. Examples might be using government land to boost home-building, launching new benefits associated with food insecurity and doubling down on credits for heat pumps that could save people money heating their homes.
With little money in the coffers, Arnold said the government has to focus on smaller policies that zero in on the issues that are salient to Canadians.
"That's probably what the government is looking for more in this situation is not a big new spending program that's going to cost a lot of money, but smaller things they can do that signal to voters that they're aligned with them on their values and that they're doing things to help with what matters most to them," said Arnold.
That means voters shouldn't expect big social programs, but policies that more closely resemble the recently announced mortgage charter and the efforts to get grocery stores to stabilize prices in the wake of high inflation, he said.
The struggle for any government is that so much of its energy is taken up by the day-to-day tasks of governing the country, leaving little time and energy for fresh thinking.
"If you want to win government, you have to not think like a government. And you've got to create processes that pull you out of that," said Meredith. "What's important is that you run a policy process actually outside of the government that is not pulled into these day-to-day debates and time pressures and trade-offs."
Complicating things for the government is that its agenda isn't entirely its own. Under the governance agreement with the NDP, the Liberal government has also had to take charge of priorities, like the national dental program, that may not have been at the top of the agenda otherwise.
"Liberals have talked about dental care before but, left to their own devices, that may not have been the thing that they put their fiscal heft behind. There's just less money to go elsewhere," said Arnold.
Variyan said many of the issues that brought current Liberals into politics are still relevant, whether it's climate change, Indigenous reconciliation, or expanding the government's universal day-care program. The party also has to be realistic about the type of election campaign to expect.
"We're running for a fourth term. Of course, it's going to be challenging. Of course, it's not going to be a cakewalk or the type of change campaign that we ran in 2015," said Variyan.
National Post
stthomson@postmedia.com

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Prime ministers; Elections

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; prime; minister; justin; trudeau; thing

Title: Trudeau wants four more years. But what for?

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Document 425 of 500

Canada wants to be an EV nation, but will consumers come along for the ride?

Author: Coulton, Marisa

Publication info: Coulton, Marisa. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-wants-be-ev-nation-will-consumers-come/docview/2918721010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canada+wants+to+be+an+EV+nation%2C+but+will+consumers+come+along+for+the+ride%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Coulton%2C+Marisa&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government wants electric vehicles to be the obvious choice - and eventually the only choice - for Canadian drivers, but so far that doesn't appear to be the case.
At Raman Grewal's Kia and Subaru dealership in Whitehorse, Yukon, for example, there is considerable demand for EV and plug-in hybrid vehicles, but many of his customers still have "range anxiety." That means they're worried the battery will die before they can find a charging station, and that they'll be left stranded on the Alaska Highway between B.C. and the Yukon.
"Currently, EVs work if you are commuting in around (the) 200 kilometres per day range," Grewal wrote in an email. "(Especially) in our cold climate in the north. Some customers are living at greater distances or are living off grid without hydro."
In urban centres, which currently have lower rates of EV adoption than the surrounding suburbs, there are other issues, said Jimmy Hearn, author of a recent report by S&P Global that found zero-emission vehicles accounted for a record 13.3 per cent of new vehicles purchased in the third quarter of 2023.
Urbanites tend to rely on street parking where it is harder to find EV charging stations, Hearn said, whereas suburbanites can install charging stations at their homes.
Even in Montreal, which is home to more than 2,000 public charging station ports, such concerns are front of mind, according to Norman John Hébert, president and chief operating officer of Groupe Park Avenue, which operates a Mercedes-Benz dealership in the city that sells EVs. Wait times can be an issue, too. And then there's the sticker price.
"Generally, our customers find EV prices to be too high compared with an equivalent format of ICE (internal combustion engine) vehicle," Hébert said in an email.
The average sticker price of an EV is $73,500, according to Canadian Black Book, around $6,000 more than a new gas powered car, which tops $67,000 according to a recent price index report by AutoTrader, an online car marketplace.
Though S&P forecasts EV market share will only continue to grow, reaching 18.4 per cent by the end of 2024, there are signs consumer demand is not keeping up with soaring expectations.
In recent months, some automakers have scaled back EV production targets, citing sluggish sales and faltering demand. Ford Motor Co. recently announced that it would reduce production of its F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck, because demand was lower than expected. General Motors Co. announced that it, too, would slow plans for electric truck production, delaying the launch of a Detroit-based factory to 2025.
Ford Motor Co. battery powered F-150 Lightning trucks under production at their Rouge Electric Vehicle Center in Dearborn, Michigan, 2022.
As Ottawa embarks on its ambitious mandate of having 100 per cent of all new car sales be electric by 2035 - a target that goes hand in hand with tens of billions of dollars in subsidies for EV supply chain projects - getting consumers on board is seen as critical by many in the industry. If they can't be convinced EVs are the best option, reaching those goals will be an uphill climb.
"We have to make sure the consumer is coming along at the pace required to support this transition," said Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturer's Association.
To ensure there is adequate demand for EVs, the government must enhance the grid, provide consumers with incentives, keep investment flowing into the North American EV supply chain, and build more charging infrastructure, he said.
"Achieving these very aggressive sales targets requires a 'Team Canada' approach to boosting EV sales across Canada," he said.
Without it, governments could be forced to rely more heavily on the costly subsidies that continue to play a major role in convincing consumers to buy EVs.
People who buy EVs from BMW Aurora, a dealership north of Toronto, generally qualify for federal incentives of up to $5,000, said Thomas An, assistant sales manager.
At Grewal's dealership in Whitehorse, meanwhile, customers can take advantage of the federal government rebate of around $5,000 on purchases and leases, plus a provincial rebate of up to $5,000 - a perk that is unavailable in Ontario because that province's $14,000 rebate was eliminated by Doug Ford when he became premier in 2018.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford in London, Ont.
Some of the variance in demand for EVs across dealerships - they account for 20 per cent of car sales for An, but 43.5 per cent for Grewal - is likely due to the differences in subsidies, said Meena Bibra, senior policy adviser at Clean Energy Canada.
S&P's Hearn concurred, noting that Quebec and B.C., which lead the pack in terms of EV sales, also both have strong EV incentives.
In the third quarter of 2023, EV sales made up around 26.4 per cent of all new vehicle registrations in B.C., where consumers can get up to a $4,000 rebate on a new EV, and 22.8 per cent of sales in Quebec, where consumers can get an even higher rebate of $7,000.
"If you compare Toronto's ZEV (zero-emission vehicle) growth to Montreal and Vancouver, Toronto ... is trailing behind those two smaller cities," said Hearn. There are around 8,800 EVs in Toronto, compared to 10,100 in Vancouver and 12,100 in Montreal, likely because Ontario lacks a provincial EV incentive, notes the S&P report.
"From what I see in the data, the vehicles that people are registering in British Columbia and Quebec are the vehicles that meet the provincial ZEV initiatives and rebates as well as the federal rebates," Hearn said. Customers are stacking the incentives in order to get the most bang for their buck.
While Bibra said recent news coverage suggests EV uptake at some dealerships has slowed, the big picture data says otherwise.
"When we look at just the hard facts, the sales continue to increase," she said. "It's really important to look at what's happening as an overarching trend."
Indeed, new ZEV sales fluctuate year to year, but are increasing on the whole: they totalled 55,600 countrywide, up from just 5,200 in the same quarter in 2017.
At the national level, Canada has made good progress in securing investments in the EV supply chain, part of its bid to become an EV superpower, said Kingston.
The signature investments so far include Volkswagen AG's plans to build a battery plant in St. Thomas, Ont., supported by $13 billion in government funding and Stellantis NV-LG Energy Solution Ltd.'s battery plant in Windsor, Ont., supported by $15 billion in funding.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arriving to make an announcement on a Volkswagen AG electric vehicle battery plant at the Elgin County Railway Museum in St. Thomas, Ont., 2023.
Others include Ford's plan to retool its factory Oakville to assemble EVs and battery packs, as well as a $35-million investment by Bobaek America Inc., a Korean electric vehicle parts manufacturer, toward the construction of an EV factory in Windsor.
But Kingston and a number of dealerships pointed to the need for better infrastructure as a key stumbling block to widespread adoption.
"The government should definitely implement more EV charging infrastructures in the cities," said An.
Grewal, too, called for better charging infrastructure on the Alaska Highway.
Though most customers appreciate the savings on gas, which amount to around $3,000 a year, EVs are still only the favoured option for a particular subset of consumers: those commuting short distances, those who are willing to wait several months to over a year for their car, and those who are "innovators that are interested in new technology," said Hébert.
Many of his customers seem willing to pay for EVs that have found a "sweet spot" for pricing, such as the Audi Q4 e-Tron, which retails for $64,000, the BMW i4 which retails for between $55,000 to $80,000, and the Volvo XC40 Recharge, which starts at around $60,000.
But it's not enough to cater to Canadians with means, said Bibra; the federal government needs to work harder to reach "mainstream" Canadians who fall into the low-to-middle income brackets.
Watershed year for EVs paving way for billions in investment
Ottawa sets timeline for full EV adoption
Lion Electric job cuts a sign of leaner financial reality
Simply mandating that 100 per cent of car sales be electric by 2035 does not ensure the goal will be met, said Kingston.
"I'd like to see a more co-ordinated approach led by the federal government but with other levels of government participating - provinces and municipalities - on a very well-thought-through plan to achieve the targets that the government has established," Kingston said. "We can't do this on our own."
. Email: mcoulton@postmedia.com

Trudeau attacks Conservatives in address to Liberal caucus; Ottawa retreat; Targeting MPs rare move for prime minister

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-attacks-conservatives-address-liberal/docview/2918645767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trudeau+attacks+Conservatives+in+address+to+Liberal+caucus%3B+Ottawa+retreat%3B+Targeting+MPs+rare+move+for+prime+minister&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took aim not just at Pierre Poilievre in a speech Thursday, but at the Conservative leader's MPs and party candidates in a partisan speech ahead of the new sitting of parliament.
Trudeau spoke to MPs at the start of a two-day caucus retreat in Ottawa. Trudeau regularly attacks Poilievre, who has gained a significant lead in national polls, but rarely attacks specific Conservative MPs or candidates.
He said Poilievre only seems to be welcoming party insiders or ideologues into his version of the Conservative party. He called out MP Branden Leslie, who won for the party in the Manitoba seat of Portage Lisgar, for being against a ban on conversion therapy.
He also attacked MP Leslyn Lewis, as someone "who dines with far right German politicians and leads the call to withdraw Canada from the United Nations. (She) does not speak for Canada, she may speak for the Conservative Party of Canada but she does not speak for Canada."
Lewis has sponsored a parliamentary petition calling for Canada to leave the UN.
Trudeau suggested Poilievre is picking candidates who will represent the party's views instead of those of their constituents.
"They welcome Roman Baber as a candidate in the GTA even though his extremism got him kicked out of Ontario's Conservative Party. Their new MP in Oxford was a hand-picked insider who'd never been to the riding."
Trudeau's caucus has been split in recent weeks over the war in Gaza. Several MPs have called on Canada to oppose South Africa's push to have Israel's actions declared a genocide at the International Court of Justice (ICJ), while other MPs have said the government should let the court decide.
The prime minister has been vague, saying only that Canada supports the International Court of Justice, but that doesn't mean it accepts "the premise" of South Africa's charges.
Trudeau said the dispute in caucus is part of their strength, because they are bringing diverse views to the table.
"We have a range of voices and perspectives on some pretty big issues including around the Middle East. But of course, Canadians hold a range of views and perspectives on such difficult issues."
Trudeau also repeated previous attacks against the Conservatives, arguing they don't support Ukraine.
"We'll stand up for our friends. But it's about more than that. It's about standing up and standing beside Ukrainians who are fighting and dying for their sovereignty, their territorial integrity, but also fighting for the international rules based order."
He suggested Poilievre is not giving Canadians enough detail about what he would do in government.
"They don't talk about the solutions that they're proposing because they have none," he said.
Trudeau has faced some criticism from within. Liberal MP Ken McDonald told media last week he wasn't sure if Trudeau was the right leader and said a leadership review could be good for the party. McDonald reversed himself on Thursday and several other MPs said they fully support Trudeau as they headed into caucus.
Trudeau cautioned his MPs to stay focused on the needs of their constituents.
"There's a lot of distractions, a lot of things going on around this place that Canadians more or less pay attention to. But what we have to pay attention to, and what we do pay attention to, is bringing our communities'voices to here in Ottawa."
National Post rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 427 of 500

Ontario not ready for cap on foreign students

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-not-ready-cap-on-foreign-students/docview/2918645724/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario+not+ready+for+cap+on+foreign+students&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government's decision this week to substantially reduce the number of foreign student visas is the right thing to do, but it undermines the finances of Ontario's colleges and universities and will hamper their ability to serve the province's students.
Making Ontario's post-secondary sector significantly reliant on foreign student tuition was an unsustainable, unwise decision that was sure to end badly. Now it has, and Premier Doug Ford's government seems unready to respond.
It's not like federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller's move to cut the country's foreign student visas from 600,000 to 360,000 came out of the blue. The government has b een telegraphing it for months.
That wasn't the first warning the Ford government had. Ontario's auditor general criticized overreliance on international tuition in both 2021 and 2022. Late last year, an expert panel appointed by the Ford government warned that "Many colleges and universities have passed the point where they could survive financially with only domestic students."
So far, all the Ford government has done is repeat a second-rate federal Liberal talking point about cracking down on "bad actors," those being strip mall campuses that licence the curriculum of public community colleges. All with the full knowledge of the provincial government, of course.
There are three bad actors in this story, but none of them operate out of a strip mall.
First, the federal government. It turned foreign student training into a back door immigration system with no limits. Students could work for three years while they studied, then for two or three additional years after that. In all, there are about one million students here on visas now. Only belatedly has the federal government come to admit that flooding the country with unofficial immigrants might contribute to Canada's housing shortage.
The surge of foreign students worked remarkably well for the Ontario government. Foreign students pay absurd tuitions, as much as $14,300 a year for a college student and $46,443 a year for a university student. According to the Ontario AG, foreign students accounted for 45 per cent of university tuition revenue and 68 per cent of college tuition fees.
The influx of foreign student money papered over the government's own neglect of the post-secondary sector. In 2019, Ford cut tuition, then froze it. The government's direct support for the sector has been meagre. The province's funding per university student is only 57 per cent of what the rest of the country spends, and it's even worse for college students at 44 per cent.
Finally, there are the universities and colleges themselves. They have overcome inadequate tuition and government funding by milking foreign students for all they are worth, becoming dependent on their tuition fees in the process.
The absurdity of the situation was illustrated earlier this month when international students at Algoma University's Brampton campus conducted protests after receiving failing marks. One student got right to the heart of the transaction with a sign saying "CAD 26000 are not enough?" One of his compatriots missed the point altogether with his sign, which read "Education is not for sale." Of course it is, and these students have the receipts to prove it. The university, an obscure Sudbury institution that has set up shop in Brampton to grab some cash, fixed the issue by putting the students'marks on a bell curve. Smart move. In 2021-22, the Brampton campus generated 65 per cent of the university's revenue. You have to keep the customers happy.
Unfortunately for the Ford government, the years of pretending none of this was happening are coming to an end. The federal government hasn't shut off the student tap entirely, but it's time for Ontario to figure out how to pay for universities and colleges without an ever-increasing flow of foreign student tuition.
The first challenge will be distributing visa quotas, something the province has not done previously. The new visas are based on population, implying that Ontario might get about 130,000 study visas compared to 300,000 in 2023.
Private colleges that were given a veneer of credibility by licensing curriculum from real community colleges should be cut off. Colleges overall will require a significant trim and a return to their mandate of job training for people in their own communities. They can afford it, for now. Foreign student training has been lucrative. In 2022, all but one Ontario college posted a surplus, with the average being $27 million across the system.
Universities are in a different situation. The Council of Ontario Universities says at least 10 Ontario universities are forecasting deficits this year, amounting to $175 million. Next year, the total is expected to be $273 million. A reduction in foreign student tuition will exacerbate that.
This is all a worrisome situation for Ontario students and their parents. The Ford government needs to put a credible fix in place, quickly.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist, author and former Ontario PC candidate. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 428 of 500

Trudeau wants four more years in office, but for what?; PM's primary motivation appears to be blocking poilievre, but some liberals wonder if that's enough

Author: Thomson, Stuart

Publication info: Thomson, Stuart. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-wants-four-more-years-office-what-pms/docview/2918645709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trudeau+wants+four+more+years+in+office%2C+but+for+what%3F%3B+PM%27s+primary+motivation+appears+to+be+blocking+poilievre%2C+but+some+liberals+wonder+if+that%27s+enough&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Thomson%2C+Stuart&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has made one thing clear: he's not going anywhere.
Faced with plummeting poll numbers and a surging Conservative Opposition, Trudeau has said in numerous recent interviews that there is too much unfinished business for him to walk away now. He has also painted Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as a threat to the entire Liberal project and vowed not to run away from an electoral showdown with the man who would dismantle his government's work.
"You know me well enough, you know what I believe ... do you actually think I could walk away from this fight right now?" said an animated Trudeau in a holiday interview with his friend and former radio host Terry DiMonte.
But what will inspire seemingly exhausted Liberals to endure another few sittings of the House of Commons and a gruelling election campaign against a fierce opponent? It could be that Poilievre himself will be enough motivation for Liberal partisans who are inclined to agree with Trudeau that Poilievre will dismantle their progressive agenda.
And Poilievre would likely agree with the prime minister, too.
The Opposition leader has vowed to cancel some of the government's most cherished accomplishments, like the federal carbon tax, and to defund the CBC, which still attracts most of its remaining support from the Canadian left.
But some Liberals warn that asking for votes just to protect Liberal polices or painting Poilievre as a threat to democracy likely won't be enough.
"We watched political parties on the opposition benches think that they would be successful in elections trying to demonize Stephen Harper or tell Canadians just how scary and shadowy he was," said Carlene Variyan, a former Liberal strategist and staffer.
"That type of campaign in and of itself wasn't successful, so I think there's an understanding that it can't just be that the other guy loses; you have to win the hearts and minds of the voters that you're trying to get out to the ballot box," said Variyan.
And without new ideas, the government runs the risk of looking tired or worn out, said Dan Arnold, the chief strategy officer at Pollara Strategic Insights, who oversaw the Liberal party's research operation in the last three federal elections.
"It's important for governments to always be fresh and to have that drive and that energy. That's something voters certainly look towards, especially for governments that have been around for a long time," said Arnold.
Polls suggest that voters aren't feeling particularly inspired by Liberal ideas these days. A survey released this week by the Angus Reid Institute found that 63 per cent of people who intend to vote for the Liberals are doing it primarily to block the Conservatives from getting into power.
But if Liberals are searching for a big-spending agenda that will knock Canadians off their feet and bring them back to Trudeau's camp, they may be out of luck.
Finances are tighter than they have ever been during the government's tenure, probably ruling out any pricey new ideas the Liberals may have considered in better times, said Tyler Meredith, the former head of fiscal and economic policy for Trudeau and the founding partner of Meredith Boessenkool Policy Advisors.
That should push the Liberals to think more creatively, he said. Examples might be using government land to boost homebuilding, launching new benefits associated with food insecurity and doubling down on credits for heat pumps that could save people money heating their homes.
With little money in the coffers, Arnold said the government has to focus on smaller policies that zero in on the issues that are salient to Canadians.
"That's probably what the government is looking for more in this situation, is not a big new spending program that's going to cost a lot of money, but smaller things they can do that signal to voters that they're aligned with them on their values and that they're doing things to help with what matters most to them," said Arnold.
That means voters shouldn't expect big social programs, but policies that more closely resemble the recently announced mortgage charter and the efforts to get grocery stores to stabilize prices in the wake of high inflation, he said.
The struggle for any government is that so much of its energy is taken up by the day-to-day tasks of governing the country, leaving little time and energy for fresh thinking.
"If you want to win government, you have to not think like a government. And you've got to create processes that pull you out of that," said Meredith. "What's important is that you run a policy process actually outside of the government that is not pulled into these day-to-day debates and time pressures and trade-offs."
Complicating things for the government is that its agenda isn't entirely its own. Under the governance agreement with the NDP, the Liberal government has also had to take charge of priorities, like the national dental program, that may not have been at the top of the agenda otherwise.
"Liberals have talked about dental care before but, left to their own devices, that may not have been the thing that they put their fiscal heft behind. There's just less money to go elsewhere," said Arnold.
Variyan said many of the issues that brought current Liberals into politics are still relevant, whether it's climate change, Indigenous reconciliation, or expanding the government's universal daycare program. The party also has to be realistic about the type of election campaign to expect.
"We're running for a fourth term. Of course, it's going to be challenging. Of course, it's not going to be a cakewalk or the type of change campaign that we ran in 2015," said Variyan.
National Post stthomson@postmedia.com

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Prime ministers; Elections; Political campaigns; Interviews

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: prime; minister; justin; trudeau; thing; clear

Document 429 of 500

NDP candidate blames 'white fragility' for Jama ejection

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-candidate-blames-white-fragility-jama/docview/2918645335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=NDP+candidate+blames+%27white+fragility%27+for+Jama+ejection&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal NDP candidate and riding executives in Guelph sent a letter blaming "white fragility" for the removal of an Ontario MPP from the provincial NDP caucus after a falling out over her offensive comments about the Hamas terrorist attacks against Israel on Oct. 7.
The letter, obtained by National Post, demanded that Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles immediately reinstate Hamilton-Centre MPP Sarah Jama to the party's caucus, describing "dismay" and "anger" over the party's decision to remove her last October.
Jama had initially apologized after responding to the Hamas Oct. 7 terror attacks by blaming Israel for the "occupation of Palestine," but was later ejected after Stiles said Jama's subsequent "unilateral actions" were undermining the caucus.
The letter was co-signed by several local leaders of the federal NDP in Guelph.
Among those who cosigned was Janice Folk-Dawson, who was acclaimed as the federal NDP's candidate for Guelph in September.
"Since her election, MPP Jama has been an outspoken advocate for people in Hamilton Centre, pushing for equitable housing, meaningful (disabilities) support, among other important campaigns," read the letter, which National Post confirmed was sent by riding executives to Stiles in late October.
It expresses "shock" over Stiles's claims that Jama created an "unsafe work environment" for her colleagues and fellow party members.
"The language used is eerily reminiscent of the language used to discredit and vilify people of colour, particularly women, drawing on tropes of the 'angry black woman,'" the letter continues.
"The vague nature of these allegations do not bolster confidence that the decision to remove MPP Jama from the caucus was not motivated by white fragility and an inability to handle dissent from a young, racialized member of the caucus."
Folk-Dawson is a longtime labour activist and leader at the Canadian Union of Public Employees and former executive vice-president of the Ontario Federation of Labour.
The letter accuses the Ontario NDP of "breaking the trust" of activists and grassroots members who want a stronger position on what they describe as the "ongoing violence and oppression against Palestinian people."
Less than a month after Stiles'office received the letter, the Ontario NDP provincial council voted 130 to 60 to reject a motion welcoming Jama back into the caucus, according to a November article published in The Trillium.
Members of the Guelph riding association who spoke off the record to National Post described their party as one being taken over by a small but vocal minority of activists, who they say are forcing many longtime members to cut ties with the NDP.
Israel has proven to be a divisive issue within the NDP, both provincially and federally.
Pro-Palestinian protesters and some party delegates stormed the Hamilton Convention Centre in October during the federal NDP's annual convention, a week after the terrorist attacks, which killed more than 1,000 Israelis and saw hundreds more taken hostage. They demanded an emergency resolution condemning Israel and supporting the right of Palestinian "resistance."
Jama's expulsion from the Ontario NDP (ONDP) caucus came shortly before the Progressive Conservative government passed a motion censuring her, preventing her from speaking in the Ontario legislature.
"T he decision to remove (Jama) from the caucus has now completely overshadowed the anti-democratic, anti-free speech, punitive censorship by the Ford government," the Guelph letter read.
"Many people have understandably connected the dots, and assumed that the expulsion of MPP Jama from the caucus was directly related to her support for Palestinian liberation, despite Marit Stiles'claims to the contrary."
"We have heard from multiple people who have previously supported the ONDP that they now feel the ONDP has taken the side of Ford and the PC's, choosing to ignore the calls for a ceasefire and an end to the illegal occupation of Palestine, in the name of avoiding controversy," it says.
The Guelph NDP riding association did not respond to National Post's request for comment, nor did the Ontario and federal NDP. Folk-Dawson also did not respond to questions.
However, sources within the Ontario NDP tell the National Post trouble within the party has been brewing for years.
"Talk of a 'split'in the NDP is an exaggeration," one longtime New Democrat told the National Post, describing the trouble being caused by a small but very vocal members, who until recently didn't really hold that much sway in party policy.
"The party leadership have long tolerated the antics of a small group of dissidents."
The source, who would speak only on condition of anonymity, agreed that this year's Hamilton convention was an important turning point.
"At the last federal convention, they went from a mere nuisance to a direct threat, when they attempted to storm the convention hall and disrupt Jagmeet Singh's keynote speech," the source said.
"Convention staff and police held them back."
Currently held by threeterm Liberal MP Lloyd Longfield, Guelph has been a reliably Liberal federal riding for three decades.
National Post bpassifiume@postmedia.com

Document 434 of 500

Randall Denley: Ontario is not prepared for a cap on international students

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-ontario-is-not-prepared-cap-on/docview/2918596388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Ontario+is+not+prepared+for+a+cap+on+international+students&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government's decision this week to substantially reduce the number of foreign student visas is the right thing to do, but it undermines the finances of Ontario's colleges and universities and will hamper their ability to serve the province's students.
Making Ontario's post-secondary sector significantly reliant on foreign student tuition was an unsustainable, unwise decision that was sure to end badly. Now it has, and Premier Doug Ford's government seems unready to respond.
It's not like federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller's move to cut the country's foreign student visas from 600,000 to 360,000 came out of the blue. The government has been telegraphing it for months
That wasn't the first warning the Ford government had. Ontario's auditor general criticized overreliance on international tuition in both2021 and 2022 Late last year, an expert panel appointed by the Ford government warned that "Many colleges and universities have passed the point where they could survive financially with only domestic students."
So far, all the Ford government has done is repeat a second-rate federal Liberal talking point about cracking down on "bad actors," those being strip mall campuses that licence the curriculum of public community colleges. All with the full knowledge of the provincial government, of course.
There are three bad actors in this story, but none of them operate out of a strip mall.
First, the federal government. It turned foreign student training into a back door immigration system with no limits. Students could work for three years while they studied, then for two or three additional years after that. In all, there are about one million students here on visas now. Only belatedly has the federal government come to admit that flooding the country with unofficial immigrants might contribute to Canada's housing shortage.
Randall Denley: Olivia Chow has Trudeau Liberals in a tight corner - for now
Randall Denley: A cure for paperwork and the solution for Ontario's family doctor shortage
The surge of foreign students worked remarkably well for the Ontario government. Foreign students pay absurd tuitions, as much as $14,300 a year for a college student and $46,443 a year for a university student. According to the Ontario AG, foreign students accounted for 45 per cent of university tuition revenue and 68 per cent of college tuition fees.
The influx of foreign student money papered over the government's own neglect of the post-secondary sector. In 2019, Ford cut tuition, then froze it. The government's direct support for the sector has been meagre. The province's funding per university student is only 57 per cent of what the rest of the country spends, and it's even worse for college students at 44 per cent.
Finally, there are the universities and colleges themselves. They have overcome inadequate tuition and government funding by milking foreign students for all they are worth, becoming dependent on their tuition fees in the process.
The absurdity of the situation was illustrated earlier this month when international students at Algoma University's Brampton campus conducted protests after receiving failing marks. One student got right to the heart of the transaction with a sign saying "CAD 26000 are not enough?" One of his compatriots missed the point altogether with his sign, which read "Education is not for sale." Of course it is, and these students have the receipts to prove it. The university, an obscure Sudbury institution that has set up shop in Brampton to grab some cash, fixed the issue by putting the students' marks on a bell curve. Smart move. In 2021-22, the Brampton campus generated 65 per cent of the university's revenue. You have to keep the customers happy.
Unfortunately for the Ford government, the years of pretending none of this was happening are coming to an end. The federal government hasn't shut off the student tap entirely, but it's time for Ontario to figure out how to pay for universities and colleges without an ever-increasing flow of foreign student tuition.
The first challenge will be distributing visa quotas, something the province has not done previously. The new visas are based on population, implying that Ontario might get about 130,000 study visas compared to 300,000 in 2023.
Private colleges that were given a veneer of credibility by licensing curriculum from real community colleges should be cut off. Colleges overall will require a significant trim and a return to their mandate of job training for people in their own communities. They can afford it, for now. Foreign student training has been lucrative. In 2022, all but one Ontario college posted a surplus, with the average being $27 million across the system.
Universities are in a different situation. The Council of Ontario Universities says at least 10 Ontario universities are forecasting deficits( //www.msn.com/en-ca/news/canada/international-student-cap-will-hit-university-and-college-finances-especially-in-ontario/ar-BB1hd8xO ) this year, amounting to $175 million. Next year, the total is expected to be $273 million. A reduction in foreign student tuition will exacerbate that.
This is all a worrisome situation for Ontario students and their parents. The Ford government needs to put a credible fix in place, quickly.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist, author and former Ontario PC candidate. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Business indexing term: Industry: 92812 :‎ International Affairs 61121 :‎ Junior Colleges

Subject: College students; Tuition; Federal government; Foreign students; University students; Passports & visas; Curricula; College campuses; Immigration; Community colleges

Identifier / keyword: federal; decision; substantially; reduce; number; foreign

Federal NDP candidate backs accusation 'white fragility' behind caucus ejection of MPP Sarah Jama

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/federal-ndp-candidate-backs-accusation-white/docview/2918586734/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Federal+NDP+candidate+backs+accusation+%27white+fragility%27+behind+caucus+ejection+of+MPP+Sarah+Jama&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal NDP candidate and riding executives in Guelph sent a letter blaming "white fragility" for the removal of an Ontario MPP from the provincial NDP caucus after a falling out over her offensive comments about the Hamas terrorist attacks against Israel on Oct. 7.
The letter, obtained by the National Post, demanded that Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles immediately reinstate Hamilton-Centre MPP Sarah Jama to the party's caucus, describing "dismay" and "anger" over the party's decision to remove her last October.
Jama had initially apologized after responding to the Hamas Oct. 7 terror attacks by blaming Israel for the "occupation of Palestine," but was later ejected after Stiles said Jama's subsequent "unilateral actions" were undermining the caucus.
The letter was co-signed by several local leaders of the federal NDP in Guelph, including Janice Folk-Dawson, who was acclaimed as the federal NDP's candidate for Guelph in September.
"Since her election, MPP Jama has been an outspoken advocate for people in Hamilton Centre, pushing for equitable housing, meaningful (disabilities) support, among other important campaigns," read the letter, which National Post confirmed was sent by riding executives to Stiles in late October.
It expresses "shock" over Stiles's claims that Jama created an "unsafe work environment" for her colleagues and fellow party members.
"The language used is eerily reminiscent of the language used to discredit and vilify people of colour, particularly women, drawing on tropes of the 'angry black woman,'" the letter continues.
"The vague nature of these allegations do not bolster confidence that the decision to remove MPP Jama from the caucus was not motivated by white fragility and an inability to handle dissent from a young, racialized member of the caucus."
I'm reflecting on my role as a politician who is participating in this settler colonial system, and I ask that all politicians do the same. #FreePalastine
- Sarah Jama (@SarahJama_) October 10, 2023
Folk-Dawson is a long-time labour activist and leader at the Canadian Union of Public Employees and former executive vice-president of the Ontario Federation of Labour.
The letter accuses the Ontario NDP of "breaking the trust" of activists and grassroots members who want a stronger position on what they describe as the "ongoing violence and oppression against Palestinian people."
Less than a month after Stiles' office received the letter, the Ontario NDP provincial council voted 130 to 60 to reject a motion welcoming Jama back into the caucus, according to a November article published in The Trillium
Members of the Guelph riding association who spoke off the record to National Post described their party as one being taken over by a small but vocal minority of activists, who they say are forcing many long-time members to cut ties with the NDP.
Israel has proven to be a divisive issue within the NDP, both provincially and federally.
Right now @NDP #FreePalaestine
- Leftist Activist ?????? (@Ward6Kris) October 14, 2023
Pro-Palestinian protestors and some party delegates stormed the Hamilton Convention Centre in October during the federal NDP's annual convention, a week after the terrorist attacks, which killed more than 1,000 Israelis and saw hundreds more taken hostage. They demanded an emergency resolution condemning Israel and supporting the right of Palestinian "resistance."
Jama's expulsion from the Ontario NDP (ONDP) caucus came shortly before the Progressive Conservative government passed a motion censuring her, preventing her from speaking in the Ontario legislature.
"The decision to remove (Jama) from the caucus has now completely overshadowed the anti-democratic, anti-free speech, punitive censorship by the Ford government," the Guelph letter read.
Despite candidate's antisemitic comments, Ontario NDP still favoured to win Thursday byelection
FIRST READING: The Canadian taxpayer money funding and enabling Hamas
"Many people have understandably connected the dots, and assumed that the expulsion of MPP Jama from the caucus was directly related to her support for Palestinian liberation, despite Marit Stiles' claims to the contrary."
"We have heard from multiple people who have previously supported the ONDP that they now feel the ONDP has taken the side of Ford and the PC's, choosing to ignore the calls for a ceasefire and an end to the illegal occupation of Palestine, in the name of avoiding controversy," it says.
The Guelph NDP riding association did not respond to National Post's request for comment, nor did the Ontario and federal NDP. Folk-Dawson also did not respond to questions.
Ontario Federation of Labour executive vice-president Janice Folk-Dawson gives a speech in April 2023. Folk-Dawson has been acclaimed as the federal NDP's candidate for Guelph, Ont.
However, sources within the Ontario NDP tell the National Post trouble within the party has been brewing for years.
"Talk of a 'split' in the NDP is an exaggeration," one long-time New Democrat told the National Post, describing the trouble being caused by a small but very vocal members, who until recently didn't really hold that much sway in party policy.
"The party leadership have long tolerated the antics of a small group of dissidents."
The source, who would speak only on condition of anonymity, agreed that this year's Hamilton convention was an important turning point.
"At the last federal convention, they went from a mere nuisance to a direct threat, when they attempted to storm the convention hall and disrupt Jagmeet Singh's keynote speech," the source said.
"Convention staff and police held them back."
Currently held by three-term Liberal MP Lloyd Longfield, Guelph has been a reliably Liberal federal riding for three decades.
National Post
bpassifiume@postmedia.com

Document 436 of 500

Trudeau attacks Conservatives, from Poilievre to MPs and candidates, in address to Liberal caucus

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-attacks-conservatives-poilievre-mps/docview/2918581093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trudeau+attacks+Conservatives%2C+from+Poilievre+to+MPs+and+candidates%2C+in+address+to+Liberal+caucus&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took aim not just at Pierre Poilievre in a speech Thursday, but at the Conservative leader's MPs and party candidates in a partisan speech ahead of the new sitting of parliament.
Trudeau spoke to MPs at the beginning of a two-day caucus retreat in Ottawa. Trudeau regularly attacks Poilievre, who has gained a significant lead in national polls, but rarely attacks specific Conservative MPs or candidates.
He said Poilievre only seems to be welcoming party insiders or ideologues into his version of the Conservative party. He called out MP Branden Leslie, who won for the party in the Manitoba seat of Portage Lisgar, for being against a ban on conversion therapy. He also named MP Leslyn Lewis, who has sponsored a parliamentary petition calling for Canada to leave the UN.
"Leslyn Lewis, who dines with far right German politicians and leads the call to withdraw Canada from the United Nations does not speak for Canada, she may speak for the Conservative Party of Canada, but she does not speak for Canada."
Lewis has sponsored a parliamentary petition calling for Canada to leave the UN.
Trudeau suggested Poilievre is picking candidates who will represent the party's views instead of those of their constituents.
"They welcome Roman Baber as a candidate in the GTA even though his extremism got him kicked out of Ontario's Conservative Party. Their new MP in Oxford was a handpicked insider who'd never been to the riding."
Former justice minister David Lametti resigning as Liberal MP to join law firm
Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act was ruled unconstitutional. What happens now?
Trudeau's caucus has been split in recent weeks over the war in Gaza. Several MPs have called on Canada to oppose South Africa's push to have Israel's actions declared a genocide at the International Court of Justice, while other MPs have said the government should let the court decide.
The prime minister has been vague saying only that Canada supports the ICJ, but that doesn't mean it accepts "the premise" of South Africa's charges. A preliminary ruling in that case is expected Friday.
Trudeau said the dispute in caucus is part of their strength, because they are bringing diverse views to the table.
"We have a range of voices and perspectives on some pretty big issues including around the Middle East. But of course, Canadians hold a range of views and perspectives on such difficult issues."
Trudeau also repeated previous attacks against the Conservatives arguing they don't support Ukraine.
"We'll stand up for our friends. But it's about more than that. It's about standing up and standing beside Ukrainians who are fighting and dying for their sovereignty, their territorial integrity, but also fighting for the international rules based order."
He suggested Poilievre is not giving Canadians enough detail about what he would do in government.
"They don't talk about the solutions that they're proposing because they have none." he said. "People are facing big complex problems. They don't need slogans. They need thoughtful solutions."
Trudeau has faced some criticism from within, Liberal MP Ken McDonald told media last week he wasn't sure if Trudeau was the right leader and said a leadership review could be a good thing for the party.
McDonald reversed himself on Thursday and several other MPs said they fully support Trudeau as they headed into caucus. Liberal whip Ruby Sahota said she spoke with McDonald about his comments, but didn't say what she told him.
Trudeau cautioned his MPs to stay focused on the needs of their constituents.
"There's a lot of distractions, a lot of things going on around this place that Canadians more or less pay attention to. But what we have to pay attention to, and what we do pay attention to is bringing our communities voices to here in Ottawa."
National Post
rtumilty@postmedia.com

Business indexing term: Industry: 81394 :‎ Political Organizations

Subject: Conservatism; Candidates; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties

FIRST READING: Canada signs over an area the size of Western Europe to Inuit control

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-canada-signs-over-area-size-western/docview/2918492514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Canada+signs+over+an+area+the+size+of+Western+Europe+to+Inuit+control&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
Ottawa has just inked an agreement that constitutes the biggest land transfer in Canadian history - while ranking as one of the largest-ever examples of a government effectively returning territory to Indigenous control.
The Nunavut Lands and Resources Devolution Agreement has gone mostly unnoticed outside the Arctic. But the 239-page agreement - signed last week by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - takes roughly two million square kilometres of Crown land previously under federal control and transfers it to the Government of Nunavut.
It means that roughly one fifth of Canada's entire land mass will soon be falling under new management.
An official statement called the agreement "the largest land transfer in Canada's history." The statement added that "once it comes into force on April 1, 2027, it will allow Nunavummiut (Nunavut residents) "to make decisions about public lands, freshwater, and non-renewable resources."
Pleasure to meet with the Prime Minister to discuss next steps in implementing devolution, housing and Arctic Security and Sovereignty.
- Premier P.J. Akeeagok (@j_akeeagok) January 19, 2024
Nunavut will effectively be getting the same control over its lands and resources currently exercised by the provinces. It's also the last of Canada's three territories to receive jurisdiction over lands, oil and mineral rights; Yukon's devolution occurred in 2003 and the Northwest Territories underwent devolution in 2014.
But Nunavut is notable as the only Canadian subnational jurisdiction that is under majority Indigenous control; 85.4 per cent of the population is Inuit. As such, federal and Inuit officials framed the agreement as a win for Indigenous self-determination.
Nunavut Tunngavik Inc. (NTI) - the official treaty representative for Nunavut Inuit - was a key negotiator for devolution and a signatory of the final agreement. In a statement, NTI President Aluki Kotierk called devolution "a crucial step towards Nunavut Inuit having autonomy over the land we've called home for centuries."
18, 2024, ?????, ????.???? ?????? ??????? ??? ???? ???? ?????? ?????? ?????????? ???? ?????? ?????????? ????...
Posted by Nunavut Tunngavik Inc. on Thursday, January 18, 2024
"We, the people most invested in our homeland, will be the ones managing our natural resources," said Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok.
In comments published by NNSL Media, Akeeagok described his "contentedness" in knowing that Nunavummiut currently in high school will one day "sit here in this legislature and make a final decision on a mining project that's happening in the territory, not someone who lives in Ottawa that has no reality of Nunavut."
The total lands transferred by the agreement are roughly equivalent in size to two British Columbia, three states of Texas or the entirety of Western Europe.
They're larger even than the amount of land that came under Canada's control at its 1867 founding. At the moment of Confederation, Canada consisted only of New Brunswick, Nova Scotia and truncated pieces of modern-day Quebec and Ontario.
Chris Selley: Violating the Charter with the Emergencies Act came from a broken system
Islamic group calling for Shariah law cancels Canadian event after U.K. declares it a terrorist entity
Arguably, the only larger Canadian land transfer occurred in 1870, when the United Kingdom arranged for Rupert's Land - a region comprising the entirety of the Hudson Bay drainage basin - to come under Ottawa's jurisdiction.
But the Rupert's Land transfer was different in that it wasn't a straight handover of sovereign territory. Canada still had to secure title and resource rights through the signing of numbered treaties that extinguished Indigenous claims to the land.
The vast territories transferred by devolution are treeless and mostly lacking in infrastructure. Nunavut has no road connection to the rest of Canada, its electricity comes entirely from diesel generators and travel between its main communities is mostly done either by air or snow machine.
A report by Agence France-Presse, however, noted that Nunavut will shortly be in control of "rich Arctic lands" comprising the "richest resource deposits in the country."
Trade publications similarly made note of Nunavut's natural resources falling under local control, with one saying the agreement could "attract prospectors seeking diamonds, gold and other deposits."
Nunavut is already home to one of the world's richest iron-ore mines, as well as three active gold mines
IN OTHER NEWS
Environment and Climate Change Canada got an awful lot of flak last week after news emerged that they were reportedly mulling a crackdown on wood smoke emissions from bagel shops and pizzerias. In the words of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the Trudeau government was leading "a crusade against pizza ovens." But in a lengthy series of social media posts, the agency said that they were simply calling around to bagel shops and pizzerias to add smoke data to the National Pollutant Release Inventory. So it's not a gear-up to a crackdown on wood-fired ovens, but it is filling out a dataset that could potentially be used by a government to justify such a crackdown in future. Which isn't the craziest idea, given that civic officials in Montreal have been openly promising for years to bring the hammer down on the city's famed network of wood-fired restaurants.
At a press availability broadcast by CPAC, above, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was asked why he decided to take a free $90,000 Jamaican vacation over Christmas given that he spends so much time talking about the country's tough economic situation. Trudeau replied, "As many Canadians did, I stayed with friends over the holidays." When reporter Marieke Walsh said that most Canadians do not have access to free $90,000 vacations, Trudeau replied, "As many Canadians did, I stayed with friends over the holidays."
The City of Edmonton has undergone a bizarre violent attack of as-yet indeterminate motive. On Tuesday, a man armed with a rifle entered Edmonton City Hall and began shooting randomly while also throwing petrol bombs. Nobody was injured and the man was taken into custody by police. Bezhani Sarvar, 28, has been charged with a host of firearms and explosives violations related to the shooting - although nothing explicitly terroristic.
Amazingly, this is not the first time that the region has seen a serious act of violence directed at a municipal building for seemingly no reason. In 2018, a 21-year-old named Kane Kosolowsky orchestrated a pair of large explosions in the parkade of the Edmonton-area Strathcona County Community Centre. Nobody was hurt by the blasts, but Kosolowsky was found dead at the scene from a self-inflicted gunshot wound. He'd also arrived at the parkade in a car containing at least five registered firearms. "We may never know the true motives of Kane Kosolowsky," said police at the time
With the Emergencies Act back in the news, it might be worth remembering that invoking the act came up a lot during the COVID-19 pandemic, even before a single Freedom Convoy trucker had arrived in Ottawa. In April 2021, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh sent a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau imploring him to invoke the Emergencies Act in order to streamline the distribution of vaccines (although he didn't specify why extraordinary measures were required to do this). And right in the opening days of the pandemic, Erin O'Toole (who became Conservative later that summer) released the above call for a "war footing," asking that the feds invoke the Emergencies Act to "prohibit travel" and "enforce self-isolation."
Speaking of terrorism, a group called Hizb ut Tahrir Canada was set to host a conference outside Toronto on Saturday advocating for the resurrection of a Muslim caliphate governed by Shariah law. That roughly describes the goals of ISIS, albeit presumably by slightly different methods. Anyways, the conference was suddenly cancelled after Hizb ut Tahrir was declared a terrorist entity by the British government. According to a former Hizb ut Tahrir member who spoke to the National Post, the group's Canadian supporters were likely spooked to stay home lest they too be branded as terrorists. Although they needn't have worried; the Vancouver-based group Samidoun is considered a terrorist entity by both Israel and Germany. Neither designation has stopped Samidoun from becoming one of Canada's most active organizers of extremist anti-Israel demonstrations.
