Document 438 of 500

Foreign student cap to hit ontario hard; Some schools rely heavily on higher tuition

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foreign-student-cap-hit-ontario-hard-some-schools/docview/2918378466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Foreign+student+cap+to+hit+ontario+hard%3B+Some+schools+rely+heavily+on+higher+tuition&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals'cap on international students could hit some universities and colleges in Ontario particularly hard, as schools there have become reliant on the higher tuition fees those students pay to keep their books balanced.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced the cap on Monday, saying his department will issue no more than 360,000 new visas this year, 35 per cent lower than last year, and keep them at that number for two years. The visas will be handed out proportionally to provinces based on their population, which will force even larger cuts at some schools in Ontario.
Schools in the province have been aggressive about bringing in international students and the new cap could mean a 50 per cent reduction in the number of new students schools can admit. Miller was still working with provinces on specific allocations, but Ontario, British Columbia and Nova Scotia were expected to have to make the biggest reductions.
Other provinces that were not as aggressive about recruiting could actually be able to increase the number of international students they bring in.
The federal government specifically exempted Masters and PhD students from the change, but otherwise will leave the decision on how to use allocations to provincial governments, forcing premiers to pick whether to send more international students or dollars to schools.
Mikal Skuterud, an economics professor at the University of Waterloo, said provinces that have to reduce their international student numbers should see which schools produce the best long-term financial outcomes for graduates, which is data the government already has.
"Avoid the politics, look at the data, let the data do the talking. I really would encourage them to do that."
Skuterud said the international student program became a way to bring in low-wage labour in many cases, which it never should have been.
"The student program is not about plugging holes in low-skilled labour markets, but that's kind of what it's become in a lot of ways."
He said the federal decision to lower the number of hours an international student can work, brought in last fall, should also help.
Alongside the labour market implications, provincial governments will have to decide how to handle the impact on universities and colleges, which, especially in Ontario, have become reliant on international student money.
Ontario Auditor General Bonnie Lysyk warned in 2022 that the provinces'institutions were becoming too reliant on international students to keep their finances in order.
"A high reliance on international student enrolment by universities poses risks outside of the ministry's and the universities'control," she said Lysyk examined the system as a whole with detailed audits of some schools. She found many Ontario schools relied heavily on international students to make their finances work. Algoma University in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for example has a satellite campus in Brampton where 90 per cent of students were international students. She found that the Brampton campus was responsible for half the school's enrolment and 65 per cent of its revenue.
After Laurentian University sought creditor protection, Lysyk examined that school's books and found they took a significant hit when Saudi Arabia pulled students from the school during a diplomatic dispute with Ottawa.
"Laurentian lost 130 students from Saudi Arabia. These students would have paid an estimated $3 million in tuition revenues and ancillary fees over the full length of their degrees," she said.
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, said the cut to international students is coming at a time when universities are struggling with a tuition freeze and no new money coming from the provincial government.
"Before today's announcement, there were at least 10 universities facing sizable operating deficits, totalling $175 million this year and growing to $273 million next year," he said.
Orsini said universities have been more responsible about bringing in international students and called on the government to give them more time to adapt.
Ontario Advanced Education Minister Jill Dunlop wasn't available for an interview, but in a statement following Miller's decision she said the province would focus on bad actors in the system.
"We know some bad actors are taking advantage of these students with false promises of guaranteed employment, residency, and Canadian citizenship. We've been engaging with the federal government on ways to crack down on these practices, like predatory recruitment."
In 2021, Lysyk raised the same concerns she had with the province's universities with the province's colleges, noting they were growing primarily to serve international students.
"Between 2012/13 and 2020/21, public colleges experienced a 15 per cent decline in domestic student enrolments but a 342 per cent growth in international student enrolments," she said.
In addition to bringing international students to their own campuses, some Ontario colleges started partnerships with private colleges where the college's curriculum was taught at private colleges with a licensing fee in place.
Students would attend the private colleges but receive a diploma from the public college if they graduated. Algonquin College in Ottawa operates such an arrangement with CDI college in North York and Mississauga, bringing in $4.3 million a year. Fanshawe College in London has a similar program and while the school doesn't break down the revenue in its financial statements, it notes enrolment was almost 20 per cent higher than the school expected in 2022.
In addition to the cap, Miller announced people taking part in these programs would no longer be able to claim a postgraduate work permit, making them much less desirable for international students.
National Post rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 439 of 500

Emergencies Act karma comes back to bite the Liberals

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/emergencies-act-karma-comes-back-bite-liberals/docview/2918377443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Emergencies+Act+karma+comes+back+to+bite+the+Liberals&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is not surprising that judges often disagree after witnessing the same evidence. They are, after all, simply lawyers in robes.
Federal Court Justice Richard Moseley ruled on Tuesday that the Liberal government did not have reasonable grounds to use the Emergencies Act to clear Freedom Convoy protesters in 2022, and doing so was an unreasonable overreach that infringed Canadians'Charter rights Nearly a year earlier, Justice Paul Rouleau's Public Order Emergency Commission resolved, albeit reluctantly, that the government was justified and the Act's threshold was met.
"I did not come to this conclusion easily, as I do not consider the factual basis for it to be overwhelming and I acknowledge there is significant strength to the argument against reaching it," Rouleau wrote in a decision that was hailed as a total vindication by the government.
What can we surmise about why these two seasoned judges ruled the way they did and what is the likely effect of Moseley's decision? Both justices were clear that the protests were objectionable and represented an unacceptable breakdown of public order.
But there appear to have been two pivotal differences between the way Rouleau and Moseley interpreted the evidence that explain why they came to differing conclusions.
Anyone who watched the public inquiry hearings will remember the appearance of then public safety minister Marco Mendicino. He testified that on Feb. 13, 2022 he had been told by then RC-MP commissioner Brenda Lucki that there were likely armed protesters in Coutts, Alta. (That was only confirmed the day after the conversation with a raid on two camper trucks and a mobile home, that found 14 firearms, ammunition and body armour).
The prospect was enough to spook Mendicino, who then conveyed the message to the government's Incident Response Group on the afternoon of Feb. 13.
By the evening, at a full cabinet meeting, Justin Trudeau had all but taken the decision to invoke the Emergencies Act. Mendicino told Rouleau: "It underlined to me that there was a hardened cell of individuals, armed to the teeth with lethal firearms, who possessed a willingness to go down with the cause."
Mendicino said he was worried about a potential chain reaction.
"Lives literally hung in the balance ... we were potentially seeing an escalation of serious violence. It was a threshold moment for me."
Rouleau appears to have accepted the "chain reaction" theory as a rationale for invoking the act, giving credence to the government's rationalization that there was a situation that was endangering the lives and safety of Canadians.
Moseley was far more dubious about Mendicino's testimony, as well he might have been. The former minister is a congenial man but, as I once described him, someone apt to fluff their putts under pressure.
Moseley explicitly rejected the idea the threshold to invoke the act was reached, based on the arms cache in Coutts. "The potential for serious violence, or being able to say there was no potential for serious violence, was, of course, a valid reason for concern. But in my view, it did not satisfy the test required to invoke the Act, particularly as there was no evidence of a similar hardened cell elsewhere in the country, only speculation," he wrote.
It is clear that Mendicino overreacted. On Feb. 13, the blockades in Coutts, Emerson, Man., and at the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor, Ont. were all largely resolved using existing police powers. Also on the 13th, Lucki told Mendicino a new, co-ordinated plan for the protest in Ottawa, involving RCMP, Ontario Provincial Police and the Ottawa Police Service, was ready to go.
Mendicino was informed by the Mounties, OPP and CSIS that there was no serious threat to Canada's sovereignty, as the act required as "a tool of last resort."
However, the key members of cabinet were already on the edge on the 13th: Justin Trudeau had received a call two days before from U.S. President Joe Biden, concerned about U.S. auto plants in the northeast facing closure because of the Ambassador Bridge blockade. Opinion polls showed seven out of 10 Canadians wanted the government to use force to remove the protesters - people who Trudeau considered had "unacceptable views." It probably didn't take much to push the cabinet, and Mendicino's news about Coutts may have been the final nudge. They have been trying to rationalize it ever since.
The other key difference between Rouleau and Moseley's interpretations appears to have been that the former placed little weight on the testimony of 20 or so police officers who rejected the idea that they needed new legislative tools, particularly ones that did not add to operational capacity.
Lucki testified that she did not believe police had employed "all the available tools," another necessity under the act that states the emergency needs to be of such a proportion that it exceeds the capacity of a province to deal with it.
Moseley said the situation in Coutts had been dealt with by the RCMP employing the Criminal Code, while provincial forces in other provinces had taken the lead. "Except for Ottawa, the record does not indicate that the police in local jurisdictions were unable to deal with the protests." He wrote Ottawa was unique because the OPS were unable to enforce the rule of law.
Moseley rejected the government claim that it required the Emergencies Act to, for example, order tow trucks to remove tractor trailers from Parliament Hill.
"It is not clear to me why this could not have been dealt with using provincial legislation," he wrote.
The biggest difference between the two decisions is that, while Rouleau's report is gathering dust, Moseley's ruling is now the law.
What does it mean? For one thing, the decision is likely to end the freezing of bank accounts without clear standards and independent oversight, after Moseley ruled that the Trudeau government "unjustifiably violated Canadians'rights to be free from unreasonable search and seizure" by doing so. Targeting the bank accounts of the protesters and their supporters turned out to be a blunt instrument that affected joint account holders and credit cards issued in the accounts of other family members.
For another, it will make it a lot harder to use the act in any form in future, something that may not have been the case after Rouleau reported. Any government will have second thoughts if it thinks the entrails of its decision-making will be probed in public after the fact.
The government might try to test Parliament by amending the existing act to make it easier to invoke, for example by removing the more taxing CSIS definition of "a threat to national security." But it is not clear that it would win support in the House of Commons and it could very well undermine the government's appeal of the Federal Court's decision, which could find its way to the Supreme Court within 18 months.
The Liberals responded quickly Tuesday in announcing their plan to appeal, in all likelihood before anyone in cabinet had even read the decision. That's understandable: they believe Rouleau gave them a clean bill of health. In their eyes they did nothing wrong because how can you have done wrong when you have good intentions? But anyone who has looked closely at the evidence probably thinks they were lucky to get away with Rouleau's inquiry findings and Moseley has simply furnished some judicial karma.
National Post jivison@criffel.ca

Document 441 of 500

January 24, 2024 (Page A4)

Document 446 of 500

Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act was ruled unconstitutional. What happens now?

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeaus-use-emergencies-act-was-ruled/docview/2918289681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+use+of+the+Emergencies+Act+was+ruled+unconstitutional.+What+happens+now%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - In a landmark ruling, a Federal Court judge ruled Tuesday that the Liberal government unconstitutionally and unjustifiably invoked the Emergencies Act in response to ongoing Freedom Convoy blockades in early 2022.
So what does that mean for the government?
What did the Federal Court rule exactly?
On Feb. 14, 2022, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government announced it was invoking the exceptional powers of the Emergencies Act for the first time in response to ongoing, weeks-long Freedom Convoy blockades in Ottawa that thwarted police control.
The Act, invoked for just over one week, was used to freeze some convoy participants' bank accounts, compel tow truck companies to co-operate with local police clearing out blockades and mark parts of downtown Ottawa as a no-go zone. It was also meant to prevent protesters who had blockaded the US-Canada border in Ontario and Alberta from returning.
Civil liberty groups quickly opposed the measure in court. Now, nearly two years later, Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley said they were mostly right: the Freedom Convoy did not meet the high threshold of a threat to national security necessary to invoke the law. The "harm" to Canada's economy did not equate to a threat or the use of serious violence.
The government also breached certain ("peaceful") Canadians' Charter rights when blocking some bank accounts and banning them from downtown Ottawa.
What was the government's response?
Within hours of the ruling's publication, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland promised to appeal it. Is that based on a solid and meditated legal analysis by government lawyers? Probably not, says Leah West, national security law expert and assistant professor at Carleton University.
"The fact that there was the response that 'we're going to appeal' before a full reckoning or appreciation of the decision shows that the response is a political one and the appeal would be of a political nature," she said.
Does the ruling mean the Emergencies Act is unconstitutional?
Nope. Mosley made it very clear that his decision only weighs in on the Trudeau government's use of the Emergencies Act in February 2022, and not on the legality of the Act as a whole.
Had any of the opponents to the invocation argued the Act should be invalidated, Mosley said he would have considered the issue meritless anyway.
"This case was not about the constitutionality of the Act but, rather, how it was applied in this instance," he wrote.
Are there any legal consequences to Mosley's decision?
Lots! But likely not in the immediate.
The immediate political impact is far greater for Justin Trudeau and his government, says Université Laval constitutional law professor Patrick Taillon.
"On a political and symbolic level, the impact is enormous. This changes the equation in the lead up to the next federal election," he said.
"On a partisan level, this is far more advantageous for (Conservative leader) Pierre Poilievre than for Justin Trudeau. The fact that a court, with all of its authority, says that it reflected, wrote a 190 page ruling and found that the Trudeau government illegally violated the Charter will have a lot of weight. It will put the government on the defensive."
John Ivison: Emergencies Act karma comes back to bite the Liberals
Chris Selley: Violating the Charter with the Emergencies Act came from a broken system
The main legal consequence is that the ruling now sets a clearer - and possibly higher - bar for future governments to invoke the Act, according to experts.
"It wasn't clear, and you want (the moment) when an emergency meets the definition such that you can invoke these extreme powers to be very freaking clear," West said.
She added that Mosley's ruling will also prevent any future "clever stretching" of the application of the Act in future potential emergency situations.
West noted the ruling also hammers the point that the Emergencies Act needs to be modernized to better reflect modern-day threats that weren't captured when it was enacted in 1988. That was also recommended by Public Order Emergency Commission head Paul Rouleau last year.
Taillon agreed with the need to reform the law. He also said the ruling sets a higher threshold for future governments to invoke the Act, which concerns him.
"In an emergency situation, you don't want the straitjacket to be too tight either. You don't want to create a chilling effect where governments, in an emergency situation or crisis... that is more important than the one in 2022, say they can't invoke the Act because of a court's order," Taillon said.
Joanna Baron, executive director of the Canadian Constitution Foundation which was part of the successful lawsuit against the government, disagreed.
"I say the ruling restores the threshold to what it was intended to be" when Brian Mulroney's Progressive Conservative government enacted the Act in 1988, Baron said. "I wouldn't say it raises (the threshold)."
What about people affected by Emergency Act measures, can they sue or something?
Quite possibly! But if so, they'll have to do it fast.
West, Taillon and Baron agreed that the ruling could offer some opportunity to individuals impacted by the powers of the Act (like those who had their bank accounts frozen during the Convoy protests) to launch a civil lawsuit against authorities.
"It's not impossible that that occurs. It's like the judge said that the public order emergency is invalid, so the temporary rules that were put in place against (some protestors) ... are cancelled. So the consequences of those measures are attackable," Taillon said.
But they'll have to overcome a number of significant hurdles, the most significant likely being Ontario's two-year statute of limitations for most civil cases. If their bank accounts were frozen in February 2022 and it's now late January 2024...
"Nobody knows for sure what the implications are going to be," Baron said. "It certainly can't hurt that you now have a judicial precedent and a judicial declaration that the government acted unlawfully. That would be something like circumstantial evidence of negligent behavior that could ground a civil claim."
"But I would be lying if I said anybody knows for sure," she added.

Document 447 of 500

International student cap will hit university and college finances, especially in Ontario

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/international-student-cap-will-hit-university/docview/2918289672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=International+student+cap+will+hit+university+and+college+finances%2C+especially+in+Ontario&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Liberals' cap on international students could hit some schools in Ontario particularly hard, as schools there have become reliant on the higher tuition fees those students pay to keep their books balanced.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced the cap on Monday, saying his department will issue no more than 360,000 new visas this year, 35 per cent lower than last year, and keep them at that number for two years.
The visas will be handed out proportionally to provinces based on their population, which will force even larger cuts at some schools in Ontario. Schools in the province have been aggressive about bringing in international students and the new cap could mean a 50 per cent reduction in the number of new students schools can admit. Miller was still working with provinces on specific allocations, but Ontario, British Columbia and Nova Scotia were expected to have to make the biggest reductions. Other provinces that were not as aggressive about recruiting could actually be able to increase the number of international students they bring in.
The federal government specifically exempted Masters and PhD students from the change, but otherwise will leave the decision on how to use allocations to provincial governments, forcing premiers to pick whether to send more international students or dollars to schools.
Mikal Skuterud, an economics professor at the University of Waterloo, said provinces that have to reduce their international student numbers should see which schools produce the best long-term financial outcomes for graduates, which is data the government already has.
"Avoid the politics, look at the data, let the data do the talking. I really would encourage them to do that."
Skuterud said the international student program became a way to bring in low-wage labour in many cases, which it never should have been.
"The student program is not about plugging holes in low skilled labour markets, but that's kind of what it's become in a lot of ways."
Ontario Auditor General Bonnie Lysyk has been critical of the province's universities and colleges for growing primarily to serve international students.
He said the federal decision to lower the number of hours an international student can work, brought in last fall, should also help.
Alongside the labour market implications, provincial governments will have to decide how to handle the impact on universities and colleges, which, especially in Ontario, have become reliant on international student money.
Ontario's Auditor General Bonnie Lysyk warned in 2022 that the provinces' institutions were becoming too reliant on international students to keep their finances in order.
"A high reliance on international student enrolment by universities poses risks outside of the Ministry's and the universities' control," she said
Lysyk examined the system as a whole with detailed audits of some schools. She found many Ontario schools relied heavily on international students to make their finances work. Algoma University in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for example has a satellite campus in Brampton where 90 per cent of students were international students. She found that the Brampton campus was responsible for half the school's enrolment and 65 per cent of its revenue.
After Laurentian University sought creditor protection in 2021, Lysyk examined that school's books and found they took a significant hit when Saudi Arabia pulled students from the school during a diplomatic dispute with Ottawa.
"Laurentian lost 130 students from Saudi Arabia. These students would have paid an estimated $3 million in tuition revenues and ancillary fees over the full length of their degrees," she said.
Steve Orsini, President and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities said the cut to international students is coming at a time when universities are struggling with a tuition freeze and no new money coming from the provincial government.
"Before today's announcement, there were at least 10 universities facing sizable operating deficits, totalling $175 million this year and growing to $273 million next year," he said.
Orsini said universities have been more responsible about bringing in international students and called on the government to give them more time to adapt.
"We believe the federal government must take the time to target those who do not provide supports to students, as well as those that have seen incredible growth over the past two years."
Universities, colleges worried after federal Liberals announce international student cap
Jamie Sarkonak: Visa cap won't stop relentless exploitation of international students
Ontario's Advanced Education Minister Jill Dunlop wasn't available for an interview, but in a statement following Miller's decision she said the province would focus on bad actors in the system.
"We know some bad actors are taking advantage of these students with false promises of guaranteed employment, residency, and Canadian citizenship. We've been engaging with the federal government on ways to crack down on these practices, like predatory recruitment."
In 2021, Lysyk raised the same concerns she had with the province's universities with the province's colleges, noting they were growing primarily to serve international students.
"Between 2012/13 and 2020/21, public colleges experienced a 15 per cent decline in domestic student enrolments but a 342 per cent growth in international student enrolments," she said.
Brenda Bereczki, director of communications for Conestoga College, which has seen huge growth in international students, said they worry these changes are being rushed and coming in the middle of recruitment season for the fall semester.
"We worry the timelines for such substantive changes are too short to engage in the kind of meaningful consultation we need. The announcement has created a great deal of uncertainty. We are committed to putting our students first and want to reduce the anxiety about what this means for them," she said in an email.
She said the international students they bring in are filling key labour gaps and shouldn't be turned away.
"The international students we welcome at Conestoga are essential to bringing in top talent for key sectors of our workforce and typically come with an existing diploma or degree so they can move quickly into the labour market."
In addition to bringing international students to their own campuses, some Ontario colleges started partnerships with private colleges where the college's curriculum was taught at private colleges with a licensing fee in place.
Students would attend the private colleges but receive a diploma from the public college if they graduated. Algonquin College in Ottawa operates such an arrangement with CDI college in North York and Mississauga, bringing in $4.3 million a year. Fanshawe College in London has a similar program and while the school doesn't break down the revenue in its financial statements it notes enrolment was almost 20 per cent higher than the school expected in 2022.
In addition to the cap, Miller announced people taking part in these programs would no longer be able to claim a postgraduate work permit, making them much less desirable for international students.
National Post
rtumilty@postmedia.com

Location: Saudi Arabia

Title: International student cap will hit university and college finances, especially in Ontario

Document 448 of 500

John Ivison: Emergencies Act karma comes back to bite the Liberals

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/john-ivison-emergencies-act-karma-comes-back-bite/docview/2918274196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=John+Ivison%3A+Emergencies+Act+karma+comes+back+to+bite+the+Liberals&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is not surprising that judges often disagree after witnessing the same evidence. They are, after all, simply lawyers in robes.
Federal Court Justice Richard Moseley ruled on Tuesday that the Liberal government did not have reasonable grounds to use the Emergencies Act to clear Freedom Convoy protestors in 2022, and doing so was an unreasonable overreach that infringed Canadians' Charter rights
Nearly a year earlier Justice Paul Rouleau's Public Order Emergency Commission resolved, albeit reluctantly, that the government was justified and the Act's threshold was met.
"I did not come to this conclusion easily, as I do not consider the factual basis for it to be overwhelming and I acknowledge there is significant strength to the argument against reaching it," Rouleau wrote in a decision that was hailed as a total vindication by the government.
Christine Van Geyn: Emergencies Act was clearly unjustified - Liberals face steep path to appeal
Chris Selley: Violating the Charter with the Emergencies Act came from a broken system
What can we surmise about why these two seasoned judges ruled the way they did and what is the likely impact of Moseley's decision?
Both justices were clear that the protests were objectionable and represented an unacceptable breakdown of public order.
But there appear to have been two pivotal differences between the way Rouleau and Moseley interpreted the evidence that explain why they came to differing conclusions.
Anyone who watched the public inquiry hearings will remember the appearance of then public safety minister Marco Mendicino. He testified that on Feb. 13th 2022 he had been told by then RCMP commissioner Brenda Lucki that there were likely armed protestors in Coutts, Alta. (That was only confirmed the day after the conversation with a raid on two camper trucks and a mobile home, that found 14 firearms, ammunition and body armour).
The prospect was enough to spook Mendicino, who then conveyed the message to the government's Incident Response Group on the afternoon of the 13th.
By the evening, at a full cabinet meeting, Justin Trudeau had all but taken the decision to invoke the Emergencies Act. Mendicino told Rouleau: "It underlined to me that there was a hardened cell of individuals, armed to the teeth with lethal firearms, who possessed a willingness to go down with the cause."
Mendicino said he was worried about a potential chain reaction. "Lives literally hung in the balance...we were potentially seeing an escalation of serious violence. It was a threshold moment for me."
Rouleau appears to have accepted the "chain reaction" theory as a rationale for invoking the act, giving credence to the government's rationalization that there was a situation that was endangering the lives and safety of Canadians.
Moseley was far more dubious about Mendicino's testimony, as well he might have been. The former minister is a congenial man but, as I once described him, someone apt to fluff their putts under pressure.
Moseley explicitly rejected the idea the threshold to invoke the act was reached, based on the arms cache in Coutts. "The potential for serious violence, or being able to say there was no potential for serious violence, was, of course, a valid reason for concern. But in my view, it did not satisfy the test required to invoke the Act, particularly as there was no evidence of a similar hardened cell elsewhere in the country, only speculation," he wrote.
It is clear that Mendicino overreacted.
On the 13th, the blockades in Coutts, Emerson, Man., and at the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor, Ont. were all largely resolved using existing police powers. Also on the 13th, Lucki told Mendicino a new, co-ordinated plan for the protest in Ottawa, involving RCMP, Ontario Provincial Police and the Ottawa Police Service, was ready to go.
Mendicino was informed by the Mounties, OPP and CSIS that there was no serious threat to Canada's sovereignty, as the act required as "a tool of last resort."
However, the key members of cabinet were already on the edge on the 13th: Justin Trudeau had received a call two days before from President Joe Biden, concerned about U.S. auto plants in the northeast facing closure because of the Ambassador Bridge blockade. Opinion polls showed seven out of 10 Canadians wanted the government to use force to remove the protestors - people who Trudeau considered had "unacceptable views." It probably didn't take much to push the cabinet, and Mendicino's news about Coutts may have been the final nudge. They have been trying to rationalize it ever since.
The other key difference between Rouleau and Moseley's interpretations appears to have been that the former placed little weight on the testimony of 20 or so police officers who rejected the idea that they needed new legislative tools, particularly ones that did not add to operational capacity.
Lucki testified that she did not believe police had employed "all the available tools," another necessity under the act that states the emergency needs to be of such a proportion that it exceeds the capacity of a province to deal with it.
Moseley said the situation in Coutts had been dealt with by the RCMP employing the Criminal Code, while provincial forces in other provinces had taken the lead. "Except for Ottawa, the record does not indicate that the police in local jurisdictions were unable to deal with the protests." He wrote Ottawa was unique because the OPS were unable to enforce the rule of law.
Moseley rejected the government claim that it required the Emergencies Act to, for example, order tow trucks to remove tractor trailers from Parliament Hill. "It is not clear to me why this could not have been dealt with using provincial legislation," he wrote.
The biggest difference between the two decisions is that, while Rouleau's report is gathering dust, Moseley's ruling is now the law.
What does it mean?
For one thing, the decision is likely to end the freezing of bank accounts without clear standards and independent oversight, after Moseley ruled that the Trudeau government "unjustifiably violated Canadians rights to be free from unreasonable search and seizure" by doing so.Targeting the bank accounts of protestors turned out to be a blunt instrument that affected joint account holders and credit cards issued in the accounts of other family members.
For another, it will make it a lot harder to use the act in any form in future, something that may not have been the case after Rouleau reported. Any government will have second thoughts if it thinks the entrails of its decision-making will be probed in public after the fact.
The government might try to test Parliament by amending the existing act to make it easier to invoke, for example by removing the more taxing CSIS definition of "a threat to national security." But it is not clear that it would win support in the House of Commons and it could very well undermine the government's appeal of the Federal Court's decision, which could find its way to the Supreme Court within 18 months.
The Liberals responded quickly Tuesday in announcing their plan to appeal, in all likelihood before anyone in cabinet had even read the decision. That's understandable: they believe Rouleau gave them a clean bill of health.
In their eyes they did nothing wrong because how can you have done wrong when you have good intentions?
But anyone who has looked closely at the evidence probably thinks they were lucky to get away with Rouleau's inquiry findings and Moseley has simply furnished some judicial karma.
National Post
jivison@criffel.ca( mailto:jivison@criffel.ca )

Chris Selley: Violating the Charter with the Emergencies Act came from a broken system

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-violating-charter-with-emergencies/docview/2918162701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chris+Selley%3A+Violating+the+Charter+with+the+Emergencies+Act+came+from+a+broken+system&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If we are to learn anything useful from the weeks-long blockade of downtown Ottawa two years ago - a big "if," and a probable "no" - it's important to focus as much on what Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley didn't say in his Tuesday decision as on what he did.
In his ruling on a challenge by several civil liberties groups to the government's use of the act, Mosley did not find that invoking the Emergencies Act violated protesters' right to freedom of association, for example.
"They were free to communicate with each other in pursuit of their collective goals and form whatever organization they thought necessary to do so elsewhere," he wrote - just not in a weeks-long blockade of a G7 capital or while blocking a Canada-U.S. border crossing.
Mosley did not find that the Emergencies Act violated Section 7 of the Charter, which codifies the right to "life, liberty and security of the person."
"The regulations prohibited only a narrow, defined range of activities and did so for no more than nine days," he wrote. "The regulations were tailored to limit constitutional rights no more than reasonably necessary to address the issues."
Crucially, Mosley did not find that invoking the Emergencies Act violated protesters' Charter right to peaceful assembly
"Gatherings that employ physical force, in the form of enduring or intractable occupations of public space that block local residents' ability to carry out the functions of their daily lives, in order to compel agreement (with the protesters' objective), are not constitutionally protected," government lawyers argued.
Mosley agreed entirely.
Court rules Liberals' use of Emergencies Act was unjustified, unreasonable
Emergencies Act was clearly unjustified - Liberals face steep path to appeal
His ruling is by no means sympathetic to the Freedom Convoy. "(At the time) I considered the events that occurred in Ottawa and other locations in January and February 2022 went beyond legitimate protest and reflected an unacceptable breakdown of public order," he wrote. "I had and continue to have considerable sympathy for those in government who were confronted with this situation."
Had he been in that situation at the time, he admitted, he probably would have supported the Emergency Act. That's where most people were: A Maru Public Opinion poll released three days after it was invoked found two-thirds of respondents nationwide wanted the protesters cleared out by hook, crook or war measures, even if "people who will not leave may get hurt, or worse."
The figure would be something like 99 per cent, I suspect, if a big honking convoy pitched camp outside every Canadian's house for a month and the Emergencies Act (or the threat thereof) was the only way to get rid of them ... which in the end, in Ottawa, it kind of was.
The biggest victors in Mosley's ruling, to my mind, are less the Freedom Convoy crowd than this country's minuscule community of principled civil libertarians - people who oppose government overreach even when they dislike the "victims" of it and can't see any other obvious way to solve the problem at hand.
Mosley found that the seizing of bank accounts under Emergencies Act regulations breached Section 8 of the Charter, which guarantees freedom from unreasonable search or seizure of property.
"Of particular concern ... is that there was no standard applied to determine whether someone should be the target of the (economic) measures or (any) process to allow them to question that determination," wrote Mosley. "As described by (RCMP) Supt. (Denis) Beaudoin in cross-examination, it was all informal and ad hoc." Not good.
More fundamentally, Mosley argued, the government's notion that the occupation represented a "national emergency" or a "national security threat" simply never held up.
"While I agree that ... the situation was critical and required an urgent resolution by governments, the evidence, in my view, does not support the conclusion that it could not have been effectively dealt with under other laws of Canada, as it was in Alberta, or that it exceeded the capacity or authority of a province to deal with it," Mosley wrote, referring to necessary preconditions under the Emergencies Act.
Quebec shut down trucker protests in Quebec with a stern warning rather than the Emergencies Act, Mosley noted, and Alberta dealt with the blockade at the Coutts border crossing under existing laws and regulations as well, despite the presence of weapons and genuine extremists.
And, well, there's the rub: Basically, as it often is in matters such as these, this is pretty much all Ontario's fault.
It's one thing to have the legal "capacity" and "authority" to clear out protesters who step over the line. All provinces have that. It's another thing to have the willingness to do it.
In early 2022, Ottawa police had none. Ontario police forces have none as a general rule ... though the relatively quick and painless end to the blockade of the Detroit-Windsor crossing showed it is, in fact, possible for Ontario cops to enforcea court injunction without first defying it for weeks. So maybe it's more accurate to say this is pretty much all Ottawa's fault - the city, not the feds. The federal government did overreach, clearly, but should never have been forced to decide whether to take extraordinary measures in the first place.
That a Federal Court judge would in 100-odd pages both sympathize with the government's decision and find it to have been ultra vires and Charter-deficient is a pretty perfect outcome for a broken country like Canada: The only things that work, we mustn't do. Pending appeal to the Supreme Court, at least.
National Post
cselley@postmedia.com

Document 450 of 500

Invoking Emergencies Act 'Not Justified,'Court Rules; Judge Finds Government'S Decision Violated Charter.Expect An Appeal, Freeland Says

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/invoking-emergencies-act-not-justified-court/docview/2918027533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Invoking+Emergencies+Act+%27Not+Justified%2C%27Court+Rules%3B+Judge+Finds+Government%27S+Decision+Violated+Charter.Expect+An+Appeal%2C+Freeland+Says&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal government's decision to invoke the Emergencies Act in response to the 2022 Freedom Convoy protests was unreasonable, unjustified and violated the Charter, the Federal Court has ruled.
In a lengthy ruling published Tuesday, Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley Mosley sided with civil liberties groups who argued the Liberal government went beyond its powers, violated the Charter and was not justified in its historic invocation of the Emergencies Act in February 2022.
"I have concluded that the decision to issue the Proclamation (of the Emergencies Act) does not bear the hallmarks of reasonableness - justification, transparency and intelligibility - and was not justified," Mosley wrote.
Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said the government did not agree with Mosley's decision and promised to appeal it.
"The public safety of Canadians was under threat, our national security, which includes our national economic security, was under threat," Mosley's ruling stems from four legal challenges by civil liberty groups, including the Canadian Civil Liberties Association (CCLA) and the Canadian Constitutional Foundation (CCF).
The groups argued the Liberals exceeded their powers when they invoked the Act on Feb. 14, 2022, in response to ongoing Freedom Convoy blockades in Ontario and Alberta.
At the time, the Act was largely used to freeze some convoy participants'bank accounts, compel tow truck companies to co-operate with local police clearing out blockades and mark parts of downtown Ottawa as a no-go zone.
"Emergency is not in the eye of the beholder. Emergency powers are necessary in extreme circumstances, but they are also dangerous to democracy. They should be used sparingly and carefully. They cannot be used even to address a massive and disruptive demonstration if that could have been dealt with through regular policing and laws," the CCLA said in a statement.
In coming to his conclusion, the judge found that using the Act to create a nogo zone in downtown Ottawa to clear out protesters infringed on "peaceful" participants'freedom of expression.
He was also "concerned" about how government went about freezing some protesters'bank accounts without concern for collateral effects, namely on family members and joint account holders.
Thus, the measure breached their Charter right to be secure against unreasonable search and seizure, he concluded.
"Someone who had nothing to do with the protests could find themselves without the means to access necessaries for household and other family purposes while the accounts were suspended. There appears to have been no effort made to find a solution to that problem while the measures were in effect," reads the ruling.
The Emergencies Act relies on a definition of a threat to the security of Canada embedded in the legislation that governs CSIS. When justifying its invocation, the Liberals argued they had reasonable grounds to believe the protests posed a threat to national security and could not be dealt with any other way.
Mosley disagreed. "The record does not support a conclusion that the Convoy had created a critical, urgent and temporary situation that was national in scope and could not effectively be dealt with under any other law of Canada," he wrote. "The harm being caused to Canada's economy, trade and commerce, was very real and concerning but it did not constitute threats or the use of serious violence to persons or property," he added.
The ruling does not propose any remedies nor does it declare any part of the Emergencies Act unconstitutional. "This case was not about the constitutionality of the Act but, rather, how it was applied in this instance," the judge wrote.
Displaying an uncommon level of introspection in a ruling, Mosley admitted that at the outset of the hearing, his view was "leaning" toward the belief that the government's invocation of the Act was reasonable.
He said he considered the events of the convoy to have gone "beyond legitimate protest" and were an "unacceptable breakdown of public order." He also admitted that had he been at the government's table at the time of the decision, he may have agreed it was necessary to invoke the Act.
"I acknowledge that in conducting judicial review of that decision, I am revisiting that time with the benefit of hindsight and a more extensive record of the facts and law than that which was before the (government)," he wrote.
But months of time to "carefully deliberate" on the evidence and arguments, as well as arguments by the CCF and CCLA, changed his mind.
"Their participation in these proceedings has demonstrated again the value of public interest litigants. Especially in presenting informed legal argument. This case may not have turned out the way it has without their involvement," he wrote.
Mosley's conclusion contrasts starkly with that of Commissioner Paul Rouleau, who headed the public inquiry into the government's use of the Emergencies Act last year. In his report, he concluded the Liberals were justified in using the Act but appeared to leave the door open to a different conclusion by the Federal Court.
"I have concluded that cabinet was reasonably concerned that the situation it was facing was worsening and at risk of becoming dangerous and unmanageable," he said, but noted the evidence was not "overwhelming," and "reasonable and informed" people could reach a different conclusion.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre reacted to the news on X (formerly Twitter) by placing the blame solely on Trudeau. "He caused the crisis by dividing people. Then he violated Charter rights to illegally suppress citizens. As PM, I will unite our country for freedom," he wrote.
National Post

Document 451 of 500

Canada Has Become A Very Scary Place To Own A Vehicle Right Now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-has-become-very-scary-place-own-vehicle/docview/2918025278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canada+Has+Become+A+Very+Scary+Place+To+Own+A+Vehicle+Right+Now&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Seven Ford pickup trucks were stolen by thieves on Sunday night across three locations in central Ontario, marking the latest incident in Canada's growing trend of vehicle thefts.
The Ford F-150s, most of which were 2023 models, were pilfered from residences in Barrie, Orangeville and Mono, with at least one of the thefts being caught on video surveillance.
The spree is just the latest incident in a problem that has been surging over the last few years. Industry reports have noted that both Ontario and Quebec have seen a nearly 50 per cent increase in vehicle thefts since 2022. According to a 2023 report from the Canadian Finance and Leasing Association (CFLA), a vehicle is now stolen every six minutes.
Here's what to know about Canada's auto theft crisis.
WHERE ARE ALL THE STOLEN CARS HEADED?
Recently, a Toronto man who had two cars stolen in the same year used multiple AirTags to track his 2022 GMC Yukon XL after it was stolen from his driveway.
According to that report, the vehicle was taken to a nearby rail yard and then the Port of Montreal, before ending up at a used car facility in the United Arab Emirates.
That journey is in line with previous reports, that have found stolen vehicles from Canada are increasingly ending up in Africa and the Middle East.
Last September, an investigation by CBC found dozens of stolen vehicles, many with Ontario and Quebec licence plates, in a car lot in Ghana, the second-most populous country in West Africa.
The vehicles, some of which still contained their Canadian registration documents, were being off-loaded through various online channels, including social media, with buyers paying close to market price.
WHY IS AUTO THEFT AT RECORD LEVELS IN CANADA?
The problem has been increasing for years but accelerated during the pandemic.
According to the not-forprofit insurance industry group Équité Association, vehicle thefts in Canada soared in 2022, with both Quebec and Ontario seeing spikes of around 50 per cent, while Atlantic Canada saw an increase of more than 34 per cent.
Last June, a report from CFLA noted that Toronto saw a 300 per cent increase in vehicle thefts from 2015 to 2022, with the problem growing progressively worse each year. In 2022, more than 9,600 vehicles were stolen in Toronto alone.
Other reports have noted an increased demand for vehicles and auto parts that has intensified due to supply chain disruptions affecting the automotive industry, while advancements in car technology, like keyless entry systems, have made it easier for tech-savvy thieves to nab vehicles.
The involvement of organized crime has also led to the establishment of largescale, well-resourced vehicle theft operations with the proceeds funding domestic drug trafficking, international terrorism and other efforts.
Others point to a lack of enforcement.
"We've become a global donor in stolen vehicles," Michael Rothe of the Canadian Financing and Leasing Association told CBC. "From our perspective, it's a lack of enforcement."
WHATMODELS ARE MOST LIKELY TO BE STOLEN?
The most stolen vehicle in Canada in 2022 was the Honda CR-V, for the second year in a row.
Per data from the Équité Association, here is the full list of the top five most stolen vehicles in 2022, by make and model: 1. 2020 Honda CR-V 2. 2022 Dodge Ram 1500 series 3. 2020 Ford F150 series 4. 2020 Lexus RX series 5. 2021 Toyota Highlander According to Équité, criminals are increasingly targeting newer vehicles to maximize profits. The vehicles get sold overseas or, in some cases, are given a new Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) to deceive unsuspecting Canadian buyers domestically.
WHAT ARE THE FEDS PLANNING TO DO ABOUT IT?
Earlier this week, the federal Liberals announced a national summit on auto theft aimed at getting provinces and industry officials together to address the growing issue of cars being stolen and shipped abroad.
The government says an uptick in stolen cars has prompted it to plan a Feb. 8 meeting to stop organized crime from profiting off of cars stolen on Canadian streets.
The Liberals point to industry estimates that state the rates of auto theft went up in Quebec by half in 2022 compared with the year prior, and to a similar degree in Ontario.
They say police across the Greater Toronto Area have seen carjackings double during that time and soar 300 per cent since 2015.
The government says gangs steal cars and work with organized-crime groups to send them to the Middle East and Africa, or use them within Canada to commit crimes before destroying the vehicles.
That's despite Ottawa insisting it has strong laws aimed at preventing thefts and tracking protocols meant to stop the stolen cars from being exported in shipping containers.
The summit is in line with calls from industry experts, who have increasingly called for a co-ordinated, national response to rising auto thefts, including more prosecutions and theft prevention education.
The Équité Association has also called on Transport Canada to update Canada's federal Motor Vehicle Safety Regulations. The regulations have not been updated since 2007, before the era of keyless and remote start technologies.
"Criminals are now taking advantage of the outdated standards," said Bryan Gast, a certified fraud investigator with Équité Association. "They can quickly and easily exploit these vulnerabilities, which has led to this significant increase in stolen vehicles across Canada."
Last May, the Ontario government announced it was investing $51 million over the next three years to combat vehicle theft, including establishing "auto theft prosecution teams" to investigate and prosecute criminal organizations, but others say more action is needed at the federal level.
National Post, with additional reporting from The Canadian Press

Subject: Organized crime; Automobile theft; Robbery; Vehicles; Criminal investigations

January 23, 2024 (Page A1)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/january-23-2024-page-a1/docview/2919613868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apdnnationalpost&amp;atitle=January+23%2C+2024+%28Page+A1%29&amp;title=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=26&amp;issue=73&amp;spage=A1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 453 of 500

January 23, 2024 (Page A5)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/january-23-2024-page-a5/docview/2919613847/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 458 of 500

Canada has become a very scary place to own a vehicle right now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-has-become-very-scary-place-own-vehicle/docview/2917893616/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canada+has+become+a+very+scary+place+to+own+a+vehicle+right+now&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Seven Ford pickup trucks were stolen by thieves on Sunday night across three locations in central Ontario, marking the latest incident in Canada's growing trend of vehicle thefts.
The Ford F-150s, most of which were 2023 models, were pilfered from residences in Barrie, Orangeville and Mono, with at least one of the thefts being caught on video surveillance.
The spree is just the latest incident in a problem that has been surging over the last few years. Industry reports have noted that both Ontario and Quebec have seen a nearly 50 per cent increase in vehicle thefts since 2022. According to a 2023 report from the Canadian Finance and Leasing Association (CFLA), a vehicle is now stolen every six minutes.
Here's what to know about Canada's auto theft crisis.
Stolen Canadian cars are ending up in Africa and auto industry says not enough is being done to stop it
Auto theft is a 'national crisis' - the most and least stolen cars in Canada
Where are all the stolen cars headed?
Recently, a Toronto man who had two cars stolen in the same year used multiple AirTags to track his 2022 GMC Yukon XL after it was stolen from his driveway.
According to that report, the vehicle was taken to a nearby rail yard and then the Port of Montreal, before ending up at a used car facility in the United Arab Emirates.
That journey is in line with previous reports, that have found stolen vehicles from Canada are increasingly ending up in Africa and the Middle East
Last September, an investigation by CBC found dozens of stolen vehicles, many with Ontario and Quebec license plates, in a car lot in Ghana, the second-most populous country in West Africa.
The vehicles, some of which still contained their Canadian registration documents, were being off-loaded through various online channels, including social media, with buyers paying close to market price.
Why is auto theft at record-breaking levels in Canada?
The problem has been increasing for years but accelerated during the pandemic.
According to the not-for-profit insurance industry group Équité Association, vehicle thefts in Canada soared in 2022, with both Quebec and Ontario seeing spikes of around 50 per cent, while Atlantic Canada saw an increase of more than 34 per cent.
Last June, a report from CFLA noted that Toronto saw a 300 per cent increase in vehicle thefts from 2015 to 2022, with the problem growing progressively worse each year. In 2022, more than 9,600 vehicles were stolen in Toronto alone.
Other reports have noted an increased demand for vehicles and auto parts that has intensified due to supply chain disruptions affecting the automotive industry, while advancements in car technology, like keyless entry systems, have made it easier for tech-savvy thieves to nab vehicles.
The involvement of organized crime has also led to the establishment of large-scale, well-resourced vehicle theft operations with the proceeds funding domestic drug trafficking, international terrorism and other efforts.
Others point to a lack of enforcement.
"We've become a global donor in stolen vehicles," Michael Rothe of the Canadian Financing and Leasing Association told CBC "From our perspective, it's a lack of enforcement."
What vehicle models are most likely to be stolen?
The most stolen vehicle in Canada in 2022 was the Honda CR-V, for the second year in a row.
Per data from the Équité Association, here is the full list of the top 10 most stolen vehicles in 2022, by make and model:
1. 2020 Honda CR-V
2. 2022 Dodge Ram 1500 series
3. 2020 Ford F150 series
4. 2020 Lexus RX series
5. 2021 Toyota Highlander
6. 2019 Honda Civic
7. 2021 Jeep Grand Cherokee
8. 2020 Land Rover Range Rover
9. 2006 Chevrolet/GMC Silverado/Sierra 1500 series
10. 2021 Jeep Wrangler
According to Équité, criminals are increasingly targeting newer vehicles to maximize profits. The vehicles get sold overseas or, in some cases, are given a new Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) to deceive unsuspecting Canadian buyers domestically.
Here are the 10 vehicles that were least likely to be stolen in 2022:
1. Chevrolet Volt
2. Kia Niro 5DR
3. Cadillac XT5
4. Buick Envision
5. Mini Cooper Countryman
6. Volvo XC90
7. Volkswagen Beetle
8. Chevrolet Corvette
9. Hyundai Ioniq
10. Mazda Mazda2
What are the feds planning to do about it?
Earlier this week, the federal Liberals announced a national summit on auto theft aimed at getting provinces and industry officials together to address the growing issue of cars being stolen and shipped abroad.
The government says an uptick in stolen cars has prompted it to plan a Feb. 8 meeting to stop organized crime from profiting off of cars stolen on Canadian streets.
The Liberals point to industry estimates that state the rates of auto theft went up in Quebec by half in 2022 compared with the year prior, and to a similar degree in Ontario.
They say police across the Greater Toronto Area have seen carjackings double during that time and soar 300 per cent since 2015.
The government says gangs steal cars and work with organized-crime groups to send them to the Middle East and Africa, or use them within Canada to commit crimes before destroying the vehicles.
That's despite Ottawa insisting it has strong laws aimed at preventing thefts and tracking protocols meant to stop the stolen cars from being exported in shipping containers.
The summit is in line with calls from industry experts, who have increasingly called for a co-ordinated, national response to rising auto thefts, including more prosecutions and theft prevention education.
The Équité Association has also called on Transport Canada to update Canada's federal Motor Vehicle Safety Regulations. The regulations have not been updated since 2007, before the era of keyless and remote start technologies.
"Criminals are now taking advantage of the outdated standards," said Bryan Gast, a certified fraud investigator with Équité Association. "They can quickly and easily exploit these vulnerabilities, which has led to this significant increase in stolen vehicles across Canada."
Last May, the Ontario government announced it was investing $51 million over the next three years to combat vehicle theft, including establishing "auto theft prosecution teams" to investigate and prosecute criminal organizations, but others say more action is needed at the federal level.
How can you protect your vehicle?
Vehicle owners are increasingly adopting the strategy of discreetly installing GPS devices in their vehicles, ensuring they can be tracked in case of theft. But that's only half the battle.
Last July, after a Toronto man had a Range Rover stolen from his driveway for the second time, he tracked it to Montreal and then travelled there to recover it himself.
Stephen Taub, 64, said a radio-frequency tracking device showed the vehicle ended up in Toronto's east end before arriving in a shipping container at the Port of Montreal days later.
Taub told The Canadian Press that the provider of the tracking device shared the car's location with the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA), which then set the container aside.
But CBSA reportedly told Taub it could take up to four months to open the container due to staffing shortages and the container could still be shipped in the interim. So Taub travelled to the CBSA office in Montreal and met with the agency, which then arranged for him to retrieve his Range Rover the next day.
"If I didn't go there, I never would have got the car back," he said.
Halton Regional Police Services also advise that the majority of the vehicles are being stolen from residential driveways, usually in the overnight hours. They suggest securing your vehicle with the following tips:
Park your vehicle in a locked/secure garage, if possible.
Lock the on-board diagnostic port using a device that blocks access to where thieves reprogram the vehicle's key fob
Use a steering wheel locking device to deter theft
Invest in an aftermarket global positioning system (GPS) tracker as it may assist in the recovery of the vehicle if it is stolen
When not in use, place vehicle key fob inside a radio frequency shielding bag/pouch to block cell signals
Consider purchasing a quality video surveillance system and ensure your cameras are properly placed and functioning for 24-hour use
Never hide a spare key in your car or outside your residence
Never leave personal identification or valuables in your vehicle
Remove technologic and other removable accessories from view when not in your vehicle
Invest in anti-theft devices, such as ignition or fuel kill switches, steering wheel, gearshift, and hood locks, steering column collars and lectric alarms
With files from National Post, The Canadian Press

Business indexing term: Corporation: FCA US LLC; Industry: 51741 :‎ Satellite Telecommunications

Document 459 of 500

Jamie Sarkonak: Crackdown on student visas is a welcome change, but will it be enough?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jamie-sarkonak-crackdown-on-student-visas-is/docview/2917891021/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jamie+Sarkonak%3A+Crackdown+on+student+visas+is+a+welcome+change%2C+but+will+it+be+enough%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With any hope, the Wild West era of international studentry is coming to a close. Don't get too optimistic in the short term, though.
On Monday, Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a two-year cap on international student permits that will amount to a 35 per cent reduction in study visa approvals overall (and a 50 per cent reduction in Ontario). International students at private schools offering public curriculums will also be barred from obtaining postgraduate work permits, and work permits for student spouses will be restricted to graduate and professional programs.
These new restrictions were desperately needed, but they're only a start. This week's cut is merely expected to reduce the total new permits issued in 2024 to 360,000 - that's the equivalent of one year's international admissions under the first year of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's rule. In December 2015, there were roughly 350,000 international students in Canada, according to Statistics Canada (That number rose to 620,000 in 2021, and reached 900,000 in 2023).
As for the immediate impact on student numbers? For starters, the cap doesn't affect those who were accepted in 2023 and before, and their visas last three years. Many of them will be here for a while yet. If we assume, very generously, that half of the 900,000 international students currently in Canada graduate and leave in 2024, and another 360,000 are accepted per the new cap, the total ends up at around 800,000. That number of students in Canada would still be vastly higher than in 2021.
But let's say the caps actually manage to turn the international student clock back three years and restore the conditions of 2021. Will we be much better off? Probably not.
Canada in 2021 was third in the world for foreign student attraction, despite being about 40th in population size and still managing the regulatory response to COVID. Problems of student exploitation were known at the time, and the public was starting to catch on.
In particular, people noticing that the program had morphed into a labour program and citizenship factory for students.
The primary market for this seemed to be India: one Statistics Canada study in 2021 noted the drastic rise in students from there. From 2000 to 2004, only four per cent of international college students were of Indian origin, but from 2015 to 2019, they accounted for 67 per cent of college study permits. At the university level, permits for Indian students grew from five to 21 per cent.
Beyond campus, the deluge of international students was adding to the immense pressure felt in the housing market, affecting both their rental prospects and those of locals.
On the student side of the equation, the surge in admissions came at a steep cost. While the stereotypical international students of old tended to be rich or at least "comfortable," many of the new admits were destitute. Indian farming families, hoping for a better future, increasingly took to mortgaging or even selling the farm to cover tuition for one child (farm revenue often wasn't enough; the Walrus in 2021 found an example of a family that made $9,000 per year, which fell far short of the necessary $20,000 for tuition alone).
Some of these students were forced to live on the margins, resulting in misery. One Brampton funeral home reported that it was sending the bodies of Indian international students about once per month in 2021. In 2022, the funeral home told CBC that the number had doubled.
Then, there's the exploitation. A 2019 study by a Punjabi community group in the Peel region found that sex for money was "common"; in 2023, an international education industry magazine echoed the finding, reporting that a number of Toronto massage parlours were advertising Indian students for sex.
So no, going back a couple years isn't going to get us very far.
Perhaps the bigger game changer of Monday was the crackdown on work permits. With the end of postgraduate work permits for private colleges teaching public curriculums entirely, demand for these strip mall "learning facilities" should be seriously stifled. And with spousal work permits being restricted to grad and professional programs, this will hopefully barricade another pathway for cheap labour to enter the country.
Spousal work permits for study-permit holders was a well-intentioned policy to keep families together, but it fell victim to abuse (to the delight of employers seeking cheap labour in Canada, for sure). As early as 2017, advertisements by Indian families seeking international-study-eligible wives for their sons could be found, evidencing the loophole. The idea: marry one's son to a study permit holder to obtain him a work permit. Instead of encouraging families to stay together, the Canadian policy was encouraging matrimonial coercion of young women.
Universities, and especially private colleges, will whine and cry that Miller's tightening of the student pipeline will do more harm than good. The financial crunch will hurt, complained the president of Thompson Rivers University. The Atlantic region will suffer, said the Association of Atlantic Universities. But the status quo wasn't working for the broader public, which needs a place to live more than it needs a bustling local college, nor was it working for the many international students facing exploitation.
This week's announcement is an improvement over nothing, but care will need to be taken to ensure the problems actually get fixed. A return to the state of affairs in 2021 will be nowhere near enough.
National Post
Sabrina Maddeaux: International students are suffering. The Liberals need to take action
Sabrina Maddeaux: Liberals keep exploitative immigration policies fully intact

Business indexing term: Industry: 61131 :‎ Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools 92812 :‎ International Affairs

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; College admissions; Passports & visas; Private colleges & universities; Private schools; Permits; Immigration

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Identifier / keyword: international; studentry; coming; close; optimistic; short
