Document 460 of 500

FIRST READING: The Parks Canada plan to retool Anne of Green Gables to include 'diverse' voices

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-parks-canada-plan-retool-anne-green/docview/2917838408/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+The+Parks+Canada+plan+to+retool+Anne+of+Green+Gables+to+include+%27diverse%27+voices&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
Parks Canada has announced a plan to remake some of the main pilgrimage sites for Anne of Green Gables fans in order to include narratives from "marginalized communities."
In a 23-page strategic outline, the agency outlined its goal to present "new narratives, perspectives and voices" at Anne of Green Gables sites on Prince Edward Island, particularly those of "cultures not currently presented."
"Through 2033, the number of opportunities at the site to engage visitors in learning about and connecting to a diversity of cultures will increase," reads one of its strategic objectives.
The retooling is all part of a new standalone management plan for L.M. Montgomery's Cavendish National Historic Site.
The site includes two locations on the north end of Prince Edward Island with ties to Lucy Maud Montgomery, author of the Anne of Green Gables series. The first is the farm house where Montgomery grew up, while the second is the neighbouring Green Gables farmstead which inspired much of the locations referenced in the novels.
Both sites used to be managed under the auspices of the larger Prince Edward Island National Park, but are now getting a dedicated management strategy.
The original book series by L.M. Montgomery isn't big on ethnic diversity, which is largely a consequence of the relatively homogenous environment in which it takes place. The version of P.E.I. inhabited by Anne was almost exclusively populated by people of English, Scottish or Irish descent.
Still, the island in Montgomery's time would have had a small presence of Mi'kmaq families. According to one recent analysis by the Confederation Centre of the Arts: Indigenous people were "not quite invisible" in 19th century P.E.I. It added, "they seem a natural subject for Anne's romantic imagination, but they are curiously absent from her world."
The island's most conspicuous minority group at the time would have been French-speaking Acadians, and they don't come out well in the books. The whole reason Anne is brought to the island as an adoptee is because her adoptive parents require help around the farm and refuse to hire Acadians.
"There's never anybody to be had but those stupid, half-grown little French boys; and as soon as you do get one broke into your ways and taught something he's up and off to the lobster canneries or the States," says Anne's adoptive mother, Marilla Cuthbert, in chapter one of Anne of Green Gables.
Poilievre slams Trudeau for 'incomprehensible' position on genocide case against Israel
Jamie Sarkonak: 'Trans-affirming toolkit' tells teachers to put ideology above biology
In recent years, even the P.E.I.-based L.M. Montgomery Institute has issued statements accusing their namesake of being a purveyor of colonialist white supremacy. In 2022, the institute even hosted a conference of scholars who "have experience discussing Montgomery's work in connection to conversations on queer theory and gender, colonialism, and diversity in literature."
Parks Canada outlined its new inclusivity goals under the heading "working with kindred spirits" - which employs a line used by Anne in the novels.
"The themes of friendship and belonging are central to the popularity of L.M. Montgomery's Anne of Green Gables," writes the agency.
"Parks Canada is committed to engaging with Indigenous and marginalized communities to tell their stories."
This isn't the first time that something connected to the Anne of Green Gables universe has undergone a modern revision to include contemporary views that were not present in the original narrative.
The 2017 TV series Anne with an E added several non-white characters not present in the original franchise, as well as writing in gay and lesbian backstories for several existing characters whose sexual identity was not hinted at by Montgomery. This included the addition of a storyline featuring a young Mi'kmaq girl named Ka'kwet. Casting calls for the series' Indigenous characters included the line "INDIGENOUS MUST TRULY BE INDIGENOUS."
This is why, when the series was cancelled by Netflix in 2019, one critic derided its disappearance as "damaging to Indigenous viewers."
Ironically, given all the attempts to diversify Anne of Green Gables, one of the book's most loyal fanbases is non-white.
The book series has long been noted for its outsized popularity in Japan, which is largely due to the fact that a Japanese translation of Anne of Green Gables was one of the first novels introduced to the country during its postwar occupation by the United States.
It's why P.E.I. has hosted no less than two visits from the Japanese royal family, and why Japan is even host to a dedicated replica of the Green Gables site.
IN OTHER NEWS
The Trudeau government is slowly coming around to the idea that bringing in one million newcomers per year might be having deleterious effects on housing affordability (which, as we often mention in this newsletter, is a matter of simple arithmetic). And so, they've taken an axe to student visas. Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a two-year cap on student visas which could translate to a reduction in international students of as much as 50 per cent in some jurisdictions.
If you're reading this, there's a higher-than-average probability that you've had your car stolen or are currently stealing a car. In both Quebec and Ontario, car thefts have risen by half in little more than a year. Compared to 2015, meanwhile, the national rate of stolen cars is up by an incredible 300 per cent. And so, at a Liberal cabinet summit in Montreal over the weekend, the assembled ministers announced plans to hold another summit specifically about car theft. The National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft will work with "key stakeholders on efforts to combat auto theft."
The Angus Reid Institute just conducted a poll not only of the parties that Canadians plan to vote for, but why And what emerged is that the incumbent Liberal/NDP coalition is held together by not much more than a hatred of the Conservatives. Of Canadians who planned to vote Conservative, the majority (63 per cent) said they were doing it because they "want to support Poilievre and what the party stands for." But 62 per cent of Liberal voters said they only supported Trudeau to "block the Conservatives." One third of NDP voters, meanwhile, said they'd freely abandon Team Orange if they thought a Liberal vote would be better placed to block the Conservatives. When party morale gets this low, it can result in some pretty big Election Day swings. Not only are the Tories way ahead in the polls, but their supporters are itching to mark a ballot for the Conservatives. Canada's largest single electoral demographic is people who don't bother to vote; voter turnout was just 62.2 per cent in 2021. As such, a motivated Conservative base could result in an unforeseen surge of people who have been happy to sit out prior elections - while a disillusioned Liberal and NDP base stays home.
Angus Reid Institute

Document 461 of 500

John Ivison: We've already gone too far on MAID. Parliament can still stop us going further

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/john-ivison-weve-already-gone-too-far-on-maid/docview/2917776882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=John+Ivison%3A+We%27ve+already+gone+too+far+on+MAID.+Parliament+can+still+stop+us+going+further&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I appear to have reached the stage in life where I am prepared to write outraged epistles to publishers and threaten to cancel my subscription after reading something with which I vehemently disagree.
That was the initial reaction when I read an opinion article on the National Newswatch website on the subject of the expansion of medically assistance in dying (MAID) to people suffering mental disorders.
Since it turns out I don't have a National Newswatch subscription to threaten to cancel, I am forced to ventilate in public.
The article in question by Jocelyn Downie offended on multiple levels.
Downie, the James S. Palmer Chair of Public Policy and Law at Dalhousie University's Schulich School of Law, argued that "the courts have spoken" on the issue of cases where mental disorders are the sole underlying medical condition (MAID MD-SUMC), effectively denying Parliament any agency in the matter.
In effect, she said, the government should get out of the way and allow those with mental issues access to MAID from this March a move that was initially scheduled for March 2023, but which was postponed, the government said, to ensure it could "move forward on this sensitive and complex issue in a prudent and measured way."
The fact that a parliamentary special committee is set to report on that very issue by the end of this month, is apparently neither here nor there to Downie. She claims that all the necessary steps are in place, from data collection to a national curriculum for assessors. There is "strong evidence" that the bodies that regulate psychiatrists and nurses are ready, she said.
This, even though the chair of the Canadian Psychological Association (CPA), Alison Freeland, acknowledged at parliamentary committee hearings in November: "I don't think I can say from a CPA perspective that all the readiness is there" (and she supports expansion).
Colby Cosh: Canada's circular debate over medical assistance in dying
Shawn Whatley: Assisted suicide activists should not be running our MAID program
Safe implementation appears in Downie and Freeland's opinions to be a secondary consideration to the belief that people with mental disorders have a constitutional right to access state assisted death.
To recap: when the Supreme Court ruled that prohibition of medically assisted dying violated the Charter in the Carter decision in 2015, it forced the new Liberal government to come up with legislation.
That response, which restricted MAID to people whose death was "reasonably foreseeable," was challenged by a Quebec Superior Court judge in the Truchon case, who said that this interpretation was too narrow, and that access should be opened up to people who were suffering but were not at the end of life.
The Liberals decided not to appeal the Truchon decision (an announcement Justin Trudeau made on a Quebec talk show in the midst of an election) and the government was forced to respond with new legislation, Bill C-7, that broadened access but explicitly excluded mental illness. It did so based on the belief that screening for mental illness is prone to a high degree of error.
At committee, MPs heard expert testimony from mental health professionals who said that while some people were destined for a lifetime of anguish, others had conditions that were treatable. The problem is, it is extremely difficult to distinguish between the two.
That is where it would have ended had the Senate not added an amendment to expand access to those with mental disorders, at the behest of Senator Stan Kutcher, a former head of psychiatry at Dalhousie, who claimed the legislation was discriminatory. The amendment was adopted, after minimal debate in the House, and the government punted the problem down the road by introducing a sunset clause that would not come into effect until March, 2023 - a date that was further extended to this March.
This is the basis for Professor Downie's confidence that "the courts have spoken and the government knows it."
But the courts have not spoken with any finality. "It should be absurd to anyone that a lower court decision in Quebec determines what Parliament should be doing. It is absurd that anyone argues Parliament should legislate based on 'here's how the Supreme Court of Canada might rule'," argues Trudo Lemmens, Professor of the School Chair in Health Law and Policy in the Faculty of Law at the University of Toronto.
He and 24 other law professors from across the country wrote to the prime minister arguing that the constitutionality, or lack of constitutionality, of an exclusion clause for MAID MD-SUMC has not been tested in the courts. Lemmens and his colleagues argued there is no legal requirement for the government to expand MAID because the Supreme Court has not reviewed the evidence on whether mental illness can reliably be diagnosed as irremediable.
The previous justice minister, David Lametti, was on record as opposing the expansion but he is gone and his successor, Arif Virani, has only said the government is weighing up its options over whether to seek a deferral.
It is yet another example of this government's obsession with short-term headlines. It appears it did not appeal the Truchon decision because it was popular in Quebec on the eve of an election. It is inexplicable why it allowed Kutcher's amendment to stand and make it into legislation without a broader public airing.
A private member's bill by Conservative MP Ed Fast that would have killed the expansion revealed how contentious the move remains. It was defeated by 17 votes, but only because the Bloc supported the government. The NDP voted with the Conservatives and several Liberals.
The government has every reason to be concerned about being pushed into a decision. An Angus Reid Institute survey from last February reveals strong support (61 per cent) for the existing MAID legislation. Most Canadians, including this one, agree that consenting adults facing intolerable pain should have the right to a compassionate end to their lives; that there should be an enlightened balance between access and protection.
But that support drops in half (31 per cent) when respondents are asked about extending MAID to those whose sole medical condition is mental illness.
The latest statistics we have are that 44,958 people died by MAID between 2016 and the end of 2022, more than the number of Canadian military deaths in the Second World War.
The trend lines are worrying: 31.2-per-cent more cases in 2022 over the previous year.
The fear is that those numbers will really blast off if access becomes too open. There are plenty of signs that Canada has gone too far already, without lifting more restrictions.
As Lemmens points out, despite the safeguards in place, accessors have reasonably broad discretion in an area with a 50-per-cent accuracy rate of prediction.
There is data from Europe that show many people seeking psychiatric MAID have unresolved socio-economic suffering.
Canada is the only jurisdiction that does not see MAID as a last resort - it does not require the patient to be in a state of treatment when he or she seeks medically assisted dying.
A 2:1 majority of Ontario Medical Association psychiatrists oppose MAID MD-SUMC, even though 80 per cent support medically assisted dying in other circumstances.
In these circumstances, the federal government should show some mettle, refuse to accept Parliament's role of rubber-stamp that some activists would bestow upon it, and put an end to a domino effect that will almost certainly see the extension of MAID to mature minors become the next frontier.
National Post
jivison@criffel.ca( mailto:jivison@criffel.ca )

Subject: Legislation; Psychiatrists; Parliaments; Assisted suicide; Mental disorders

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 462 of 500

Matthew Lau: What CEOs earn is shareholders' business and nobody else's

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/matthew-lau-what-ceos-earn-is-shareholders/docview/2917776717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Matthew+Lau%3A+What+CEOs+earn+is+shareholders%27+business+and+nobody+else%27s&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Every January, the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives (CCPA), an Ottawa-based socialist think-tank, tries to convince everyone to be outraged at Canada's most successful corporate executives by releasing its annual report comparing the compensation of the country's top 100 CEOs versus the average worker. Every year their report misuses statistics to make faulty comparisons (more on this later), and then through heroic use of non sequiturs, concludes government must raise taxes on top-earning executives to make everyone better off. An innovation this year is that for the first time CCPA gives a provincial breakdown of its results, so as to let Canadians know which provinces should be most outraged by the compensation of top CEOs.
What we learn from this new geographic breakdown is that Ontario should be the outrage capital of Canada. Although in 2022 Ontario accounted for only 38.9 per cent of Canada's population, 46 of the country's top 100 CEOs are headquartered in the province. Even worse, the average compensation of Ontario's top CEOs was higher than in any other province. Ontario's 46 averaged $18.5 million, compared to just $11.8 million on average for the 54 in the rest of Canada. The CCPA computes the ratio between average compensation for CEOs on its top 100 list to the average workers' earnings in each province, finding it highest in Ontario. Cue the outrage.
But an important fact undermines the CCPA's argument that ordinary people should be unhappy about top CEO compensation: the average worker in Ontario is better paid than the average worker in any other province except Alberta. What's more: not only does the average Alberta worker earn more than the average worker in any other province, but Alberta is also the only province except Ontario with an over-representation of top CEOs. It has 13 of the top 100 but just 11.6 per cent of Canada's population.
Conversely, Atlantic Canada accounts for 6.5 per cent of Canada's population but only three of its top 100 CEOs - two in Nova Scotia and one in Newfoundland and Labrador - and these three CEOs earned markedly less than average among the top 100. But having fewer top CEOs that are paid less doesn't seem to help average workers: in all four Atlantic provinces, average worker pay is below the national average. The same pattern holds in Manitoba and Saskatchewan: both provinces are slightly underrepresented among top CEOs; those who do make the list earn less than top dollar; and average worker pay in the two provinces is below the national average.
So the CCPA's new provincial twist on its traditional January story actually undermines its case for mass outrage at top-earning CEOs. This is not surprising: the socialist theory that top CEOs and capitalists get rich by exploiting the poor or average worker is nonsense that economists have whacked down time and again. If the socialists were right, as Thomas Sowell has pointed out, then "in a country with lots of billionaires, you should correspondingly have great amounts of poor people. But if you compare the actual data, there are more billionaires in the United States than in Africa and the Middle East put together, and yet the standard of living of the poor in the United States is higher than that of people in Africa and the Middle East."
Going back to its national data, CCPA proclaims its latest report finds a "new all-time record" because the ratio between the compensation of the average CEO on its top 100 list and the average worker in Canada reached a high of 246, up from 243 in the previous year. But of course each year is likely to set a record: Canada's labour force is growing, and by keeping the number of CEOs on its list constant, the CCPA report skims a slimmer percentage of top CEOs each year. The number of people employed through the year in Canada was up an average of four per cent in 2022. To keep its report comparable to the previous year, the CCPA should have expanded its list to the top 104 CEOs. And if you do that - surprise! - the ratio in its latest report would actually fall - to 241. Which is not a new record. This mis-sampling effect might seem to make a small difference each year, but compounds over time: the CCPA's comparisons back to its first report in 2010 therefore exaggerate changes in inequality over the years.
Matthew Lau: Higher education would benefit from lower subsidies
Matthew Lau: Budget advice for the Trudeau Liberals: Cut spending!
Matthew Lau: Guilbeault, 'policymaker of the year,' killing economic growth
The CCPA report was never very useful to begin with. Faulty comparisons aside, what shareholders, acting through their elected boards, decide to pay CEOs is shareholders' business and no one else's. There is certainly no reason to think compensating CEOs well has any negative effect on average workers or anyone else - as the CCPA's provincial data demonstrate.
Matthew Lau is a Toronto writer.

Document 463 of 500

Universities, colleges worried after federal Liberals announce international student cap

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/universities-colleges-worried-after-federal/docview/2917776716/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Universities%2C+colleges+worried+after+federal+Liberals+announce+international+student+cap&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Universities, colleges and the provincial governments that oversee them were waiting for the details Monday, but warned some schools could close, after the government announced new caps on international students.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced next year's new student visas will be capped at 360,000, a number 35 per cent below last year's levels, and will stay at that level until at least 2025. Visas are good for three years, so students already here won't be impacted, but the reduction will lead to many fewer new students being admitted.
Miller also announced that students attending private colleges that license curriculum from public colleges, a trend which has been growing, will no longer qualify for post-graduate work permits.
Miller first began raising concerns last year about the number of international student permits and he called on provinces to be more proactive, looking into private colleges and the quality of education they were offering.
"Clearly there has not been action as quickly as we wanted it to be taken," he said.
He said putting a cap on the number of applicants was a blunt instrument, but he saw no other option and would be working with provinces to solve some of the bigger issues over the life of the two-year freeze.
"Did we get it right? We'll see, but we need to work with provinces in the meantime to make sure that they are doing their jobs."
'Out of control': Immigration minister says he wants to reduce international student arrivals
Record levels of international students straining Canada's housing supply further
Previously there was no cap on the number of international student visas and applicants only need to show they had been accepted by an institution provincial governments had approved and that they had sufficient funds to support themselves.
In December, Miller raised the amount of funds new students had to have for their living expenses and also reduced the number of hours they can work from 40 hours a week to 20 hours.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre accused the Liberals of buck-passing and said while he would also have instituted a cap, the government is alone in being responsible for this.
"Let's not blame the students. Let's not blame other levels of government. Let's blame the one man who is responsible for this disaster and that is our incompetent prime minister," he said.
The cap will be spread out across provinces in line with their populations that means that provinces like B.C., Ontario and Nova Scotia that are bringing in high numbers of international students will have an even tougher cap with a reduction of 50 per cent or more.
Miller said it will be up to provinces to decide how to utilize their international student allotment, but he said he also wants to see them provide schools with appropriate funding to stop schools from treating international students as a cash cow.
"I'm not the minister of post secondary education underfunding. I'm the minister of immigration and clearly in the last decade or so post-secondary institutions in Canada have been underfunded."
Ontario Advanced Education Minister Jill Dunlop said in a statement that her government intends to crack down on colleges offering poor quality education.
"We know some bad actors are taking advantage of these students with false promises of guaranteed employment, residency, and Canadian citizenship. We've been engaging with the federal government on ways to crack down on these practices," she said.
B.C.'s Advanced Education Minister Selina Robinson said they know there are private colleges offering substandard education, using international students and it has to end.
"It's egregious. I think it needs to be reined in and better managed and that's the work that we've been undertaking is to figure out what levers we need here to do that," she said.
Robinson said in terms of oversight the system relies on students complaining about the quality of institutions and their education, which she said was probably a mistake.
"In hindsight I think that system doesn't work well. If your family has been saving for years to go to a Canadian school, and you're concerned that if you complain that you'll get sent home, you're less likely to complain," she said.
Robinson said her government wants to work with the federal government on the problem, because they do need international students to deal with real gaps in the labour market.
"We need health care workers. We need licensed practical nurses. We need early childhood educators and our institutions, many of them are delivering exactly that and using international students to do that, so we don't want to compromise those programs."
Philip Landon, interim president of Universities Canada, said he was relieved the government seems to be mostly focusing on private colleges.
"We actually think that's the key measure here and we think that's the measure that will reduce the numbers and really address the problems that we have seen," he said.
Landon said they are concerned about the cap because it's an arbitrary number that will send a message that Canada isn't open to new students. He said it is going to hurt universities, some of which are already struggling.
"This is in a climate of really decreased funding from the provinces for universities and this is just going to be a further reduction in their ability to do the great work that they do," he said. "In certain provinces, universities are under considerable stress, if this continues to contribute to that stress, we may see universities unable to continue."
In a statement, Colleges and Institutes Canada said they are waiting for all the details and for information from provinces about how they will apply the cap, but they are concerned about how it will hit institutions.
"This approach, characterized by Minister Miller as a "blunt instrument," will have far-reaching consequences across the sector, especially in key regions, including the possibility of layoffs, closures and increased tuition fees - all of which will inevitably affect both Canadian and international students," the association said.
The group said this new announcement should lead to more government funding to colleges to offset the loss of international students.
"Chronic public underinvestment in post-secondary education puts Canada's world-class system at risk. Without significant reinvestment, we risk compounding the challenges facing Canada's economy and society, especially in the context of fierce global competition."
National Post
rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 464 of 500

Why guaranteed basic income proposal is likely doomed

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-guaranteed-basic-income-proposal-is-likely/docview/2917703461/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Why+guaranteed+basic+income+proposal+is+likely+doomed&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The controversial idea of a guaranteed basic income, which could cost tens of billions each year, might be making its way to the House of Commons this spring.
But the chances of a guaranteed income becoming actual policy are "very close to zero," said Kevin Milligan, an economics professor at the University of British Columbia who specializes in government social spending.
"No government could afford it. Any government who was wise would see that this is not a good way to work on the important issues of poverty," Milligan said.
The Senate has been studying Ontario Senator Kim Pate's Bill S-233, which strives to develop a national framework to provide people over 17 in Canada with access to a "guaranteed livable basic income," including temporary workers, permanent residents and refugee claimants.
The bill would not actually implement a guaranteed basic income (GBI), but would force the finance minister to consult with provinces and territories, as well as Indigenous and other groups, to prepare a report setting out a framework for the policy.
If it passes a final vote in the Senate, the bill will head to the House of Commons. Liberals and Conservatives are currently refusing to take a clear position on the bill, arguing that they are waiting for the legislation to reach the lower chamber before they decide where they stand.
That sparked speculation in the following days from right-wing commentators such as the Western Standard's Cory Morgan and the Post Millennial's David Krayden, and also Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis, who wondered aloud on social media whether a basic income will end up becoming Trudeau's policy Hail Mary, with his party continuing to tank in the polls.
On Dec. 29, in sharing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's new video on "debtonation" on Facebook, Lewis blamed the federal Liberals for creating "the debt problem that's connected to our inflation crisis" and wondered if Canadians would trust them again once "our traditional safety nets are destroyed."
"My guess is after they burn it all down, they will propose a solution where they have control: let's see ... a digital and programmable Universal Basic Income perhaps?" she asked.
Many progressive senators and Liberal MPs have been championing the idea of a basic income for years, and it seemed to take on new life during the pandemic as Ottawa rolled out large, widespread income supports. But even then, Trudeau did not seem very enthusiastic about the idea: When he was asked about it, he said he was more interested in staying focused on controlling COVID-19.
The notion of a basic income is not new. Nobel laureate and free-market economist Milton Friedman championed the idea of a negative income tax - which would provide a certain basic income to people below a certain earning threshold, while eliminating all other government welfare programs.
Friedman's version, promising more efficiency, less bureaucracy and the freedom for recipients to spend their income as they see fit, appeals to some conservatives.
When he was the opposition finance critic, Poilievre wrote in National Post in 2018 that "many basic income supporters have the right goal." Deputy party leader Melissa Lantsman told broadcaster Stephen LeDrew in 2020 that she "somewhat agree(s) with that" and that "Conservatives can own" the issue of a basic income.
The progressive version is different: Politicians and economists on the left have been instead championing a basic income on top of other welfare subsidies. They argue that the income would, in time, lift recipients out of poverty, reducing their reliance on other social supports.
"We're talking about ensuring that people have adequate incomes but we're not talking about some Keynesian model that leaves everybody with just a bit of money and no other supports," said Sen. Pate last year.
That's why UBC's Milligan compares trying to define a basic income to nailing Jell-O to a wall. "Because any time you kind of nail it down to what you think they mean, it changes," he said.
Senator Pate, having been appointed under Trudeau, comes at it from the progressive side. She said Canada already provides supports similar to a basic income for some groups: the Guaranteed Income Supplement for low-income seniors; the Canada Child Benefit for families with children; and the soon-to-be Canada Disability Benefit for people with disabilities, for example.
"It's like we're inching toward it. Why not just take the next step?" she said.
-
But passing bills that are not government priorities can be a tedious and long process. S-233 was first introduced in the Senate in December 2021, at the same time as an identical one, C-223, was introduced in the House of Commons by NDP MP Leah Gazan.
Gazan's bill has remained untouched in the House, whereas the Senate bill was adopted on division in second reading in April 2023 to be further studied at committee.
Even Conservative senators agreed to take a further look at S-233, despite claiming their caucus does not support it.
"I think that the light of day will expose the weaknesses in this bill," said the Conservative party's critic for the bill, Nova Scotia Senator Michael L. MacDonald, at the time. Sen. Pate, however, claims that there are "many, many people within all of the parties who are supportive" of her bill, and she remains hopeful its study in committee will wrap up soon after the Senate returns next month and be submitted to a final vote in the Upper Chamber in the spring.
Attempts have been made in the past to pilot basic income projects, but any promising results ran up against serious cost concerns. There was Manitoba's "Mincome" in the 1970s, Ontario's brief pilot in 2017-2018, and P.E.I. has been running a targeted basic income pilot program with a few hundred recipients since 2021.
British Columbia and Quebec have studied it but concluded that a targeted approach is better than a universal basic income.
"The needs of people in this society are too diverse to be effectively answered simply with a cheque from the government," wrote the B.C. Basic Income panel in its December 2020 report on the concept. "A basic income is a very costly approach to addressing any specific goal, such as poverty reduction."
The Parliamentary Budget Officer produced an analysis in 2021 that concluded that a national guaranteed basic income using parameters of Ontario's 2017 pilot project could cut Canada's poverty rate by half in just one year, but at a hefty cost of $91 billion in 2024-25 and $93 billion in 2025-26. That's nearly double what Ottawa spends, for example, on health transfers to the provinces.
"So I think the probability of a UBI (universal basic income) being implemented in Canada on a widespread basis, not as small pilot or something, is very close to zero," said Milligan.
Even Sen. Pate admits that there are impediments for political parties to move forward with a basic income, given the initial cost of the measure and the fact that any given party is unlikely to reap all of the benefits of cracking down on poverty in Canada in one electoral cycle.
But she remains hopeful that she will see some sort of basic income become reality in her lifetime.
"I think it's much like medicare," she said. "Many people thought it was a good idea, (but) people were afraid what it would cost and afraid that they wouldn't see the benefit within the electoral cycle. And then eventually, Saskatchewan just decided to do it and once it started rolling out, everybody wanted in."
Milligan said he agrees with the B.C. panel's approach, which is to "roll up your sleeves and do the hard work of expanding, improving, replacing, revamping our existing social support system."
"It's complicated, it's messy, it's hard work," he said. "It's not a magical solution, but I don't believe in magical solutions. I think a lot of the basic income stuff is just people looking for a magical solution to a tough problem."
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 465 of 500

We've gone too far on MAID; Parliament still has the power stop the slide

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/weve-gone-too-far-on-maid-parliament-still-has/docview/2917700264/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=We%27ve+gone+too+far+on+MAID%3B+Parliament+still+has+the+power+stop+the+slide&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I appear to have reached the stage in life where I am prepared to write outraged epistles to publishers and threaten to cancel my subscription after reading something with which I vehemently disagree.
That was the initial reaction when I read an opinion article on the National Newswatch website on the subject of the expansion of medically assistance in dying (MAID) to people suffering mental disorders.
Since it turns out I don't have a National Newswatch subscription to threaten to cancel, I am forced to ventilate in public.
The article in question by Jocelyn Downie offended on multiple levels.
Downie, the James S. Palmer Chair of Public Policy and Law at Dalhousie University's Schulich School of Law, argued that "the courts have spoken" on the issue of cases where mental disorders are the sole underlying medical condition (MAID MD-SUMC), effectively denying Parliament any agency in the matter.
In effect, she said, the government should get out of the way and allow those with mental issues access to MAID from this March a move that was initially scheduled for March 2023, but which was postponed, the government said, to ensure it could "move forward on this sensitive and complex issue in a prudent and measured way."
The fact that a parliamentary special committee is set to report on that very issue by the end of this month is apparently neither here nor there to Downie.
She claims that all the necessary steps are in place, from data collection to a national curriculum for assessors. There is "strong evidence" that the bodies that regulate psychiatrists and nurses are ready, she said.
This, even though the chair of the Canadian Psychological Association (CPA), Alison Freeland, acknowledged at parliamentary committee hearings in November: "I don't think I can say from a CPA perspective that all the readiness is there" (and she supports expansion).
Safe implementation appears in Downie and Freeland's opinions to be a secondary consideration to the belief that people with mental disorders have a constitutional right to access state assisted death.
To recap: when the Supreme Court ruled that prohibition of medically assisted dying violated the Charter in the Carter decision in 2015, it forced the new Liberal government to come up with legislation.
That response, which restricted MAID to people whose death was "reasonably foreseeable," was challenged by a Quebec Superior Court judge in the Truchon case, who said that this interpretation was too narrow, and that access should be opened up to people who were suffering but were not at the end of life.
The Liberals decided not to appeal the Truchon decision (an announcement Justin Trudeau made on a Quebec talk show in the midst of an election) and the government was forced to respond with new legislation, C7, that broadened access but explicitly excluded mental illness. It did so based on the belief that screening for mental illness is prone to a high degree of error.
At committee, MPs heard expert testimony from mental health professionals who said that while some people were destined for a lifetime of anguish, others had conditions that were treatable. The problem is, it is extremely difficult to distinguish between the two.
That is where it would have ended had the Senate not added an amendment to expand access to those with mental disorders, at the behest of Senator Stan Kutcher, a former head of psychiatry at Dalhousie, who claimed the legislation was discriminatory. The amendment was adopted, after minimal debate in the House, and the government punted the problem down the road by introducing a sunset clause that would not come into effect until March, 2023 - a date that was further extended to this March.
This is the basis for Downie's confidence that "the courts have spoken and the government knows it."
But the courts have not spoken with any finality.
"It should be absurd to anyone that a lower court decision in Quebec determines what Parliament should be doing. It is absurd that anyone argues Parliament should legislate based on 'here's how the Supreme Court of Canada might rule,'" argues Trudo Lemmens, professor of the school chair in health law and policy in the Faculty of Law at the University of Toronto.
He and 24 other law professors from across the country wrote to the prime minister arguing that the constitutionality, or lack of constitutionality, of an exclusion clause for MAID MD-SUMC has not been tested in the courts. Lemmens and his colleagues argued there is no legal requirement for the government to expand MAID because the Supreme Court has not reviewed the evidence on whether mental illness can reliably be diagnosed as irremediable.
The previous justice minister, David Lametti, was on record as opposing the expansion but he is gone and his successor, Arif Virani, has only said the government is weighing up its options over whether to seek a deferral.
It is yet another example of this government's obsession with short-term headlines. It appears it did not appeal the Truchon decision because it was popular in Quebec on the eve of an election. It is inexplicable why it allowed Kutcher's amendment to stand and make it into legislation without a broader public airing.
A private member's bill by Conservative MP Ed Fast that would have killed the expansion revealed how contentious the move remains. It was defeated by 17 votes, but only because the Bloc supported the government. The NDP voted with the Conservatives and several Liberals.
The government has every reason to be concerned about being pushed into a decision. An Angus Reid Institute survey from last February reveals strong support (61 per cent) for the existing MAID legislation. Most Canadians, including this one, agree that consenting adults facing intolerable pain should have the right to a compassionate end to their lives; that there should be an enlightened balance between access and protection.
But that support drops in half (31 per cent) when respondents are asked about extending MAID to those whose sole medical condition is mental illness.
The latest statistics we have are that 44,958 people died by MAID between 2016 and the end of 2022, more than the number of Canadian military deaths in the Second World War.
The trend lines are worrying: 31.2-per-cent more cases in 2022 over the previous year. The fear is that those numbers will really blast off if access becomes too open. There are plenty of signs that Canada has gone too far already, without lifting more restrictions.
As Lemmens points out, despite the safeguards in place, accessors have reasonably broad discretion in an area with a 50-per-cent accuracy rate of prediction. There is data from Europe that show many people seeking psychiatric MAID have unresolved socio-economic suffering. Canada is the only jurisdiction that does not see MAID as a last resort - it does not require the patient to be in a state of treatment when he or she seeks medically assisted dying. A 2:1 majority of Ontario Medical Association psychiatrists oppose MAID MD-SUMC, even though 80 per cent support medically assisted dying in other circumstances.
In these circumstances, the federal government should show some mettle, refuse to accept Parliament's role of rubber-stamp that some activists would bestow upon it, and put an end to a domino effect that will almost certainly see the extension of MAID to mature minors become the next frontier.
National Post jivison@criffel.ca

Subject: Legislation; Court decisions; Psychiatrists; Parliaments; Assisted suicide; Mental disorders; Federal government

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

FIRST READING: Canada's rapidly fraying social cohesion

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-canadas-rapidly-fraying-social/docview/2917507109/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Canada%27s+rapidly+fraying+social+cohesion&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign uphere.
TOP STORY
Polls are beginning to show signs that Canadians are breaking with their traditional reputation for tolerant social cohesion.
In just the last few months, there's been a significant breakdown in the numbers of Canadians viewing immigration as an automatic good. At the same time, tensions between Canada's various demographics are on the upswing - most notably when it comes to Canadian Muslims.
For decades, immigration polls have typically showed that the average Canadian is skeptical about increasing immigration levels, and worried that newcomers aren't integrating. But these fears were usually checked by an overall acceptance that immigration was a net good for the country.
In 2010, an Angus Reid Institute poll found respondents evenly split on whether immigration was a net positive (39 per cent vs. 39 per cent). But of those, 62 per cent of respondents still deemed multiculturalism as "good for the country."
Nine years later, on the eve of the 2019 federal election a similar Angus Reid poll found 61 per cent saying that Canada's economic growth was "in peril" without immigration. On the question of whether immigrants were taking jobs from Canadians, a resounding 71 per cent said "no."
Lately, Canadians aren't so sure.
A November Leger poll found a clear plurality of Canadians now saying that immigration was having a "net negative impact" on the country's economy.
Of respondents, 45 per cent agreed that "current levels of immigration" were making Canada's economy worse, while only 26 per cent took the opposite view that Canada's immigration levels were having a "net positive impact."
The Leger results still showed a Canada that supported immigration in principle, with 76 per cent praising its contributions to the "the cultural diversity of Canada." But these sentiments were checked by clear majorities of respondents who said increased immigration was worsening the housing crisis (75 per cent) and worsening affordability (58 per cent).
The results of an Abacus Data poll from November were even starker, with pluralities of Canadians saying immigration rates were too high, and that immigration was making the country "worse."
A near-unprecedented 67 per cent said immigration targets were either "high" or "way too high." A mere 24 per cent said immigration made the country "better," against 43 per cent who said it made Canada "mostly worse off" (21 per cent) or "much worse off" (22 per cent).
"For a long time, it was conventional wisdom that Canada is unique among peer nations in our openness and acceptance of immigration," read an Abacus analysis. "But as this survey highlights, conventional wisdom is being challenged."
The Century Initiative - a pro-immigration non-profit seeking to grow Canada's population to 100 million by 2100 - regularly commissions a survey from the Environics Institute on Canadian attitudes towards immigration.
For years, these surveys reliably showed increasing support for immigration and greater confidence that newcomers were adopting "Canadian values."
But the Century Initiative's most recent survey, conducted in the first week of September, showed steep drop-offs in pro-immigration feeling.
"There has been a significant increase in the belief that there is too much immigration to Canada, due in large part to a jump in the proportion citing concerns about how newcomers might be contributing to the current housing crisis," it stated.
In 2022, the survey had found 58 per cent of respondents agreeing that "Canada needs more immigrants to increase its population." Only a year later, just 47 per cent agreed - an even tie with the respondents who said "no."
Canada has always been a comparatively high-immigration country. But in the immediate wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Trudeau government has dialled up immigration rates to the highest in Canadian history.
The annual intake of permanent residents was boosted to 450,000 per year, as compared to 300,000 as recently as 2018. Add in exponential increases in foreign students, temporary foreign workers and refugee claimants, and Canada took in more than a million newcomers in both 2022 and 2023.
According to the most recent figures from Statistics Canada, in just three months (from July 1 to Oct. 1) Canada added an extra 430,635 people - a number of people larger than the population of Saskatoon or Halifax.
As to whether this is indeed affecting Canada's housing unaffordability, it's a matter of simple arithmetic. In 2022, there were just 219,942 new homes completed in Canada, most of them apartments. Even if each new home was shared by five renters, that would only be enough to break even on the country's net migration numbers.
"I'll put it bluntly: We've fallen into the population trap," was the recent conclusion of Stéfane Marion, chief economist at National Bank of Canada, as to how federal immigration policies were damaging the economy.
If Canadians are becoming newly skeptical of immigration, the available polls mostly show it as an economic issue; Canadians are breaking faith with immigrants not for nativist or xenophobic reasons, but because they see them as a net drain on living standards.
But these trends are all happening simultaneous to an unprecedented public outpouring of extremist demonstrations sparked by the Israel-Hamas war. And it's these scenes that seem to be driving Canadians towards newfound levels of distrust and suspicion of their neighbours.
Two men charged in connection with notorious anti-Israel protest at Toronto mall
It's the disruptors versus Mark Carney in the battle for the soul of Davos
In November, a Postmedia-commissioned Leger poll asked Canadians specifically about what the Israel-Hamas conflict was doing to the country's social fabric.
A majority (51 per cent) agree that Canadian society was not doing enough to instill newcomers with values "of tolerance of other faiths, races, and orientations." A slightly larger majority (55 per cent) said the "best way" for Canada to deal with new immigrants was to encourage them to leave behind whatever cultural identity was "incompatible" with Canada's "values and traditions."
Above all, respondents of every age, ethnicity and political orientation expressed skepticism in the maxim "diversity is our strength." Only 26 per cent said diversity was "definitely a strength"; roughly the same proportion (21 per cent) who said it was "often causing problems."
The majority (56 per cent) took a mixed view; diversity "can" provide strength, but it can also introduce conflict.
Probably the most visible rift that's emerged as a result of the last three months is that mainstream views of Canadian Muslims - which were generally trending upwards - have suddenly dropped off a cliff.
The Angus Reid Institute will regularly ask Canadians whether they think select mainstream religions are "benefitting or damaging" Canadian society.
When they asked in 2022, 29 per cent gave the "damaging" label to Islam; not a great showing, but about the same proportion that respondents gave to Catholicism (31 per cent).
When pollsters asked again in 2023, just a few weeks after the events of Oct. 7, the sentiments on all the other major religions mostly remained unchanged.
But the number of respondents calling Islam a "damaging" force for Canada had spiked to 43 per cent; more than triple the number (13 per cent) who called the faith a net benefit.
IN OTHER NEWS
It was huge news in Nunavut, even if the rest of Canada didn't really notice. But Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was in the territory on Thursday to sign a devolution agreement transferring control over lands and resources to local authorities. In the words of Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok, it's the "largest land transfer in Canada's history." And given that Nunavut is one of the world's largest subnational jurisdictions, it charts as one of the biggest land transfers ever - at least in the post-colonial era.
A video clip of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at a Nunavut press conference got a fair bit of play around the internet in recent days - although likely not for the reasons he intended. "The Conservative Party under Pierre Poilievre has made it very, very clear that they want to take Canada backwards," Trudeau told reporters. The line got attention because it's not all that different from what the Conservatives themselves are saying. On housing, affordability, inflation, taxation and a few other metrics, their platform is indeed heavily focused on getting Canada back to pre-Trudeau levels. Trudeau, however, was inferring that they'd undo his government's work on climate change and Indigenous reconciliation.
Canada is a crime-ridden den of theft and inequity, according to a new report by the U.S.-based International Coalition Against Illicit Economies. While Canada has long had a bit of a money laundering problem, this new report says that the world's criminals have noticed Canada's inability to police financial crimes within its borders. As such, the country is becoming a "major global refuge for transnational crime networks." Write the authors, "Canada remains a financial haven for kingpins, kleptocrats, oligarchs, and corrupt officials to reinvest stolen funds from their countries in real estate, energy, mining, and other sectors."
It's been a while since we've mentioned NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh in this newsletter, so here's a video of him in Edmonton criticizing the Trudeau government for only building "luxury condos" as part of its housing plans. "Developers get rich, you get gouged," he said. The "luxury condos" he's referencing are units that would be rented out at 80 per cent of local market rates (which, given that this is Edmonton, are already much lower than equivalent rents in major Ontario cities). The rich developers, meanwhile, include a consortium of Indigenous-run homebuilders, including Métis Capital Housing Corp. and Paul Band Property Management Corp. Anyway, the National Post's Chris Selley highlighted this video as possible evidence as to why the NDP are currently having their lunch eaten by the Conservatives on affordability issues.
Get all of these insights and more into your inbox by signing up for the First Reading newsletter here

Subject: Polls & surveys; Population; Canadians; Multiculturalism & pluralism; Immigrants; Muslims; Prime ministers; Immigration; Israel Hamas War-2023

Document 467 of 500

'No government could afford it': Why a newly proposed guaranteed basic income is likely doomed

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-government-could-afford-why-newly-proposed/docview/2917499645/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27No+government+could+afford+it%27%3A+Why+a+newly+proposed+guaranteed+basic+income+is+likely+doomed&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The controversial idea of a guaranteed basic income, which could cost tens of billions each year, might be making its way to the House of Commons this spring.
But the chances of a guaranteed income becoming actual policy are "very close to zero," said Kevin Milligan, an economics professor at the University of British Columbia who specializes in government social spending.
"No government could afford it. Any government who was wise would see that this is not a good way to work on the important issues of poverty," Milligan said.
The Senate has been studying Ontario Senator Kim Pate's Bill S-233, which strives to develop a national framework to provide people over the age of 17 in Canada with access to a "guaranteed livable basic income," including temporary workers, permanent residents and refugee claimants.
The bill would not actually implement a guaranteed basic income (GBI), but would force the finance minister to consult with provinces and territories, as well as Indigenous and other groups, to prepare a report setting out a framework for the policy.
If it passes a final vote in the Senate, the bill will head to the House of Commons. Liberals and Conservatives are currently refusing to take a clear position on the bill, arguing that they are waiting for the legislation to reach the lower chamber before they decide where they stand.
That sparked speculation in the following days from right-wing commentators such as the Western Standard's Cory Morgan and the Post Millenial's David Krayden, and also Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis, who wondered aloud on social media whether a basic income will end becoming Trudeau's policy Hail Mary, with his party continuing to tank in the polls.
On Dec. 29 in sharing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's new video on "debtonation" on Facebook, Lewis blamed the federal Liberals for creating "the debt problem that's connected to our inflation crisis" and wondered if Canadians would trust them again once "our traditional safety nets are destroyed."
"My guess is after they burn it all down, they will propose a solution where they have control: let's see... a digital and programmable Universal Basic Income perhaps?" she asked.
Many progressive senators and Liberal MPs have been championing the idea of a basic income for years, and it seemed to take on new life during the pandemic as Ottawa rolled out large, widespread income supports. But even then, Trudeau did not seem very enthusiastic about the idea: When he was asked about it, he said he was more interested in staying focused on controlling COVID-19.
The notion of a basic income is not new. Nobel laureate and free-market economist Milton Friedman championed the idea of a negative income tax - which would provide a certain basic income to people below a certain earning threshold, while eliminating all other government welfare programs.
Friedman's version, promising more efficiency, less bureaucracy and the freedom for recipients to spend their income as they see fit, appeals to some conservatives.
When he was the opposition finance critic, Poilievre wrote in the National Post in 2018 that"many basic income supporters have the right goal." Deputy party leader Melissa Lantsman told broadcaster Stephen LeDrew in 2020 that she "somewhat agree(s) with that" and that "Conservatives can own" the issue of a basic income.
The progressive version is different: Politicians and economists on the left have been instead championing a basic income on top of other welfare subsidies. They argue that the income would in time lift recipients out of poverty, reducing their reliance on other social supports.
"We're talking about insuring that people have adequate incomes but we're not talking about some Keynesian model that leaves everybody with just a bit of money and no other supports," said Sen. Pate last year.
That's why UBC's Milligan compares trying to define a basic income to nailing Jell-O to a wall. "Because any time you kind of nail it down to what you think they mean, it changes," he said.
Senator Pate, having been appointed under Trudeau, comes at it from the progressive side. She said Canada already provides supports similar to a basic income for some groups: the Guaranteed Income Supplement for low-income seniors; the Canada Child Benefit for families with children; and the soon-to-be Canada Disability Benefit for people with disabilities, for example.
"It's like we're inching towards it. Why not just take the next step?" she said.
But passing bills that are not government priorities can be a tedious and long process. S-233 was first introduced in the Senate in December 2021, at the same time as an identical one, C-223, was introduced in the House of Commons by NDP MP Leah Gazan.
Gazan's bill has remain untouched in the House, whereas the Senate bill was adopted on division in second reading in April 2023 to be further studied at committee.
Even Conservative senators agreed to take a further look at S-233, despite claiming their caucus does not support it.
"I think that the light of day will expose the weaknesses in this bill," said the Conservative party's critic for the bill, Nova Scotia Senator Michael L. MacDonald, at the time.
Sen. Pate however claims that there are "many, many people within all of the parties who are supportive" of her bill, and she remains hopeful its study in committee will wrap up soon after the Senate returns next month and be submitted to a final vote in the Upper Chamber in the spring.
Chris Selley: Universal basic income is not coming to Canada
Liberals considering a universal basic income, but economists have tough questions
Attempts have been made in the past to pilot basic income projects, but any promising results ran up against serious cost concerns. There was Manitoba's "Mincome" in the 1970s, Ontario's brief pilot in 2017-2018, and P.E.I. has been running a targeted basic income pilot program with a few hundred recipients since 2021.
British Columbia and Quebec have studied it but concluded that a targeted approach is better than a universal basic income.
"The needs of people in this society are too diverse to be effectively answered simply with a cheque from the government," wrote the B.C. Basic Income panel in its Dec. 2020 report on the concept. "A basic income is a very costly approach to addressing any specific goal, such as poverty reduction."
The Parliamentary Budget Officer produced an analysis in 2021 that concluded that a national guaranteed basic income using parameters of Ontario's 2017 pilot project could cut Canada's poverty rate by half in just one year, but at a hefty cost of $91 billion in 2024-25 and $93 billion in 2025-26. That's nearly double what Ottawa spends, for example, on health transfers to the provinces.
"So I think the probability of a UBI (universal basic income) being implemented in Canada on a widespread basis, not as small pilot or something, is very close to zero," said Milligan.
Even Sen. Pate admits that there are impediments for political parties to move forward with a basic income, given the initial cost of the measure and the fact that any given party is unlikely to reap all of the benefits of cracking down on poverty in Canada in one electoral cycle.
But she remains hopeful that she will see some sort of basic income become reality in her lifetime.
"I think it's much like medicare," she said. "Many people thought it was a good idea, (but) people were afraid what it would cost and afraid that they wouldn't see the benefit within the electoral cycle. And then eventually, Saskatchewan just decided to do it and and once it started rolling out, everybody wanted in."
Milligan said he agrees with the B.C. panel's approach, which is to "roll up your sleeves and do the hard work of expanding, improving, replacing, revamping our existing social support system."
"It's complicated, it's messy, it's hard work," he said. "It's not a magical solution, but I don't believe in magical solutions. I think a lot of the basic income stuff is just people looking for a magical solution to a tough problem."
National Post
calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 468 of 500

Michael Taube: Toronto's anti-sledding Karen brigade should remember the joys of childhood

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-torontos-anti-sledding-karen/docview/2917178945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+Toronto%27s+anti-sledding+Karen+brigade+should+remember+the+joys+of+childhood&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto recently decided in its (ahem) infinite wisdom to ban tobogganing at 45 hills in public parks because they feel they're "unsafe." This led to two opposing reactions. The cadre of (mostly) left-leaning Karens, with parental tears in their eyes and wearing permanent boo-boo faces, breathed heavy sighs of relief. The more sensible-thinking Torontonians, a number that's getting smaller each year, either laughed or were outraged. As city councillor Brad Bradford nicely put it, "frankly, it's embarrassing."
When people call Toronto the "no fun city," they ain't Whistlin' Dixie. Fun packed its bags and stormed out of Hogtown long ago. It won't be returning anytime soon.
Now, Mayor Olivia Chow, along with city council, could attempt to work with city officials to modify the tobogganing ban and dramatically lower the number of affected hills. They could even vote to reverse the ban altogether.
They won't, of course. Most of them are card-carrying members of the left-leaning Karen, permanent boo-boo face crowd.
If that wasn't bad enough, there's another significant issue at play. Chow, along with many city councillors, have clearly forgotten their childhood. They don't remember what it was like to enjoy the winter months with their families and friends. While there hasn't been much snow in the city this year, those who either grew up in Toronto or another cold-weather city in Canada and beyond know how magical this season is.
Looking at the fresh coat of snow on the ground always made me think of one of my favourite children's books, Ezra Jack Keats's The Snowy Day. Much like Peter, the young boy who enjoyed making snow tracks with his boots, throwing snowballs and making snow angels, I also couldn't wait to go out into the "deep, deep snow."
We went skiing, skating, snowshoeing and sledding. We had snowball fights and built snow forts. We tried to find two identical snowflakes without any luck.
And, for God's sake, almost all of us went tobogganing on any hill we could find!
I went with my family and friends every winter. I still remember my hard plastic blue toboggan with fondness. It was always freezing cold, even when I wore snow pants as a young lad. I didn't care about the hill's conditions, a typical combination of snow and hard ice (among other things) that could make you go at a fairly high speed and occasionally launch you into the air. If I fell, I had a good laugh, dusted myself off and went back up the hill for another adventure.
I also took my son tobogganing at a park near our home for many years. Seeing the joy on his face as he zoomed up and down the hill in an air-filled toboggan that was far more comfortable than my wintery vehicle gave me enormous pleasure. When I occasionally went with him, it was a shared experience that we could both enjoy without a care in the world.
These early years are supposed to be a time of exhilaration, exploration and imagination. Placing rules and regulations on fun, enjoyment and a carefree environment for young children is bloody offensive.
Sure, you can get hurt going down a hill in a toboggan. You can also get hurt in a variety of other ways, including skiing, skating - or being hit by a car walking down the street at any given moment. There's no real way to prevent these types of accidents from happening. The world can be (and is) an unsafe place, but you shouldn't put the fear of God in young, developing minds to make them scared of tobogganing in a park like their parents and grandparents used to do.
That's what city officials, the mayor and councillors have either largely forgotten, or choose to forget.
Some would argue the City of Toronto was simply trying to protect itself from any potential legal action. If someone gets injured tobogganing on a hill in a city park, they could start a lawsuit and possibly win a large settlement. According to the Canadian Institute for Health Information, there were "170 tobogganing-related hospitalizations recorded in Canada" between 2020-2021, many involving young children. That's far less than ice skating hospitalizations (522) falls on the ice (6,590) or even playground injuries (1,446) during this same time period, but a number that has to be considered.
Yet, there were more realistic ways to prevent this from happening. Put up signs clearly absolving the city from responsibility for tobogganing accidents on these hills, for instance. Have city workers come out to thoroughly check the conditions of every park hill more frequently. Install snow fencing, hay bales and the like, as Bradford suggested. The easiest solution of all? Let parents decide for their children whether the hill is safe for tobogganing rather than more state - or, in this case, city - interference in our lives.
Meanwhile, are Toronto police really going to go from park to park to issue fines for breaking a preposterous tobogganing ban? If so, what an enormous waste of time, money and resources.
Perhaps Ontario Premier Doug Ford should step in and bring this matter to an end. Otherwise, maybe Chow and city councillors should buy or borrow a toboggan, go down one of these oh-so-scary hills and remember what it was like to be a child and enjoy winter.
National Post

Subject: Bans; Winter; Skating; Ice

Location: Canada
