Document 473 of 500

Chris Selley: Low-energy Jagmeet Singh is squandering what should be the NDP's moment

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-low-energy-jagmeet-singh-is/docview/2916878059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chris+Selley%3A+Low-energy+Jagmeet+Singh+is+squandering+what+should+be+the+NDP%27s+moment&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal New Democrats recently announced a bit of a leadership shake-up: Anne McGrath, whose many past roles have included chief of staff to Jack Layton and principal secretary to Rachel Notley, is stepping down as party president to become principal secretary for Jagmeet Singh. The press release said the changes were about "building momentum for the next election," which New Democrats could surely use: Over the last two years the Conservatives have surged to roughly 40 per cent in the polls; the Liberals have tanked to roughly 25 per cent; and the NDP is right where it was before, at 20 per cent or less.
This demographics underpinning this are unprecedented - the most recent Abacus Data poll has the Conservatives leading among 18-to-29-year-olds with 32 per cent of the vote - and absolutely damning to Singh, if not an existential threat to the party's future.
No one disputes what's driving the shift: Canada's insane housing market has thrown a wrench into many millennial-and-younger voters' plans for the future. And if the Conservatives don't have all the answers, well, the Liberals have been in power for eight years. And according to Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. (CMHC) statistics released this week we're constructing seven-per-centfewer homes nationwide in 2023 than we did in 2022.
Only very recently did the Liberals suddenly get serious on this file, deploying one of their only conspicuously competent ministers to the portfolio, withholding federal money for housing projects that succumb to NIMBY influences and holding press conferences whenever and wherever possible.
Trouble is, people notice the numbers associated with these announcements and roll their eyes. Last week, Justin Trudeau went to Guelph, Ont. and boasted of federal money dedicated to "fast-tracking about 750 new housing units in the next couple of years." A two-bedroom condo in Guelph within walking distance of the GO train, which gets you to Toronto in a glacial 90 minutes, is currently on the market for $679,900. In Guelph. That city is many more than a few hundred extra units from sanity.
What Poilievre has tapped into here isn't just the number-one issue of our time - when the Liberals are mooting limiting immigration, you know they've made a hell of a mess - but anger at the politicians at every level who sat back and let it happen. Show this moment in the abstract to a socialist political science professor, and he would tell you this was a perfect moment for an NDP breakthrough. Many younger, ideological leftists are practically on their knees begging Singh to bust out of the political centre and go full-on class war.
Maybe that's what he was trying to do in avideo released this week in which he tears a strip off a new housing development in Edmonton's Griesbach neighbourhood. Instead he pretty much united the country in frustration.
"Only 13 per cent of the homes ... will be affordable," he says of the development.
"(Trudeau) is building luxury condos you can't afford," Singh tweeted along with the video "With his plan ... developers get rich, you get gouged."
Federal money in Griesbach is funding two projects One, by the City of Edmonton Non-Profit Housing Corp., will feature 85 rental units, with 43 designated for "affordable use" - "predominantly for recent immigrants, women fleeing violence with their children and low-income families," per a city staff report The other, by Métis Capital Housing Corporation, will (per CMHC) "consist of approximately 127 units for Métis and other Indigenous individuals and families, including 50 units dedicated to women and children fleeing violence."
This is the development Singh goes after? What the bloody hell?
Even if the developments did include "luxury condos," the term "luxury condo" is progressive language from the 1980s, when Prosecco-o'clock socialists tended to see new highrises as a sort of stain on proper urban living. Yuppies lived in condos. In the movie Wall Street, Gordon Gecko's underlings lived in condos. It was terrible.
I was 11 years old when Wall Street was released. I'm 47 now. Nobody younger than me has any familiarity with this world view. They just want somewhere they can live where they might be able to build a bit of capital, as most of their parents did. As economist Mike Moffatt observed on X, for just about anyone under 40 and unaided by generational wealth, condos are starter homes: In London, Ont., he noted, 81 per cent of homes priced under $400,000 (of which there aren't many!) are condos.
"Condos are entry level homes, affordable homes, and retirement/downsizing homes," former Toronto chief planner Jennifer Keesmaat, whose 2018 mayoral campaign was run by Brian Topp and the NDP machine, wrote on X, responding to Singh's video. "This is rhetoric meant to inflame and divide."
All in all, a job very crappily done by Singh and his newly restaffed office. If the NDP aspire to anything more than extracting a few concessions here and there from the governing party in a minority Parliament - and I'm not really sure they do - they need more than a new principal secretary. They need a new leader, post-haste. Someone with a pulse.
National Post
cselley@postmedia.com
Chris Selley: More pearls of nonsense from Jean Chrétien
Chris Selley: It's far too late for the Liberals to save the CBC from Poilievre

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Subject: Affordable housing; Condominiums

Location: Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Identifier / keyword: federal; democrats; recently; announced; leadership; mcgrath

Title: Chris Selley: Low-energy Jagmeet Singh is squandering what should be the NDP's moment

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 20, 2024

Section: NP Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 474 of 500

'No government could afford it'; Why the guaranteed basic income is likely doomed

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-government-could-afford-why-guaranteed-basic/docview/2916758078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27No+government+could+afford+it%27%3B+Why+the+guaranteed+basic+income+is+likely+doomed&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The controversial idea of a guaranteed basic income, which could cost tens of billions each year, might be making its way to the House of Commons this spring.
But the chances of a guaranteed income becoming actual policy are "very close to zero," said Kevin Milligan, an economics professor at the University of British Columbia who specializes in government social spending.
"No government could afford it. Any government who was wise would see that this is not a good way to work on the important issues of poverty," Milligan said.
The Senate has been studying Ontario Senator Kim Pate's Bill S-233, which strives to develop a national framework to provide people over the age of 17 in Canada with access to a "guaranteed livable basic income," including temporary workers, permanent residents and refugee claimants.
The bill would not actually implement a guaranteed basic income (GBI), but would force the finance minister to consult with provinces and territories, as well as Indigenous and other groups, to prepare a report setting out a framework for the policy.
If it passes a final vote in the Senate, the bill will head to the House of Commons. Liberals and Conservatives are currently refusing to take a clear position on the bill, arguing that they are waiting for the legislation to reach the lower chamber before they decide where they stand.
That sparked speculation in the following days from right-wing commentators such as the Western Standard's Cory Morgan and the Post Millennial's David Krayden, and also Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis, who wondered on social media whether a basic income will end up becoming Trudeau's policy Hail Mary, with his party continuing to tank in the polls.
On Dec. 29 in sharing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's new video on "debtonation" on Facebook, Lewis blamed the federal Liberals for creating "the debt problem that's connected to our inflation crisis" and wondered if Canadians would trust them again once "our traditional safety nets are destroyed."
"My guess is after they burn it all down, they will propose a solution where they have control: let's see... a digital and programmable Universal Basic Income perhaps?" she asked.
Many progressive senators and Liberal MPs have been championing the idea of a basic income for years, and it seemed to take on new life during the pandemic as Ottawa rolled out large, widespread income supports. But even then, Trudeau did not seem very enthusiastic about the idea: When he was asked about it, he said he was more interested in staying focused on controlling COVID-19.
The notion of a basic income is not new. Nobel laureate and free-market economist Milton Friedman championed the idea of a negative income tax - which would provide a certain basic income to people below a certain earning threshold, while eliminating all other government welfare programs.
Friedman's version, promising more efficiency, less bureaucracy and the freedom for recipients to spend their income as they see fit, appeals to some conservatives.
When he was the opposition finance critic, Poilievre wrote in the National Post in 2018 that "many basic income supporters have the right goal." Deputy party leader Melissa Lantsman told broadcaster Stephen LeDrew in 2020 that she "somewhat agree(s) with that" and that "Conservatives can own" the issue of a basic income.
The progressive version is different: Politicians and economists on the left have been instead championing a basic income on top of other welfare subsidies. They argue that the income would in time lift recipients out of poverty, reducing their reliance on other social supports.
"We're talking about ensuring that people have adequate incomes but we're not talking about some Keynesian model that leaves everybody with just a bit of money and no other supports," said Sen. Pate last year.
That's why UBC's Milligan compares trying to define a basic income to nailing Jell-O to a wall. "Because any time you kind of nail it down to what you think they mean, it changes," he said.
Senator Pate, having been appointed under Trudeau, comes at it from the progressive side. She said Canada already provides supports similar to a basic income for some groups: the Guaranteed Income Supplement for low-income seniors; the Canada Child Benefit for families with children; and the soon-tobe Canada Disability Benefit for people with disabilities, for example.
"It's like we're inching towards it. Why not just take the next step?" she said.
But passing bills that are not government priorities can be a tedious and long process. S-233 was first introduced in the Senate in December 2021, at the same time as an identical one, C-223, was introduced in the House of Commons by NDP MP Leah Gazan.
Gazan's bill has remain untouched in the House, whereas the Senate bill was adopted on division in second reading in April 2023 to be further studied at committee.
Even Conservative senators agreed to take a further look at S-233, despite claiming their caucus does not support it.
"I think that the light of day will expose the weaknesses in this bill," said the Conservative party's critic for the bill, Nova Scotia Senator Michael L. MacDonald, at the time. Sen. Pate however claims that there are "many, many people within all of the parties who are supportive" of her bill, and she remains hopeful its study in committee will wrap up soon after the Senate returns next month and be submitted to a final vote in the Upper Chamber in the spring.
Attempts have been made in the past to pilot basic income projects, but any promising results ran up against serious cost concerns. There was Manitoba's "Mincome" in the 1970s, Ontario's brief pilot in 2017-2018, and P.E.I. has been running a targeted basic income pilot program with a few hundred recipients since 2021.
British Columbia and Quebec have studied it but concluded that a targeted approach is better than a universal basic income.
"The needs of people in this society are too diverse to be effectively answered simply with a cheque from the government," wrote the B.C. Basic Income panel in its Dec. 2020 report on the concept. "A basic income is a very costly approach to addressing any specific goal, such as poverty reduction."
The Parliamentary Budget Officer produced an analysis in 2021 that concluded that a national guaranteed basic income using parameters of Ontario's 2017 pilot project could cut Canada's poverty rate by half in just one year, but at a hefty cost of $91 billion in 2024-25 and $93 billion in 2025-26. That's nearly double what Ottawa spends, for example, on health transfers to the provinces.
"So I think the probability of a UBI (universal basic income) being implemented in Canada on a widespread basis, not as small pilot or something, is very close to zero," said Milligan.
Even Sen. Pate admits that there are impediments for political parties to move forward with a basic income, given the initial cost of the measure and the fact that any given party is unlikely to reap all of the benefits of cracking down on poverty in Canada in one electoral cycle.
But she remains hopeful that she will see some sort of basic income become reality in her lifetime.
"I think it's much like medicare," she said. "Many people thought it was a good idea, (but) people were afraid what it would cost and afraid that they wouldn't see the benefit within the electoral cycle. And then eventually, Saskatchewan just decided to do it and once it started rolling out, everybody wanted in." Milligan said he agrees with the B.C. panel's approach, which is to "roll up your sleeves and do the hard work of expanding, improving, replacing, revamping our existing social support system."
"It's complicated, it's messy, it's hard work," he said. "It's not a magical solution, but I don't believe in magical solutions. I think a lot of the basic income stuff is just people looking for a magical solution to a tough problem."
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Jagmeet Singh squanders NDP's moment; Making a mess of housing crisis opportunity

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jagmeet-singh-squanders-ndps-moment-making-mess/docview/2916757907/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+squanders+NDP%27s+moment%3B+Making+a+mess+of+housing+crisis+opportunity&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal New Democrats recently announced a bit of a leadership shake-up: Anne McGrath, whose many past roles have included chief of staff to Jack Layton and principal secretary to Rachel Notley, is stepping down as party president to become principal secretary for Jagmeet Singh. The press release said the changes were about "building momentum for the next election," which New Democrats could surely use: Over the last two years the Conservatives have surged to roughly 40 per cent in the polls; the Liberals have tanked to roughly 25 per cent; and the NDP is right where it was before, at 20 per cent or less.
The demographics underpinning this are unprecedented - the most recent Abacus Data poll has the Conservatives leading among 18-to-29-year-olds with 32 per cent of the vote - and absolutely damning to Singh, if not an existential threat to the party's future.
No one disputes what's driving the shift: Canada's insane housing market has thrown a wrench into many millennial-and-younger voters'plans for the future. And if the Conservatives don't have all the answers, well, the Liberals have been in power for eight years. And according to Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. (CMHC) statistics released this week we're constructing seven per cent fewer homes nationwide in 2023 than we did in 2022.
Only very recently did the Liberals suddenly get serious on this file, deploying one of their only conspicuously competent ministers to the portfolio, withholding federal money for housing projects that succumb to NIMBY influences and holding press conferences whenever and wherever possible.
Trouble is, people notice the numbers associated with these announcements and roll their eyes. Last week, Justin Trudeau went to Guelph, Ont., and boasted of federal money dedicated to "fast-tracking about 750 new housing units in the next couple of years." A two-bedroom condo in Guelph within walking distance of the GO train, which gets you to Toronto in a glacial 90 minutes, is currently on the market for $679,900. In Guelph. That city is many more than a few hundred extra units from sanity.
What Poilievre has tapped into here isn't just the No. 1 issue of our time - when the Liberals are mooting limiting immigration, you know they've made a hell of a mess - but anger at the politicians at every level who sat back and let it happen. Show this moment in the abstract to a socialist political science professor, and he would tell you this was a perfect moment for an NDP breakthrough. Many younger, ideological leftists are practically on their knees begging Singh to bust out of the political centre and go full-on class war.
Maybe that's what he was trying to do in a video released this week in which he tears a strip off a new housing development in Edmonton's Griesbach neighbourhood. Instead he pretty much united the country in frustration.
"Only 13 per cent of the homes ... will be affordable," he says of the development.
"(Trudeau) is building luxury condos you can't afford," Singh tweeted along with the video. "With his plan ... developers get rich, you get gouged."
Federal money in Griesbach is funding two projects. One, by the City of Edmonton Non-Profit Housing Corp., will feature 85 rental units, with 43 designated for "affordable use" - "predominantly for recent immigrants, women fleeing violence with their children and low-income families," per a city staff report. The other, by Métis Capital Housing Corporation, will (per CMHC) "consist of approximately 127 units for Métis and other Indigenous individuals and families, including 50 units dedicated to women and children fleeing violence."
This is the development Singh goes after? What the bloody hell? Even if the developments did include "luxury condos," the term "luxury condo" is progressive language from the 1980s, when Proseccoo'clock socialists tended to see new highrises as a sort of stain on proper urban living. Yuppies lived in condos. In the movie Wall Street, Gordon Gecko's underlings lived in condos. It was terrible.
I was 11 years old when Wall Street was released. I'm 47 now. Nobody younger than me has any familiarity with this world view. They just want somewhere they can live where they might be able to build a bit of capital, as most of their parents did. As economist Mike Moffatt observed on X, for just about anyone under 40 and unaided by generational wealth, condos are starter homes: In London, Ont., he noted, 81 per cent of homes priced under $400,000 (of which there aren't many!) are condos.
"Condos are entry level homes, affordable homes, and retirement/downsizing homes," former Toronto chief planner Jennifer Keesmaat, whose 2018 mayoral campaign was run by Brian Topp and the NDP machine, wrote on X, responding to Singh's video. "This is rhetoric meant to inflame and divide."
All in all, a job very crappily done by Singh and his newly restaffed office. If the NDP aspire to anything more than extracting a few concessions here and there from the governing party in a minority Parliament - and I'm not really sure they do - they need more than a new principal secretary. They need a new leader, post-haste. Someone with a pulse.
National Post cselley@postmedia.com

Document 476 of 500

Moderate middle reassessing woke left in wake of Oct. 7 attacks; Support of Hamas spurring public revulsion

Author: Levitt, Howard

Publication info: Levitt, Howard. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/moderate-middle-reassessing-woke-left-wake-oct-7/docview/2916757124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Moderate+middle+reassessing+woke+left+in+wake+of+Oct.+7+attacks%3B+Support+of+Hamas+spurring+public+revulsion&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.2&amp;au=Levitt%2C+Howard&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the prelude to Pharoah releasing the Israelites from slavery, Jews were asked, as God's final plague in the Story of Passover, to spread lamb blood on the front of their homes so that the Angel of Death would pass over and thereby spare their first sons.
As part of Jewish ritual, Jews place an identifying mezuzah outside the doors of their homes. But today, in Canada and in much of the world, Jews are removing those mezuzahs in fear as rallies calling for the eradication of Jews in Israel and sometimes abroad as well take over the streets of our cities, with Jewish homes and business attacked, including by firebombing, and individuals targeted. Remember, these rallies started as the celebration of a pogrom well before Israel's counterattack.
Some of the useful idiots from the woke left supporting Hamas at those rallies and waving its flags are unaware of what the group stands for and do not realize that they will be next in line.
The Hamas Charter tells us as much. In article seven, Hamas says it wants its control to "spread all over the world," not just Palestine. It calls Jews "Zionist invaders," and calls for Jews to be killed, wherever they are hiding, "no matter how long it will take."
Or, as one colourful quote puts it: "The time will not come until Muslims will fight the Jews (and kill them); until the Jews hide behind rocks and trees, which will cry: Muslim! there is a Jew hiding behind me, come on and kill him! And take article eight, which declares that, "Jihad is its path and death for the case of Allah its most sublime belief."
Most westerners have never taken this nonsense seriously and believed the declarations of the radical Islamists to be childish optics, assuming they will grow up and ultimately become reasonable. I have long warned that you should believe what Iran, Hamas, Hezbollah and other Islamist extremists say. They are not you. It is not hyperbole. They actually mean it. After Oct. 7, people have come to realize that.
The rallies on our streets, every single one of them calling for the extermination of Jews in Israel (or beyond), have presented opportunities as well as problems.
The problems are obvious. The opportunities arise from the fact that the woke left and radical Islam have been exposed and the response has been public revulsion. But for this push back, the presidents of Harvard and the University of Pennsylvania would not have resigned and there would not now be significant pressure to revamp universities, which have become a cesspool for indoctrinating students to hate western traditions and, indeed, education itself.
There has been newfound pressure on police to clamp down on these rallies which had terrorized our communities. Attempts to "cancel Christmas" and attacks on Jewish bakeries and delicatessens have not been helpful to the Hamas supporters'PR campaign.
Proof? Survey after survey showing opposition to these demonstrations.
A Leger-Postmedia poll found that 75 per cent of Canadians believe that non-citizens should face deportation if they publicly express hatred towards a minority group or support a terrorist organization such as Hamas.
In the United States, according to a PBS NewsHour/Marist poll conducted Nov. 6 to Nov. 9, the majority said they sympathize with Israel. As well, the Palestinian solidarity protests have not been supported publicly by the vast majority of politicians. As the protests have become even more vicious, sentiment has moved even further against them. It is not just moderates who are repulsed. Governments in Europe are quickly moving or being elected to stop the tide of Muslim immigration, such that innocent moderate Muslims have become the victims of their radical cohorts. If they were smart, they would be loudly denouncing them. A few are, such as the Council of Muslims against Antisemitism, but not enough. It is in the interest of the majority of Muslims in Canada who are moderate to speak up - and loudly. They must repudiate the naked extremism and hatred on our streets.
When it comes to the woke left, the response has been equally damning.
The director of the University Alberta Sexual Assault Centre, Samantha Pearson, was fired in November for supporting an open letter by Ontario MPP Sarah Jama (who was kicked out the NDP caucus) questioning whether Israeli women were actually raped in the Oct. 7 attacks. Other traditional supporters of women's rights have been silent when it comes to the plight of Jewish women on Oct. 7, nor did such "feminist" crusaders change their tone when an investigation in the politically friendly New York Times reported that more than 30 women's bodies had been found with their pants torn off AND nails and gunshots in their groins, among other atrocities. Evidence has come out by released hostages that the Hamas'hostages, men and women, have been subject to continuing brutal rapes by their captors in Gaza and preventing them from telling that story is the likely reason Hamas did not release some of the women captives when all female hostages were supposed to be.
Yet the woke traditional feminist left has maintained its silence. Outrageous and an abdication of any future moral authority. As the saying has become, "Me Too-except for Jew." Or, as Bari Weiss put it, "Believe all women," has become, "Don't believe even the confessions of rapists if their victims are Jews."
Society has noticed and the moderate middle has been re-evaluating the left, which has revealed itself to be morally bankrupt.
Unions that did not renounce the Hamas attacks have left many of their members feeling disillusioned and disenfranchised. (Disclosure: My firm is representing members of CUPE who are suing the union and its president, Fred Hahn, over its response to the attacks).
Like wise,moderate women have realized that left-wing feminist leaders who have ignored or justified the massacre and sexual assault of women in Israel have lost the right to lead.
The self-immolation of the left flowing from their abdication of responsibility in light of the Hamas pogrom of Oct. 7 might be the end of the ascendancy of the woke left. We are past the end of the beginning of the move movement and at the beginning of its end.
Financial Post

Document 479 of 500

January 19, 2024 (Page A6)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/january-19-2024-page-a6/docview/2919614144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apdnnationalpost&amp;atitle=January+19%2C+2024+%28Page+A6%29&amp;title=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=25&amp;issue=70&amp;spage=A6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 482 of 500

Notley's legacy of division; NDPer attacked hard-working farm families

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notleys-legacy-division-ndper-attacked-hard/docview/2916544210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Notley%27s+legacy+of+division%3B+NDPer+attacked+hard-working+farm+families&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Rachel Notley political death watch is over at long last! Congratulations to everyone who marked Tuesday, Jan. 16, on their dead pools.
The 17th premier of Alberta announced on Tuesday that she'll be stepping down as leader of the Alberta NDP after nearly a decade in the role.
Notley has been largely invisible since losing her second straight provincial election seven months ago, allowing United Conservative Party central casting to wheel out Steven Guilbeault, the federal environment minister, as the province's de facto opposition leader. Why she waited for an extreme cold snap to finally take her "walk in the snow" is anybody's guess.
Notley cemented her place in the province's history books in 2015, when she led the Alberta NDP to a historic majority government, breaking 44 years of uninterrupted Progressive Conservative rule. While she lasted just a single term as premier, she stayed on as party leader following the NDP's 2019 election loss, making an unsuccessful bid to reclaim the premier's office four years later.
Notley, who learned politics at the knee of her father, Grant Notley (a one-time Alberta NDP leader himself), can also look back at a long and distinguished tenure as a legislator. First elected to Alberta's legislative assembly in 2008, she has represented Edmonton-Strathcona without interruption for just under 16 years, making her the longest-serving MLA currently in the legislature.
But due to her myriad electoral accomplishments, Notley will carry the more dubious legacy of bringing her toxic brand of "woke" politics to the province.
Eschewing the universal humanism and pragmatic approach to governing of the left-leaning Prairie populists who came before her (her father among them), Notley instead embraced a more ideological brand of social democracy, instinctively seeing politics as a zero-sum game pitting different social classes against one another. (She once famously signalled her Marxist virtues by sporting a wrist watch bearing the visage of mass-murdering Cuban revolutionary Che Guevara.)
Notley's north star, throughout her four rocky years as premier, was to pit Albertan against Albertan whenever possible. One of her early moves was to appoint anti-fossil fuel activist (and graduate of the Steven Guilbeault school of civil disobedience) Tzeporah Berman to a provincial oilsands advisory group - a clear act of provocation against the province's oil and gas sector. Berman would go on to pocket more than $20,000 for her services as a taxpayer-funded critic of Alberta's most important industry.
Notley also found time to attack the hard-working farm families who, quite literally, built the province with their bare hands. The Notley government's Bill 6 sought to remake farm kids into a new class of peasant proletariat, placing the province's Workers'Compensation Board between them and their parents. A groundswell of opposition from rural Albertans forced Notley to water down the bill, but the episode nevertheless revealed her disdain for the agrarian classes her own father once championed.
When Notley wasn't sowing division at home, she was busy trying to convince the chattering classes in Central Canada that she wasn't "one of those" Albertans. Once caught on camera calling her home province an "embarrassing cousin," Notley never let her duties as premier get in the way of her rubbing elbows with southern Ontario NDP A-listers.
In 2016, Notley was the guest of honour at a $9,975-per-plate Toronto fundraiser hosted by then-Ontario NDP leader Andrea Horwath. Over the years, she's barely hidden her ambition to one day ditch the sleepy backwater of Edmonton and make her mark on Ottawa as federal NDP leader.
Notley scraped the bottom of the barrel in her political swan song last spring, throwing out all manner of personal attacks at her opponent, Danielle Smith, in one of the most negative, scorched-earth campaigns in the province's history. This was all for naught, in the end, as Notley grossly overestimated the public's trepidation toward Smith and her own personal appeal.
Notley did, however, succeed in driving the wedge between urban and rural Alberta even further in her last-ditch attempt to win back power.
To get a sense of how Notley transformed Alberta politics for the worse, one need only look at the map from last year's provincial election. A province that had the most cohesive political culture in the country is now a house divided - split down the middle between the vegan bakeries of Whyte Avenue and Inglewood, and the family farms and oil rigs of the province's heartland.
As Rachel Notley looks back at her political legacy, she'll have to come to terms with the fact that she's left behind a province that's meaner, less charitable and more divided than the one she inherited.
So long, Rachel. You won't be missed.
National Post

Document 483 of 500

Marouf at helm of anti-Israel web broadcaster; Liberals' former anti-racism consultant

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/marouf-at-helm-anti-israel-web-broadcaster/docview/2916540817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Marouf+at+helm+of+anti-Israel+web+broadcaster%3B+Liberals%27+former+anti-racism+consultant&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Laith Marouf, the government-funded anti-racism consultant whose record of hateful tweets became an embarrassing controversy for the Liberals, is now the director of an anti-Israel online broadcaster.
Free Palestine Television (FPTV) was launched after the Hamas massacre of Israeli civilians on Oct. 7 and has featured footage of Hamas's attacks on Israeli soldiers as well as manoeuvres from Hamas-affiliated militias such as the mujahedeen Brigades and Al-Qassam Brigades, which operate out of Gaza and the West Bank. It also published speeches with live translation to English from Hezbollah leader Sayyed Hassan Nasrallah and it publishes press releases attributed to various terror groups, such as Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iranbacked militias.
Marouf, who's identified on its site as FPTV's founder, is a senior consultant with the Community Media Advocacy Centre, which had a $122,000 contract with the Liberal government in 2022 to develop an anti-racism strategy for the broadcasting sector.
It subsequently emerged that Marouf had a history of hateful social media posts about Jews, francophones and Black people.
One read: "You know all those loud mouthed bags of human feces, a.k.a. the Jewish White Supremacists; when we liberate Palestine and they have to go back to where they come from, they will return to being low voiced b-----s of their Christian/Secular White Supremacist Masters."
The tweets were exposed in news reports and the government cancelled the contract in August 2022, although it was revealed that the Trudeau government had known about the tweets a month earlier and had done nothing.
Marouf 's lawyer at the time said the posts were not directed at all Jews, just certain ones.
Canadian Heritage has since changed the way it vets funding requests for community and anti-racism projects.
The federal government has sought to recoup its funds from Marouf through a collections agency, the Canada Revenue Agency and the courts, but has reported no success. Reports in October 2022 said Marouf was living in Beirut.
Since the national scandal over funding to Marouf's organization, he has become a regular on various left-wing social media programs, offering opinions on Palestinian politics, Israel and other geopolitical issues. He has also appeared several times on Critical Hour, a podcast hosted by Sputnik News, a stateowned Russian broadcaster, and on programs by Russian propaganda network Russia Today.
FPTV's website says it launched "with the outbreak of the Al-Aqsa Flood" - the name Hamas and other Palestinian groups have given to the Oct. 7 massacre. The online station is based out of Lebanon, and is staffed primarily by students, professors and community members, Marouf said in an interview.
"Knowing that there is a huge gap left in news programming in English that is supportive of resistance and the right to liberation ...
we decided to bring together our communities here to start a project, a community television station," Marouf said in the interview with Activist News Network in November 2023.
The Middle East Forum, an American conser vative think tank, published a report on Friday claiming that FPTV had links to the Lebanon-based group Al-Tajammu. According to Michael Barak, a researcher at the Institute for counterterrorism at Israel's Reichmann University, Al-Tajammu is "an international pro-Iranian platform to leverage the resistance axis against the U.S., Israel and their allies."
Marouf described the attacks on Al-Tajammu as "frivolous" and accused the Middle East Forum of being a "racist organization."
Marouf and FPTV also downplayed any connections to Al-Tajammu.
In a series of posts to X on Saturday, FPTV alleged that "the largest Jewish White Supremacist accounts in Apartheid Canada are mounting a disinformation campaign against our Community Television station," and that it "is unaffiliated with any political party or groups, including Al-Tajammu which hosted our studios for a brief time & we have since moved as we expanded."
The Middle East Forum report, and subsequent social media posts, suggested that Free Palestine TV was "established" in Canada.
Marouf told National Post: "It's not established in Canada. It's not based in Canada. There's no connection there."
In mid-December, FPTV hosted a fundraiser for the channel on Zoom, which featured guest speakers who are prominent figures in Indigenous advocacy in Canada. Among them were Sleydo', also known as Molly Wickham, a Wet'suwet'en chief and prominent opponent of the Coastal GasLink pipeline in British Columbia, Judy Da Silva, a prominent member of the Grassy Narrows First Nation in northwestern Ontario, and Isaac Murdoch, an artist and member of the Serpent River First Nation. National Post did not attend the event and was unable to confirm that all the guests appeared.
National Post

Document 484 of 500

Chow puts Trudeau Liberals, Toronto MPs in tight corner; Tax proposal is bold political manoeuvering

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-puts-trudeau-liberals-toronto-mps-tight/docview/2916538140/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chow+puts+Trudeau+Liberals%2C+Toronto+MPs+in+tight+corner%3B+Tax+proposal+is+bold+political+manoeuvering&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city of Toronto's proposed 2024 budget is both outrageous and a master class in political manoeuvring. At a time when people are struggling financially, who would suggest a 10.5 per cent tax increase, then threaten to boost it by an additional six per cent if the federal government doesn't pony up more money? And yet, Toronto needs a big strategy to solve its big financial problem, a looming $1.8-billion budget deficit.
The city has been struggling with budget shortfalls for years, never taking dramatic action. The city's new plan is certainly bold and it's based on shrewd political calculation.
The premise of the budget is that Toronto is unique - a city so large, so special, that it deserves financial help not only from Ontario taxpayers, but from every taxpayer in the country. The case for that is a bit weak. Under the heading "The Challenges Toronto Faces Are Unique," the city budget presentation raises no fewer than 16 reasons why this is supposedly the case. But it lists things like rising inflation, increased costs and reliance on property taxes, which are common to all cities.
Fortunately, Toronto only has to convince two people that it's super special and one of them is already on side. Ontario Premier Doug Ford has stepped up to help his native city in a big way. The provincial government is providing $1.2 billion in operating money over three years, as well as $3 billion in capital support over 10 years.
The city's budget presentation features a picture of Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow shaking hands, looking delighted over what they call the "New Deal." No doubt Franklin Delano Ford hopes Toronto voters remember the largesse at election time. He's certainly come a long way since last year's mayoral byelection, when he said a Chow victory would be "an unmitigated disaster."
Now it's Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's turn to face the Chow challenge. He has already been wrong-footed. Rather than make a generic request for support, the city wants $250 million to cover the cost of operating 4,800 shelter beds that are occupied by refugees. It's a reasonable request. The federal government likes to pat itself on the back for accepting refugees, but then it leaves costs to someone else. Why should people who pay property taxes in Toronto bear the burden of this federal program? Chow has put Trudeau and Toronto MPs in an uncomfortable position. Either the federal government comes up with $250 million, fast, or the city will impose what it's calling the "Federal Impacts Levy," driving the total property tax increase up to 16.5 per cent and blaming the feds.
This is a game of political chicken and it's a good bet that the Liberals will blink. No doubt they realize that their political future depends on support in Toronto. Why risk that to save what is pocket change by federal squandering standards? Toronto has strengthened its position by finally proposing to do something about the city's years of under-taxation and overspending. A consulting study done for the city last year showed that Toronto's property tax rates are significantly below what almost all other Ontario municipalities charge. The difference is particularly marked when Toronto is compared to neighbouring jurisdictions. Nearly half of them have tax rates that are at least 30 per cent higher than the city of Toronto's. Even with a 10.5 per cent tax rate increase, Toronto's tax rates would still be lower than in many cities.
The city's draft budget proposes $620 million in spending reductions, about two-thirds of that the result of line-by-line reviews of spending by the city and its many agencies. That makes it a lot tougher for federal politicians to argue that the city is asking for more money without cutting its own costs.
Toronto's adjustments to spending and taxing are difficult, but long overdue. For years, city politicians took the easy route of keeping property taxes at the rate of inflation without making the tough choices required to keep spending in sync.
Even with federal and provincial support, it will take some political courage to pass the proposed 10.5 per cent tax increase. The ever canny Chow has left herself some wiggle room. She has not yet publicly endorsed the staff-proposed budget, although it's impossible to think that she is not behind the concept.
When she ran last year, Chow promised a "moderate" tax increase. No one could argue 10.5 per cent is that, but the mayor has little choice but to follow through after pressuring the two other governments to act.
If she doesn't, she won't look quite as smart as she does today.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 487 of 500

Document 492 of 500

Poilievre slams 'incompetent' Montreal and Quebec City mayors for drop in housing construction

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-slams-incompetent-montreal-quebec-city/docview/2916462866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Poilievre+slams+%27incompetent%27+Montreal+and+Quebec+City+mayors+for+drop+in+housing+construction&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stirred up a storm in Quebec by calling Montreal mayor Valérie Plante and Quebec City mayor Bruno Marchand "incompetent" on X Thursday and then doubling down on his statement when confronted.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said he was "extremely disappointed" by Poilievre's "contempt" for the Quebec big-city mayors and called on him to apologize.
"It's been several times now that he's demonstrated disdain and ignorance on how things work between the federal government and the provinces," said Trudeau in Nunavut, on Thursday. "I think it's high time that he apologizes for his behaviour," he added.
Poilievre was criticizing last year's historic drop in construction builds in Quebec which, according to recent data from the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation, shows that there were 32 per cent less shovels in the ground in the province than the year prior.
The data was even more alarming for the cities of Montreal, which saw construction builds drop by 37 per cent, and the Quebec City area, which saw a 40 per cent drop.
Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of allocating billions of dollars to the "incompetent mayors, Marchand and Plante, who are blocking construction sites."
He promised to tie federal funds to the number of houses and apartments built in cities if he becomes prime minister.
Plante shot back at Poilievre, also on X, reminding him that federal funds for housing in Quebec are given to the province and not directly to municipalities.
"Common sense is also understanding the financing mechanisms specific to each province," she wrote, referring to the Conservatives' French slogan "gros bon sens."
Marchand said Poilievre was insulting not only Quebec City's elected officials but also all his civil servants working on the housing files.
"It's petty politics. Quebec does not deserve this contempt," he wrote on X.
Even the Union des municipalités du Québec, which represents most of the province's cities, put out a statement on the same platform to condemn Poilievre's "disparaging remarks" and invited him to "avoid making simplistic shortcuts and to show some respect."
Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet said that people could like or dislike Plante and Marchand, but they were still elected by their constituents.
He added that Poilievre had gotten "no one" elected in Quebec yet.
Poilievre responded to Plante and Marchand on X by accusing them of blocking construction in their respective cities while their constituents are struggling with a housing crisis and said that Trudeau was encouraging their failures by injecting more money into bureaucracy.
On the other hand, he congratulated the cities of Trois-Rivières, Victoriaville and Saguenay for building more housing than before.
The federal and Quebec governments announced last November that they would each invest $900 million over the next four years to accelerate housing construction in Quebec.
"This new agreement with the government of Quebec will mean more ambitious plans right across the province to build more homes faster," said Trudeau at the time.
The $1.8-billion investment is expected to create 8,000 social and affordable housing units and help build tens of thousands of additional homes in the longer term.
CHMC economist Bob Dugan told Radio-Canada that the lack of construction builds in Quebec was caused by a limited borrowing capacity because of higher interest rates and labour shortages, but said those pressures are expected to ease in a few months.
The province's lack of construction builds, however, dragged down the national average which was down 7 per cent in 2023 compared to the year prior. By removing Quebec from the equation, builds across the country only dropped by 2 per cent.
It is not the first time that Poilievre has insinuated that Marchand, a progressive first-time mayor, is "incompetent."
The Conservative leader made a similar accusation on the margins of his party's convention in Quebec City last September when asked about possible cost overruns for Marchand's deeply unpopular tramway project in the city.
Poilievre told reporters that he would not "throw away billions of dollars for mismanaged projects by incompetent politicians."
Marchand said in a radio interview later that day that he hopes Poilievre does not become prime minister.
Freeland touts government subsidies to Davos leaders looking to invest in Canada's 'decarbonization'
Trudeau government needs to clarify stance on 'genocide' claims against Israel, ambassador says

Document 493 of 500

Randall Denley: Olivia Chow has the Trudeau Liberals in a tight corner - for now

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-olivia-chow-has-trudeau-liberals/docview/2916442063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Olivia+Chow+has+the+Trudeau+Liberals+in+a+tight+corner+-+for+now&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city of Toronto's proposed 2024 budget is both outrageous and a master class in political manoeuvring. At a time when people are struggling financially, who would suggest a 10.5-per-cent tax increase, then threaten to boost it by an additional six per cent if the federal government doesn't pony up more money?
And yet, Toronto needs a big strategy to solve its big financial problem, a looming $1.8-billion budget deficit. The city has been struggling with budget shortfalls for years, never taking dramatic action. The city's new plan is certainly bold and it's based on shrewd political calculation.
The premise of the budget is that Toronto is unique - a city so large, so special, that it deserves financial help not only from Ontario taxpayers, but from every taxpayer in the country. The case for that is a bit weak. Under the heading "The Challenges Toronto Faces Are Unique," the city budget presentation raises no fewer than 16 reasons why this is supposedly the case. But it lists things like rising inflation, increased costs and reliance on property taxes, which are common to all cities.
Fortunately, Toronto only has to convince two people that it's super special and one of them is already on side. Ontario Premier Doug Ford has stepped up to help his native city in a big way. The provincial government is providing $1.2 billion in operating money over three years, as well as $3 billion in capital support over 10 years.
Olivia Chow's tax hike would be unnecessary and destructive
Don't worry Alberta, freezing in the dark will become normal
The city's budget presentation features a picture of Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow shaking hands, looking delighted over what they call the "New Deal." No doubt Franklin Delano Ford hopes Toronto voters remember the largesse at election time. He's certainly come a long way since last year's mayoral byelection, when he said a Chow victory would be "an unmitigated disaster."
Now it's Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's turn to face the Chow challenge. He has already been wrong-footed. Rather than make a generic request for support, the city wants $250 million to cover the cost of operating 4,800 shelter beds that are occupied by refugees. It's a reasonable request. The federal government likes to pat itself on the back for accepting refugees, but then it leaves costs to someone else. Why should people who pay property taxes in Toronto bear the burden of this federal program?
Chow has put Trudeau and Toronto MPs in an uncomfortable position. Either the federal government comes up with $250 million, fast, or the city will impose what it's calling the "Federal Impacts Levy," driving the total property tax increase up to 16.5 per cent and blaming the feds.
This is a game of political chicken and it's a good bet that the Liberals will blink. No doubt they realize that their political future, such as it is, depends on support in Toronto. Why risk that to save what is pocket change by federal squandering standards?
Toronto has strengthened its position by finally proposing to do something about the city's years of under-taxation and overspending. A consulting study done for the city last year showed that Toronto's property tax rates are significantly below what almost all other Ontario municipalities charge. The difference is particularly marked when Toronto is compared to neighbouring jurisdictions. Nearly half of them have tax rates that are at least 30-per-cent higher than the city of Toronto's. Even with a 10.5-per-cent tax rate increase, Toronto's tax rates would still be lower than in many cities.
The city's draft budget proposes $620 million in spending reductions, about two-thirds of that the result of line-by-line reviews of spending by the city and its many agencies. That makes it a lot tougher for federal politicians to argue that the city is asking for more money without cutting its own costs.
Toronto's adjustments to spending and taxing are difficult, but long overdue. For years, city politicians took the easy route of keeping property taxes at the rate of inflation without making the tough choices required to keep spending in sync.
Even with federal and provincial support, it will take some political courage to pass the proposed 10.5-per-cent tax increase. The ever canny Chow has left herself some wiggle room. She has not yet publicly endorsed the staff-proposed budget, although it's impossible to think that she is not behind the concept.
When she ran last year, Chow promised a "moderate" tax increase. No one could argue 10.5 per cent is that, but the mayor has little choice but to follow through after pressuring the two other governments to act. If she doesn't, she won't look quite as smart as she does today.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Costs Budget deficits Property taxes Tax increases; Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices

Subject: Tax rates; Mayors; Costs; Politics; Cities; Budget deficits; Pro perty taxes; Tax increases

Identifier / keyword: proposed; budget; outrageous; master; class; political

Title: Randall Denley: Olivia Chow has the Trudeau Liberals in a tight corner - for now

Document 494 of 500

Controversial anti-racism consultant Laith Marouf now running anti-Israel 'Free Palestine Television' channel

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/controversial-anti-racism-consultant-laith-marouf/docview/2916377539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Controversial+anti-racism+consultant+Laith+Marouf+now+running+anti-Israel+%27Free+Palestine+Television%27+channel&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Laith Marouf, the government-funded anti-racism consultant whose record of hateful tweets became an embarrassing controversy for the Liberals, is now the director of an anti-Israel online broadcaster.
Free Palestine Television (FPTV) was launched after the Hamas massacre of Israeli civilians on Oct. 7 and has featured footage of Hamas's attacks on Israeli soldiers as well as manoeuvres from Hamas-affiliated militias such as the Mujahideen Brigades and Al-Qassam Brigades, which operate out of Gaza and the West Bank. It also published speeches with live translation to English from Hezbollah leader Sayyed Hassan Nasrallah and it publishes press releases attributed to various terror groups, such as Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iran-backed militias.
Marouf, who's identified on its site as FPTV's founder, is a senior consultant with the Community Media Advocacy Centre, which had a $122,000 contract with the Liberal government in 2022 to develop an anti-racism strategy for the broadcasting sector.
It subsequently emerged that Marouf had a history of hateful social media posts about Jews, Francophones and Black people.
One read: "You know all those loud mouthed bags of human feces, a.k.a. the Jewish White Supremacists; when we liberate Palestine and they have to go back to where they come from, they will return to being low voiced b--s of their Christian/Secular White Supremacist Masters."
The tweets were exposed in news reports and the government cancelled the contract in August 2022, although it emerged that the Trudeau government had known about the tweets a month earlier and had done nothing.
Marouf's lawyer at the time said the posts were not directed at all Jews, but just certain ones.
Ottawa taking legal action to recoup anti-racism funds from Laith Marouf: bureaucrat
Jamie Sarkonak: Federal government still hasn't managed to collect Laith Marouf's revoked grant
Canadian Heritage has since changed the way it vets funding requests for community and anti-racism projects.
The federal government has sought to recoup its funds from Marouf through a collections agency, the Canada Revenue Agency and the courts, but has reported no success. Reports in October 2022 said Marouf was living in Beirut.
Since the national scandal over funding to Marouf's organization, he has become a regular on various left-wing social media programs, offering opinions on Palestinian politics, Israel and other geopolitical issues. Marouf has also appeared several times on Critical Hour, a podcast hosted by Sputnik News, a state-owned Russian broadcaster, and on programs by Russian propaganda network Russia Today
FPTV's website says it launched "with the outbreak of the Al-Aqsa Flood" - the name Hamas and other Palestinian groups have given to the Oct. 7 massacre. The online station is based out of Lebanon, and is staffed primarily by students, professors and community members, Marouf said in an interview.
"FPTV's goal is to support the resistance from a media standpoint and to incite free peoples to move and fight Zionism and imperialism wherever they exist," the online channel's website says.
"Knowing that there is a huge gap left in news programming in English that is supportive of resistance and the right to liberation ... we decided to bring together our communities here to start a project, a community television station," Marouf said in an interview with Activist News Network in November 2023.
The Middle East Forum, an American conservative think tank, published a report on Friday claiming that FPTV had links to the Lebanon-based group Al-Tajammu. According to Michael Barak, a researcher at the Institute for Counter-Terrorism at Israel's Reichmann University, Al-Tajammu is "an international pro-Iranian platform to leverage the resistance axis against the U.S., Israel and their allies."
Marouf described the attacks on Al-Tajammu as "frivolous" and accused the Middle East Forum of being a "racist organization."
Marouf and FPTV also downplayed any connections to Al-Tajammu.
In a series of posts to X on Saturday, FPTV alleged that "the largest Jewish White Supremacist accounts in Apartheid Canada are mounting a disinformation campaign against our Community Television station," and that it "is unaffiliated with any political party or groups, including Al-Tajammu which hosted our studios for a brief time & we have since moved as we expanded."
Marouf said Al-Tajammu was one of "many" Lebanese community associations that supported the drive to create FPTV, and that he had been invited to give a presentation about it at one of the group's Zoom meetings.
"So our connection with Al-Tajammu is very brief and ... we don't have any financial or board connections to them," he said.
The Middle East Forum report, and subsequent social media posts, suggested that Free Palestine TV was "established" in Canada.
Marouf told National Post: "It's not established in Canada. It's not based in Canada. There's no connection there."
In mid-December, FPTV hosted a fundraiser for the channel on Zoom, which featured various guest speakers, several of them prominent figures in Indigenous advocacy in Canada. Among the expected speakers were Sleydo', also known as Molly Wickham, a Wet'suwet'en chief and prominent opponent of the Coastal GasLink pipeline in British Columbia, Judy Da Silva, a prominent member of the Grassy Narrows First Nation in northwestern Ontario, and Isaac Murdoch, an artist and member of the Serpent River First Nation. National Post did not attend the event and was unable to confirm that all the guests appeared.
Earlier this month, the channel hosted a live broadcast and translation of a speech from Nasrallah, the secretary-general of Hezbollah, a banned terrorist organization in Canada. It also hosted a speech from Nasrallah in early November.
"I cannot expect anything but more valiant, honourable positions from Sayyed Hasan Nasrallah," Marouf said on FPTV on Nov. 2. "This man ... has never said anything that he hasn't delivered on."

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks Television stations; Industry: 51612 :‎ Television Broadcasting Stations

Subject: White supremacy; Racism; Social networks; Jewish people; Community; Television stations; Scandals; Militia groups

Document 495 of 500

Jesse Kline: Don't worry Alberta, freezing in the dark will become normal

Author: Kline, Jesse

Publication info: Kline, Jesse. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jesse-kline-dont-worry-alberta-freezing-dark-will/docview/2916358179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jesse+Kline%3A+Don%27t+worry+Alberta%2C+freezing+in+the+dark+will+become+normal&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kline%2C+Jesse&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta's unprecedented emergency alert over the weekend warning residents to reduce power consumption or face rolling blackouts as temperatures dropped below -45 C in parts of the province offers a taste of what's to come if Ottawa pushes ahead with its net-zero timeline.
Demand for electricity in the province reached an all-time high on Thursday, but supply was able to keep pace until a cold weather system that gripped much of western North America led to a confluence of events that threatened to destabilize the grid.
On Friday, wind generation ground to a halt due to a lack of wind and frigid temperatures, which makes wind turbines unsafe to operate. Neighbouring British Columbia, Saskatchewan and Montana were also in a deep freeze, which limited their ability to export power. A couple thermal generators shut down due to the weather and mechanical issues, while two gas-fired power plants also went offline due to maintenance and weather-related issues.
As a result, the Alberta Electric System Operator (AESO) issued four alerts over four days. The greatest threat occurred on Saturday evening, which prompted the Alberta Emergency Management Agency to send an alert asking residents to conserve power by turning off lights, cooking with microwaves instead of electric ovens, powering down space heaters, using battery-powered laptops and unplugging electric vehicles.
Albertans pulled through, reportedly reducing the load by around 200 megawatts within minutes. But in an era in which everything from cars to stoves to furnaces run on electricity, asking an entire province to turn out the lights and stop charging devices is not a great solution.
The good news is that the series of events that occurred over the weekend are unlikely to happen all that often, and a number of new gas generators expected to come online later this year should provide more reliable base-load power in the medium term.
The bad news is that federal regulations designed to electrify transportation and home heating, coupled with clean electricity regulations intended to phase out fossil-fuel generation, will exacerbate the problem in the coming years.
Alberta currently has relatively few electric vehicles on the road, with EVs constituting just 3.5 per cent of new vehicle registrations in 2022. Yet the federal government will require 20 per cent of new vehicles to be electric by 2026, increasing to 60 per cent by the end of the decade and 100 per cent in 2035.
The AESO's latest "Long-term Outlook," released in 2021, predicted that by 2041, Alberta will see between 200,000 and two-million EVs hit the road. Given the federal mandate, it's likely that the upper end of that estimate will prove more realistic. And if you think electricity demand is high now, just you wait: plugging in two-millions cars would draw twice as much power as the entire city of Calgary.
But cars and trucks are only part of the equation. The federal carbon tax, which is set to more than double by 2030, coupled with subsidies for green retrofits, will see more Albertans switching away from gas furnaces, water heaters, dryers and stoves - all of which will put further strain on the electrical grid, especially during cold snaps, when heat pumps have to work overtime (if they work at all).
At the same time, the federal government is trying to strong-arm the provinces into decarbonizing their grids by 2035 - just in time for the mass influx of power-hungry EVs. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has promised to fight Ottawa's net-zero electricity regulations and there's no guarantee the Liberals will be in power long enough to see them through. But the uncertainty the Liberals have created will undoubtedly reduce investment in new gas plants, leaving provinces like Alberta to scramble for new sources of base-load power to handle the coming spike in demand.
Options do exist, but it will take a huge amount of private investment or government funds to replace existing coal and natural gas generators, which constituted over 80 per cent of Alberta's energy mix in 2022, or implement costly carbon capture systems, while vastly increasing generating capacity.
On Monday, Alberta-based Capital Power Corp. announced an agreement with Ontario Power Generation to "jointly assess the development and deployment of grid-scale small modular (nuclear) reactors" in the province. If they deem them to be viable, the province's first reactor could be operational by 2035.
But that's a big if, given that the technology is very much in its infancy and the province would have to create a whole new regulatory regime to deal with it. And even if it did come to fruition, Alberta would need many more to charge all the EVs expected to be on the road.
Other technologies, such as grid-scale batteries and pump-storage hydroelectricity, can also be used to provide base-load power by storing excess energy produced from wind and solar. Alberta currently only has a handful of battery storage facilities and will need many more if it hopes to supply sufficient power to the grid during peak times.
But that would mean vastly increasing its clean energy production, which is hampered by the government's moratorium on new green energy projects and limited by short days during wintertime and the fact that wind turbines can't operate below -30 C. And then there's the issue of batteries only being able to supply the grid for short periods of time.
None of these problems are insurmountable, but they will be costly, as electricity prices will have to be high enough to attract investment in nuclear, renewables and energy-storage facilities. They will also take time, which is not something Ottawa seems all that interested in providing.
It's highly unlikely that a power grid that's so reliant on fossil fuels will be able to achieve net zero in just over a decade, while supplying enough electricity to power a fleet of new EVs and electric appliances. So get ready for phones blaring directives to turn out the lights, and cold nights without any power.
After Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau implemented the National Energy Program in the early 1980s, it was common to see bumper stickers in Alberta reading, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark." It seems as though Trudeau the younger is intent on enacting his revenge - ensuring westerners will be the ones freezing in the dark.
National Post
jkline@postmedia.com
Twitter.com/accessd
Jesse Kline: Trudeau's clean electricity regulations are a direct assault on provincial rights
Jesse Kline: Steven Guilbeault's bad-faith plan to ban gas-powered cars
